After the verb رأيتُ (I saw), the object takes the accusative case, so the dual suffix is ـَيْنِ (-ayni) instead of ـانِ (-āni). This case distinction in the dual is important: use ـانِ when the dual noun is the subject (nominative), and ـَيْنِ when it's the object or follows a preposition. This system provides clarity about grammatical function.

items.question: _____ جميلتانِ.
items.explanation: الحديقتانِ (al-ḥadīqatāni) is the dual of حديقة (garden). When a feminine noun ending in ة becomes dual, the ة changes back to ت before adding the dual suffix: حديقة → حديقتان. The adjective جميلتانِ also takes the dual feminine form. Dual agreement means both the noun AND its adjective must be in dual form — unlike non-human plurals which take feminine singular. This dual agreement rule applies to adjectives, verbs, and demonstratives.

items.question: الطالبانِ _____ في المكتبة.
items.translation: (The two students are studying in the library.)
items.explanation: يدرسانِ (yadrusāni) is the dual verb form — the present tense for 'they two (m.) study.' When the subject is dual, the verb must also take a dual suffix. For masculine dual: يدرسانِ, for feminine dual: تدرسانِ. The dual verb suffix ـانِ is added to the base form. This dual verb conjugation is unique to Arabic among major modern languages and represents a level of grammatical precision that most languages have lost over time.

items.question: اشتريتُ _____ من الفاكهة.
items.explanation: كيلوَيْنِ (kīluwayni) is the accusative dual of كيلو (kilo). After اشتريتُ (I bought), the object is accusative, requiring the ـَيْنِ suffix. Measurement words frequently appear in dual form for everyday quantities: كوبَيْنِ (two cups), متريْنِ (two meters), ساعتَيْنِ (two hours). The dual form is commonly used with numbers and measurements in practical daily Arabic — at the market, in cooking, and when telling time.

items.question: _____ مشغولانِ اليوم.
items.explanation: المعلمانِ (al-muʿallimāni) is the dual of معلم (teacher). The adjective مشغولانِ (mashghūlāni) also takes the dual form to agree. In Arabic, you'll encounter the dual form frequently in news: البلدانِ (the two countries), الرئيسانِ (the two presidents), الفريقانِ (the two teams). Dual forms are especially common in sports reporting, diplomatic news, and any context where pairs are discussed.

items.question: في _____ فقط.
items.explanation: دقيقتَيْنِ (daqīqatayni) is the genitive dual of دقيقة (minute). After the preposition في, the noun takes the genitive case, using the ـَيْنِ suffix. Time expressions commonly use the dual: ساعتَيْنِ (two hours), يومَيْنِ (two days), أسبوعَيْنِ (two weeks), شهرَيْنِ (two months), سنتَيْنِ (two years). These dual time expressions are essential for scheduling, planning, and telling stories in Arabic.

items.question: هاتانِ _____ جديدتانِ.
items.explanation: هاتانِ (hātāni) is the feminine dual demonstrative meaning 'these two.' It matches سيارتانِ (sayyāratāni, 'two cars') in the feminine dual. Arabic demonstratives have dual forms: هذانِ/هاتانِ (these two m./f.), ذانِكَ/تانِكَ (those two m./f.). The demonstrative must agree with the noun in gender and number. This triple agreement system (demonstrative + noun + adjective all in dual) shows Arabic's remarkable grammatical precision.

items.question: لي _____.
items.explanation: أختانِ (ukhtāni) is the dual of أخت (sister). Family vocabulary in dual form is common: أخوانِ (two brothers), ولدانِ (two children), ابنتانِ (two daughters). Arabic speakers use the dual naturally in family discussions — 'I have two brothers' is لي أخوانِ, not لي اثنان من الإخوة. The dual form is more concise and natural-sounding than using the number + plural construction.

items.question: _____ تعملانِ في المستشفى.
items.translation: (The two (female) doctors work at the hospital.)
items.explanation: الطبيبتانِ (aṭ-ṭabībatāni) is the feminine dual of طبيبة (female doctor). The verb تعملانِ (taʿmalāni) is the feminine dual verb form — notice the ت prefix (feminine) plus ـانِ (dual suffix). When both subject and verb must be dual feminine, you get this elegant construction. The precision of Arabic's gender+number system means that from the verb alone, you can tell the subject is exactly two females.

items.question: سافرَ إلى _____ عربيَّتَيْنِ.
items.explanation: دولتَيْنِ (dawlatayni) is the genitive dual of دولة (country/state). After the preposition إلى, the noun takes genitive case with the ـَيْنِ suffix. The adjective عربيَّتَيْنِ (ʿarabiyyatayni) also takes the genitive dual form to agree. This full agreement chain — preposition + dual noun + dual adjective all in genitive — demonstrates Arabic's systematic case and number agreement at work.

items.question: على _____ الطاولة.
items.explanation: جانبَيِ (jānibay) is the dual of جانب (side) in the construct state (الإضافة). When a dual noun is the first part of an إضافة, it drops the final ن: جانبانِ → جانبَيِ (before a genitive noun). This ن-dropping rule is a key feature of Arabic dual grammar. It also applies to sound masculine plurals: معلمون → معلمي (teachers of...). This rule distinguishes possession from simple dual reference.

items.question: هذانِ _____ مفيدانِ.
items.explanation: هذانِ (hādhāni) is the masculine dual demonstrative, and الدرسانِ (ad-darsāni) is the dual of درس (lesson). The adjective مفيدانِ (mufīdāni) completes the triple dual agreement. Notice that even non-human nouns can take dual adjective agreement (unlike non-human plurals which take feminine singular). The dual number is treated differently from the plural in agreement rules — dual always gets dual agreement regardless of whether the noun is human or not.

items.question: قرأتُ _____ الصحيفة.
items.translation: (I read both pages of the newspaper.)
items.explanation: صفحتَيِ (ṣafḥatay) is the dual of صفحة (page) in construct state. After the verb قرأتُ (I read), the object would normally be accusative, and in construct state the dual drops its ن. The construct (إضافة) relationship between صفحتي and الصحيفة means 'two pages of the newspaper.' This construction smoothly connects the counted item with what it belongs to, a natural and common pattern in Arabic prose.

items.question: انتظرتُ _____.
items.explanation: ساعتَيْنِ (sāʿatayni) is the accusative dual of ساعة (hour). As the object of انتظرتُ (I waited), it takes the accusative/genitive dual suffix ـَيْنِ. Duration expressions in Arabic frequently use the dual: نِمتُ ساعتَيْنِ (I slept two hours), درستُ يومَيْنِ (I studied two days). The dual in time expressions feels natural and efficient — no need for the word 'two' (اثنان/اثنتان) because the dual form itself communicates the number.

name: Future Tense (المستقبل)
description: Learn to express future actions using سوف and the prefix سـ

items.question: أنا _____ أذهبُ إلى المكتبة غداً.
items.translation: (I will go to the library tomorrow.)
items.explanation: In Arabic, the future tense is formed by adding 'سوف' (sawfa) or the prefix 'سـ' (sa-) before a present tense verb. 'سوف' is slightly more emphatic and formal than 'سـ'. Here, 'سوف أذهبُ' means 'I will go.' This is one of the simplest constructions in Arabic — no verb conjugation change needed, just add the future marker before the present tense form.

items.question: هي _____ في الجامعة السنة القادمة.
items.translation: (She will study at the university next year.)
items.explanation: The prefix 'سـ' (sa-) is the shorter alternative to 'سوف' for expressing the future. It attaches directly to the present tense verb. 'تدرسُ' (she studies) becomes 'ستدرسُ' (she will study). Both 'سـ' and 'سوف' are correct, but 'سـ' is more common in everyday speech. Note how the verb 'تدرسُ' already carries the feminine marker 'ت' at the beginning.

items.question: نحن _____ نسافرُ إلى مصر.
items.explanation: For 'we' in the future tense, place 'سوف' before the present tense 'we' form. 'نسافرُ' means 'we travel,' and 'سوف نسافرُ' means 'we will travel.' The beauty of this system is its simplicity — once you know the present tense conjugation, you can express the future with just one addition. Arabic grammarians call this المضارع المستقبل (the future present).

items.question: هم _____ من العمل مبكراً.
items.explanation: For the masculine plural 'they' in the future, attach 'سـ' to the present tense form 'يعودونَ' (they return), creating 'سيعودونَ' (they will return). The plural marker 'ون' stays intact. Notice how Arabic future is more economical than English — one prefix does the work of the English auxiliary verb 'will.' This prefix system is consistent across all subjects.

items.question: _____ أنسى هذا اليوم أبداً.
items.explanation: To negate the future ('will not'), Arabic uses 'لَنْ' (lan) followed by a verb in the subjunctive mood (منصوب). 'لَنْ أنسى' means 'I will never forget.' Unlike the affirmative future where 'سوف/سـ' precedes the indicative, 'لَنْ' changes the verb's final vowel. This creates a clear grammatical distinction between 'I will do' and 'I will not do.' The word 'أبداً' adds emphasis.

items.question: هل _____ في الحفلة؟
items.explanation: To form a future question, use the question particle 'هل' followed by the future verb. 'ستحضرُ' combines the future prefix 'سـ' with 'تحضرُ' (you attend). Arabic questions can also use rising intonation alone, but 'هل' makes the question explicit. This mirrors English 'Will you...?' — the structure is remarkably parallel, making future questions intuitive for English speakers.

items.question: الطقس _____ بارداً في الشتاء.
items.translation: (The weather will be cold in winter.)
items.explanation: The verb 'يكونُ' (to be, present) becomes 'سيكونُ' (will be) with the future prefix. Unlike present tense where Arabic often drops 'to be,' future tense requires it explicitly. 'سيكونُ بارداً' literally means 'will-be cold.' The verb كان/يكون is one of the most important in Arabic, and its future form 'سيكون' appears frequently in daily speech, news, and formal writing.

items.question: أعتقد أنها _____ تنجحَ.
items.explanation: After verbs of thinking or believing like 'أعتقد' (I think), you can use 'أنّ' (that) followed by the future construction. 'سوف تنجحَ' expresses a confident expectation. In subordinate clauses like this, 'سوف' is often preferred over 'سـ' because it sounds more deliberate. This mirrors how English uses 'will' after 'I think that...' — the future marker conveys the speaker's belief about upcoming events.

items.question: _____ يتوقفَ المطرُ قريباً.
items.explanation: Again, 'لَنْ' negates the future. Notice how 'يتوقفَ' takes a فتحة (fatḥa) on the last letter instead of ضمة (ḍamma) — this is because 'لَنْ' puts the verb in the subjunctive mood (المنصوب). Compare: 'سيتوقفُ' (it will stop, indicative) vs. 'لن يتوقفَ' (it will not stop, subjunctive). This vowel change is a key grammatical signal that Arabic speakers listen for.

items.question: الأطفال _____ يكبرونَ بسرعة.
items.explanation: With the masculine plural 'الأطفال' (children), the verb takes the 'they' form 'يكبرونَ' (they grow). Adding 'سوف' creates the future: 'سوف يكبرونَ' (they will grow up). Note that collective nouns in Arabic can take either singular or plural verb agreement depending on context. Here, the plural verb emphasizes each child individually. This flexibility is a rich feature of Arabic expression.

items.question: _____ أتصلُ بكَ عندما أصلُ.
items.translation: (I will call you when I arrive.)
items.explanation: In time clauses with 'عندما' (when), Arabic uses the future in the main clause just like English. 'سأتصلُ' uses the short prefix 'سـ' attached directly to 'أتصلُ' (I call). In casual speech, 'سـ' is strongly preferred over 'سوف' because it's quicker. The subordinate clause 'عندما أصلُ' stays in present tense, paralleling English 'when I arrive' — both languages avoid double future marking.

items.question: هل تعتقد أنهم _____ يوافقوا؟
items.explanation: In embedded questions after 'هل تعتقد أنّ' (do you think that), the future is expressed normally with 'سوف.' Note that 'يوافقوا' loses its final ن because of the subjunctive context after 'أنّ' — this is called حذف النون. The full form would be 'يوافقون' but the subjunctive shortens it. This nuance shows how Arabic grammar tracks clause relationships precisely.

items.question: المعلمة _____ الدرسَ بعد الاستراحة.
items.translation: (The teacher will explain the lesson after the break.)
items.explanation: For feminine subjects like 'المعلمة' (the female teacher), the verb uses the feminine prefix 'ت' and adds 'سـ' for future: 'ستشرحُ' (she will explain). Arabic consistently marks gender in verbs, which gives listeners immediate information about who is performing the action. The root ش-ر-ح means 'to explain/clarify' and follows the standard Form I pattern.

items.question: _____ نحتاجَ إلى مساعدة.
items.explanation: 'لَنْ نحتاجَ' means 'we will not need.' The verb 'نحتاجُ' (we need) changes its final vowel from ضمة to فتحة after 'لَنْ,' entering the subjunctive. This pattern is absolutely consistent in Arabic — whenever 'لَنْ' negates a future action, the verb's ending changes. Mastering this vowel shift is essential for correct Arabic pronunciation and is one of the hallmarks of intermediate-level competence.

items.question: _____ أبذلُ قصارى جهدي.
items.explanation: 'سوف أبذلُ قصارى جهدي' is a common motivational expression meaning 'I will exert my utmost effort.' Using 'سوف' rather than 'سـ' gives this a more emphatic, determined tone — perfect for promises and resolutions. The phrase 'قصارى جهدي' (utmost of my effort) is a beautiful idiomatic expression that combines the superlative 'قصارى' with the possessive 'جهدي.' This is everyday Arabic at its most expressive.

name: Imperative Mood (الأمر)
description: Form commands and requests in Arabic

items.question: _____ الكتابَ من فضلك.
items.explanation: The Arabic imperative (فعل الأمر) is formed from the present tense by removing the prefix and adding a sukūn to the last letter. From 'يقرأُ' (he reads), drop 'ي' and get 'قرأْ' — then add 'اِ' because the resulting cluster needs a vowel. 'اِقرأْ' means 'Read!' This is historically significant: the first word revealed in the Quran was 'اقرأ' (Read!), making this verb deeply embedded in Arabic culture.

items.question: _____ البابَ!
items.explanation: From the Form IV verb 'يُغلِقُ' (he closes), the imperative is 'أَغلِقْ' (Close!). Form IV verbs (أَفعَلَ) have a distinctive imperative pattern with a hamza prefix. The root غ-ل-ق means 'to lock/close.' In daily life, you'll hear this command frequently: 'أغلق الباب' (close the door), 'أغلق النافذة' (close the window). The sharp sukūn at the end gives commands their decisive, authoritative sound.

items.question: _____ هنا يا أولاد!
items.explanation: For plural masculine commands, add 'وا' to the singular imperative. 'اِجلِسْ' (sit! - singular) becomes 'اِجلِسوا' (sit! - plural). The vocative 'يا أولاد' (O boys!) is how you address a group. This plural imperative is essential in classrooms, where teachers constantly use it: 'اِجلِسوا' (sit!), 'اِفتحوا' (open!), 'اُكتُبوا' (write!). Each follows the same pattern of adding 'وا.'

items.question: _____ بسرعة يا فاطمة!
items.explanation: The feminine singular imperative adds 'ي' to the base form. 'تعالَ' (come! - masculine) becomes 'تعالَيْ' (come! - feminine). The verb 'تعالَ' is irregular — it doesn't follow standard imperative rules but is one of the most frequently used commands. Addressing someone by name with 'يا' is common Arabic etiquette. Gender distinction in commands shows Arabic's precision in addressing individuals respectfully.

items.question: لا _____ في المكتبة!
items.explanation: Negative commands use 'لا' + the jussive (مجزوم) form of the present tense — NOT the imperative. 'لا تتكلَّمْ' means 'don't speak.' This is fundamentally different from the affirmative imperative. English uses 'don't + verb,' and Arabic similarly uses 'لا' + present tense with sukūn. The doubled letter 'لّ' in 'تتكلَّمْ' comes from the Form V pattern (تَفَعَّلَ), which often indicates a deliberate or repeated action.

items.question: _____ يدَيْكَ قبل الأكل.
items.explanation: From 'يَغسِلُ' (he washes), remove 'يـ' to get 'غسِلْ,' then add 'اِ' for the vowel onset: 'اِغسِلْ' (Wash!). The root غ-س-ل is fundamental — it appears in everyday hygiene, religious ablution (الوضوء), and even laundry vocabulary. The phrase 'قبل الأكل' (before eating) uses the مصدر (verbal noun) of أكل, showing how Arabic nouns and verbs interconnect through shared roots.

items.question: _____ واجبَكَ قبل اللعب!
items.explanation: The Form IV verb 'يُنجِزُ' (he accomplishes/finishes) gives the imperative 'أَنجِزْ' (Finish!/Complete!). Parents and teachers use this daily. 'واجبَكَ' means 'your homework' — it's a possessive construction combining 'واجب' (duty/assignment) with 'كَ' (your, masculine). The word 'واجب' itself comes from the root و-ج-ب meaning obligation, reflecting Arabic's rich system of deriving related meanings from three-letter roots.

items.question: _____ الحقيقةَ دائماً.
items.explanation: 'قُلْ' (Say!/Tell!) is one of the most important imperatives in Arabic. It's irregular — from 'يقولُ,' you'd expect 'اُقُلْ,' but the hamza and first vowel are dropped, yielding simply 'قُلْ.' This word appears over 300 times in the Quran, making it among the most recognized Arabic words worldwide. In daily speech, 'قُلِ الحقيقة' (tell the truth) is a powerful moral instruction with deep cultural resonance.

items.question: _____ إلى المعلم جيداً.
items.explanation: The Form VIII verb 'يَستمِعُ' (he listens) gives the imperative 'اِستمِعْ' (Listen!). Form VIII (اِفتعَلَ) often carries a sense of effort or deliberateness — 'استمع' implies active, attentive listening, whereas 'سمع' is simply hearing. This distinction parallels English 'listen' vs. 'hear.' The preposition 'إلى' (to) is required: you listen 'to' someone, just like in English.

items.question: لا _____ القواعد!
items.explanation: 'لا تَنْسَ' (don't forget) uses the jussive of 'تنسى.' For verbs ending in alif maqṣūra (ى), the jussive drops the final letter: 'تنسى' → 'تَنْسَ.' This deletion pattern applies to all defective verbs (ناقص). It's a crucial grammatical point that distinguishes intermediate learners. The root ن-س-ي means 'to forget,' and 'القواعد' (the rules/grammar) comes from ق-ع-د, showing how Arabic builds vocabulary from roots.

items.question: _____ الله وتوكلْ عليه.
items.explanation: 'اُذكُرْ' (Remember!) from 'يَذكُرُ' — remove the prefix 'ي' and add 'اُ' (with ḍamma because the middle vowel is ḍamma). This verb is central to Islamic life: 'ذِكر الله' (remembrance of God) is a daily spiritual practice. The second imperative 'توكلْ' (rely/trust) is Form V, used commonly in the phrase 'توكلتُ على الله' (I place my trust in God). Together they form a complete spiritual instruction.

items.question: _____ الباب وراءَك.
items.explanation: 'اِفتَحْ' (Open!) from 'يَفتَحُ' follows the standard imperative formation. The root ف-ت-ح means 'to open' and is culturally important — 'الفاتحة' (The Opening) is the first chapter of the Quran. 'وراءَكَ' (behind you) uses the preposition 'وراء' + the suffix pronoun 'كَ.' Commands with directional words like this are common in everyday Arabic navigation and household instructions.

items.question: _____ معي يا صديقتي!
items.explanation: 'تعالَيْ' is the feminine imperative of 'تعالَ' (come). The suffix 'ي' marks feminine address. 'صديقتي' (my female friend) uses the feminine ending 'ة' → 'ت' before the possessive 'ي.' Arabic carefully maintains gender-specific forms in commands, making communication precise and respectful. The vocative 'يا' before names or titles is essential Arabic etiquette — it softens commands and shows you're addressing someone directly.

items.question: لا _____ الماءَ!
items.explanation: 'لا تُبَذِّرْ' (don't waste) uses the Form II verb 'يُبَذِّرُ' in the jussive after 'لا.' Form II (فَعَّلَ) often intensifies meaning — 'بَذَرَ' (to sow seeds) becomes 'بَذَّرَ' (to scatter/waste). This environmental message reflects modern Arabic discourse about conservation. The negative imperative formula 'لا + jussive' is essential for prohibitions, warnings, and rules in Arabic-speaking societies.

items.question: _____ الدواءَ في موعده.
items.explanation: 'خُذْ' (Take!) is the imperative of 'يأخذُ' — another irregular form where the initial hamza drops. The full pattern would be 'اُؤخُذْ' but usage over centuries simplified it to 'خُذْ.' This is one of Arabic's most common verbs. 'في موعده' (on its time/schedule) uses the preposition 'في' with the noun 'موعد' (appointment/scheduled time) and the possessive suffix 'ه.' Medical instructions frequently use this construction.

name: Verbal Nouns (المصادر)
description: Master the masdar — the Arabic verbal noun system

items.question: _____ مفيدٌ للصحة.
items.explanation: The verbal noun (مصدر) of 'سَبَحَ' (to swim) is 'سِباحة' — it functions like the English gerund 'swimming.' Form I verbal nouns don't follow a single pattern; they must often be memorized. The pattern 'فِعالة' here indicates an activity or profession. Arabic uses مصادر where English uses gerunds or infinitives: 'السباحة مفيدة' = 'Swimming is beneficial.' The definite article 'ال' with a مصدر creates a general noun.

items.question: أحبُّ _____ الكتب.
items.explanation: The مصدر of 'قَرَأَ' (to read) is 'قِراءة' (reading). After verbs of emotion like 'أحبُّ' (I love), Arabic uses the مصدر as a direct object — 'أحبُّ قراءةَ الكتب' literally means 'I love reading-of books.' This إضافة (construct state) between the مصدر and its object is fundamental to Arabic. Unlike English where 'reading books' is a present participle phrase, Arabic treats it as a noun + genitive.

items.question: _____ أفضلُ من العلاج.
items.explanation: 'الوِقاية' (prevention) is the مصدر of 'وَقى' (to protect/prevent), following the 'فِعاية' pattern. This is a famous Arabic proverb: 'الوقاية خيرٌ من العلاج' (prevention is better than cure). Proverbs in Arabic frequently use مصادر because they express universal truths about actions. 'العلاج' (treatment) is itself a مصدر from 'عالَجَ' (Form III: to treat), demonstrating how مصادر form the backbone of abstract vocabulary.

items.question: يجب _____ النظام.
items.explanation: 'احترام' (respect) is the مصدر of the Form VIII verb 'اِحترَمَ.' Form VIII مصادر follow the pattern 'اِفتِعال,' which is highly predictable. After 'يجب' (it is necessary), Arabic uses the مصدر: 'يجب احترامُ النظام' = 'respecting the system is necessary.' This construction is common in rules and regulations. The root ح-ر-م carries meanings of sanctity and respect across many derived forms.

items.question: شكراً على _____.
items.explanation: 'المُساعَدة' (help/assistance) is the مصدر of Form III verb 'ساعَدَ' (to help). Form III مصادر typically follow 'مُفاعَلة' — very predictable. After prepositions like 'على' (on/for), the مصدر takes the genitive case: 'المُساعَدةِ.' This phrase 'شكراً على المساعدة' is everyday Arabic politeness. Notice how Arabic thanks 'on' help while English thanks 'for' help — preposition choices differ between languages.

items.question: تمَّ _____ المشروع بنجاح.
items.explanation: 'إنجاز' (completion/achievement) is the مصدر of Form IV verb 'أنجَزَ' (to accomplish). Form IV مصادر follow 'إفعال,' which is extremely regular. The passive construction 'تمَّ إنجازُ' literally means 'completing was completed' — this is the most common Arabic alternative to passive voice. Instead of saying 'the project was completed,' Arabic says 'completing the project was done.' This structure dominates formal writing and news.

items.question: _____ من الأخطاء مهمٌّ.
items.explanation: 'التعلُّم' (learning) is the مصدر of Form V verb 'تعلَّمَ' (to learn). Form V مصادر follow 'تَفَعُّل' — very regular. The full phrase shows a مصدر as subject: 'التعلُّمُ مهمٌّ' (learning is important). Adding 'من الأخطاء' (from mistakes) specifies the source. This philosophical statement demonstrates how Arabic uses مصادر for abstract ideas, just as English uses gerunds. The root ع-ل-م is one of Arabic's richest roots, producing dozens of words about knowledge.

items.question: أريدُ _____ في الامتحان.
items.explanation: 'النجاح' (success) is the مصدر of 'نجَحَ' (to succeed), following the common Form I pattern 'فَعال.' After 'أريدُ' (I want), the مصدر takes the accusative: 'النجاحَ.' This is equivalent to 'I want success' or 'I want to succeed.' Arabic often uses مصدر after 'أريد' as an alternative to 'أريد أن أنجح' (I want that I succeed). Both constructions are grammatically correct and commonly used.

items.question: بعد _____ من الجامعة سأعمل.
items.translation: (After graduating from university, I will work.)
items.explanation: 'التخرُّج' (graduation) is the مصدر of Form V 'تَخَرَّجَ' (to graduate). After 'بعد' (after), the noun takes genitive: 'التخرُّجِ.' Arabic commonly uses 'بعد/قبل + مصدر' for temporal clauses: 'بعد التخرج' (after graduating), 'قبل النوم' (before sleeping). This is more concise than English, which needs a full clause or gerund phrase. The مصدر here condenses an entire clause into two words.

items.question: _____ في الطبخ تحتاج صبراً.
items.explanation: 'المَهارة' (skill/expertise) is the مصدر of 'مَهَرَ' (to be skilled), following the 'فَعالة' pattern. 'في الطبخ' uses another مصدر: 'الطبخ' (cooking, from طبَخَ). Notice how two مصادر work together in one sentence, each serving a different grammatical function — one as subject, one as object of a preposition. This density of verbal nouns is characteristic of sophisticated Arabic expression.

items.question: _____ الأمين واجبٌ وطني.
items.explanation: 'الإبلاغ' (reporting/informing) is the مصدر of Form IV 'أبلغَ' (to report). Form IV مصادر consistently follow 'إفعال.' The adjective 'الأمين' (honest/trustworthy) modifies the مصدر, creating a definite noun phrase: 'الإبلاغ الأمين' (honest reporting). 'واجب وطني' (national duty) uses two separate nouns in an إضافة construction. This sentence showcases media and civic vocabulary.

items.question: أشكرُكَ على حُسنِ _____.
items.translation: (I thank you for your good hospitality.)
items.explanation: 'الضِّيافة' (hospitality) is the مصدر of 'ضافَ' (to host). The phrase 'حُسن الضيافة' (good hospitality) combines 'حُسن' (beauty/goodness) with the مصدر in إضافة. This expression is deeply embedded in Arab culture, where hospitality is a supreme virtue. After 'على,' both words take genitive case. The root ض-ي-ف gives us 'ضَيف' (guest), 'مُضيف' (host), and 'إضافة' (addition) — all connected through the concept of receiving and adding.

items.question: الهدف هو _____ الأداء.
items.explanation: 'تحسين' (improvement) is the مصدر of Form II 'حَسَّنَ' (to improve). Form II مصادر follow 'تَفعيل,' which is one of the most productive patterns. 'تحسين الأداء' (improvement of performance) is a common phrase in business, education, and sports contexts. The مصدر here acts as a predicate after 'هو' (it is), functioning like English 'to improve.' The root ح-س-ن gives 'حَسَن' (good), 'أحسَن' (better), and 'تحسين' (improvement).

items.question: من أسباب _____ قلة النوم.
items.translation: (One of the causes of exhaustion is lack of sleep.)
items.explanation: 'الإرهاق' (exhaustion) is the مصدر of Form IV 'أرهقَ' (to exhaust). After 'أسباب' (causes of), it takes genitive: 'الإرهاقِ.' The entire phrase 'من أسباب الإرهاق' means 'among the causes of exhaustion.' Arabic medical and wellness discourse heavily relies on مصادر to describe conditions and processes. 'قلة النوم' (lack of sleep) pairs another مصدر-like noun with 'النوم' (sleep, مصدر of نامَ).

items.question: يجب _____ بين العمل والراحة.
items.translation: (One must balance between work and rest.)
items.explanation: 'التوازن' (balance) is the مصدر of Form VI 'توازَنَ' (to be balanced). Form VI مصادر follow 'تَفاعُل,' expressing mutual or reciprocal actions. 'بين العمل والراحة' (between work and rest) uses two مصادر as objects of 'بين' (between). This wellness advice demonstrates how Arabic builds complex ideas by stringing مصادر together. The root و-ز-ن relates to weight and balance, appearing in 'ميزان' (scales) and 'وَزن' (weight).

name: Adjective Agreement (مطابقة الصفة)
description: Match adjectives with nouns in gender, number, and definiteness

items.question: البنتُ _____.
items.explanation: Adjectives in Arabic must agree with the noun in gender. 'البنت' (the girl) is feminine, so 'طويل' (tall) must become 'طويلة' with the feminine marker 'ة.' This agreement is one of Arabic's most fundamental rules — every adjective that describes a feminine noun must take the 'ة' ending. English adjectives never change form, so this concept requires careful attention from English speakers. The rule is consistent and reliable across almost all adjectives.

items.question: اشتريتُ كتاباً _____.
items.explanation: When a noun is indefinite (no 'ال'), its adjective must also be indefinite. 'كتاباً' (a book) is indefinite with tanwīn, so the adjective takes tanwīn too: 'جديداً' (new). If the noun were 'الكتابَ' (the book), the adjective would be 'الجديدَ.' This definiteness agreement is a critical Arabic rule with no English parallel — English uses 'a new book' and 'the new book' without changing the adjective at all.

items.question: المعلمون _____.
items.explanation: For human masculine plurals, the adjective takes the masculine plural form. 'المعلمون' (the male teachers) requires 'ممتازون' with the plural suffix 'ون.' This sound masculine plural pattern applies to adjectives describing groups of men. The rule is straightforward: masculine plural noun → masculine plural adjective. Both use the same 'ون' ending, creating harmonious agreement that sounds natural.

items.question: الطالبات _____.
items.explanation: For human feminine plurals, the adjective takes the sound feminine plural 'ات.' 'الطالبات' (female students) requires 'مجتهداتٌ' (diligent - f. pl.). This pattern is predictable: replace the 'ة' of the feminine singular with 'ات.' The agreement chain — gender, number, definiteness, case — must all match. Here you see four layers of agreement working simultaneously, which is what gives Arabic its grammatical precision.

items.question: الكُتُبُ _____.
items.explanation: Here's a crucial Arabic rule: non-human plurals are treated as FEMININE SINGULAR for adjective agreement. 'الكُتُب' (books) is a broken plural of a non-human noun, so its adjective is feminine singular: 'مفيدةٌ' — not 'مفيدات' or 'مفيدون.' This rule surprises English speakers but is absolutely consistent in Arabic. Whether it's books, cars, countries, or ideas — any non-human plural takes a feminine singular adjective.

items.question: رأيتُ سيارةً _____.
items.explanation: Color adjectives follow a special pattern: masculine 'أَفعَل' / feminine 'فَعلاء.' 'أحمر' (red - masc.) becomes 'حمراء' (red - fem.) for the feminine noun 'سيارة.' These diptote adjectives never take tanwīn (notice 'حمراءَ' not 'حمراءً'). The color adjective patterns are: أحمر/حمراء (red), أزرق/زرقاء (blue), أخضر/خضراء (green). This distinct pattern makes color words instantly recognizable in Arabic text.

items.question: هذا البيتُ _____.
items.explanation: When an adjective is a predicate (خبر) — not directly attached to the noun — it stays indefinite even if the noun is definite. 'البيتُ كبيرٌ' = 'the house is big.' If the adjective were definite ('الكبيرُ'), it would become an attributive: 'البيتُ الكبيرُ' = 'the big house' (not a sentence). This definite/indefinite distinction between attributive and predicative adjectives is one of the most elegant features of Arabic grammar.

items.question: المباني _____.
items.explanation: Again, non-human plurals take feminine singular adjectives. 'المباني' (buildings) is a non-human plural, so 'عالٍ' (tall/high) becomes 'عاليةٌ' (feminine singular). This rule applies even to enormous or powerful things — buildings, mountains, oceans — they all grammatically behave as feminine singular when plural. This consistent rule simplifies agreement once internalized, even though it initially seems counterintuitive.

items.question: الأستاذة _____.
items.explanation: Wait — here 'الأستاذة الجديدة' functions as 'the new professor' (an attributive adjective phrase), not 'the professor is new.' Both noun and adjective are definite with 'ال,' creating a noun phrase rather than a sentence. If we wanted 'the professor is new,' we'd write 'الأستاذةُ جديدةٌ' (definite noun + indefinite adjective). This definiteness distinction is how Arabic differentiates phrases from sentences without a verb.

items.question: قرأتُ قصةً _____.
items.explanation: 'قصة' (story) is feminine, indefinite, and accusative (after the verb 'قرأتُ'). The adjective 'ممتع' must match all three: feminine (ممتعة), indefinite (tanwīn), accusative (فتحة tanwīn → ممتعةً). This triple agreement demonstrates Arabic's grammatical rigor. Participles like 'ممتع' (enjoyable, from Form IV أمتعَ) function as adjectives and follow all the same agreement rules as regular adjectives.

items.question: عندي أصدقاءُ _____.
items.explanation: 'أصدقاء' (friends) is a human plural, and 'وفيّ' (loyal) has the broken plural 'أوفياء.' For human plurals, Arabic can use either the sound plural or a broken plural form. 'أوفياءُ' follows the 'أَفعِلاء' pattern — a broken plural for adjectives. Both noun and adjective are indefinite (no 'ال') and diptote (no tanwīn). Broken plurals for adjectives are common with human referents and add literary elegance.

items.question: الأسئلة _____.
items.explanation: Once more, non-human plural + feminine singular adjective: 'الأسئلة صعبةٌ' (the questions are difficult). 'الأسئلة' is the broken plural of 'سؤال' (question) — a non-human noun. Even though there might be hundreds of questions, the adjective remains feminine singular. This rule is so consistent that breaking it immediately sounds ungrammatical to Arabic speakers. It's a non-negotiable grammatical principle.

items.question: هو رجلٌ _____.
items.explanation: 'رجل' (man) is masculine singular indefinite, so the adjective stays masculine singular indefinite: 'كريمٌ' (generous). The word 'كريم' is deeply valued in Arab culture — generosity (الكرم) is considered the highest virtue. The root ك-ر-م produces 'كريم' (generous), 'كرَم' (generosity), 'إكرام' (honoring), and 'مُكرَّم' (honored). The simplicity of masculine singular agreement makes this the baseline form from which all other agreements derive.

items.question: زُرتُ مدينةً _____.
items.explanation: 'مدينة' (city) is feminine and indefinite in the accusative case (object of 'زُرتُ'). The adjective matches: 'قديمةً' — feminine (ة), indefinite (tanwīn), accusative (fatḥa tanwīn). The word 'مدينة' itself comes from the root م-د-ن and is one of Arabic's most culturally significant words — 'المدينة المنورة' (the radiant city) is Medina's official name, showcasing adjective agreement in one of Islam's holiest place names.

items.question: هؤلاءِ الرجالُ _____.
items.explanation: 'الرجال' (men) is a human masculine plural, requiring a plural adjective. 'شجاع' (brave) has the broken plural 'شُجعان.' For human plurals, Arabic often uses broken plural adjective forms rather than sound plurals. 'شُجعان' follows the 'فُعلان' pattern, common for adjectives describing inherent qualities. The demonstrative 'هؤلاء' (these) is the plural form and doesn't change for gender — a rare case of gender-neutral Arabic grammar.

name: Comparative & Superlative (التفضيل)
description: Express comparisons and superlatives using the أفعل pattern

items.question: القاهرة _____ من عمّان.
items.explanation: The Arabic comparative (اسم التفضيل) uses the pattern 'أَفعَل.' From 'كبير' (big), the comparative is 'أكبر' (bigger). The structure is: noun + أفعل + من + compared noun. 'القاهرة أكبرُ من عمّان' = Cairo is bigger than Amman. Unlike English, which has '-er' and 'more,' Arabic uses one consistent pattern 'أفعل' for almost all comparisons. This pattern is one of the most regular and useful in the language.

items.question: مريم _____ طالبةٍ في الصف.
items.translation: (Maryam is the best student in the class.)
items.explanation: The superlative uses the SAME 'أفعل' form as the comparative, but the construction differs. For superlative: definite noun + أفعل + indefinite genitive noun. 'أفضلُ طالبةٍ' = 'best student.' Without 'من' (than), the أفعل form becomes superlative. From the root ف-ض-ل (preference/virtue), 'أفضل' means 'best/better.' Context determines whether أفعل is comparative or superlative — a beautiful grammatical economy.

items.question: الصحة _____ من المال.
items.explanation: From 'مُهِمّ' (important), the comparative is 'أهمُّ' (more important). When the root has doubled letters (like ه-م-م), the أفعل form keeps the doubling: 'أهمُّ' (not 'أهمم'). The proverb 'الصحة أهمُّ من المال' is widely used in Arabic culture, reflecting the value placed on well-being. This comparative + 'من' construction is the bread and butter of Arabic comparison.

items.question: هذا هو _____ كتابٍ قرأتُه.
items.translation: (This is the best book I've read.)
items.explanation: In the superlative construction 'أفضلُ كتابٍ' (best book), the أفعل form is the first term of إضافة (construct state), so it cannot take 'ال' or tanwīn. The second noun 'كتابٍ' is indefinite genitive. 'قرأتُه' (I read it) is a relative clause modifying 'كتاب.' This layered structure — superlative + noun + relative clause — demonstrates Arabic's capacity for complex yet clear expression.

items.question: الحياة في الريف _____ من المدينة.
items.translation: (Life in the countryside is quieter than the city.)
items.explanation: From 'هادئ' (quiet), the comparative is 'أهدأ' (quieter). When the root ends in hamza (ه-د-أ), the أفعل pattern keeps it: 'أهدأ.' The comparison 'أهدأ من' is straightforward. Living comparisons between ريف (countryside) and مدينة (city) are common conversation topics in the Arabic-speaking world, where rapid urbanization creates vivid contrasts. This comparative captures a universal sentiment across cultures.

items.question: النيل _____ نهرٍ في أفريقيا.
items.translation: (The Nile is the longest river in Africa.)
items.explanation: From 'طويل' (long/tall), 'أطول' (longest) in the superlative pattern: 'أطولُ نهرٍ' (longest river). Arabic superlatives are powerful for geographic facts and records. Notice 'نهرٍ' is indefinite genitive (required after أفعل in superlative). The Nile, known as 'النيل' in Arabic, holds deep cultural significance in Egyptian Arabic heritage. Geographic superlatives are excellent conversation starters with Arabic speakers.

items.question: هذه المسألة _____ ممّا ظننتُ.
items.translation: (This problem is easier than I thought.)
items.explanation: From 'سَهل' (easy), 'أسهل' (easier). 'ممّا' is a contraction of 'من + ما' (than what), used before clauses. 'أسهلُ ممّا ظننتُ' = 'easier than what I thought.' This contraction 'ممّا' is extremely common in spoken and written Arabic. When the compared element is a clause rather than a noun, Arabic uses 'ممّا' or 'من أن' — this parallels English 'than what/than I expected.' It's a frequent structure in daily conversations.

items.question: أخي _____ منّي بسنتين.
items.translation: (My brother is older than me by two years.)
items.explanation: 'أكبر' means both 'bigger' and 'older' depending on context. 'أكبر منّي' = 'older than me.' The preposition 'من' merges with 'ني' (me) to form 'منّي' (with شدّة). 'بسنتين' (by two years) uses the dual form 'سنتين' with the preposition 'ب' (by). This sentence demonstrates three grammatical concepts at once: comparative, merged preposition, and the dual form — all working seamlessly together.

items.question: العربية من _____ اللغات في العالم.
items.translation: (Arabic is one of the most beautiful languages in the world.)
items.explanation: The construction 'من أفعل + noun' means 'one of the most...' 'من أجمل اللغات' = 'one of the most beautiful languages.' Here 'أجمل' is in the genitive after 'من,' forming part of a superlative construction. This is a different superlative pattern from the إضافة type — it creates a partitive meaning. From 'جَميل' (beautiful), 'أجمل' (most beautiful). Arabic speakers take great pride in their language's beauty and this phrase is commonly heard.

items.question: هذا الطريق _____ من ذلك.
items.translation: (This road is shorter than that one.)
items.explanation: From 'قَصير' (short), 'أقصر' (shorter). The demonstrative pronouns 'هذا' (this) and 'ذلك' (that) frame the comparison neatly. The root ق-ص-ر gives 'قصير' (short), 'قَصر' (palace — originally a 'shortened' fortress), and 'قصّة' (story — a 'cut' narrative). This rich semantic web from a single root is typical of Arabic and makes vocabulary acquisition more intuitive once you recognize root patterns.

items.question: هي _____ امرأةٍ في الشركة.
items.translation: (She is the smartest woman in the company.)
items.explanation: From 'ذَكيّ' (smart), the comparative/superlative is 'أذكى.' When the root ends in 'ي' (defective root ذ-ك-و/ي), the أفعل pattern produces 'أفعى' — the final letter becomes أَلِف مقصورة. 'أذكى امرأةٍ' (smartest woman) uses the superlative إضافة construction. Note that أفعل التفضيل doesn't change for gender — 'أذكى' is used for both males and females, a rare gender-neutral form in Arabic.

items.question: الأمورُ _____ ممّا تبدو.
items.translation: (Things are more complicated than they seem.)
items.explanation: From 'مُعَقَّد' (complicated), the comparative form goes back to the base root ع-ق-د and forms 'أعقد.' Even when the original adjective is a derived form (Form II participle), the comparative always builds from the three-letter root using أفعل. 'ممّا تبدو' = 'than they appear.' This demonstrates an important principle: Arabic comparatives are always morphologically simple (three-letter based), regardless of how complex the original adjective might be.

items.question: القمر _____ من الشمس إلى الأرض.
items.translation: (The moon is closer than the sun to Earth.)
items.explanation: From 'قَريب' (close/near), 'أقرب' (closer/closest). The celestial comparison 'القمر أقرب من الشمس' uses straightforward comparative syntax. The root ق-ر-ب is one of Arabic's most versatile, giving 'قريب' (near), 'قرابة' (kinship), 'مُقَرَّب' (close associate), and 'تقريباً' (approximately). Understanding roots like this transforms vocabulary learning from memorization into pattern recognition.

items.question: الماء _____ شيءٍ للحياة.
items.translation: (Water is the most essential thing for life.)
items.explanation: 'أهمُّ شيءٍ' (most important thing) uses the superlative إضافة: أفعل + indefinite genitive noun. This form is used for universal truths and strong claims. The root ه-م-م undergoes unique changes in the comparative — the doubled مم shows as شدّة on the م. 'للحياة' (for life) combines the preposition 'لـ' with 'الحياة.' This sentence type appears frequently in scientific writing, religious texts, and philosophical discourse.

items.question: لا شيءَ _____ من الحرية.
items.explanation: From 'غالٍ' (precious/expensive), 'أغلى' (more precious). The structure 'لا شيءَ أغلى من' (nothing is more... than) is a powerful rhetorical form common in Arabic literature, speeches, and poetry. 'الحرية' (freedom) is one of Arabic's most emotionally charged words. The root غ-ل-و/ي means 'to be costly/precious,' and its comparative 'أغلى' ends in ألف مقصورة because the root's final letter is weak. This type of comparative expresses deep cultural values.

name: Idafa / Possessive Construct (الإضافة)
description: Master the genitive construct that links two nouns to show possession or association

items.question: _____ المدرسةِ واسعٌ.
items.explanation: In an إضافة (genitive construct), the first noun (المُضاف) NEVER takes the definite article 'ال.' It becomes definite through its connection to the second noun (المُضاف إليه). So 'فِناءُ المدرسةِ' = 'the yard of the school.' If you added 'ال' to 'فناء,' it would break the إضافة structure entirely. The first noun takes the case ending appropriate to its role in the sentence — here nominative (مرفوع) because it's the subject.

items.question: قرأتُ _____ التاريخِ.
items.explanation: Here 'كِتابَ' is the first noun in the إضافة and serves as the direct object (مفعول به), so it takes the accusative case ending (فتحة). The word appears without 'ال' because in إضافة, the first noun derives its definiteness from the second noun. 'كِتابَ التاريخِ' literally means 'book of the history' = 'the history book.' The second noun 'التاريخِ' takes the genitive case (مجرور بالكسرة) as is always required for المُضاف إليه.

items.question: هذا بيتُ _____ الجديد.
items.explanation: The second noun in إضافة (المُضاف إليه) is ALWAYS in the genitive case (مجرور). Since the neighbor is a specific person, it takes 'ال' and the kasra ending: 'الجارِ.' The adjective 'الجديد' describes 'بيت' (house), not the neighbor. In إضافة, adjectives of the first noun come AFTER the entire construct. This is a common source of confusion — placement determines which noun the adjective modifies.

items.question: سافرنا إلى _____ البحرِ.
items.explanation: After the preposition 'إلى,' the noun is in the genitive case (مجرور). Since 'شاطئ' is the first noun of an إضافة with 'البحر,' it cannot take 'ال.' It receives the kasra (genitive marker) due to 'إلى.' This creates a chain: preposition → مُضاف (genitive) → مُضاف إليه (genitive). 'شاطئِ البحرِ' = 'shore of the sea' = 'the seashore.' Both nouns end in kasra but for different grammatical reasons.

items.question: _____ الطالباتِ في القاعة.
items.translation: (The students' bags are in the hall.)
items.explanation: 'حقائبُ' is a broken plural (جمع تكسير) of 'حقيبة' (bag). As the first noun in إضافة with 'الطالباتِ,' it cannot have 'ال.' It takes damma (nominative) because it's the subject (مبتدأ) of the sentence. Broken plurals are diptotes (ممنوع من الصرف) — they take damma for nominative and fatha for both accusative and genitive, never tanwin. This is a key rule that distinguishes broken plural nouns from regular ones.

items.question: أعجبني لونُ _____ هذه.
items.translation: (I liked the color of this flower.)
items.explanation: In this إضافة, 'لونُ' is linked to 'الزهرةِ.' The demonstrative pronoun 'هذه' (this) follows and modifies 'الزهرة.' When the second noun in إضافة is definite with 'ال,' it takes kasra (genitive case). The demonstrative 'هذه' agrees with 'الزهرة' (feminine singular). This shows how modifiers of the second noun of إضافة come after the entire construct, creating a clear layered sentence structure.

items.question: معلمو _____ محترفون.
items.explanation: When a sound masculine plural (جمع مذكر سالم) ending in 'ون' is the first noun in إضافة, the final 'ن' is DROPPED. So 'معلمون' becomes 'معلمو.' This is a critical إضافة rule: the نون of the dual and sound masculine plural is always removed when followed by a مُضاف إليه. 'معلمو الجامعةِ' = 'the university's teachers.' The second noun 'الجامعةِ' takes genitive (kasra) as always required.

items.question: استمعنا إلى _____ المعلمِ.
items.explanation: 'شرحِ' is the first noun (مُضاف) in the إضافة with 'المعلمِ.' It comes after the preposition 'إلى,' which governs the genitive case (مجرور). Therefore 'شرح' takes kasra. It cannot have 'ال' because it is a مُضاف. The result is a verbal noun (مصدر) in إضافة: 'شرحِ المعلمِ' = 'the teacher's explanation.' Verbal nouns frequently appear in إضافة constructions, allowing Arabic to express complex ideas concisely.

items.question: وصلتُ قبلَ _____ الاجتماعِ.
items.translation: (I arrived before the start of the meeting.)
items.explanation: This is a chained إضافة (إضافة متسلسلة): 'قبلَ بدايةِ الاجتماعِ.' Three nouns are linked: 'قبل' (before, used as a noun of time) → 'بداية' (start) → 'الاجتماع' (the meeting). Each مُضاف cannot take 'ال,' and each مُضاف إليه takes genitive case. Only the LAST noun in the chain may have the article 'ال.' Chained إضافة constructions are extremely common in formal Arabic writing.

items.question: مكتبُ _____ في الطابقِ الثالثِ.
items.translation: (The manager's office is on the third floor.)
items.explanation: The specific manager is indicated by 'ال,' so we use 'المديرِ' with kasra (genitive as مُضاف إليه). Without 'ال,' it would mean 'a manager's office' — indefinite. This distinction matters greatly in Arabic. The first noun 'مكتبُ' has no article but is made definite through its connection to the definite 'المديرِ.' This definiteness transfer is the core function of إضافة — the first noun borrows definiteness from the second.

items.question: رائحةُ _____ جميلةٌ.
items.explanation: In this إضافة, 'رائحةُ' (smell/aroma) is linked to 'القهوةِ' (the coffee). The adjective 'جميلةٌ' describes 'رائحة,' which is feminine. Since 'رائحة' is definite through إضافة, the adjective should also be definite — yet here 'جميلةٌ' has tanwin (indefinite) because it's a predicate (خبر), not an attributive adjective (نعت). In Arabic, predicates are typically indefinite even when the subject is definite. This grammatical distinction is fundamental.

items.question: تعلمتُ في _____ الطبِّ.
items.translation: (I studied at the faculty of medicine.)
items.explanation: 'كليةِ' is governed by the preposition 'في' (in/at), so it takes the genitive case (kasra). As the first noun of إضافة with 'الطبِّ,' it drops 'ال.' This phrase 'كليةِ الطبِّ' (faculty of medicine) is a fixed إضافة expression commonly used in academic contexts. Many Arabic institutional names use إضافة: 'وزارةُ التعليمِ' (Ministry of Education), 'جامعةُ القاهرةِ' (Cairo University). Understanding إضافة is essential for navigating formal Arabic.

items.question: أخذتُ _____ صديقي.
items.explanation: Here the إضافة involves a noun with a pronoun suffix: 'صديقي' (my friend). The first noun 'قلمَ' takes the accusative (فتحة) as the direct object of 'أخذتُ' (I took). No 'ال' on 'قلم' per إضافة rules. When the مُضاف إليه has a possessive pronoun attached, it creates a double possession: 'قلمَ صديقي' = 'pen of my-friend' = 'my friend's pen.' This elegant construction allows Arabic to express complex possession concisely in just two words.

items.question: طلابُ _____ مُجتهدون.
items.explanation: When a demonstrative modifies the مُضاف إليه, it appears BEFORE the noun: 'هذا الفصلِ' (this class). The word order is: مُضاف + demonstrative + مُضاف إليه. 'طلابُ هذا الفصلِ' = 'students of this class.' The demonstrative 'هذا' is masculine singular matching 'الفصل.' The noun 'الفصلِ' still takes genitive (kasra). This construction often surprises English speakers since in English the demonstrative comes at the beginning: 'this class's students.'

items.question: بابُ _____ مفتوحٌ.
items.explanation: 'بابُ البيتِ' is a classic إضافة: 'door of the house.' The first noun 'بابُ' is nominative (subject), no 'ال.' The second noun 'البيتِ' is genitive with kasra. The predicate 'مفتوحٌ' (open) carries tanwin showing it's indefinite — predicates in Arabic nominal sentences are typically indefinite. This two-word إضافة construction is the most basic and frequent type you'll encounter in everyday Arabic, from street signs to formal documents.

name: Relative Pronouns (الأسماء الموصولة)
description: Use الذي، التي، الذين and other relative pronouns to connect clauses

items.question: الرجلُ _____ يقرأُ هو أبي.
items.translation: (The man who is reading is my father.)
items.explanation: Arabic relative pronouns must agree with their antecedent in gender and number. 'الرجلُ' (the man) is masculine singular, so we use 'الذي' (who/which - masculine singular). Unlike English where 'who' works for all, Arabic has distinct forms: الذي (masc. sing.), التي (fem. sing.), الذين (masc. plural), اللواتي/اللاتي (fem. plural), اللذان (masc. dual), اللتان (fem. dual). This agreement system is one of Arabic's most logical grammatical features.

items.question: المعلمةُ _____ تُدرّسُنا لطيفةٌ.
items.translation: (The teacher (f.) who teaches us is kind.)
items.explanation: 'المعلمةُ' (the female teacher) is feminine singular, requiring the relative pronoun 'التي.' The verb after the relative pronoun must also agree: 'تُدرّسُنا' (she teaches us) uses the feminine prefix 'تُ-.' The adjective 'لطيفةٌ' is feminine and indefinite (predicate). This sentence demonstrates how gender agreement cascades through Arabic: feminine noun → feminine relative pronoun → feminine verb → feminine adjective. Every element must harmonize.

items.question: الطلابُ _____ نجحوا سعداءُ.
items.explanation: 'الطلابُ' refers to a masculine plural group (or mixed gender), so the relative pronoun is 'الذين.' The verb 'نجحوا' (they passed) carries the masculine plural marker 'وا.' The predicate 'سعداءُ' is a broken plural of 'سعيد' (happy). Note that 'الذين' is used for masculine plurals and mixed groups. For exclusively female plurals, Arabic uses 'اللاتي' or 'اللواتي.' This gender distinction in plural relative pronouns is uniquely Arabic.

items.question: قرأتُ الكتابَ _____ اشتريتَهُ.
items.translation: (I read the book that you bought.)
items.explanation: 'الكتابَ' (the book) is masculine singular, requiring 'الذي.' A crucial Arabic rule: the relative clause must contain a resumptive pronoun (عائد) referring back to the antecedent. Here 'اشتريتَهُ' has the pronoun suffix 'هُ' (it/him) referring to 'الكتاب.' In English, 'that you bought' needs no pronoun, but Arabic REQUIRES 'اشتريتَهُ' (you bought IT). Omitting the عائد is a very common error among Arabic learners.

items.question: الطالبتانِ _____ فازتا تستحقانِ الجائزةَ.
items.translation: (The two female students who won deserve the prize.)
items.explanation: Arabic has DUAL forms for relative pronouns — a feature almost unique among world languages. 'الطالبتانِ' is feminine dual (two female students), requiring 'اللتانِ' (the two who - feminine). The masculine dual equivalent is 'اللذانِ.' The verb 'فازتا' uses the feminine dual suffix 'تا.' In the nominative case, dual forms end in 'انِ' (اللتانِ/اللذانِ). In accusative/genitive, they become 'اللتَيْنِ/اللذَيْنِ.' This precision reflects Arabic's remarkable grammatical detail.

items.question: الممرضاتُ _____ يعملنَ هنا ماهراتٌ.
items.translation: (The nurses who work here are skilled.)
items.explanation: 'الممرضاتُ' is a sound feminine plural (جمع مؤنث سالم), requiring the feminine plural relative pronoun 'اللاتي' (also written 'اللواتي'). The verb 'يعملنَ' uses the feminine plural suffix 'نَ.' Note: 'الذين' is ONLY for masculine plurals and mixed groups. Many learners mistakenly use 'الذين' for all plurals. Using 'اللاتي' for an exclusively female group is grammatically mandatory in formal Arabic, though colloquial dialects often simplify this.

items.question: لا أعرفُ _____ حدثَ.
items.explanation: 'ما' is the relative pronoun meaning 'what/that which' for non-human/abstract referents. It does NOT change for gender or number — it's invariable. 'لا أعرفُ ما حدثَ' = 'I don't know what happened.' Compare with 'مَن' (who/whoever) for human referents. These two pronouns (ما/مَن) are used when the antecedent is INDEFINITE or absent entirely. They function differently from الذي/التي which require a definite antecedent. This indefinite/definite distinction is key.

items.question: _____ يدرسْ ينجحْ.
items.explanation: 'مَن' means 'whoever/he who' for human referents. It is invariable — same form for masculine, feminine, singular, and plural. When 'مَن' is used as a conditional particle, it governs the jussive mood (مجزوم): 'يدرسْ' and 'ينجحْ' both have sukun. This is a conditional sentence (جملة شرطية): 'مَن يدرسْ ينجحْ' = 'whoever studies, succeeds.' The use of 'مَن' as both relative and conditional pronoun is a beautiful feature of Arabic grammar that creates proverb-like expressions.

items.question: الغرفةُ _____ أنامُ فيها واسعةٌ.
items.translation: (The room in which I sleep is spacious.)
items.explanation: 'الغرفةُ' (the room) is feminine singular → 'التي.' The relative clause requires a resumptive pronoun: 'فيها' (in it - feminine). The preposition 'في' + suffix 'ها' creates 'فيها,' referring back to 'الغرفة.' Without 'فيها,' the sentence would be grammatically incomplete. In English, 'the room I sleep in' can drop 'which,' but Arabic MUST include both the relative pronoun and the resumptive pronoun. This double-referencing ensures absolute grammatical clarity.

items.question: الصديقُ _____ سافرتُ معهُ عادَ.
items.translation: (The friend with whom I traveled returned.)
items.explanation: 'الصديقُ' (the friend, masc.) requires 'الذي.' The resumptive pronoun is embedded in 'معهُ' (with him), where 'هُ' refers back to 'الصديق.' The structure parallels English 'with whom,' but Arabic ALWAYS needs the pronoun suffix. 'سافرتُ معهُ' = 'I traveled with him.' The main verb 'عادَ' (returned) is the predicate about the friend. This sentence shows how Arabic relative clauses can contain preposition + pronoun combinations as their resumptive element.

items.question: البناتُ _____ يلعبنَ في الحديقةِ جاراتُنا.
items.translation: (The girls who play in the park are our neighbors.)
items.explanation: 'البناتُ' is a feminine plural noun (broken plural of بنت). For feminine human plurals, the relative pronoun is 'اللاتي' (or 'اللواتي'). The verb 'يلعبنَ' has the feminine plural prefix 'يـ' and suffix 'نَ.' Interestingly, 'بنات' as a broken plural could theoretically take 'التي' (since non-human broken plurals are treated as feminine singular), but because 'بنات' refers to HUMAN females, 'اللاتي' is required. The human/non-human distinction affects pronoun choice significantly.

items.question: شاهدتُ الفيلمَ _____ نصحتَني بهِ.
items.translation: (I watched the film that you recommended to me.)
items.explanation: 'الفيلمَ' (the film) is masculine singular → 'الذي.' The resumptive pronoun appears in 'بهِ' (about/with it): the 'هِ' refers back to 'الفيلم.' 'نصحتَني بهِ' = 'you advised me about it.' The verb 'نصح' takes the preposition 'بـ' (about/with) for the thing recommended. This shows why understanding verb-preposition combinations (حروف الجر) is essential for constructing correct relative clauses — each verb may require a different preposition with its resumptive pronoun.

items.question: كلُّ _____ قالهُ صحيحٌ.
items.explanation: 'كلُّ ما' is a common construction meaning 'everything that / all that.' Here 'ما' acts as a relative pronoun for abstract/non-specific referents. The resumptive pronoun is 'هُ' in 'قالهُ' (he said it). 'ما' is preferred over 'الذي' after 'كلّ' because the referent is general and unspecified. Common similar expressions: 'كلُّ ما أريدُهُ' (all I want), 'كلُّ ما لديّ' (all I have). These 'كلّ ما' constructions are extremely frequent in both spoken and written Arabic.

items.question: الكتبُ _____ على الرفِّ قديمةٌ.
items.translation: (The books that are on the shelf are old.)
items.explanation: 'الكتبُ' is a non-human broken plural. In Arabic grammar, ALL non-human plurals are treated as FEMININE SINGULAR for agreement purposes. This is one of the most important rules in Arabic: whether the plural is masculine or feminine, if it's non-human, it takes 'التي' (not 'الذين'). 'الكتبُ التي على الرفِّ' = 'the books that are on the shelf.' The predicate 'قديمةٌ' is also feminine singular, confirming this agreement pattern. This rule applies universally to animals, objects, and abstract concepts.

items.question: الرجلانِ _____ وصلا مُهندسانِ.
items.translation: (The two men who arrived are engineers.)
items.explanation: 'الرجلانِ' is masculine dual (two men), requiring the masculine dual relative pronoun 'اللذانِ.' The verb 'وصلا' uses the dual suffix 'ا.' The predicate 'مُهندسانِ' is also dual. In oblique cases (accusative/genitive), 'اللذانِ' becomes 'اللذَيْنِ,' just like regular dual nouns change from 'انِ' to 'يْنِ.' The dual relative pronoun system is fully productive in Modern Standard Arabic, though many dialects have lost it. Mastering it marks advanced Arabic competency.

name: Conditional Sentences (الجمل الشرطية)
description: Form 'if-then' sentences using إذا، إنْ، لو and other conditional particles

items.question: إذا _____ المطرُ، سنبقى في البيتِ.
items.translation: (If it rains, we will stay at home.)
items.explanation: 'إذا' (if/when) is used for conditions considered likely or expected. A unique Arabic grammar rule: after 'إذا,' the verb is in the PAST tense even though the meaning is present/future. 'إذا نزلَ المطرُ' literally says 'if the rain fell' but means 'if it rains.' This use of past tense for future possibility is called الماضي بمعنى المستقبل. The response clause uses future 'سنبقى.' This past-for-future pattern after 'إذا' is absolutely consistent in Arabic and never varies.

items.question: إنْ _____ ينجحْ.
items.explanation: 'إنْ' is a conditional particle (حرف شرط جازم) that puts both verbs — the condition and the result — in the jussive mood (مجزوم). 'يدرسْ' has sukun (jussive marker). The result 'ينجحْ' is also jussive. Unlike 'إذا,' which takes past tense, 'إنْ' takes the jussive present tense. 'إنْ' implies the condition is uncertain or hypothetical, while 'إذا' suggests likelihood. This semantic distinction between the two 'if' words is essential for nuanced Arabic expression.

items.question: لو _____ مالاً، لَسافرتُ.
items.translation: (If I had money, I would have traveled.)
items.explanation: 'لو' is the COUNTERFACTUAL conditional — it expresses something contrary to fact, like English 'if (only).' It always takes the past tense in both clauses. 'لو ملكتُ مالاً' = 'if I had possessed money (but I didn't).' The response clause uses 'لَـ' + past tense: 'لَسافرتُ' (I would have traveled). 'لو' implies the condition was NOT met. This makes 'لو' fundamentally different from 'إذا' (likely future) and 'إنْ' (possible future). Arabic has three distinct conditional levels — a more precise system than English.

items.question: إذا _____ الواجبَ، يمكنكَ اللعبُ.
items.translation: (If you finish the homework, you can play.)
items.explanation: After 'إذا,' the verb is in the past tense despite the future meaning: 'أنهيتَ' (you finished = if you finish). The response clause 'يمكنكَ اللعبُ' (you can play) uses the present tense. This temporal mismatch — past tense condition with present/future result — is characteristic of 'إذا' sentences. The verb 'أنهى' (to finish, Form IV) conjugates as 'أنهيتَ' for the second person masculine singular in the past tense. This pattern is used in everyday instructions, rules, and promises.

items.question: لو كنتُ مكانَكَ، _____ الحقيقةَ.
items.translation: (If I were in your place, I would tell the truth.)
items.explanation: The response to a 'لو' conditional uses 'لَـ' (the result particle) + past tense verb. 'لَقُلتُ' = 'I certainly would have said.' The 'لَـ' prefix is essential and cannot be omitted in the response clause of 'لو' conditionals. 'كنتُ مكانَكَ' = 'I was in your place' (counterfactual — I wasn't). The verb 'قال' (to say) has the past form 'قُلتُ' (I said). This لو...لَـ pattern is one of the most elegant structures in Arabic, used extensively in literature, speeches, and daily conversation.

items.question: إنْ _____ الباكرَ، ستصلُ في الوقتِ.
items.translation: (If you wake up early, you will arrive on time.)
items.explanation: 'إنْ' as a jussive conditional particle requires the jussive mood (مجزوم). 'تستيقظْ' has sukun on the last letter, indicating jussive. Compare: indicative 'تستيقظُ' (damma), subjunctive 'تستيقظَ' (fatha), jussive 'تستيقظْ' (sukun). The verb 'استيقظ' (to wake up, Form X) is a long verb where the jussive is shown clearly on the final consonant. The response clause uses 'سـ' + indicative 'ستصلُ' (you will arrive) because 'سـ' governs the indicative mood.

items.question: _____ اجتهدتَ لنجحتَ.
items.translation: (If you had worked hard, you would have succeeded.)
items.explanation: This sentence expresses a PAST counterfactual condition — the person did NOT work hard and did NOT succeed. Only 'لو' can express this meaning. 'إذا' would imply a likely future event, and 'إنْ' would suggest a possible future. 'لو اجتهدتَ لنجحتَ' = 'Had you worked hard, you would have succeeded.' The 'لَـ' in 'لنجحتَ' is the result particle. This three-way system (لو/إذا/إنْ) gives Arabic speakers precise tools to distinguish impossible, likely, and possible conditions.

items.question: إذا زارنا أحمدُ، _____ بهِ.
items.translation: (If Ahmed visits us, we will welcome him.)
items.explanation: After 'إذا,' the condition clause takes past tense (زارنا = he visited us). The response clause can take either past or present tense. Here 'رحّبنا' (we welcomed = we will welcome) uses past tense in the response too, creating a fully past-tense conditional with future meaning. This is common in Arabic: 'إذا جاءَ... قلنا' = 'if he comes... we will say.' Using past tense in both clauses of an 'إذا' sentence emphasizes certainty and conviction about the outcome. The verb 'رحّبَ' (to welcome, Form II) takes 'بـ' preposition.

items.question: لو أنّ الطقسَ _____ جميلاً لَخرجنا.
items.translation: (If the weather had been beautiful, we would have gone out.)
items.explanation: 'لو أنّ' is an extended form of the counterfactual conditional. After 'لو أنّ,' a nominal sentence follows with 'كانَ' providing the past tense element. 'لو أنّ الطقسَ كانَ جميلاً' = 'if the weather had been beautiful (but it wasn't).' The response 'لَخرجنا' uses 'لَـ' + past: 'we would have gone out.' The construction 'لو أنّ' is slightly more emphatic than simple 'لو.' 'أنّ' governs the accusative: 'الطقسَ' (accusative). This is a sophisticated construction used frequently in Arabic literature and formal speech.

items.question: إنْ لم _____ سريعاً، ستتأخر.
items.translation: (If you don't come quickly, you will be late.)
items.explanation: Negative conditionals with 'إنْ' use 'إنْ لم' + jussive. The verb 'يأتي' in jussive loses its final 'ي': 'تأتِ' (without the ي). This is because defective verbs (ending in ي or و) show the jussive mood by DROPPING the final weak letter. 'تأتِ' vs. indicative 'تأتي' vs. subjunctive 'تأتيَ.' The deletion of the weak letter is the jussive marker. 'لم' already implies negation and jussive, so combining it with 'إنْ' creates a negative conditional. This verb pattern applies to all defective verbs like يمشي، يبني، يرمي.

items.question: لولا _____ لَهلكنا.
items.translation: (Were it not for God, we would have perished.)
items.explanation: 'لولا' (were it not for / if not for) is a special conditional particle that takes a NOUN, not a verb. The noun after 'لولا' is always in the nominative case (مرفوع). 'لولا اللهُ' = 'were it not for God.' The response uses 'لَـ' + past: 'لَهلكنا' (we would have perished). 'لولا' expresses that the existence of the noun PREVENTED the result. Other examples: 'لولا المطرُ لَذهبنا' (if not for the rain, we would have gone). This construction is beloved in Arabic and frequently used in expressions of gratitude and praise.

items.question: _____ جاءَ الربيعُ، تتفتحُ الأزهارُ.
items.explanation: For habitual or certain events, 'إذا' is used rather than 'إنْ' or 'لو.' Spring ALWAYS comes, so this is a statement of natural fact, not a hypothetical. 'إذا' here means 'when/whenever' rather than 'if.' Past tense 'جاءَ' after 'إذا' for a recurring present/future event. The response 'تتفتحُ' is in the present tense (indicative), describing what regularly happens. This shows 'إذا' functioning as a temporal conjunction for general truths — similar to English 'whenever.' The distinction between 'if' and 'whenever' meanings of 'إذا' comes from context.

items.question: لو كنتِ _____ لَفهمتِ.
items.translation: (If you (f.) had been present, you would have understood.)
items.explanation: 'كانَ' and its sisters take a predicate in the ACCUSATIVE case (خبر كان منصوب). So 'حاضرةً' has fatha/tanwin (accusative marker). The feminine form 'حاضرةً' agrees with the implied subject 'أنتِ' (you, feminine). The verb 'كنتِ' uses the feminine second person: 'كُنتِ' (you were, f.). The response 'لَفهمتِ' also shows feminine agreement. This sentence demonstrates how 'لو' conditionals work with كان — the predicate of كان must be accusative regardless of the conditional context.

items.question: مهما _____ فلن أُغيّرَ رأيي.
items.translation: (No matter what you say, I won't change my mind.)
items.explanation: 'مهما' (no matter what / whatever) is a conditional particle (اسم شرط جازم) that governs the jussive mood like 'إنْ.' The verb 'يقول' in jussive for second person masculine: 'تقُلْ' — the jussive is shown by sukun (hollow verbs show jussive by shortening the vowel: تقولُ → تقُلْ). The response clause uses 'فـ' + 'لن' + subjunctive: 'فلن أُغيّرَ' (then I will not change). The 'فـ' is required when the response clause begins with certain elements like 'لن.' 'مهما' creates forceful, emphatic statements of resolve.

items.question: أينما _____ ستجدُ أصدقاءَ.
items.translation: (Wherever you go, you will find friends.)
items.explanation: 'أينما' (wherever) is another conditional particle (اسم شرط جازم) requiring the jussive mood. 'تذهبْ' has sukun. Arabic has a rich set of conditional words: 'مهما' (whatever), 'أينما' (wherever), 'كيفما' (however), 'حيثما' (wherever), 'متى' (whenever). All govern the jussive and follow the same إنْ-style pattern. The response 'ستجدُ' uses the future 'سـ' + indicative. This construction — conditional particle + jussive + future response — is a productive and versatile pattern used in proverbs, promises, and encouragement.

name: Passive Voice (المبني للمجهول)
description: Transform active sentences to passive by changing internal vowel patterns

items.question: _____ الرسالةُ أمس.
items.explanation: Arabic forms the passive by CHANGING INTERNAL VOWELS, not by adding auxiliary verbs like English 'was/were.' The active 'أَرسَلَ' (he sent) becomes passive 'أُرسِلَ' (it was sent). The pattern: first vowel becomes damma (ضمّة), vowel before last becomes kasra (كسرة). Adding the feminine marker 'تِ' for 'الرسالةُ' (feminine): 'أُرسِلَتِ.' This internal vowel change system (which applies to all verb forms) is one of Arabic's most distinctive features — no helper verbs needed, just vowel modification.

items.question: _____ العقدُ بين الشركتين.
items.translation: (The contract was signed between the two companies.)
items.explanation: Active 'وقَّعَ' (he signed, Form II) becomes passive 'وُقِّعَ' (it was signed). For Form II verbs (فعّل), the passive pattern is: 'فُعِّلَ' — first syllable gets damma, second gets kasra (under the doubled letter). The subject 'العقدُ' (the contract) is the نائب الفاعل (deputy agent) in nominative case. In passive sentences, the original object becomes the grammatical subject. Note: the agent (who signed) is typically NOT mentioned in Arabic passive — this is by design, emphasizing the action's result rather than its performer.

items.question: يُناقَشُ الموضوعُ _____ يومٍ.
items.translation: (The topic is being discussed every day.)
items.explanation: 'كلَّ' takes the accusative (فتحة) because it functions as an adverb of time (ظرف زمان), which is always in the accusative in Arabic. 'كلَّ يومٍ' = 'every day.' The passive verb 'يُناقَشُ' is the present/imperfect passive of 'ناقَشَ' (to discuss, Form III). Present passive pattern for Form III: يُفاعَلُ → يُناقَشُ. The passive present tense always starts with 'يُـ' (damma on the prefix letter). 'الموضوعُ' is the نائب الفاعل (deputy agent) in nominative. This demonstrates how time adverbs interact with passive constructions.

items.question: _____ المباراةُ بسببِ المطرِ.
items.translation: (The match was postponed because of the rain.)
items.explanation: Active 'أجَّلَ' (he postponed, Form II) → passive 'أُجِّلَ' (it was postponed). Pattern: أُفعِّلَ → أُجِّلَ. Adding feminine 'تِ' for 'المباراةُ': 'أُجِّلَتِ.' The reason uses 'بسببِ' (because of) + genitive: 'بسببِ المطرِ.' Note that in Arabic passive, you can express the CAUSE with prepositions like 'بسبب' or 'من,' but NOT the agent directly. Arabic strongly prefers the passive when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious — making it far more common in formal announcements and news reporting than casual conversation.

items.question: الأطفالُ _____ بعنايةٍ في هذا المركزِ.
items.translation: (The children are cared for carefully in this center.)
items.explanation: The verb 'اعتنى بـ' (to care for, Form VIII) takes an indirect object with 'بـ.' When passivized, the prepositional object CANNOT become the grammatical subject. Instead, the verb stays in the third person masculine singular default form: 'يُعتنى بهم' or 'يُعتنى بالأطفالِ.' Here 'الأطفالُ' is a fronted topic (مبتدأ), and the passive clause is the predicate. This is a key difference from English — Arabic passivization of intransitive-like verbs uses an impersonal construction. The damma prefix 'يُـ' marks present passive.

items.question: _____ الكتابُ إلى عشرِ لغاتٍ.
items.translation: (The book was translated into ten languages.)
items.explanation: Active 'ترجَمَ' (he translated, quadriliteral Form I) → passive 'تُرجِمَ' (it was translated). For quadriliteral verbs, the passive pattern is: تُفعلِلَ with damma on first letter and kasra on third. 'الكتابُ' becomes the نائب الفاعل (nominative). 'إلى عشرِ لغاتٍ' = 'into ten languages' — note 'عشر' is in إضافة with 'لغاتٍ.' Quadriliteral verb passives follow their own distinct pattern, separate from the three-consonant root system. Recognizing these patterns is key to reading Arabic texts fluently.

items.question: لن _____ أيُّ طالبٍ بدونِ سببٍ.
items.translation: (No student will be expelled without a reason.)
items.explanation: 'لن' (will not) governs the subjunctive mood (منصوب): 'يُفصَلَ' ends with fatha. The passive present tense base is 'يُفصَلُ' (he is dismissed) — from active 'يَفصِلُ' (he dismisses). In present passive, the prefix letter gets damma 'يُـ' and the letter before the last gets fatha: يُفعَلُ. With 'لن': يُفصَلَ (subjunctive fatha). 'أيُّ طالبٍ' = 'any student' — 'أيّ' in إضافة with indefinite noun expresses 'any.' The combination of negation + subjunctive + passive creates a strong official prohibition.

items.question: _____ النتائجُ غداً.
items.explanation: Future passive combines 'سـ' (future marker) with present passive. Active 'يُعلِنُ' (he announces, Form IV) → passive 'يُعلَنُ' (it is announced). For feminine subject 'النتائجُ' (results, broken plural treated as feminine singular): 'تُعلَنُ' → with future 'سـ': 'ستُعلَنُ.' Form IV passive pattern: يُفعَلُ (the kasra of the active Form IV changes to fatha in passive). This construction is extremely common in news broadcasts and official announcements: 'ستُعقَدُ جلسةٌ' (a session will be held).

items.question: لقد _____ القرارُ.
items.explanation: Active 'اتَّخَذَ' (he took/adopted, Form VIII) → passive 'اتُّخِذَ.' Form VIII passive: the initial vowel changes to damma and the vowel before the last consonant becomes kasra. 'لقد' (indeed/already) provides emphasis and confirms completion. The hamza-initial alif is a connective hamza (همزة وصل) that appears in Form VIII. 'القرارُ' (the decision) is the نائب الفاعل (nominative). Form VIII passives are very common in formal and bureaucratic Arabic: 'اسْتُخْدِمَ' (it was used), 'اعْتُبِرَ' (it was considered).

items.question: السيارةُ _____ أمامَ المنزلِ.
items.translation: (The car was parked in front of the house.)
items.explanation: Active 'ركَنَ' (he parked) → passive 'رُكِنَ' (it was parked). Basic Form I passive pattern: فُعِلَ (damma + kasra). Adding the feminine silent marker 'تْ' for 'السيارةُ': 'رُكِنَتْ.' Note the difference between the active feminine 'ركَنَتْ' (she parked) and passive feminine 'رُكِنَتْ' (it was parked) — only the vowels change. 'أمامَ' (in front of) is a noun of place (ظرف مكان) taking accusative. This vowel-only distinction between active and passive is why proper vowel marking (تشكيل) is so important in Arabic.

items.question: هذه الأبنيةُ _____ في القرنِ الماضي.
items.translation: (These buildings were built in the last century.)
items.explanation: Active 'بنى' (he built, defective verb) → passive 'بُنِيَ' (it was built). For defective verbs (ending in ي/و), the passive pattern becomes فُعِيَ: 'بُنِيَ.' Adding feminine 'تْ': 'بُنِيَتْ.' 'الأبنيةُ' is a broken plural treated as feminine singular for agreement. 'القرنِ الماضي' = 'the last century' (both in genitive after 'في'). Defective verb passives require careful attention because the final weak letter undergoes specific changes. Other examples: 'رُمِيَ' (it was thrown), 'دُعِيَ' (he was invited).

items.question: لم _____ الخبرُ بعدُ.
items.translation: (The news has not been confirmed yet.)
items.explanation: 'لم' (did not) requires the jussive mood (مجزوم) AND makes the present tense refer to the past. Active 'يُؤكِّدُ' (he confirms, Form II) → passive 'يُؤكَّدُ' (it is confirmed). In jussive: 'يُؤكَّدْ' (sukun). Form II passive present: the prefix gets damma 'يُـ' and the letter before last gets fatha instead of kasra: يُفعَّلُ. 'بعدُ' (yet) is built on damma (ظرف مبني على الضم) because it's cut off from its إضافة partner ('بعدَ ذلك' shortened to 'بعدُ'). This word is always damma when used meaning 'yet.'

items.question: _____ بالمشروعِ منذُ سنةٍ.
items.translation: (Work has been started on the project since a year ago.)
items.explanation: Active 'بَدَأَ بـ' (he began with) → passive 'بُدِئَ بـ' (it was begun with). Since 'بدأ' takes an indirect object with 'بـ,' the passive uses an impersonal construction: no explicit grammatical subject. 'بُدِئَ بالمشروعِ' = literally 'it was begun with the project.' The hamza in 'بُدِئَ' sits on the line because the preceding vowel is kasra and the hamza has fatha — following Arabic's hamza-seat rules. 'منذُ سنةٍ' = 'since a year.' Impersonal passives are very common in Arabic, especially in formal and academic writing.

items.question: الجُناةُ _____ أمامَ المحكمةِ.
items.translation: (The criminals were brought before the court.)
items.explanation: Active 'قدَّمَ' (he presented, Form II) → passive 'قُدِّمَ' (he was presented). For masculine plural: 'قُدِّموا' (they were presented/brought). The واو + ألف ending marks masculine plural past tense. 'الجُناةُ' (the criminals, broken plural of جانٍ) as نائب الفاعل takes nominative. 'أمامَ المحكمةِ' = 'before the court' (أمام in accusative as ظرف مكان). Legal vocabulary frequently uses passive voice in Arabic, as in 'حُكِمَ عليهِ' (he was sentenced), 'بُرِّئَ' (he was acquitted).

items.question: المحاضرةُ _____ إلى يومِ الخميسِ.
items.translation: (The lecture has been moved to Thursday.)
items.explanation: Active 'نقَلَ' (he moved/transferred) → passive 'نُقِلَ' (it was moved). Adding feminine 'تْ' for 'المحاضرةُ': 'نُقِلَتْ.' The basic Form I passive is simple and elegant: فُعِلَ (damma-kasra). 'إلى يومِ الخميسِ' uses إضافة: 'day of Thursday.' Days of the week in Arabic are nouns, not adjectives, so they appear in إضافة with 'يوم.' This passive construction is typical in administrative announcements. Arabic passive voice, with its vowel-change mechanism, is remarkably efficient — conveying in one word what English needs three words ('was moved') to express.

name: Number Agreement (تمييز العدد)
description: Master the complex rules of how Arabic numbers agree with their counted nouns

items.question: في المكتبةِ _____ كتبٍ.
items.translation: (There are five books in the library.)
items.explanation: Arabic numbers 3-10 have a REVERSE gender agreement rule (تمييز العدد): the number takes the OPPOSITE gender of the counted noun. 'كتاب' (book) is MASCULINE, so the number takes the FEMININE form: 'خمسةُ' (with taa marbuta). If counting feminine nouns, you'd use 'خمسُ' (without taa marbuta). This reverse agreement rule (مخالفة العدد للمعدود) is one of Arabic's most famous grammar rules and consistently surprises learners. Numbers 3-10 also require the counted noun in GENITIVE PLURAL: 'كتبٍ.'

items.question: حضرَ _____ طالباتٍ.
