items.explanation: 'طالبة' (female student) is FEMININE, so the number takes the MASCULINE form (no taa marbuta): 'سبعُ.' This is the reverse gender rule in action again. The counted noun 'طالباتٍ' is in the genitive plural with tanwin. 'سبعُ' takes damma (nominative) because 'سبعُ طالباتٍ' is the subject (فاعل) of 'حضرَ.' The number and counted noun form an إضافة: number (مضاف) + counted noun in genitive plural (مضاف إليه). This إضافة structure is mandatory for numbers 3-10. No exceptions.

items.question: اشتريتُ _____ أقلامٍ.
items.explanation: 'قلم' (pen) is masculine → number takes feminine form: 'ثلاثة.' It's in the accusative (مفعول به = direct object): 'ثلاثةَ' (fatha). The counted noun 'أقلامٍ' is genitive plural (مضاف إليه). For numbers 3-10, the counted noun is ALWAYS: (1) in إضافة with the number, (2) in the plural form, (3) in the genitive case. This three-part rule is absolute. Compare: 'ثلاثةَ أقلامٍ' (three pens, masc.) vs. 'ثلاثَ ساعاتٍ' (three hours, fem.) — opposite gender forms for number based on noun gender.

items.question: في الفصلِ _____ طالباً.
items.translation: (There are twenty students in the classroom.)
items.explanation: Numbers 11-99 have DIFFERENT rules from 3-10. For the tens (20, 30, 40...), the number is a noun that does NOT change for gender: 'عشرون' for both masculine and feminine counted nouns. 'عشرون' takes 'ون' in nominative (here it's the subject/خبر مقدم). In accusative/genitive it becomes 'عشرينَ.' The counted noun after tens is SINGULAR ACCUSATIVE (تمييز): 'طالباً' — NOT plural. This is the opposite of 3-10 which take plural genitive. This shift from plural to singular at number 11 is a critical Arabic number rule.

items.question: قرأتُ _____ عشرةَ كتاباً.
items.explanation: For compound numbers 11-19, the ones digit follows special rules. 'كتاب' is masculine → the ones part takes FEMININE form (reverse agreement still applies for 3-9 part): 'خمسةَ.' But the tens part 'عشرةَ' takes the SAME gender as the noun for 11-19: masculine noun → 'عشَرَ' (no taa marbuta). Wait — here we have 'عشرةَ' which is the feminine form, used with... Actually in 13-19, the ones digit reverses and the ten agrees: 'خمسةَ عشَرَ' (15, with masculine nouns). The counted noun 'كتاباً' is singular accusative (تمييز). Numbers 11-19 are the most complex in Arabic.

items.question: كانَ عمرُهُ _____ سنةً.
items.explanation: The number 11 has a special form. 'سنة' (year) is FEMININE, so BOTH parts of 11 must be FEMININE: 'إحدى عشرةَ.' For masculine nouns: 'أحدَ عشَرَ.' Unlike 3-10 (reverse gender), numbers 11 and 12 have DIRECT gender agreement — both parts match the noun's gender. 'إحدى' is the feminine of 'أحد' (one), and 'عشرةَ' is feminine of 'عشَرَ' (ten). The counted noun 'سنةً' is singular accusative. Both parts of 11 and 12 are indeclinable (مبني على الفتح) — they always end in fatha.

items.question: في البيتِ _____ غرفتانِ.
items.translation: (There are two rooms in the house.)
items.explanation: For the number 2, Arabic uses the DUAL form of the noun itself — NO separate number word is needed. 'غرفة' (room) → dual nominative: 'غرفتانِ.' The number 'اثنان/اثنتان' (two) is typically not used before the noun — the dual suffix does the work. When 'اثنان' IS used, it comes AFTER the noun for emphasis: 'غرفتانِ اثنتانِ' (two rooms, exactly two). The dual ending 'ـانِ' shows nominative case; accusative/genitive uses 'ـيْنِ': 'غرفتَيْنِ.' Arabic's built-in dual system eliminates the need for counting with 'two.'

items.question: _____ الولدُ في المسابقةِ.
items.translation: (The boy won first place in the competition.)
items.explanation: This tests understanding that for number 1, Arabic simply uses the noun without a number, or adds 'واحد' AFTER the noun as an adjective: 'ولدٌ واحدٌ' (one boy). The sentence 'فازَ الولدُ' (the boy won) shows that singular nouns inherently express 'one' without needing a number. When 'واحد/واحدة' is used, it agrees in gender and follows the noun as an adjective (not in إضافة). For ordinals, 'الأول' (first) is an adjective: 'المركزُ الأولُ' (first place). This simplicity for 1 contrasts with the complexity of 3-10.

items.question: جاءَ _____ عشَرَ رجلاً.
items.explanation: In compound numbers 13-19, the ones digit follows the REVERSE gender rule (like 3-10): 'رجل' is masculine → 'ثلاثة' (feminine form). The tens part 'عشَرَ' takes the SAME gender as the noun: masculine → 'عشَرَ' (no taa marbuta). So: 'ثلاثةَ عشَرَ' for masculine nouns, 'ثلاثَ عشْرةَ' for feminine. Both parts are indeclinable (مبني على الفتح). The counted noun 'رجلاً' is SINGULAR ACCUSATIVE — not plural like with 3-10. This transition from plural genitive (3-10) to singular accusative (11-99) is a key Arabic number milestone.

items.question: هناك _____ دولةً في الاتحادِ.
items.translation: (There are twenty-seven countries in the union.)
items.explanation: 'دولة' (country) is FEMININE → the ones digit takes the MASCULINE form (reverse agreement): 'سبعُ'... wait, 'دولة' is feminine, so per 3-10 rule, the number takes NO taa marbuta = 'سبعٌ.' The tens 'عشرون' doesn't change for gender. Connected with 'و' (and): 'سبعٌ وعشرون.' The nominative form is used here (subject position). Counted noun 'دولةً' is singular accusative. In Arabic, compound numbers put the ones FIRST: 'سبعٌ وعشرون' = literally 'seven and twenty.' This ones-first order persists in Modern Standard Arabic.

items.question: عندي _____ سنةً.
items.explanation: Tens (20, 30, 40... 90) are gender-neutral — they don't change for masculine or feminine nouns. 'ثلاثون' is nominative (as خبر of an implied sentence). In accusative/genitive: 'ثلاثينَ.' The counted noun 'سنةً' is singular accusative (تمييز). Arabic expresses age with 'عند + pronoun': 'عندي ثلاثون سنةً' = literally 'I have thirty years.' The tens are treated as sound masculine plurals in their declension: ـون (nominative), ـينَ (accusative/genitive). 'ثلاثون' from the root ث-ل-ث (three) + masculine plural suffix.

items.question: حضرَ الحفلَ _____ شخصٍ.
items.explanation: 'مئة' (or 'مائة,' both spellings accepted) means 'one hundred.' It is INVARIABLE for gender — same form for masculine and feminine counted nouns. The counted noun after 'مئة' is SINGULAR GENITIVE: 'شخصٍ' (person, genitive with tanwin). This is an إضافة: 'مئةُ شخصٍ.' For 200: 'مئتانِ/مئتَيْنِ' (dual). For 300-900: number + مئة in إضافة: 'ثلاثُمئةٍ' (300). The shift to singular genitive (from singular accusative for 11-99) at 100 is another major rule change. Arabic number grammar has three distinct zones: 3-10, 11-99, and 100+.

items.question: اشتريتُ الكتابَ بـ _____ ديناراً.
items.translation: (I bought the book for forty-five dinars.)
items.explanation: 'دينار' (dinar) is MASCULINE → ones digit reverses to feminine: 'خمسة.' After 'بـ' (preposition), the number is genitive: 'خمسةٍ.' The tens part 'أربعين' is in the genitive/accusative form (ـينَ). Together: 'خمسةٍ وأربعينَ.' The counted noun 'ديناراً' is singular accusative (تمييز for 11-99 range). This sentence shows the full complexity: preposition governing genitive → إعراب of number changes → but the general compound number structure remains ones + و + tens. Getting all parts right simultaneously is the ultimate Arabic number challenge.

items.question: _____ الكتابِ مفيدةٌ.
items.translation: (The third chapter of the book is useful.)
items.explanation: Ordinal numbers (first, second, third...) work as ADJECTIVES in Arabic. They follow the noun and agree in gender, number, case, and definiteness. 'الفصلُ' (the chapter, masculine) → 'الثالثُ' (the third, masculine). Both take 'ال' and nominative case (مرفوع). Ordinals 1-10 have distinct forms: أول، ثانٍ، ثالث، رابع... For 11+, cardinal numbers are used as ordinals: 'الصفحةُ الخامسةَ عشرةَ' (the 15th page). Ordinals are much simpler than cardinals — they follow regular adjective agreement rules with no reverse gender.

items.question: _____ ألفِ طالبٍ في الجامعةِ.
items.translation: (There are a thousand students in the university.)
items.explanation: 'ألف' (thousand) behaves like 'مئة' — the counted noun is SINGULAR GENITIVE: 'طالبٍ.' Structure: 'ألفُ طالبٍ' in إضافة. 'ألف' doesn't change for gender. For 2000: 'ألفانِ/ألفَيْنِ' (dual). For 3000-10000: number + ألف with the reverse gender rule returning: 'ثلاثةُ آلافٍ' (3000, آلاف is masculine broken plural → number is feminine). The plural of ألف is آلاف. 'ألفُ' is nominative here as the fronted predicate (خبر مقدم). Arabic's number system at 100+ returns to إضافة simplicity after the complex 11-99 zone.

name: Case Endings (علامات الإعراب)
description: Understand nominative, accusative, and genitive case markers on Arabic nouns and adjectives

items.question: جاءَ _____ إلى المدرسةِ.
items.explanation: The subject (فاعل) of a verb always takes the NOMINATIVE case (مرفوع), marked by damma (ضمّة) on the last letter: 'الطالبُ.' Arabic has three cases: nominative (مرفوع - damma), accusative (منصوب - fatha), and genitive (مجرور - kasra). The nominative is used for subjects, predicates of nominal sentences, and the predicate of كانَ in some analyses. Case endings are the backbone of Arabic grammar — they reveal every word's function in the sentence without needing word order.

items.question: رأيتُ _____ في الشارعِ.
items.translation: (I saw the teacher in the street.)
items.explanation: The direct object (مفعول به) takes the ACCUSATIVE case (منصوب), marked by fatha (فتحة): 'المعلمَ.' The verb 'رأيتُ' (I saw) is transitive, requiring a direct object. The accusative case marks objects, adverbs, the subject of إنّ and sisters, the predicate of كانَ and sisters, and many other functions. In unvoweled text, context determines case, but in formal Arabic and Quranic recitation, these endings are pronounced. Mastering case endings is essential for reading classical texts accurately.

items.question: ذهبتُ إلى _____ الكبيرِ.
items.explanation: After prepositions (حروف الجر - إلى، من، في، على، بـ، لـ...), nouns take the GENITIVE case (مجرور), marked by kasra (كسرة): 'السوقِ.' The adjective 'الكبيرِ' also takes genitive because adjectives match their noun in case, gender, number, and definiteness. This four-way agreement of adjectives is called المطابقة and is a fundamental Arabic grammar concept. The preposition 'إلى' (to) is one of the most common genitive-governing prepositions in Arabic.

items.question: الولدُ _____ مُجتهدٌ.
items.explanation: Adjectives (نعت/صفة) must agree with their noun in CASE. Since 'الولدُ' is nominative (subject/مبتدأ), the adjective 'الصغيرُ' must also be nominative (damma). The predicate 'مُجتهدٌ' is indefinite with tanwin — predicates in nominal sentences are typically indefinite in Arabic. This creates an interesting pattern: 'الولدُ الصغيرُ' (definite + definite = attributive adjective) vs. 'الولدُ مُجتهدٌ' (definite + indefinite = predicate). This definite/indefinite distinction tells you if the adjective describes or predicates.

items.question: إنّ _____ جميلةٌ.
items.explanation: 'إنّ' and its sisters (أنّ، لكنّ، ليتَ، لعلّ، كأنّ) govern the ACCUSATIVE on their subject (اسم إنّ منصوب): 'المدينةَ' with fatha. Their predicate (خبر إنّ) stays nominative: 'جميلةٌ.' These particles introduce emphasis, contrast, or wish. 'إنّ' means 'indeed/verily.' Without 'إنّ': 'المدينةُ جميلةٌ' (nominative subject). With 'إنّ': 'إنّ المدينةَ جميلةٌ' (accusative subject). This case change caused by particles is called عمل الحروف — particles literally 'work' on nouns by changing their case.

items.question: كان الجوُّ _____ أمس.
items.explanation: 'كانَ' and its sisters (أصبح، أمسى، ظلّ، بات، صار، ليس، ما زال...) take a nominative subject (اسم كان مرفوع) and an ACCUSATIVE predicate (خبر كان منصوب): 'بارداً' with fatha + tanwin. 'الجوُّ' is the subject of كان (nominative). 'بارداً' is the predicate (accusative). Compare: without كان → 'الجوُّ باردٌ' (nominative predicate). With كان → 'كانَ الجوُّ بارداً' (accusative predicate). كان literally 'enters upon' the nominal sentence and changes the predicate's case. This is one of Arabic grammar's most fundamental operations.

items.question: سلّمتُ على _____ الجديدِ.
items.explanation: After the preposition 'على' (on/upon), nouns take genitive (مجرور): 'الجارِ' with kasra. The verb 'سلّمَ على' (to greet) requires the preposition 'على.' The adjective 'الجديدِ' matches in genitive case, masculine gender, singular, and definite. Many Arabic verbs require specific prepositions: 'بحثَ عن' (to search for), 'تحدّثَ مع' (to speak with), 'فكّرَ في' (to think about). The preposition determines the case of everything that follows. Learning verb-preposition combinations is as important as learning the verbs themselves.

items.question: رأيتُ _____ جميلاً في الحديقةِ.
items.translation: (I saw a beautiful bird in the garden.)
items.explanation: The indefinite direct object takes accusative + tanwin (تنوين الفتح): 'طائراً.' Tanwin fatha is written with an extra alif: 'طائراً' (the alif is orthographic, not part of the word). The adjective 'جميلاً' is also accusative + tanwin + indefinite, matching the noun. Note the contrast: definite objects have just fatha ('الطائرَ'), while indefinite objects have fatha + tanwin ('طائراً'). The tanwin alif is a distinctive feature of Arabic script — it only appears with fatha tanwin, never with damma or kasra tanwin.

items.question: المُعلّمونَ _____ في عملِهم.
items.translation: (The teachers are dedicated in their work.)
items.explanation: Sound masculine plurals (جمع مذكر سالم) show case through their ending: nominative 'ـونَ' and accusative/genitive 'ـينَ.' 'المُعلّمونَ' is nominative (subject) → predicate 'مُخلصونَ' is also nominative. The predicate is INDEFINITE (no ال) despite the subject being definite — standard for nominal sentences. If the sentence were 'رأيتُ المعلّمينَ المُخلصينَ' (I saw the dedicated teachers), both would take 'ـينَ' for accusative. This الف/ياء alternation is the plural equivalent of damma/fatha for singular nouns.

items.question: هؤلاء المعلماتُ _____.
items.explanation: Sound feminine plurals (جمع مؤنث سالم) show nominative with damma: 'المعلماتُ.' But they have a SPECIAL rule: accusative and genitive BOTH use kasra (not fatha for accusative). So 'معلماتٍ' for both accusative and genitive. The predicate 'جديداتٌ' is nominative (damma + tanwin) and indefinite. This kasra-for-accusative exception for sound feminine plurals is one of Arabic's most notable irregularities. It applies to all ـات plurals: 'رأيتُ المعلماتِ' (I saw the teachers, kasra not fatha for accusative).

items.question: زرتُ _____ كثيرةً.
items.explanation: Broken plurals (جمع تكسير) like 'دُوَل' are often DIPTOTES (ممنوع من الصرف) — they take fatha instead of kasra in genitive and NEVER take tanwin (with one exception: when indefinite accusative, they take fatha). Actually 'دُوَلاً' here takes accusative fatha + tanwin... Some broken plurals ARE diptotes (taking fatha for genitive, no tanwin), while others are fully declinable. 'دُوَل' as a plural of 'دولة' follows regular declension. The accusative indefinite is 'دُوَلاً.' Understanding which plurals are diptotes requires memorization of patterns.

items.question: ليسَ الامتحانُ _____.
items.explanation: 'ليسَ' (is not) is one of كان's sisters — its predicate takes the ACCUSATIVE: 'صعباً' (fatha + tanwin). 'الامتحانُ' is the subject (اسم ليس مرفوع). 'ليس' is unique among كان's sisters because it only expresses present negation and has no past or future forms. Compare: 'الامتحانُ صعبٌ' (the exam is difficult, nominative predicate) vs. 'ليسَ الامتحانُ صعباً' (the exam is not difficult, accusative predicate). This accusative shift under 'ليس' is consistent and important for forming correct negative statements.

items.question: للطالبِ أخٌ _____ وأختٌ صُغرى.
items.translation: (The student has an older brother and a younger sister.)
items.explanation: 'أكبرُ' (older/bigger) is a diptote (ممنوع من الصرف) in the pattern 'أفعل.' Diptotes have TWO case markers instead of three: damma for nominative, fatha for BOTH accusative AND genitive. They NEVER take tanwin (no نون). Here 'أكبرُ' is nominative (describing 'أخٌ,' which is nominative). If it were genitive: 'أخٍ أكبرَ' (fatha, not kasra). Diptotes include: patterns أفعل, فعلان, colors/defects (أحمر), place names (مصرَ), and foreign proper nouns (إبراهيمَ). This category is critical for advanced case marking.

items.question: كتبتُ الرسالةَ _____ كبيرٍ.
items.translation: (I wrote the letter with great care.)
items.explanation: The preposition 'بِـ' (with/by) governs the genitive case: 'بِاهتمامٍ' (kasra + tanwin for indefinite genitive). The adjective 'كبيرٍ' also takes genitive + tanwin to match. 'بِاهتمامٍ كبيرٍ' = 'with great care.' Prepositional phrases in Arabic function as adverbial modifiers (حال or ظرف). The بـ preposition has many meanings: بالقلمِ (with the pen - instrument), بسرعةٍ (quickly - manner), بالسيارةِ (by car - means). Each preposition governs genitive absolutely — this is one of Arabic's most unbreakable rules.

items.question: لعلّ _____ يتحسّنُ غداً.
items.explanation: 'لعلّ' (perhaps/hopefully) is one of إنّ's sisters (أخوات إنّ). Like all of them, it puts its subject in the ACCUSATIVE: 'الطقسَ' (fatha). Its predicate stays nominative: the verbal sentence 'يتحسّنُ' (it improves). The six sisters of إنّ each add a distinct meaning: إنّ (indeed), أنّ (that), لكنّ (but), كأنّ (as if), ليتَ (I wish), لعلّ (perhaps). All six share the same grammatical operation: accusative subject + nominative predicate. Memorizing these six particles and their meanings is a milestone in Arabic grammar mastery.

name: Verb Forms / Patterns (أوزان الفعل)
description: Learn the ten Arabic verb forms (I-X) and how they modify root meanings

items.question: _____ الطفلُ العربيةَ في البيتِ.
items.explanation: Form V (تَفَعَّلَ) often expresses the REFLEXIVE of Form II. Root ع-ل-م: Form I 'عَلِمَ' (he knew), Form II 'علَّمَ' (he taught), Form V 'تعلَّمَ' (he learned = taught himself). Form V adds the prefix 'تَـ' to Form II's doubled middle letter. This reflexive relationship is highly productive: Form II 'كسَّرَ' (he smashed) → Form V 'تكسَّرَ' (it got smashed). Understanding the Form II → Form V relationship lets you predict meaning: if you know the Form II, you can often guess Form V's meaning by adding 'self' or 'became.'

items.question: _____ الطلابُ في المشروعِ.
items.explanation: Form VI (تَفَاعَلَ) expresses RECIPROCAL or mutual action. Root ع-و-ن: Form III 'عاونَ' (he helped/assisted), Form VI 'تعاونَ' (they cooperated = helped each other). Form VI adds 'تَـ' to Form III's pattern. The reciprocal meaning is key: Form III 'كاتَبَ' (he corresponded with) → Form VI 'تكاتَبَ' (they corresponded with each other). Form VI often implies multiple participants: 'تبادلوا الهدايا' (they exchanged gifts). This form is extremely common in social and political vocabulary: تفاوض (negotiate), تواصل (communicate), تعاون (cooperate).

items.question: _____ البابُ فجأةً.
items.explanation: Form VII (اِنفَعَلَ) expresses the PASSIVE or reflexive of Form I, often with spontaneous/involuntary meaning. Root ف-ت-ح: Form I 'فتَحَ' (he opened), Form VII 'انفتحَ' (it opened by itself / became open). Form VII adds the prefix 'اِنـ' to Form I. It implies no external agent — the action happens naturally: 'انكسرَ الكوبُ' (the cup broke), 'انطلقَ القطارُ' (the train departed). Form VII CANNOT be transitive — it never takes a direct object. This makes it distinct from Form V, which can sometimes take objects.

items.question: _____ الشركةُ عمّالاً جُدداً.
items.explanation: Form X (اِستَفعَلَ) often means 'to seek/request the action of Form I' or 'to consider.' Root خ-د-م: Form I 'خَدَمَ' (he served), Form X 'استخدَمَ' (he hired/employed = sought service from). The prefix 'استـ' suggests seeking or requesting. Classic examples: Form I 'غفَرَ' (he forgave) → Form X 'استغفرَ' (he sought forgiveness), Form I 'عَلِمَ' (he knew) → Form X 'استعلَمَ' (he inquired). Form X is one of the most recognizable patterns in Arabic, and many modern terms use it: استخدم (use), استثمر (invest), استقبل (receive).

items.question: _____ المديرُ بالتقريرِ.
items.explanation: Form III (فَاعَلَ) often expresses interaction with someone or trying to do something to/with another party. Root و-ف-ق: Form I (rare) → Form III 'وافقَ' (he agreed with/approved). Form III's distinctive long 'ا' after the first root letter creates a rhythmic pattern. Other examples: 'سافرَ' (he traveled), 'حاولَ' (he tried), 'ساعدَ' (he helped), 'قاتلَ' (he fought). Form III is extremely productive in everyday Arabic. The preposition 'بـ' with 'وافق' means 'approved of/agreed to.' Many Form III verbs require specific prepositions.

items.question: _____ الوزيرُ عن مشروعٍ جديدٍ.
items.explanation: Form IV (أَفعَلَ) is TRANSITIVE/CAUSATIVE — it often makes a Form I verb transitive. Root ع-ل-ن: the concept of 'being public' → Form IV 'أعلَنَ' (he announced = made public). Form IV adds a hamza prefix 'أَـ' and has a distinctive vowel pattern. Examples: 'جلَسَ' (he sat) → 'أجلَسَ' (he seated someone), 'خرَجَ' (he went out) → 'أخرَجَ' (he took out/produced). Form IV is one of the most common transitive forms. The present tense is 'يُعلِنُ' — note the damma prefix and kasra before the last letter. Form IV present ALWAYS starts with يُـ.

items.question: _____ العلماءُ الحقيقةَ.
items.explanation: Form VIII (اِفتَعَلَ) has varied meanings including reflexive, effort, or intensity. Root ك-ش-ف: Form I 'كشَفَ' (he uncovered), Form VIII 'اكتشفَ' (he discovered = uncovered with effort), Form X 'استكشفَ' (he explored = sought to uncover). The 'تـ' infix after the first root letter distinguishes Form VIII. It sometimes assimilates: root ت-ح-د → 'اتَّحَدَ' (united, the ت doubles). Form VIII is very common: اجتمعَ (gathered), اختارَ (chose), اعتقدَ (believed), انتظرَ (waited). Many everyday verbs are Form VIII, making it one of the most frequently encountered derived forms.

items.question: _____ الزجاجُ عندَ سقوطِهِ.
items.explanation: Form V (تَفَعَّلَ) as reflexive/passive of Form II: root ح-ط-م: Form II 'حطَّمَ' (he smashed/shattered it) → Form V 'تحطَّمَ' (it got shattered). The glass wasn't smashed by someone intentionally — it shattered as a result of falling. Form V often implies a gradual or natural process: 'تعلَّمَ' (learned gradually), 'تطوَّرَ' (developed), 'تحسَّنَ' (improved). The reflexive nature means the subject undergoes the action rather than performing it. This makes Form V essential for describing results, changes of state, and natural processes.

items.question: _____ اللونُ بعدَ الغسيلِ.
items.explanation: Another Form V example: root غ-ي-ر: Form II 'غيَّرَ' (he changed something) → Form V 'تغيَّرَ' (it changed / became changed). The distinction is crucial: 'غيَّرَ ملابسَهُ' (he changed his clothes — transitive) vs. 'تغيَّرَ الطقسُ' (the weather changed — intransitive). Form V converts transitive Form II verbs into intransitive ones. Other pairs: 'وقَّفَ' (he stopped something) → 'توقَّفَ' (it stopped), 'طوَّرَ' (he developed something) → 'تطوَّرَ' (it developed). This Form II/V pairing is one of Arabic's most elegant and predictable relationships.

items.question: _____ معَ زملائي في القرارِ.
items.translation: (I agreed with my colleagues on the decision.)
items.explanation: Form VIII (اِفتَعَلَ) of root و-ف-ق: 'اتّفقَ' (he agreed). When the first root letter is 'و,' it assimilates with the Form VIII 'ت' infix, creating a doubled letter: و + ت → تّ. So 'اِوتَفَقَ' becomes 'اِتَّفَقَ.' This assimilation applies to all و-initial roots in Form VIII: 'وصل' → 'اِتَّصَلَ' (he contacted), 'وحد' → 'اِتَّحَدَ' (he united). The verb 'اتّفقَ معَ' takes the preposition 'مع' (with). Understanding these phonological assimilation rules is key to recognizing Form VIII verbs from roots beginning with و.

items.question: _____ البلدُ بسرعةٍ كبيرةٍ.
items.explanation: Form V of root ط-و-ر: Form II 'طوَّرَ' (he developed something) → Form V 'تطوَّرَ' (it developed = became developed). When the middle root letter is a weak letter (و or ي), it remains visible in Form V: 'تطوَّرَ.' The adverb 'بسرعةٍ كبيرةٍ' (with great speed) uses the preposition 'بـ' + genitive. Form V is extensively used in modern Arabic for concepts of progress and change: 'تحوَّلَ' (transformed), 'تقدَّمَ' (advanced), 'تأخَّرَ' (was delayed). This makes Form V essential vocabulary for news, academic, and professional Arabic.

items.question: هل يُمكِنُكَ أن _____ هذه الوثيقةَ؟
items.explanation: Quadriliteral Form I (فَعلَلَ): 'ترجَمَ' (he translated) has FOUR root letters (ت-ر-ج-م). In the present tense, quadriliterals follow the pattern يُفَعلِلُ: 'يُترجِمُ.' After 'أن' (subjunctive): 'تُترجِمَ' (fatha). The damma prefix 'يُـ/تُـ' is characteristic of quadriliteral and Form II/III/IV present tense. Other quadriliterals: 'دحرَجَ' (he rolled), 'زلزَلَ' (he shook/quaked), 'بعثَرَ' (he scattered). Quadriliterals are less common than triliterals but include important everyday words like 'ترجَمَ,' 'تلفَنَ' (telephoned), and 'برمَجَ' (programmed).

items.question: _____ الأصدقاءُ الرسائلَ.
items.explanation: Form VI (تَفاعَلَ) of root ب-د-ل: Form III 'بادلَ' (he exchanged with someone) → Form VI 'تبادلَ' (they exchanged with each other). Form VI's reciprocal meaning shines here — multiple parties do the action mutually. The long 'ا' in Form VI's 'تفاعل' pattern marks it clearly. Other Form VI examples: 'تسامحَ' (they forgave each other), 'تشارَكَ' (they shared), 'تناقشَ' (they discussed with each other). Form VI is particularly common in describing social interactions, negotiations, and relationships — situations where two or more parties are equally involved in the action.

items.question: _____ الحادثُ اهتمامَ الجميعِ.
items.explanation: Form X (اِستَفعَلَ) of root ر-ع-ي: Form I 'رعى' (he tended/cared for) → Form X 'استرعى' (he attracted/drew = sought attention/care from). With weak final root letter (ي), Form X becomes 'اِستَفعَلَ' → 'استرعى.' The expression 'استرعى اهتمامَ' (attracted the attention of) is very common in Arabic media and formal writing. Other Form X examples with weak roots: 'استدعى' (he summoned), 'استثنى' (he excepted). Form X weak-final verbs are conjugated like any weak verb: 'يسترعي' (present), 'استرعِ' (imperative).

items.question: _____ الماءُ عندما وصلَتِ الحرارةُ إلى مئةِ درجةٍ.
items.translation: (The water boiled when the temperature reached 100 degrees.)
items.explanation: Form I (فَعَلَ) is the simplest verb pattern — the BASE form from which all others derive. Root غ-ل-ي: 'غلى' (it boiled). This is intransitive in Form I. Form IV 'أغلى' would mean 'he boiled something' (transitive). Not all roots use all ten forms — each root has its own set of attested forms. 'غلى' is a defective verb (ناقص) ending in ي, following the pattern of 'مشى' (walked), 'بكى' (cried), 'جرى' (ran). Learning which forms a root uses, and which meanings each carries, is the heart of Arabic vocabulary building. The form system makes Arabic incredibly systematic.

name: Attached Pronouns (الضمائر المتصلة)
description: Use suffix pronouns attached to verbs, nouns, and prepositions

items.question: هذا _____.
items.explanation: Arabic attaches possessive pronouns directly to nouns as suffixes. 'ي' (my) attached to 'كتاب' (book) creates 'كتابي' (my book). The suffix ي replaces tanwin and makes the noun definite — no 'ال' is used with possessive suffixes. The main possessive suffixes are: ـي (my), ـكَ (your, m.), ـكِ (your, f.), ـهُ (his), ـها (her), ـنا (our), ـكُم (your, pl.), ـهُم (their). These suffixes attach to nouns, verbs (as objects), and prepositions. This system is extremely efficient — one suffix replaces what English needs a separate word for.

items.question: أعطيتُ _____ الهديةَ.
items.explanation: When attached to verbs, pronouns function as OBJECTS. 'أعطيتُ' (I gave) + 'ـهُ' (him) = 'أعطيتُهُ' (I gave him). The same suffixes used for noun possession (ـي، ـكَ، ـهُ...) also serve as verb objects. Arabic can attach TWO object pronouns to one verb: 'أعطيتُهُ إيّاها' (I gave it to him). The first object pronoun attaches directly; the second uses the separate pronoun form (إيّا + suffix). This double-object construction is unique to Arabic among Semitic languages and allows remarkably compact expression.

items.question: الكتابُ _____.
items.explanation: Prepositions take the same pronoun suffixes: 'لـ' (for/to) + 'ـكَ' (you, m.) = 'لكَ' (for you / yours). Common preposition + pronoun combinations: 'منهُ' (from him), 'فيها' (in it/her), 'عليكَ' (upon you), 'معنا' (with us), 'إليهِم' (to them), 'بكِ' (with you, f.). These prepositional phrases function as predicates, adverbs, or complements. 'الكتابُ لكَ' uses 'لكَ' as a predicate meaning 'is yours.' Arabic ownership is often expressed with 'لـ' + pronoun rather than using a verb 'to have.'

items.question: هل رأيتَ _____؟
items.explanation: The feminine singular object pronoun 'ـها' (her/it, f.) attaches to the verb: 'رأيتَها' (you saw her). Note: 'ـها' serves for both 'her' (person) and 'it' (feminine noun). Since Arabic nouns have gender, even objects use gendered pronouns: 'المدينةُ... رأيتُها' (the city... I saw it). The vowel before 'ـها' doesn't change, making it one of the easier suffixes. Compare: 'ـهُ' (him/it, m.) changes the preceding vowel — after kasra, it becomes 'ـهِ': 'بِهِ' (with him), 'فيهِ' (in it). This vowel harmony rule for 'ـهُ/ـهِ' requires attention.

items.question: أمُّ _____ طبيبةٌ.
items.explanation: The masculine plural possessive suffix 'ـهُم' (their) attaches to 'أمّ' (mother): 'أمُّهُم' (their mother). For a female group, Arabic uses 'ـهُنَّ': 'أمُّهُنَّ.' This gender distinction in plural pronouns is a feature English lacks — English 'their' covers both. The full set of attached plural pronouns: ـنا (our), ـكُم (your, m. pl.), ـكُنَّ (your, f. pl.), ـهُم (their, m.), ـهُنَّ (their, f.). In practice, 'ـهُم' is used for mixed groups and 'ـهُنَّ' for exclusively female groups. Dual forms also exist: ـكُما, ـهُما.

items.question: سيارةُ _____ جديدةٌ.
items.explanation: 'ـنا' (our/us) is the first person plural suffix. Attached to 'سيارة' (car): 'سيارتُنا' (our car). 'ـنا' is unique because it's the SAME for possessive (our), verbal object (us), and prepositional object: 'بيتُنا' (our house), 'ساعدَنا' (he helped us), 'منّا' (from us). Most other pronouns have slight variations between these uses. The 'ة' of 'سيارة' converts to 'ت' before suffixes: 'سيارتُنا' (not 'سيارةُنا'). This is a general Arabic spelling rule: taa marbuta (ة) becomes taa maftuha (ت) when anything is appended.

items.question: المعلمةُ تُساعدُ _____.
items.explanation: When 'ي' (me/my) is attached to a VERB as an object, a protective 'ن' (نون الوقاية) is added: 'تُساعدُني' (she helps me), not 'تُساعدُي.' This نون الوقاية (nun of protection) prevents the verb ending from being confused with possessive constructions. Compare: 'كتابي' (my book, no noon needed) vs. 'علّمَني' (he taught me, noon required). The noon appears with ALL verbs: 'يسألُني' (he asks me), 'أعطاني' (he gave me), 'ساعدوني' (they helped me). It also appears with 'إنّ' → 'إنّني' (indeed I) and 'لعلّ' → 'لعلّني' (perhaps I).

items.question: بيتُ _____ قريبٌ من هنا.
items.explanation: Arabic distinguishes gender in second person pronouns: 'ـكَ' (your, masculine) vs. 'ـكِ' (your, feminine). 'بيتُكِ' = 'your house' (speaking to a woman). This gender distinction extends through all second person forms: 'أنتَ/أنتِ' (you), 'لكَ/لكِ' (for you), 'رأيتُكَ/رأيتُكِ' (I saw you). English lacks this entirely. The kasra ending of 'ـكِ' makes it clearly distinct from the fatha of 'ـكَ' in pronunciation. Arabic's consistent gender marking in pronouns, verbs, and adjectives means speakers always know the addressee's gender.

items.question: أخبرتُ _____ بالخبرِ.
items.explanation: 'أخبرَ' (to tell/inform) takes a direct object (person) and 'بـ' (about the thing). So: 'أخبرتُهُم بالخبرِ' = 'I informed them about the news.' The suffix 'ـهُم' (them, m.) attaches directly to the past tense verb. With present tense: 'أُخبِرُهُم' (I inform them). In Arabic, the pronoun object integrates seamlessly with the verb — no separate word needed. This creates very compact sentences. Compare English 'I informed them' (3 words) vs. Arabic 'أخبرتُهُم' (1 word containing: verb + subject marker + object). This compactness is characteristic of Arabic's agglutinative features.

items.question: هذه رسالةٌ _____.
items.explanation: 'لـ' (for) + 'ـكُم' (you, m. pl.) = 'لكُم' (for you all). The second person plural suffix 'ـكُم' addresses a group (masculine or mixed). For an exclusively female group: 'ـكُنَّ' → 'لكُنَّ.' Arabic has separate dual forms too: 'ـكُما' → 'لكُما' (for you two). This precision in addressing audiences is reflected throughout Arabic culture — letters, speeches, and even social media posts carefully choose the right pronoun form. The damma in 'ـكُم' distinguishes it from singular 'ـكَ' (fatha) and 'ـكِ' (kasra).

items.question: قابلتُ صديقَ _____ في السوقِ.
items.translation: (I met her friend in the market.)
items.explanation: Here 'ـها' attaches to a NOUN in an إضافة-like construction: 'صديقَها' (her friend). The noun 'صديق' takes accusative (fatha) as the object of 'قابلتُ' (I met). The suffix 'ـها' makes the noun definite (like ال does). Combined with إضافة rules: 'صديقَ أختي' (my sister's friend) and 'صديقَها' (her friend) are parallel structures — both make 'صديق' definite through connection. The suffix never changes for case — only the noun it's attached to changes: 'صديقُها' (nom.), 'صديقَها' (acc.), 'صديقِها' (gen.).

items.question: أستاذُ _____ عادلٌ.
items.explanation: Arabic has DUAL pronouns — a feature shared by very few languages. 'ـكُما' (your, dual — addressing exactly two people) is used for both masculine and feminine pairs. 'أستاذُكُما' = 'your (two's) professor.' The complete dual pronoun set: 'أنتُما' (you two), 'ـكُما' (your, two), 'هُما' (they two), 'ـهُما' (their, two). These dual forms are fully productive in Standard Arabic. 'أستاذُكُما عادلٌ' treats the dual as requiring singular verb/adjective agreement when the possessed noun (أستاذ) is singular. Dual pronouns reflect Arabic's mathematical precision.

items.question: عندَ _____ سؤالٌ.
items.explanation: 'عند' (at/with) + 'ـي' (me) = 'عندي' (I have = at me). Arabic expresses possession using prepositions + pronouns: 'عندي' (I have), 'عندكَ' (you have), 'عندهُ' (he has), 'لدَيَّ' (I have, more formal). There is NO verb 'to have' in Arabic — this prepositional construction is the standard. The word 'عند' is a ظرف مكان (adverb of place) that takes the genitive. 'عندي سؤالٌ' is a nominal sentence: 'عندي' (predicate, fronted) + 'سؤالٌ' (subject, delayed, indefinite). This structure is one of the very first Arabic learners must master.

items.question: إنَّ _____ مسؤولون عن النتائجِ.
items.translation: (Indeed they are responsible for the results.)
items.explanation: 'إنّ' + attached pronoun: 'إنَّهُم' (indeed they). The pronoun becomes the subject of إنّ (اسم إنّ) and is in the accusative by position. When إنّ takes a pronoun subject instead of a noun, the pronoun attaches directly: 'إنّني' (indeed I), 'إنّكَ' (indeed you), 'إنّها' (indeed she/it). The predicate 'مسؤولون' (responsible, m. pl.) is nominative. This 'إنّ + pronoun' construction is one of the most common sentence starters in Arabic: 'إنّهُ مِنَ الضروريِّ' (it is necessary), 'إنّها فكرةٌ جيدةٌ' (it is a good idea).

items.question: اتصلَ _____ صديقٌ قديمٌ.
items.explanation: The verb 'اتصلَ بـ' (to call/contact) requires the preposition 'بـ' (indicating the person contacted). 'بـ' + 'ـي' (me) = 'بي.' Note: after kasra, 'ـهُ' becomes 'ـهِ': 'بِهِ' (with him). But 'ـي' doesn't change: 'بي' (with me). The pronoun 'ي' after prepositions doesn't take نون الوقاية (that's only for verbs). Common verb + preposition + pronoun phrases: 'فكّرَ فيهِ' (thought about him), 'بحثَ عنها' (searched for her), 'تحدّثَ معنا' (spoke with us). These constructions are the building blocks of Arabic communication.

name: Demonstrative Pronouns (أسماء الإشارة)
description: Use هذا، هذه، هؤلاء and other demonstratives for near and far reference

items.question: _____ كتابٌ مفيدٌ.
items.explanation: 'هذا' is the masculine singular near demonstrative (this). It's used with masculine singular nouns. In 'هذا كتابٌ مفيدٌ,' 'هذا' is the subject (مبتدأ) and 'كتابٌ مفيدٌ' is the predicate (خبر). When a demonstrative stands alone as subject with an indefinite predicate, it forms a predicative sentence: 'this is a book.' Arabic demonstratives are fully gendered and numbered: هذا (this, m.s.), هذه (this, f.s.), هذانِ (these two, m.), هاتانِ (these two, f.), هؤلاء (these, pl.). This precision exceeds English.

items.question: _____ الفتاةُ ذكيةٌ.
items.explanation: 'هذه' is the feminine singular near demonstrative (this, f.). 'الفتاةُ' (the girl) is feminine. When a demonstrative PRECEDES a definite noun, it creates an attributive construction: 'هذه الفتاةُ' = 'this girl' (not 'this is the girl'). The sentence 'هذه الفتاةُ ذكيةٌ' = 'this girl is smart.' Compare: 'هذه فتاةٌ ذكيةٌ' = 'this is a smart girl.' The presence or absence of 'ال' on the following noun changes the entire meaning. This distinction between predicative and attributive demonstratives is crucial for clear communication.

items.question: _____ هم أصدقائي.
items.explanation: 'هؤلاء' is the near demonstrative for PLURALS — used for all human plurals regardless of gender. Unlike singular demonstratives (هذا/هذه), the plural form doesn't distinguish masculine from feminine. 'هؤلاء' applies to a group of men, women, or mixed. The predicate 'أصدقائي' (my friends) uses the إضافة: أصدقاء + ي. For NON-HUMAN plurals, Arabic uses the feminine singular demonstrative 'هذه': 'هذه الكتبُ' (these books), because non-human plurals are grammatically treated as feminine singular. This human/non-human split affects demonstrative choice.

items.question: _____ الرجلُ طبيبٌ.
items.explanation: 'ذلك' is the masculine singular FAR demonstrative (that). Arabic has a complete near/far system: near (هذا/هذه) vs. far (ذلك/تلك). 'ذلك الرجلُ' = 'that man.' The word 'ذلك' is composed of 'ذا' (that, masculine) + 'لـ' (distance marker) + 'كَ' (addressee particle). The internal structure reveals its meaning: pointing to something distant. 'ذلك' is also used idiomatically to mean 'that is' or as a discourse connector: 'ذلكَ لأنَّ...' (that is because...). It appears frequently in the Quran and classical literature in formulaic expressions.

items.question: _____ السيارةُ غاليةٌ.
items.explanation: 'تلك' is the feminine singular far demonstrative (that, f.). 'السيارةُ' is feminine → 'تلك السيارةُ' (that car). 'تلك' is composed of elements similar to 'ذلك' but with the feminine base 'تِ.' The predicate 'غاليةٌ' is feminine + indefinite (tanwin), as expected for the predicate of a definite subject. The far demonstratives are used both for physical distance ('that car over there') and figurative/referential distance ('that idea we discussed'). In writing, 'ذلك/تلك' often refer to previously mentioned items, functioning as anaphoric pronouns.

items.question: _____ أبناءُ الجيرانِ.
items.explanation: 'أولئك' is the far demonstrative plural (those) for human referents. Like 'هؤلاء' (these), it's gender-neutral for human plurals. 'أولئك أبناءُ الجيرانِ' = 'those are the neighbors' sons.' The construction 'أبناءُ الجيرانِ' is an إضافة: 'sons of the neighbors.' For non-human plurals at a distance, Arabic uses 'تلك' (feminine singular far): 'تلك الكتبُ' (those books). This mirrors the near system: هؤلاء for human plurals, هذه for non-human plurals. Memorizing this human/non-human split in demonstratives is essential.

items.question: قرأتُ _____ الكتابَ في يومين.
items.translation: (I read this book in two days.)
items.explanation: In this sentence, 'هذا' functions as an ADJECTIVE modifying 'الكتابَ.' The structure is: verb + demonstrative + definite noun. 'هذا الكتابَ' = 'this book' (accusative, object of قرأتُ). The demonstrative is invariable for case — 'هذا' always looks the same whether nominative, accusative, or genitive. Only the noun it modifies shows case changes: 'هذا الكتابُ' (nom.), 'هذا الكتابَ' (acc.), 'هذا الكتابِ' (gen.). This case-invariability of demonstratives simplifies their usage significantly compared to relative pronouns.

items.question: ما _____؟
items.explanation: 'ما هذه؟' = 'what is this?' (referring to something feminine). The interrogative 'ما' (what) combines naturally with demonstratives for basic questions. If you know the object is masculine: 'ما هذا؟' If you don't know the gender yet, 'ما هذا' is the default. In spoken Arabic, 'ما هذا/هذه' is one of the first phrases learned. Note: 'ما' for 'what' is different from 'ما' for negation (ما + past verb = didn't). Context always clarifies. The question intonation in Arabic rises at the end, similar to English, or uses question particles like 'هل' for yes/no questions.

items.question: _____ الطالبانِ متفوقانِ.
items.explanation: Arabic has DUAL demonstratives — another feature unique to Arabic. 'هذانِ' (these two, masculine) is the dual near demonstrative. For feminine dual: 'هاتانِ.' The noun 'الطالبانِ' is masculine dual nominative (ـانِ). The predicate 'متفوقانِ' (excellent, dual) also takes dual marking. In accusative/genitive case, the duals change: 'هذَيْنِ/هاتَيْنِ.' Example: 'رأيتُ هذَيْنِ الطالبَيْنِ' (I saw these two students). Dual demonstratives are mandatory in formal Arabic — using هذا or هؤلاء for exactly two items is grammatically incorrect.

items.question: _____ هي الحقيقةُ.
items.explanation: 'هذه هي الحقيقةُ' = 'this is the truth.' Here 'هي' functions as a copular pronoun (ضمير الفصل) that separates the subject from a definite predicate. Without 'هي': 'هذه الحقيقةُ' would mean 'this truth' (attributive). With 'هي': 'هذه هي الحقيقةُ' means 'this IS the truth' (predicative). The ضمير الفصل (separating pronoun) is a brilliant Arabic syntactic device that resolves potential ambiguity between attributive and predicative constructions. It always agrees with the subject in gender and number.

items.question: _____ الأيامُ لن تعودَ.
items.explanation: 'تلك' (feminine singular far) is used here because 'الأيامُ' (the days) is a non-human broken plural — which Arabic grammar treats as FEMININE SINGULAR. So: 'تلك الأيامُ' (those days), not 'أولئك الأيامُ.' This rule that non-human plurals take feminine singular agreement is UNIVERSAL in Arabic — it affects demonstratives, adjectives, verbs, and relative pronouns. 'لن تعودَ' uses feminine singular verb 'تعودَ' (it/she will return) with 'لن' (will not) + subjunctive. The nostalgic phrase 'تلك الأيام' is commonly used in Arabic poetry and song.

items.question: قابلتُ _____ الطالبتَيْنِ أمسِ.
items.translation: (I met those two female students yesterday.)
items.explanation: 'هاتَيْنِ' is the feminine dual demonstrative in the ACCUSATIVE/GENITIVE case. Since 'الطالبتَيْنِ' is the object of 'قابلتُ' (accusative), the dual demonstrative must also be in the accusative form: 'هاتَيْنِ' (not nominative 'هاتانِ'). This case change mirrors regular dual nouns: ـانِ → ـيْنِ. Compare masculine: 'هذانِ' (nom.) → 'هذَيْنِ' (acc./gen.). Dual demonstratives are the ONLY demonstratives that change for case — all others (هذا، هذه، هؤلاء، ذلك، تلك، أولئك) are indeclinable. This makes dual demonstratives uniquely complex.

items.question: في _____ اللحظةِ فهمتُ كلَّ شيءٍ.
items.explanation: 'تلك اللحظةِ' (that moment) — 'لحظة' is feminine → 'تلك' (far feminine). After 'في,' both take genitive: 'في تلكَ اللحظةِ.' Demonstratives used with temporal nouns create time references: 'في ذلك الوقتِ' (at that time), 'في هذا اليومِ' (on this day), 'منذُ تلك اللحظةِ' (since that moment). The far demonstrative often implies past time reference — 'that moment' suggests looking back. Arabic uses demonstratives + time nouns extensively for narrative time markers, making them essential for storytelling and historical writing.

items.question: _____ ما أردتُ قولَهُ.
items.translation: (That is what I wanted to say.)
items.explanation: 'ذلك' here refers to abstract content — what was previously said or discussed. When referring to a general idea, situation, or discourse content, Arabic typically uses 'ذلك' (masculine far) as the default abstract reference. 'ما أردتُ قولَهُ' = 'what I wanted to say it' (with resumptive pronoun 'هُ' in 'قولَهُ'). Or 'ما' as relative pronoun: 'what I wanted saying-of-it.' 'ذلك ما...' (that is what...) is a very common Arabic discourse formula used in discussions, conclusions, and summaries. It functions as a powerful cohesive device in text.

items.question: أحبُّ _____ المكانَ كثيراً.
items.explanation: 'أحبُّ هذا المكانَ كثيراً' = 'I love this place a lot.' 'المكانَ' (the place, masculine) is accusative (object of أحبّ), and 'هذا' precedes it as a demonstrative adjective. 'كثيراً' (a lot) is an adverb in the accusative (حال or مفعول مطلق). The verb 'أحبُّ' (I love) has a doubled final letter (from root ح-ب-ب). When demonstratives precede their nouns in the accusative position, the entire phrase functions as the object. The demonstrative itself doesn't show case marking. 'هذا' remains invariable while 'المكانَ' carries the accusative fatha.

name: Kana and Its Sisters (كان وأخواتها)
description: Master the verbs that modify nominal sentences by changing the predicate to accusative

items.question: كانَ الولدُ _____ في المدرسةِ.
items.explanation: 'كانَ' enters upon a nominal sentence and puts its predicate in the ACCUSATIVE: 'مُجتهداً' (fatha + tanwin). Without كان: 'الولدُ مُجتهدٌ' (nominative predicate). With كان: 'كانَ الولدُ مُجتهداً' (accusative predicate). The subject 'الولدُ' stays nominative (اسم كان مرفوع). This is كان's fundamental operation — it 'enters upon' (تدخل على) the nominal sentence. كان also inflects for tense, person, and number: 'كنتُ' (I was), 'كانوا' (they were), 'يكونُ' (he is/will be). It's the most versatile and common of its sisters.

items.question: أصبحَ الطقسُ _____ في الشتاءِ.
items.explanation: 'أصبحَ' (became, in the morning / came to be) is one of كان's sisters. Like كان, it takes a nominative subject and accusative predicate: 'أصبحَ الطقسُ بارداً.' Originally, 'أصبحَ' meant 'to enter the morning' (from صُبح = morning), but it evolved to mean 'became' in general. Other time-based sisters with similar evolution: 'أمسى' (entered evening → became), 'أضحى' (entered forenoon → became), 'باتَ' (spent the night → became overnight). Each originally referenced a specific time of day but now primarily expresses change of state.

items.question: ليسَ الامتحانُ _____.
items.explanation: 'ليسَ' (is not) is the NEGATION sister of كان. It only exists in one form — no past or future tense variations. 'ليسَ الامتحانُ سهلاً' = 'the exam is not easy.' Subject 'الامتحانُ' is nominative, predicate 'سهلاً' is accusative. 'ليس' conjugates for person: 'لستُ' (I am not), 'لستَ' (you are not), 'ليسوا' (they are not), 'ليستْ' (she/it is not). It's the primary way to negate nominal sentences in formal Arabic. In dialects, 'مش/مو/مب' often replace ليس, but in MSA, ليس remains standard. Its lack of a past/future tense makes it grammatically unique.

items.question: ما زالَ المطرُ _____.
items.explanation: 'ما زالَ' (still is / continues to be) is a كان sister that requires the negative particle 'ما' to convey its meaning. Without 'ما': 'زالَ' means 'ceased/disappeared.' WITH 'ما': 'ما زالَ' means 'has not ceased = still is.' This is a beautiful example of how negation creates positive meaning. 'ما زالَ المطرُ مُنهمِراً' = 'the rain has not ceased being heavy = the rain is still heavy.' Similar constructions: 'ما برِحَ' (still), 'ما فتِئَ' (still), 'ما انفكَّ' (still). All need 'ما' to function as continuous-state verbs.

items.question: صارَ الطالبُ _____.
items.explanation: 'صارَ' (became/turned into) emphasizes TRANSFORMATION from one state to another. It's one of كان's sisters with an accusative predicate: 'طبيباً.' Unlike 'أصبحَ' which can mean 'became' generally, 'صارَ' specifically implies a deliberate or significant change. 'صارَ الطالبُ طبيباً' = the student TRANSFORMED into a doctor. 'صارَ' conjugates fully: 'صرتُ' (I became), 'يصيرُ' (he becomes), 'صاروا' (they became). In modern Arabic, 'صار' is also extremely common in dialects (particularly Levantine and Iraqi) where it serves as 'became' and even 'happened.'

items.question: ظلَّ الرجلُ _____ طوالَ اليومِ.
items.translation: (The man remained silent all day long.)
items.explanation: 'ظلَّ' (remained/continued) is a كان sister expressing CONTINUATION of a state. 'ظلَّ الرجلُ صامتاً' = 'the man remained silent.' The predicate 'صامتاً' is accusative (خبر ظلّ منصوب). 'طوالَ اليومِ' = 'all day long' — 'طوال' is a ظرف زمان (accusative) in إضافة with 'اليوم.' 'ظلَّ' originally meant 'to be in shade/shadow' (from ظِلّ = shade), evolving to mean 'to remain.' It conjugates: 'ظللتُ' (I remained), 'يظلُّ' (he remains). Doubled-letter verbs like ظلّ follow special conjugation rules when adding suffixes.

items.question: باتَ الطفلُ _____ طوالَ الليلِ.
items.explanation: 'باتَ' (spent the night being / became overnight) originally meant 'to spend the night.' As a كان sister, it takes accusative predicate: 'مُستيقظاً' (awake). 'باتَ الطفلُ مُستيقظاً' = 'the child spent the night awake.' 'مُستيقظ' is the active participle of Form X 'استيقظَ' (to wake up). 'طوالَ الليلِ' = 'throughout the night.' 'باتَ' adds a temporal dimension — the state existed during nighttime. In modern journalistic Arabic, 'بات' has evolved to simply mean 'became': 'باتَ واضحاً أنّ...' (it became clear that...) — losing its nighttime connotation.

items.question: لن _____ الأمرُ صعباً.
items.explanation: 'لن' (will not) + present tense of كان = future negation with subjunctive mood. 'يكونَ' takes fatha (subjunctive marker): 'لن يكونَ الأمرُ صعباً.' This shows كان in the present/future: 'يكونُ' (he is/will be). With negation particles: 'لن يكونَ' (will not be, subjunctive), 'لم يكنْ' (was not, jussive — note the shortened form dropping the و), 'لا يكونُ' (is not being, indicative). كان is the most completely conjugated verb in Arabic — it appears in all tenses, moods, and with all particles. Mastering كان's various forms is essential for grammatical fluency.

items.question: أمسَتِ السماءُ _____.
items.explanation: 'أمسى' (became in the evening / entered evening) is a كان sister. 'أمسَتِ' has the feminine marker 'ت' for 'السماءُ' (the sky, f.). Predicate 'غائمةً' is accusative feminine: fatha + tanwin + taa marbuta. Like 'أصبحَ' and 'باتَ,' 'أمسى' originally had a specific time reference (evening/مساء) but now generally means 'became.' These time-based كان sisters create an elegant temporal system: أصبحَ (morning), أضحى (forenoon), ظلَّ (continued/shade time), أمسى (evening), باتَ (night). Each can describe state changes at its respective time.

items.question: ما فتِئَ العالِمُ _____ في أبحاثِهِ.
items.translation: (The scientist continues to work on his research.)
items.explanation: 'ما فتِئَ' (continues / doesn't stop) is a كان sister that requires 'ما' (like ما زالَ). Its predicate can be a nominal (منصوب) OR a verbal sentence. Here 'يعملُ' (he works) is a present tense verb functioning as the predicate. When the predicate of كان-sisters is a verbal sentence, no accusative marker appears — the verb simply follows. 'ما فتِئَ' is more literary than 'ما زالَ' but carries the same meaning. 'أبحاثِهِ' (his research, plural of بحث) uses the possessive suffix هِ. Verbal predicates after كان-sisters show the action in progress.

items.question: كانتِ الحديقةُ _____ بالأزهارِ.
items.explanation: 'كانتِ' is the feminine form of كان (with 'ت' for 'الحديقةُ'). The predicate 'مليئةً' is feminine accusative (matching the feminine subject). 'بالأزهارِ' = 'with flowers' (بـ + الأزهار, genitive). The adjective 'مليء/مليئة' (full) takes the preposition 'بـ' for what it's full of. Notice the complete feminine agreement chain: كانتِ (feminine verb) + الحديقةُ (feminine noun) + مليئةً (feminine adjective). This three-way feminine agreement is typical of Arabic sentences with كان-sisters — every element must harmonize in gender.

items.question: سيكونُ الغدُ _____ إن شاءَ اللهُ.
items.explanation: 'سيكونُ' (will be) is كان in the future: 'سـ' + present 'يكونُ.' The predicate 'أفضلَ' (better) is in the accusative. But why fatha without tanwin? Because 'أفضل' is a diptote (ممنوع من الصرف) — it follows the أفعل comparative pattern. Diptotes take fatha for BOTH accusative and genitive, and NEVER tanwin. So: nominative 'أفضلُ,' accusative 'أفضلَ,' genitive 'أفضلَ.' 'إن شاءَ اللهُ' (God willing) is the most common Arabic expression of hope/intention. 'شاءَ' = 'willed' and 'اللهُ' is the subject (nominative).

items.question: لم يكنِ _____ حاضراً في الاجتماعِ.
items.translation: (The manager was not present at the meeting.)
items.explanation: 'لم يكنْ' is the NEGATED PAST of كان: 'لم' + jussive. The full form 'يكونُ' becomes 'يكنْ' in jussive (the و drops). The subject 'المديرُ' is NOMINATIVE (اسم كان مرفوع) — negation doesn't change this. The predicate 'حاضراً' is accusative. 'لم يكنِ المديرُ حاضراً' = 'the manager was not present.' The kasra on 'يكنِ' before 'المديرُ' is a phonological linking vowel (التقاء الساكنين — avoiding two consecutive sukuns). This 'لم يكن' construction is the standard past negation for كان throughout Arabic.

items.question: كونوا _____ في إجاباتِكم.
items.explanation: 'كونوا' is the IMPERATIVE of كان for masculine plural: 'be!' The predicate takes the accusative: 'دقيقينَ' (precise, m. pl. accusative). Sound masculine plurals show accusative with 'ـينَ' (vs. nominative 'ـون'). 'كونوا دقيقينَ' = 'be precise!' (commanding a group). Imperative forms: 'كُن' (be, m.s.), 'كوني' (be, f.s.), 'كونوا' (be, m.pl.), 'كُنَّ' (be, f.pl.). The imperative of كان is used for commands, advice, and exhortations: 'كُن صادقاً' (be honest), 'كونوا مستعدّين' (be prepared). It's a powerful rhetorical tool in Arabic speeches and writing.

items.question: ما دامَ الجوُّ _____ سنذهبُ إلى الشاطئِ.
items.translation: (As long as the weather is nice, we will go to the beach.)
items.explanation: 'ما دامَ' (as long as) is a special كان sister expressing DURATION of a condition. Like other sisters, its predicate is accusative: 'جميلاً.' 'ما دامَ الجوُّ جميلاً' = 'as long as the weather is beautiful.' The 'ما' here is a المصدرية الظرفية (masdariyya expressing time), meaning 'during the duration of.' 'ما دامَ' is unique among كان-sisters because it exclusively expresses temporal conditioning — 'for as long as X is Y.' The response clause 'سنذهبُ' (we will go) states what will happen during that condition. This construction is elegant for expressing conditional continuity.

name: Inna and Its Sisters (إنّ وأخواتها)
description: Particles that change nominal sentence case endings

items.question: _____ الجوَّ جميلٌ اليومَ.
items.explanation: 'إنَّ' is the emphatic particle meaning 'indeed/verily' that starts a nominal sentence. It puts its subject (اسم إنَّ) in the accusative case: 'الجوَّ' (accusative). The predicate stays nominative: 'جميلٌ'. إنَّ is used at the beginning of sentences for emphasis. Its sisters include أنَّ (that), كأنَّ (as if), لكنَّ (but), ليتَ (I wish), لعلَّ (perhaps). Each changes the case of the subject to accusative while keeping the predicate nominative.

items.question: علمتُ _____ الامتحانَ صعبٌ.
items.translation: (I learned that the exam is difficult.)
items.explanation: 'أنَّ' means 'that' and is used after verbs of knowing, saying, or believing. It functions like إنَّ by putting its subject in accusative: 'الامتحانَ'. The difference: إنَّ starts independent sentences, while أنَّ comes after verbs or prepositions. 'علمتُ أنَّ الامتحانَ صعبٌ' — I learned that the exam is hard. أنَّ introduces subordinate noun clauses and is extremely common in Arabic written and spoken discourse.

items.question: الكتابُ مفيدٌ _____ ثمنَهُ غالٍ.
items.translation: (The book is useful but its price is expensive.)
items.explanation: 'لكنَّ' means 'but/however' and is one of إنَّ's sisters. Like all sisters, it puts its subject in the accusative: 'ثمنَهُ'. It introduces a contrasting idea. Note: 'لكنَّ' (with shadda) governs case, while 'لكنْ' (without shadda, with sukun) is a coordinating conjunction that doesn't affect case. The former is a حرف ناسخ (abrogating particle), the latter is a حرف عطف (conjunction).

items.question: _____ السماءَ صافيةٌ.
items.explanation: 'كأنَّ' means 'as if / it seems that' and is used for similes and comparisons. It puts its subject in the accusative: 'السماءَ'. The predicate remains nominative: 'صافيةٌ'. كأنَّ is widely used in literary Arabic for vivid comparisons: 'كأنَّ وجهَها القمرُ' (as if her face were the moon). It creates rhetorical imagery while following the same grammatical rules as its sisters — accusative subject, nominative predicate.

items.question: _____ الشبابَ يعودُ.
items.explanation: 'ليتَ' expresses a strong, often impossible wish — 'I wish / if only'. Like its sisters, it puts the subject in the accusative: 'الشبابَ'. ليتَ is unique among إنَّ's sisters because it typically expresses wishes that cannot come true: 'ليتَ الزمانَ يرجعُ' (if only time would return). For possible wishes, Arabic uses 'لعلَّ' (perhaps/hopefully). This distinction between ليتَ (impossible) and لعلَّ (possible) is essential.

items.question: _____ الطلابَ ينجحون.
items.explanation: 'لعلَّ' means 'perhaps/hopefully/maybe' and expresses hope or expectation for something possible. It puts its subject in the accusative: 'الطلابَ'. Unlike ليتَ (which is for impossible wishes), لعلَّ is for realistic hopes: 'لعلَّ المطرَ ينزلُ' (perhaps rain will come). It's commonly used in the Quran: 'لعلَّكم تتَّقون' (so that you may become righteous). لعلَّ adds nuance of expectation or anticipation to Arabic expression.

items.question: إنَّ _____ مُخلصاتٌ في العملِ.
items.translation: (Indeed, the teachers are sincere in work.)
items.explanation: The subject of إنَّ (اسم إنَّ) goes in the accusative case. For sound feminine plurals ending in ـات, the accusative is marked with kasra, not fatha: 'المُعلِّماتِ' (not المُعلِّماتَ). This is an exception to the general accusative marker. Sound feminine plurals use kasra for both genitive AND accusative cases. Only the nominative is marked differently with damma: 'المُعلِّماتُ'. This pattern applies to all sound feminine plurals like طالباتٍ, مُهندساتٍ, etc.

items.question: إنَّ المُعلِّمينَ _____.
items.explanation: The predicate of إنَّ (خبر إنَّ) stays in the NOMINATIVE case, even though the subject is accusative. For sound masculine plurals, the nominative is marked by ـونَ: 'حاضرونَ'. Don't be tricked — 'حاضرينَ' is accusative/genitive, which would be wrong for the predicate here. The rule is clear: إنَّ changes only the subject's case (to accusative). The predicate keeps its normal nominative case. This mirrors كانَ's behavior but in reverse: كان changes the predicate, إنَّ changes the subject.

items.question: إنَّ هذا الكتابَ _____ جداً.
items.explanation: After إنَّ, the predicate (خبر إنَّ) remains nominative: 'مفيدٌ' with tanwin damma. Note: 'هذا' is a demonstrative pronoun (مبتدأ) followed by 'الكتابَ' as the subject of إنَّ in the accusative. The sentence structure is: إنَّ + اسم إنَّ (accusative) + خبر إنَّ (nominative). Always keep the predicate nominative with all of إنَّ's sisters. This is one of the most frequently tested Arabic grammar points in exams.

items.question: _____ في الاجتهادِ نجاحاً.
items.explanation: When إنَّ's predicate comes before its subject (تقديم الخبر على الاسم), this is called fronted predicate construction. Here 'في الاجتهادِ' (in diligence) is the fronted predicate (a prepositional phrase), and 'نجاحاً' is the delayed subject in the accusative. This word order is common when the predicate is a prepositional phrase or adverb. Fronting the predicate creates emphasis on the location or circumstance rather than the subject itself.

items.question: قالَ المعلِّمُ إنَّ _____ سهلٌ.
items.translation: (The teacher said that the lesson is easy.)
items.explanation: After verbs of saying (قالَ), إنَّ introduces reported speech and puts its subject in the accusative: 'الدرسَ' (with fatha on the end). Note that إنَّ keeps its فتحة on the hamza when it starts reported speech after قالَ. The subject 'الدرسَ' must be accusative (اسم إنَّ منصوب). This construction is extremely common in Arabic: 'قالَ/أخبرَ/ذكرَ إنَّ...' followed by an accusative subject and nominative predicate.

items.question: لكنَّ الحقيقةَ _____ عن ذلكَ.
items.translation: (But the truth is different from that.)
items.explanation: The predicate of لكنَّ stays nominative, just like all of إنَّ's sisters. 'مختلفةٌ' carries tanwin damma (nominative). The subject 'الحقيقةَ' is in the accusative (fatha). Remember: ALL of إنَّ's sisters (إنَّ, أنَّ, كأنَّ, لكنَّ, ليتَ, لعلَّ) follow the same pattern — accusative subject, nominative predicate. لكنَّ is particularly useful because it introduces contrast and concession in Arabic discourse.

items.question: كأنَّ الأطفالَ _____ صغيرةٌ.
items.translation: (As if the children are small birds.)
items.explanation: This is a complex sentence with كأنَّ. 'الأطفالَ' is the accusative subject of كأنَّ. The predicate here is a nominal predicate consisting of 'عصافيرُ صغيرةٌ' (small birds), which is nominative. 'عصافيرُ' is diptote (ممنوع من الصرف) because it is a broken plural of the pattern فَعاليل. Diptotes take damma in the nominative but never take tanwin. كأنَّ is the primary tool for metaphorical and simile constructions in Arabic.

items.question: إنَّ _____ واسعةٌ.
items.explanation: The definite noun 'الحديقةَ' is the subject of إنَّ, so it takes the accusative case marked by fatha. Being definite (with الـ), it takes a visible fatha rather than tanwin. The predicate 'واسعةٌ' is nominative with tanwin damma. This is the most basic and common إنَّ sentence pattern: إنَّ + definite noun (accusative) + adjective (nominative). Mastering this pattern is essential for understanding how إنَّ and its sisters reshape nominal sentences.

items.question: ليتَ _____ أطولُ.
items.explanation: ليتَ puts its subject in the accusative: 'النهارَ' (with fatha). The predicate 'أطولُ' stays nominative but doesn't take tanwin because it's a comparative/superlative form (اسم تفضيل — diptote, ممنوع من الصرف). ليتَ expresses longing for something unattainable — wishing the day were longer is a poetic, often impossible desire. Arabic poetry and literature frequently use ليتَ for nostalgic or wistful expressions: 'ليتَ الشبابَ يعودُ يوماً'.

name: Hal Clause (الحال)
description: Circumstantial expressions describing the state of the subject

items.question: جاءَ الطالبُ _____.
items.explanation: The حال (circumstantial accusative) describes the state or condition of the subject or object when the action occurs. It is ALWAYS in the accusative case: 'راكضاً' (running). The حال answers the question 'كيف؟' (how?). Here, 'راكضاً' tells us HOW the student came. The حال must be indefinite (نكرة) and its owner (صاحب الحال) must be definite (معرفة). This is a fundamental Arabic grammar rule: definite owner + indefinite حال.

items.question: شربتُ الشايَ _____.
items.explanation: Here the حال 'ساخناً' describes the state of the OBJECT 'الشايَ' (the tea), not the subject. The tea was hot when I drank it. The حال is always accusative and indefinite. Note the difference: 'الشايَ الساخنَ' (the hot tea — adjective, definite) vs. 'الشايَ ساخناً' (the tea while hot — حال, indefinite). Adjectives (نعت) match their noun in definiteness; حال is always indefinite. This distinction is critical for precise Arabic expression.

items.question: رأيتُ الأطفالَ _____ في الحديقةِ.
items.translation: (I saw the children playing in the park.)
items.explanation: The حال can be a verbal sentence (جملة فعلية) — here the present tense verb 'يلعبونَ' (they are playing) serves as حال. When a sentence functions as حال, it's called جملة حالية. For verbal sentence حال, the verb is usually in the present tense (مضارع) indicating simultaneous action. The verbal form 'يلعبونَ' is more natural than the participle 'لاعبينَ' in this context, though both are grammatically possible in Arabic.

items.question: خرجَ من البيتِ _____ حقيبتَهُ.
items.translation: (He left the house carrying his bag.)
items.explanation: The active participle (اسم الفاعل) 'حاملاً' functions as حال in the accusative case, describing the subject's state while leaving. Active participles are very common as حال because they naturally describe ongoing states. 'حاملاً حقيبتَهُ' — carrying his bag. The participle 'حاملاً' is transitive here, taking 'حقيبتَهُ' as its object. Arabic active participles can govern objects just like their corresponding verbs. This makes them versatile grammatical tools.

items.question: دخلَ المديرُ _____ يبتسمُ.
items.translation: (The director entered while he was smiling.)
items.explanation: The جملة حالية (circumstantial clause) is introduced by 'و' (واو الحال) + pronoun + verb. 'وهو يبتسمُ' means 'while he is smiling.' The واو الحال is one of the most distinctive features of Arabic syntax — it introduces a clause describing the circumstances. The pronoun 'هو' refers back to the subject 'المديرُ'. The verb after the pronoun is usually مضارع (present tense) indicating a simultaneous action. This construction is ubiquitous in Arabic narrative.

items.question: سافرَ _____ عن وطنِهِ.
items.translation: (He traveled content away from his homeland.)
items.explanation: 'راضياً' (content/satisfied) is a حال in the accusative, describing the subject's emotional state during travel. Note: 'راضٍ' is the nominative/genitive form (with tanwin kasra on a defective noun ending in ي). The accusative form restores the ي: 'راضياً'. Defective nouns (الاسم المنقوص) behave specially: they lose their final ي in the nominative and genitive when indefinite, but keep it in the accusative. This is a key grammatical detail for accuracy.

items.question: لا تأكلْ _____.
items.explanation: Even in negative imperative sentences, the حال remains accusative: 'واقفاً' (standing). The حال here describes the state of the implied subject (أنتَ — you) during the prohibited action. The prohibition 'لا تأكلْ' uses لا الناهية + مضارع مجزوم. Adding the حال specifies under what condition the action is forbidden — it's not eating itself that's forbidden, but eating WHILE standing. Context-dependent prohibitions like this are common in Arabic.

items.question: جلسَ الضيوفُ _____ الطعامَ.
items.explanation: When the حال refers to a plural subject, it must agree in number and gender. 'الضيوفُ' (guests) is a broken plural meaning a group of males. The حال is 'منتظرينَ' — sound masculine plural in the ACCUSATIVE/GENITIVE (both use ـينَ). The nominative would be 'منتظرونَ' (ـونَ), which is wrong for حال. Agreement rules: the حال agrees with صاحب الحال in gender and number, while always staying accusative and indefinite.

items.question: عادَ الجنديُّ _____ من المعركةِ.
items.translation: (The soldier returned victorious from the battle.)
items.explanation: 'منتصراً' (victorious) is a حال in the accusative describing the soldier's state upon return. This single-word حال (حال مفردة) is the simplest form. Arabic distinguishes three types of حال: (1) مفردة — single word like 'منتصراً', (2) جملة فعلية — verbal sentence like 'يضحكُ', (3) جملة اسمية — nominal sentence like 'وعيناهُ تدمعان' (with his eyes tearing). All three describe the circumstance of the action.

items.question: استيقظتُ _____ تُشرقُ.
items.translation: (I woke up while the sun was rising.)
items.explanation: This is a nominal sentence functioning as حال (جملة اسمية حالية), introduced by واو الحال. 'والشمسُ تُشرقُ' — while the sun was rising. The nominal clause has: واو + subject (الشمسُ, nominative) + predicate (تُشرقُ). Nominal حال clauses always need واو الحال to connect them. Verbal حال clauses may or may not have واو. This distinction is important: 'جاءَ يركضُ' (came running — no واو needed) vs. 'جاءَ والشمسُ تغربُ' (came while the sun was setting — واو required).

items.question: قابلتُهُ _____ من السفرِ.
items.translation: (I met him returning from the trip.)
items.explanation: Here 'عائداً' (returning) is a حال describing the OBJECT (the attached pronoun ـهُ) not the subject. The حال can describe either the subject or the object. When it describes the object, the owner of the حال (صاحب الحال) is the object pronoun. 'قابلتُهُ عائداً' — I met him [while he was] returning. To say 'I met him while I was returning,' you'd restructure: 'قابلتُهُ وأنا عائدٌ'. Context and word order usually clarify who the حال describes.

items.question: ماذا تفعلُ _____ في الليلِ؟
items.translation: (What do you do alone at night?)
items.explanation: 'وحيداً' (alone) is a حال in the accusative, describing the subject's state (أنتَ — implied you). حال works in ALL sentence types: declarative, interrogative, negative, imperative. In questions, it specifies the circumstances being asked about. 'ماذا تفعلُ وحيداً؟' — what do you do while alone? The حال restricts the scope of the question. Without it, the question is general; with it, it's specific to the condition of being alone.

items.question: رجعَ الطلابُ _____ من الرحلةِ.
items.translation: (The students returned happy from the trip.)
items.explanation: 'سعداءَ' is the plural حال in the accusative. It's a broken plural (جمع تكسير) of 'سعيد' on the pattern فُعَلاء. Broken plurals of this pattern are diptote (ممنوع من الصرف), so they take fatha without tanwin in the accusative: 'سعداءَ' (not سعداءً). The nominative would be 'سعداءُ' (damma, no tanwin). Diptote status matters for حال because it affects the ending. Always check if a broken plural is diptote before adding case markers.

items.question: تكلَّمَ المحاضرُ _____ بثقةٍ.
items.explanation: Multiple حال expressions can occur in one sentence. Here 'واقفاً' is حال and 'بثقةٍ' (with confidence) is a prepositional phrase also functioning as حال. When two حال descriptions coexist, they can both describe the same صاحب الحال. Arabic allows stacking of circumstantial expressions: 'تكلَّمَ واقفاً مبتسماً بثقةٍ' — he spoke standing, smiling, with confidence. Each adds a layer of detail to the action, creating rich, nuanced descriptions.

items.question: لا تنَمْ _____ شيئاً.
items.explanation: 'غيرَ آكلٍ' is a negative حال meaning 'without eating/not having eaten.' The word 'غيرَ' is in the accusative (functioning as حال) and 'آكلٍ' is in the genitive (مضاف إليه). This is the idiomatic way to negate a حال using the إضافة structure. Alternatives include 'وأنتَ لم تأكلْ' (while you haven't eaten) as a clausal حال. The 'غير + genitive participle' construction is more concise and literary, frequently appearing in classical and modern standard Arabic.

name: Tamyiz / Specification (التمييز)
description: Nouns clarifying vague quantities or comparisons

items.question: اشتريتُ كيلوغراماً _____.
items.explanation: التمييز (specification/distinction) is a noun in the accusative that clarifies a vague quantity or measure. 'كيلوغراماً' is a vague amount — a kilogram of WHAT? 'تفاحاً' (apples) specifies it. This type is called تمييز ملفوظ (explicit/stated tamyiz) because it clarifies a mentioned quantity. It's always accusative and indefinite: 'تفاحاً' with tanwin fatha. Common measures triggering tamyiz include weight, volume, area, distance, and numerical quantities.

items.question: في الصفِّ عشرونَ _____.
items.translation: (In the class there are twenty students.)
items.explanation: Numbers 11-99 in Arabic take their counted noun (معدود) as تمييز in the accusative SINGULAR: 'عشرونَ طالباً' (twenty students). Despite meaning plural, the tamyiz after these numbers is grammatically singular. This is unique to Arabic: the number is plural in meaning but the tamyiz is singular in form. Numbers 3-10 take plural genitive, while 11-99 take singular accusative tamyiz. This counting system is one of Arabic's most distinctive grammar features.

items.question: شربتُ كوباً _____.
items.explanation: A container or measure word followed by its content uses tamyiz: 'كوباً ماءً' (a cup of water). 'ماءً' is تمييز منصوب clarifying what fills the cup. Note that 'ماء' takes the special alif-seat for tanwin fatha: 'ماءً'. Other examples: 'صحناً أرزاً' (a plate of rice), 'قارورةً حليباً' (a bottle of milk). This tamyiz pattern is essential for expressing measures and quantities in daily Arabic conversation about food, shopping, and recipes.

items.question: كم كتاباً _____ هذا الأسبوعَ؟
items.translation: (How many books did you read this week?)
items.explanation: After 'كم' الاستفهامية (interrogative how many), the tamyiz is accusative singular: 'كم كتاباً' (how many books). The verb 'قرأتَ' (you read, past tense, masculine) completes the question. 'كم' is one of the key triggers for tamyiz in Arabic. Note: 'كم' الخبرية (exclamatory) takes genitive: 'كم كتابٍ قرأتُ!' (so many books I've read!). The case of the tamyiz distinguishes interrogative from exclamatory كم — accusative vs. genitive respectively.

items.question: الحديقةُ أجملُ منظراً _____ الشارعِ.
items.translation: (The garden is more beautiful in scenery than the street.)
items.explanation: This example shows تمييز محوَّل (transferred tamyiz or tamyiz of ratio). 'منظراً' clarifies WHAT is more beautiful — the scenery, not everything about the garden. It specifies the aspect of comparison. 'من' introduces the compared element in comparative sentences (أفعل التفضيل + من). Transferred tamyiz is called محوَّل because it can be rephrased as the subject: 'منظرُ الحديقةِ أجملُ' (the garden's scenery is more beautiful). It adds precision to broad comparisons.

items.question: طابَ المكانُ _____.
items.explanation: 'هواءً' is تمييز محوَّل عن الفاعل (tamyiz transferred from the subject). The original meaning is: 'طابَ هواءُ المكانِ' (the climate of the place became pleasant). The tamyiz shifts the original subject to the accusative to specify WHAT about the place is pleasant. This type of tamyiz is common with verbs expressing qualities: 'كَبُرَ الولدُ عقلاً' (the boy is great in mind), 'حَسُنَ الطالبُ خُلقاً' (the student is good in character).

items.question: عندي خمسةَ عشرَ _____.
items.explanation: The compound number 'خمسةَ عشرَ' (fifteen) takes singular accusative tamyiz: 'قلماً' (a pen). ALL compound numbers (11-19) and decade numbers (20-90) in Arabic require تمييز العدد in the singular accusative. This contradicts English intuition where '15 pens' uses the plural. Arabic grammar treats these numbers as inherently expressing plurality, so the specified noun is singular. Compare with 3-10: 'خمسةُ أقلامٍ' (five pens — plural genitive). The system changes at 11.

items.question: زادَ الطالبُ _____ في هذا الفصلِ.
items.translation: (The student increased in knowledge this semester.)
items.explanation: 'عِلماً' is تمييز محوَّل عن الفاعل (tamyiz transferred from the subject). The base meaning: 'زادَ عِلمُ الطالبِ' (the student's knowledge increased). By using tamyiz, the student becomes the subject and 'عِلماً' specifies what increased about them. Verbs of increase, decrease, and change commonly take tamyiz: 'نقصَ المالُ قيمةً' (the money decreased in value), 'ازدادَ الأمرُ صعوبةً' (the matter increased in difficulty). Tamyiz adds precision to these verbs.

items.question: كفى بالله _____.
items.explanation: 'شهيداً' (witness) is tamyiz after the verb 'كفى' (to suffice). This Quranic expression shows how tamyiz specifies the ROLE or CAPACITY in which Allah suffices. 'كفى بالله شهيداً' is a famous verse pattern. The باء in 'بالله' is زائدة (extra/emphatic) — God is actually the subject. Other Quranic examples: 'كفى بالله حسيباً' (God suffices as a reckoner), 'كفى بالله وكيلاً' (God suffices as a guardian). This religious and literary usage makes tamyiz culturally significant.

items.question: هذه الأرضُ أكثرُ _____ من تلكَ.
items.translation: (This land is more fertile than that one.)
items.explanation: After the superlative/comparative form 'أكثرُ' (more), tamyiz specifies the aspect: 'خِصباً' (fertility). The pattern أفعَلُ تفضيل + تمييز is extremely common: 'أكثرُ عدداً' (more in number), 'أعلى قيمةً' (higher in value), 'أقلُّ تكلفةً' (less in cost). The tamyiz is always accusative singular indefinite. This pattern allows Arabic speakers to make nuanced comparative statements by specifying exactly what aspect is being compared.

items.question: في المكتبةِ ألفُ كتابٍ _____ مجلةً.
items.translation: (In the library there are a thousand books and fifty magazines.)
items.explanation: 'خمسونَ' is a decade number (from the عقود: عشرون، ثلاثون... تسعون) that takes singular accusative tamyiz: 'مجلةً'. Note 'ألفُ' (1000) takes singular GENITIVE: 'كتابٍ' — different from the decade numbers. Also, 'خمسونَ' here is nominative (subject of a new clause). The Arabic number system has different tamyiz rules at each tier: 3-10 (plural genitive), 11-99 (singular accusative), 100+ (singular genitive). This complexity is unique to Arabic.

items.question: امتلأتِ العينُ _____.
items.explanation: 'دموعاً' (tears) is tamyiz clarifying what filled the eye. The verb 'امتلأَ' (to fill up, become full) commonly takes tamyiz to specify the content. Original form: 'امتلأتْ دموعُ العينِ' → transferred to: 'امتلأتِ العينُ دموعاً'. This is تمييز محوَّل عن الفاعل. Other examples with filling verbs: 'فاضَ القلبُ سروراً' (the heart overflowed with joy), 'امتلأَ الكوبُ ماءً' (the cup filled with water). Tamyiz beautifully expresses what substances or emotions fill containers.

items.question: ازدادتِ المدينةُ _____ في السنواتِ الأخيرةِ.
items.translation: (The city increased in population in recent years.)
items.explanation: 'سكاناً' (population/inhabitants) is tamyiz specifying what increased about the city. 'ازدادَ' (to increase) is a Form VIII verb that commonly triggers tamyiz. The pattern 'ازدادَ + subject + tamyiz' is extremely frequent in modern Arabic, especially in news and academic writing: 'ازدادَ الإنتاجُ كمّيةً' (production increased in quantity), 'ازدادتِ المشكلةُ تعقيداً' (the problem increased in complexity). Tamyiz is essential for precise analytical language.

items.question: أحدَ عشرَ _____ حضروا الاجتماعَ.
items.explanation: The compound number 'أحدَ عشرَ' (eleven) takes singular accusative tamyiz: 'رجلاً'. Numbers 11-12 are unique: both parts agree with the counted noun in gender. For masculine: 'أحدَ عشرَ رجلاً', for feminine: 'إحدى عشرةَ امرأةً'. Numbers 13-19: the first part disagrees in gender while عشر agrees: 'ثلاثةَ عشرَ رجلاً' (thirteen men — ثلاثة is feminine despite counting males). This gender pattern in compound numbers is notoriously tricky.

items.question: النيلُ أطولُ أنهارِ العالمِ _____.
items.translation: (The Nile is the longest river in the world in length.)
items.explanation: 'طولاً' (in length) is tamyiz specifying the dimension of the superlative 'أطولُ'. While 'أطولُ أنهارِ العالمِ' already means 'the longest river,' adding 'طولاً' makes it emphatically precise — longest specifically in length (not fame, not importance). This may seem redundant but it's a standard Arabic rhetoric device called تأكيد (emphasis/specification). Other examples: 'أكبرُ مساحةً' (largest in area), 'أعلى ارتفاعاً' (highest in elevation). Tamyiz with superlatives adds rhetorical precision.

name: Absolute Object (المفعول المطلق)
description: Verbal nouns emphasizing or specifying the verb's action

items.question: نجحَ الطالبُ _____ باهراً.
items.explanation: The مفعول مطلق (absolute/cognate object) is a verbal noun (مصدر) derived from the same root as the verb, used in the accusative to emphasize or specify the action. 'نجحَ نجاحاً' — succeeded a success. It intensifies or describes the manner of the verb. Here 'باهراً' is an adjective modifying the مفعول مطلق, meaning 'brilliant success.'
