The مفعول مطلق must share the same root as the verb (ن-ج-ح). It answers 'what kind of success?' or 'how much success?'

items.question: ضربَهُ _____ شديدةً.
items.explanation: 'ضربةً' is a مفعول مطلق that is a مرة noun (single-instance noun), indicating one occurrence of the action. It's from the pattern فَعلة. 'ضربَ ضربةً' — he hit one hit. Adding 'شديدةً' (severe) describes the quality of that single hit. Arabic has two specialized مصدر patterns: مصدر المرة (once: فَعلة) for one occurrence, and مصدر الهيئة (manner: فِعلة) for how something is done. The مفعول مطلق can use either one for nuanced meaning.

items.question: أحبُّها _____ كبيراً.
items.explanation: 'حُبّاً' is the مصدر (verbal noun) of 'أحبَّ' (to love). Using مفعول مطلق here intensifies the emotion: not just 'I love her' but 'I love her a great love.' The adjective 'كبيراً' modifies the مفعول مطلق, specifying the magnitude. This construction is heavily used in Arabic poetry and romantic literature. The مفعول مطلق with emotional verbs creates powerful expressions: 'كرِهَهُ كُرهاً شديداً' (hated him intensely), 'فرِحَ فرحاً عظيماً' (rejoiced greatly).

items.question: شكرتُهُ _____ جزيلاً.
items.explanation: 'شُكراً جزيلاً' is one of the most common مفعول مطلق expressions in Arabic — 'thank you very much' literally means 'a profuse thanking.' The verb 'شكرَ' shares the root ش-ك-ر with 'شُكراً'. In daily usage, 'شُكراً' alone (without the verb) is understood as an abbreviated مفعول مطلق where the verb is implied: '[أشكرُكَ] شُكراً.' Many common Arabic expressions are actually مفعول مطلق with deleted verbs: 'عفواً' (pardon), 'حقاً' (truly), 'صبراً' (patience!).

items.question: _____ واحدةً ثم ذهبَ.
items.explanation: 'نظرةً' is a مفعول مطلق using the مصدر المرة pattern (فَعلة) to indicate a single occurrence. 'نظرَ نظرةً واحدةً' — he looked one single look. The adjective 'واحدةً' emphsizes the single occurrence. This is different from the regular مصدر 'نظراً' which would indicate the general action of looking. The مرة form is practical: 'ضربَ ضربةً' (struck once), 'قفزَ قفزةً' (jumped once), 'صرخَ صرخةً' (screamed once). It quantifies the action to exactly one instance.

items.question: فهمتُ الدرسَ _____ تاماً.
items.translation: (I understood the lesson with complete understanding.)
items.explanation: 'فهماً' is the مصدر of 'فهِمَ' (to understand), used here as مفعول مطلق to emphasize the completeness of understanding. 'تاماً' (complete) modifies it. Note that مفعول مطلق can coexist with other objects in the same sentence — here 'الدرسَ' is the مفعول به (direct object) and 'فهماً' is the مفعول مطلق. Arabic sentences can have multiple منصوبات (accusative elements) each serving different functions. The order is typically: verb + subject + مفعول به + مفعول مطلق.

items.question: لا تُبذِّرْ _____ إسرافاً.
items.explanation: Even in negative imperative sentences, مفعول مطلق remains accusative: 'لا تُبذِّرْ تبذيراً'. The Form II مصدر 'تبذيراً' (from بذَّرَ/يُبذِّرُ) follows the pattern تفعيل. Each Arabic verb form has its own مصدر pattern: Form I varies, Form II = تفعيل, Form III = مُفاعلة/فِعال, Form IV = إفعال, etc. Knowing the مصدر pattern for each form is crucial for constructing مفعول مطلق correctly. Here, 'إسرافاً' acts as a descriptive adjective of the مفعول مطلق.

items.question: سبحانَ اللهِ! أُسبِّحُهُ _____.
items.translation: (Glory be to God! I glorify Him with glorification.)
items.explanation: 'تسبيحاً' is the Form II مصدر of 'سبَّحَ' (to glorify/praise), functioning as مفعول مطلق. Interestingly, 'سُبحانَ' in 'سُبحانَ اللهِ' is itself a مفعول مطلق with a deleted verb: '[أُسبِّحُ] سُبحانَ اللهِ.' It's one of the most recognizable مفعول مطلق constructions in Islamic Arabic. 'سُبحانَ' is a frozen مصدر that only appears in إضافة with 'الله' or 'ـهُ'. Religious expressions frequently contain مفعول مطلق: 'حمداً' (praise), 'صبراً' (patience).

items.question: سارَ الجيشُ _____ منظَّماً.
items.translation: (The army marched in an organized march.)
items.explanation: 'سَيراً' is the مصدر of 'سارَ' (to march/walk), a hollow verb (أجوف). For hollow verbs, the مصدر preserves the original root: سارَ (root: س-ي-ر) → سَير. The adjective 'منظَّماً' describes the type of marching. مفعول مطلق with military and movement verbs creates vivid imagery in Arabic prose: 'هجمَ هجوماً عنيفاً' (attacked violently), 'تقدَّمَ تقدُّماً سريعاً' (advanced rapidly). These constructions are common in news reporting and historical narrative.

items.question: _____ كثيراً في الإجازةِ.
items.translation: (I traveled a lot during the vacation.)
items.explanation: The مفعول مطلق 'سفراً' emphasizes the quantity of traveling, modified by 'كثيراً' (much/a lot). 'سافرَ' (to travel) is a Form III verb — its مصدر on the pattern فَعَل is 'سَفَر'. The past tense 'سافرتُ' (I traveled) + 'سفراً كثيراً' creates an emphatic statement about extensive travel. العدد (quantifying) مفعول مطلق uses adjectives like كثيراً, قليلاً, بعضَ — specifying how much of the action occurred rather than its quality or manner.

items.question: احترمَهُ _____ كبيراً.
items.explanation: 'احتراماً' is the Form VIII مصدر of 'احترمَ' (to respect). Form VIII مصادر follow the pattern افتعال: احتِرام, اجتِماع, انتِظار, etc. Using مفعول مطلق with emotional and social verbs intensifies the sentiment. Arabic excels at this kind of emphatic expression: 'احترمَهُ احتراماً كبيراً' is stronger than simply 'احترمَهُ كثيراً'. The مفعول مطلق construction adds weight and formality, making it preferred in speeches, formal writing, and eloquent expression.

items.question: تغيَّرَ الوضعُ _____ جذرياً.
items.explanation: 'تغيُّراً' is the Form V مصدر of 'تغيَّرَ' (to change). Form V مصادر follow the pattern تفعُّل: تغيُّر, تعلُّم, تقدُّم, etc. 'جذرياً' (radical/fundamental) describes the nature of the change. The مفعول مطلق here functions as a النوع (type-specifying) absolute object — it tells us what KIND of change occurred. This is common in analytical and journalistic Arabic: 'تطوَّرَ تطوُّراً ملحوظاً' (developed noticeably), 'تحسَّنَ تحسُّناً كبيراً' (improved greatly).

items.question: أنجزَ المشروعَ _____ رائعاً.
items.translation: (He completed the project with remarkable completion.)
items.explanation: 'إنجازاً' is the Form IV مصدر of 'أنجزَ' (to accomplish/complete). Form IV مصادر follow the pattern إفعال: إنجاز, إعلان, إرسال, etc. Note that both 'المشروعَ' (direct object) and 'إنجازاً' (مفعول مطلق) are in the accusative but serve different grammatical functions. The مفعول به identifies WHAT was completed; the مفعول مطلق describes HOW it was completed. Arabic parsing (إعراب) distinguishes these carefully based on their relationship to the verb.

items.question: سجدَ _____ طويلةً.
items.explanation: 'سجدةً' is the مصدر المرة (single-instance noun) of 'سجدَ' (to prostrate). Using the فَعلة pattern indicates ONE prostration, modified by 'طويلةً' (long). This is distinct from the regular مصدر 'سُجوداً' which would mean prostration in general. Religious vocabulary frequently uses مفعول مطلق: 'ركعَ ركعةً' (bowed one bow), 'دعا دعاءً' (supplicated a supplication). These terms appear constantly in descriptions of prayer (صلاة) and worship rituals in Islamic texts.

items.question: صبراً _____! إنَّ الفرجَ قريبٌ.
items.explanation: 'صبراً' is a مفعول مطلق with a deleted verb: '[اصبِروا] صبراً' — [be patient] with patience. This is called نائب عن المفعول المطلق (substitute for the absolute object) where the verb is understood from context. 'أيُّها الناسُ' is the vocative form for definite nouns with 'الـ': يا + أيُّها + الاسم. Arabic commonly uses standalone مفعول مطلق as exclamations: 'عفواً!' (pardon!), 'شُكراً!' (thanks!), 'حقّاً!' (really!). These are among the most practical مفعول مطلق forms.

name: Exception Particles (أدوات الاستثناء)
description: Words used to exclude items from a general statement

items.question: حضرَ الطلابُ _____ محمداً.
items.explanation: 'إلّا' is the primary exception particle in Arabic, meaning 'except/other than.' In a complete affirmative sentence (where the المستثنى منه is mentioned), the noun after إلّا is accusative: 'محمداً'. This is called استثناء تام مُثبت (complete, affirmative exception). The sentence is complete because the general group (الطلابُ) is mentioned, and affirmative because there's no negation. In this type, the مستثنى (exception) MUST be accusative. This is the most basic exception pattern in Arabic.

items.question: ما حضرَ أحدٌ _____ عليٌّ.
items.explanation: In a complete NEGATIVE sentence (استثناء تام منفي), the noun after إلّا can follow the case of the المستثنى منه (بدل) instead of being forced into the accusative. Here 'أحدٌ' is nominative (subject), so 'عليٌّ' is also nominative as a بدل (substitute). Alternative: 'إلّا عليّاً' (accusative) is also acceptable. The negative exception gives speakers a choice: follow the case of the noun before إلّا (preferred in modern Arabic) or use accusative (always valid). Both are grammatically correct.

items.question: ما رأيتُ في الحديقةِ _____ أطفالاً.
items.translation: (I saw nothing in the park except children.)
items.explanation: In this negative complete exception, the مستثنى 'أطفالاً' follows the case of the implied المستثنى منه. Since the implied general noun would be the object of 'رأيتُ' (accusative), 'أطفالاً' is accusative as a بدل. The sentence is negative (ما) and the general category is implied (I saw nothing... except children). In negative exceptions, Arabic speakers overwhelmingly prefer using بدل (matching case) over forced accusative. This creates more natural, flowing sentences.

items.question: كلُّ شيءٍ يفنى _____ اللهَ.
items.explanation: This is a complete affirmative exception — 'كلُّ شيءٍ' (everything) is the المستثنى منه, and 'اللهَ' (God) is the مستثنى, mandatory accusative after إلّا in complete affirmative sentences. This sentence echoes Quran 28:88: 'كلُّ شيءٍ هالكٌ إلّا وجهَهُ.' Exception constructions appear frequently in Quranic and religious texts, dealing with divine attributes and absolutes. The particle إلّا creates powerful theological statements about divine uniqueness and transcendence.

items.question: جاءَ الفريقُ _____ محمدٍ.
items.explanation: 'غيرَ' is a noun meaning 'other than' that functions like إلّا but takes its exception in the GENITIVE (إضافة): 'غيرَ محمدٍ.' 'غيرَ' itself takes the case that the مستثنى would take after إلّا. In a complete affirmative sentence, the مستثنى after إلّا would be accusative, so 'غيرَ' is accusative. But in a negative sentence, 'غيرُ' could be nominative (بدل): 'ما جاءَ غيرُ محمدٍ.' غير adapts its own case while always putting its مضاف إليه in the genitive — a dual-case system.

items.question: ما فازَ _____ المُجتهدُ.
items.explanation: This is the استثناء مُفرَّغ (emptied/incomplete exception) — the المستثنى منه is absent. When there's no general group mentioned, the noun after إلّا takes whatever case the sentence requires: here 'المُجتهدُ' is the subject (فاعل) so it's NOMINATIVE. In مُفرَّغ exceptions, إلّا is essentially ignored for case purposes — parse the sentence as if إلّا weren't there. 'فازَ المُجتهدُ.' This type effectively means 'only' and is extremely common in Arabic.

items.question: لا أعرفُ أحداً في المدينةِ _____ أخي.
items.translation: (I don't know anyone in the city except my brother.)
items.explanation: After إلّا in a negative complete sentence, the مستثنى 'أخي' follows the case of المستثنى منه 'أحداً' (أحد is accusative as object of أعرفُ). As بدل, 'أخي' would also be accusative — but the ياء possessive pronoun makes the ending invisible (the fatha is absorbed). This demonstrates how attached pronouns interact with exception syntax. The sentence has double negation cues: 'لا' (negation) + 'أحداً' (anybody → nobody). Exception after negation creates 'only' meaning.

items.question: نجحَ الطلابُ _____ اثنينِ.
items.explanation: 'ما عدا' is an exception phrase meaning 'except for / apart from.' When preceded by 'ما' المصدرية, 'عدا' becomes a verb in a مصدرية construct, and the noun after it is accusative (مفعول به): 'ما عدا اثنينِ.' Without 'ما,' عدا can function as a preposition taking genitive: 'عدا اثنينِ' (genitive). The 'ما + عدا' combination is preferred in modern Arabic because it unambiguously forces the accusative. Similarly, 'ما خلا' works the same way.

items.question: أحبُّ الفواكهَ _____ الموزِ.
items.explanation: 'سِوى' functions like 'غير' — it's a noun meaning 'other than' that takes the exception in the GENITIVE: 'سِوى الموزِ.' سِوى itself takes the case that the مستثنى would take after إلّا. In this affirmative complete sentence, it would be accusative, but سِوى is indeclinable (مبنية) in most dialects. The genitive after سِوى (مضاف إليه) is mandatory. سِوى is more literary than غير and appears frequently in formal Arabic, poetry, and Quranic commentary.

items.question: كلُّهم حضروا _____ خالدٍ.
items.explanation: 'خلا' as a preposition takes the genitive: 'خلا خالدٍ.' It means 'except/apart from,' similar to عدا. Both خلا and عدا can function as either prepositions (taking genitive) or verbs (taking accusative). As preposition: 'خلا خالدٍ' (genitive). As verb with ما: 'ما خلا خالداً' (accusative). This dual nature is unique to Arabic exception particles. In practice, the genitive (prepositional) usage is more common in speech, while the accusative (ما + verb) is more literary.

items.question: ما في البيتِ إلّا _____.
items.translation: (There is nothing in the house except a cat.)
items.explanation: This is a مُفرَّغ (incomplete) exception — no المستثنى منه is stated. In مُفرَّغ, the noun after إلّا takes the case required by the sentence: here 'قطةٌ' is the subject of the existential sentence (مبتدأ مؤخر), so it's NOMINATIVE with tanwin damma. Parse it as: 'ما في البيتِ قطةٌ' (there is no cat) → add إلّا → 'ما في البيتِ إلّا قطةٌ' (nothing except a cat = only a cat). The مُفرَّغ exception is the most common type in conversational Arabic.

items.question: حضرَ الجميعُ _____ المديرَ.
items.explanation: Complete affirmative exception: 'الجميعُ' (everyone) is the المستثنى منه, 'المديرَ' is the مستثنى, mandatorily accusative. 'الجميعُ' is one of several words that trigger the 'complete' exception pattern, along with: كلّ (all), جميع (all), كافة (entire), سائر (the rest). When these all-encompassing words appear, the exception is necessarily from a complete set. This makes the accusative case after إلّا obligatory, no ambiguity about بدل is possible here.

items.question: لم يشاركْ في السباقِ _____ ثلاثةُ لاعبينَ.
items.translation: (Only three players participated in the race.)
items.explanation: Negative مُفرَّغ exception: no المستثنى منه, so 'ثلاثةُ' takes the case the sentence demands — here it's the subject (فاعل) of 'يشاركْ,' so it's NOMINATIVE. 'لاعبينَ' is مضاف إليه (genitive). Remember: in مُفرَّغ exceptions, إلّا doesn't affect the case at all. The sentence effectively means: 'شاركَ ثلاثةُ لاعبينَ فقط' (only three players participated). This negative + إلّا = 'only' equivalence is foundational to understanding Arabic exception syntax.

items.question: لا أَمَلَ _____ في العملِ.
items.translation: (There is no hope except in work.)
items.explanation: مُفرَّغ exception with a prepositional phrase: 'في العملِ' is the exception after إلّا. When the exception is a prepositional phrase rather than a noun, it functions as خبر (predicate) or متعلق (related element). 'لا أملَ إلّا في العملِ' — no hope exists except in work. The 'لا' here is لا النافية للجنس (categorical negation), making 'أملَ' in the accusative (مبني على الفتح). The prepositional phrase is the predicate. This type of exception appears frequently in motivational and philosophical Arabic texts.

items.question: كلُّ الأبوابِ مُغلقةٌ _____ هذا البابَ.
items.translation: (All doors are closed except this door.)
items.explanation: Complete affirmative exception with a nominal sentence. The المستثنى منه is 'كلُّ الأبوابِ,' and the مستثنى 'هذا البابَ' is accusative. Note 'هذا' is a demonstrative pronoun (indeclinable) and 'البابَ' follows it with فتحة showing the accusative. Exception rules apply to nominal sentences (الجمل الاسمية) just as they do to verbal sentences. Whether the main clause uses a verb or not, the same three exception patterns (complete affirmative, complete negative, and مُفرَّغ) govern the case of the مستثنى.

name: Superlative Form (أفعل التفضيل)
description: Expressing the highest degree of a quality

items.question: هذا هو _____ كتاب في المكتبة (This is the best book in the library)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أفضل' (best) follows the أفعل pattern derived from the root ف-ض-ل. In Arabic, superlatives are formed by applying the أفعل template to a three-letter root. When used with a definite noun in an إضافة construction, it conveys 'the best of' something.

items.question: القاهرة هي _____ مدينة في مصر (Cairo is the largest city in Egypt)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أكبر' (largest/biggest) comes from the root ك-ب-ر meaning big. Arabic superlatives using the أفعل pattern remain masculine singular regardless of the noun they describe. This is a key difference from adjective agreement rules.

items.question: هذا _____ يوم في حياتي (This is the happiest day in my life)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أسعد' (happiest) derives from سَعِدَ (to be happy). When a superlative is the first term of an إضافة (construct state), it takes no tanwin and no definite article. The second noun provides the definiteness through the possessive relationship.

items.question: الفيل _____ من الحصان (The elephant is bigger than the horse)
items.explanation: When أفعل is followed by 'من' (than), it functions as a comparative rather than a superlative. The structure 'أفعل + من' is the standard comparative construction in Arabic. Note that the same أفعل form serves both comparative and superlative functions depending on context.

items.question: هي _____ طالبة في الصف (She is the smartest student in the class)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أذكى' (smartest) derives from ذَكِيّ (smart). For roots ending in a weak letter (like ي), the أفعل pattern becomes أفعَى. Despite describing a female student, the superlative remains in the أفعل masculine form when used in إضافة construction.

items.question: هذا المطعم يقدم _____ طعام (This restaurant serves the best food)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أطيب' (best/most delicious) comes from طَيِّب (good/delicious). In the أفعل pattern, doubled roots like ط-ي-ب maintain their form. The إضافة construction here links the superlative directly to the indefinite noun طعام without an article.

items.question: الشتاء هو _____ فصل في السنة (Winter is the coldest season of the year)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أبرد' (coldest) follows the regular أفعل pattern from بَرَدَ (to be cold). Arabic uses this same pattern consistently across hundreds of adjective roots. The definite article on الشتاء and السنة frames this as an absolute superlative statement.

items.question: _____ ما يمكن فعله هو الانتظار (The best thing to do is wait)
items.explanation: Here أفضل is followed by 'ما' (what/that which), creating a common construction meaning 'the best of what.' This pattern أفعل + ما is extremely productive in Modern Standard Arabic and is used in everyday speech to express optimal choices or recommendations.

items.question: هذا الطريق _____ من ذلك (This road is longer than that one)
items.explanation: The comparative 'أطول' (longer/longest) from طَوِيل (long/tall) demonstrates the dual nature of أفعل forms. With 'من' it means 'longer than,' while in إضافة it means 'the longest.' Context determines which reading applies, making Arabic comparatives remarkably versatile.

items.question: هي من _____ النساء في القرية (She is among the most beautiful women in the village)
items.explanation: When أفعل is used with a plural definite noun in إضافة (أجمل النساء), it means 'the most beautiful of the women.' This construction is called superlative إضافة. The أفعل form always stays masculine singular, even when referring to feminine or plural nouns.

items.question: الماء هو _____ مشروب للصحة (Water is the most beneficial drink for health)
items.explanation: The superlative 'أنفع' (most beneficial) derives from نَفَعَ (to benefit). This form demonstrates how Arabic can derive superlatives from virtually any descriptive root. The إضافة with مشروب creates clarifying context: 'the most beneficial of drinks.'

items.question: هذه المشكلة _____ مما تظن (This problem is more difficult than you think)
items.explanation: The comparative 'أصعب' (more difficult) with 'مما' (than what) creates a complex comparison. 'مما' is a contraction of 'من + ما' (than what). This construction compares reality against perception, a sophisticated use of the comparative that appears frequently in formal Arabic.

items.question: الكتاب _____ بكثير من الفيلم (The book is far better than the movie)
items.explanation: Adding 'بكثير' (by much) after a comparative intensifies the comparison. This adverbial phrase modifies the degree of the comparative أفعل. It is equivalent to English 'far better' or 'much better.' Arabic uses this construction frequently in literary and media criticism.

items.question: ما _____ هذا المنظر! (How beautiful this view is!)
items.explanation: The exclamatory construction 'ما أفعل' expresses wonder and amazement, equivalent to 'How [adjective]!' in English. This is called أسلوب التعجب (style of exclamation). The 'ما' here is exclamatory, not interrogative, followed by the أفعل form as a verb of amazement.

items.question: هو _____ الطلاب حضوراً (He is the most regular student in attendance)
items.explanation: When a quality cannot form أفعل directly (like انتظام), Arabic uses 'أكثر + تمييز' (accusative specification). Here أكثر حضوراً means 'most in terms of attendance.' This analytical superlative construction extends the system to adjectives that do not fit the أفعل pattern.

name: Adverbs (الظروف)
description: Words modifying verbs to express time, place, or manner

items.question: سأزورك _____ (I will visit you tomorrow)
items.explanation: The time adverb 'غداً' (tomorrow) takes tanwin fatha (accusative) because adverbs of time in Arabic typically appear in the accusative case (منصوب). This is called ظرف زمان (adverb of time) and functions as a مفعول فيه, indicating the time container of the action.

items.question: وصلت _____ إلى المدرسة (I arrived early to school)
items.explanation: The manner adverb 'باكراً' (early) uses the tanwin fatha ending characteristic of Arabic adverbs. Many manner adverbs in Arabic are formed by adding tanwin fatha to adjectives or nouns. This transform from adjective to adverb is one of the most productive patterns in Arabic grammar.

items.question: الأطفال يلعبون _____ (The children play outside)
items.explanation: The place adverb 'خارجاً' (outside) is a ظرف مكان (adverb of place). Like time adverbs, place adverbs take the accusative case in Arabic. The word خارج can also function as a preposition when followed by a noun in إضافة (خارج البيت = outside the house).

items.question: هو _____ ما يذهب إلى السوق (He always goes to the market)
items.explanation: The frequency adverb 'دائماً' (always) indicates habitual action. Arabic frequency adverbs include دائماً (always), غالباً (usually), أحياناً (sometimes), نادراً (rarely), and أبداً (never). They typically appear before or after the verb, with flexible positioning unlike English.

items.question: تكلم المعلم _____ (The teacher spoke slowly)
items.explanation: Arabic forms many manner adverbs using the preposition 'بـ' (with/by) plus a noun: ببطء (with slowness = slowly). This prepositional phrase construction is equivalent to the English '-ly' adverb formation. It is one of the most common ways to express manner in both MSA and dialects.

items.question: جلست _____ الطاولة (I sat behind the table)
items.explanation: The spatial adverb 'خلف' (behind) is a ظرف مكان that takes the noun following it into إضافة (genitive). Arabic spatial adverbs like أمام (in front of), فوق (above), تحت (below), and بين (between) all function this way, governing the genitive case on the following noun.

items.question: _____ نذهب إلى الحديقة (Sometimes we go to the park)
items.explanation: The frequency adverb 'أحياناً' (sometimes) can appear at the beginning of a sentence for emphasis, similar to English fronting. It derives from the plural of حين (time/moment). Placing adverbs sentence-initially in Arabic adds rhetorical weight and is common in both writing and formal speech.

items.question: أنهى العمل _____ (He finished the work quickly)
items.explanation: The manner adverb 'سريعاً' (quickly) is formed by adding tanwin fatha to the adjective سريع (fast). This is the simplest adverb formation in Arabic. The adverb typically follows the verb and its object, though word order flexibility allows other positions for stylistic emphasis.

items.question: سافرنا _____ بالقطار (We traveled north by train)
items.explanation: Directional adverbs like 'شمالاً' (northward) take tanwin fatha in the accusative. The four cardinal directions شمال (north), جنوب (south), شرق (east), غرب (west) all function as adverbs when given the accusative ending, indicating the direction of movement or orientation.

items.question: لن أفعل ذلك _____ (I will never do that)
items.explanation: The adverb 'أبداً' (never/ever) is exclusively used with negative constructions in Arabic. With لن (will not) it means 'never.' Unlike English where 'ever' can appear in questions and positives, Arabic أبداً is strictly limited to negative contexts. This makes it a negative polarity item.

items.question: وقف _____ الباب (He stood in front of the door)
items.explanation: The locative adverb 'أمام' (in front of) creates an إضافة with the following noun. Arabic locative adverbs function as semi-prepositions: they govern the genitive case like prepositions but can themselves be governed by other prepositions (من أمام = from in front of), giving them dual grammatical behavior.

items.question: اتصل بي _____ (Call me immediately)
items.explanation: The adverb 'فوراً' (immediately) conveys urgency and instant timing. It derives from فَور (immediacy/boiling point). This adverb is commonly used in imperative contexts and official communications. Its accusative tanwin marking follows the standard Arabic adverbial pattern for time expressions.

items.question: يدرس _____ للامتحان (He studies a lot for the exam)
items.explanation: The quantitative adverb 'كثيراً' (a lot/much) modifies the verb's intensity. It contrasts with قليلاً (a little). These quantity adverbs are technically مفعول مطلق (absolute object) constructions, describing the degree or extent of the verbal action rather than its manner or time.

items.question: _____ درسنا هذا الموضوع (We already studied this topic)
items.explanation: The temporal adverb 'سابقاً' (previously/already) derives from the active participle سابق (preceding). Many Arabic adverbs are formed from active or passive participles with tanwin fatha. This participle-to-adverb conversion is highly productive, allowing virtually any participle to serve adverbially.

items.question: جلسنا _____ في الحديقة (We sat together in the park)
items.explanation: The adverb 'معاً' (together) indicates collective action. It derives from مَعَ (with) plus the adverbial tanwin. Arabic distinguishes between معاً (together as adverb), مع (with as preposition), and سوياً (together, more formal). Each has slightly different usage contexts and register associations.

name: Conjunctions (أدوات الربط)
description: Connecting clauses and ideas in Arabic sentences

items.question: ذهبت إلى السوق _____ اشتريت فاكهة (I went to the market and bought fruit)
items.explanation: The conjunction 'و' (and) is the most fundamental Arabic connector, called واو العطف. It joins two elements of equal grammatical status. Unlike English 'and,' Arabic و is written attached to the following word with no space. It can connect nouns, verbs, sentences, and even paragraphs in flowing Arabic prose.

items.question: هل تريد شاياً _____ قهوة؟ (Do you want tea or coffee?)
items.explanation: The disjunctive conjunction 'أو' (or) presents alternatives or choices. In questions, it creates selection queries. Arabic distinguishes between أو (inclusive/exclusive or) and أم (exclusive or in questions). 'أم' is used specifically in هل questions presenting two defined alternatives, while أو is more general.

items.question: أحب السفر _____ لا أملك المال (I love traveling but I don't have money)
items.explanation: The adversative conjunction 'لكن' (but/however) introduces contrast or contradiction. It signals that the second clause opposes or qualifies the first. In Arabic grammar, لكن is classified as a حرف استدراك (particle of rectification). The strengthened form لكنّ takes an accusative subject noun.

items.question: أكلت الطعام _____ شربت الماء (I ate the food then drank the water)
items.explanation: The sequential conjunction 'ثم' (then/afterwards) indicates chronological ordering with a time gap between events. It differs from 'فـ' which implies immediate succession. Arabic is precise about temporal relationships: و (simultaneous), فـ (immediate sequence), ثم (delayed sequence), each conveying different temporal spacing.

items.question: درست كثيراً _____ نجحت في الامتحان (I studied a lot so I passed the exam)
items.explanation: The conjunction 'فـ' (so/then) here indicates result or consequence. As فاء السببية (causal fa), it connects cause to effect. The فـ is one of Arabic's most versatile particles: it can indicate sequence (فاء العطف), result (فاء السببية), or explanation (فاء التفسيرية), depending on context and what follows it.

items.question: لن أذهب _____ كنت متعباً (I won't go because I'm tired)
items.explanation: The causal conjunction 'لأنني' (because I) combines لأنّ (because) with the pronoun suffix ني. The base form لأنّ is composed of لـ (for) + أنّ (that), creating a causal subordinator. أنّ and its derivatives are among the most important subordinating tools in Arabic, always followed by a noun or pronoun in the accusative.

items.question: سأنتظر _____ تأتي (I will wait until you come)
items.explanation: The conjunction 'حتى' (until/so that) introduces temporal endpoints or purpose clauses. When followed by a present tense verb, حتى can indicate purpose (so that) with the verb in subjunctive mood (منصوب). When followed by a past tense verb, it indicates a temporal limit (until). This dual function makes حتى contextually rich.

items.question: _____ درست جيداً ستنجح (If you study well, you will succeed)
items.explanation: The conditional conjunction 'إذا' (if/when) introduces real, likely conditions. Arabic distinguishes إذا (likely/real conditions) from لو (unlikely/hypothetical conditions). إذا typically takes a past tense verb in the condition clause even for future meaning, a unique feature of Arabic conditional grammar.

items.question: سأساعدك _____ ساعدتني (I will help you provided that you helped me)
items.explanation: The conditional phrase 'بشرط أن' (on the condition that/provided that) introduces stipulations. It is followed by a verb in the subjunctive mood (منصوب). Arabic has several conditional phrases: بشرط أن, على أن, شريطة أن, all requiring the subjunctive, reflecting Arabic's rich system of mood-governing particles.

items.question: _____ أنه مريض ذهب إلى العمل (Although he is sick, he went to work)
items.explanation: The concessive conjunction 'رغم' (although/despite) introduces a concession. It can be followed by أنّ + clause or by a noun. Arabic has several concessive expressions: رغم أن, مع أن, بالرغم من, على الرغم من. They all signal that the main clause holds true despite the obstacle stated in the subordinate clause.

items.question: لا أعرف _____ سيأتي أم لا (I don't know whether he will come or not)
items.explanation: The interrogative particle 'هل' functions as an indirect question marker meaning 'whether.' In direct questions هل expects yes/no answers, but in indirect speech after verbs like أعرف (I know) or أتساءل (I wonder), it introduces embedded questions. The أم لا (or not) completes the binary choice.

items.question: اجتهد _____ تنجح (Work hard so that you succeed)
items.explanation: The purpose conjunction 'كي' (in order to/so that) introduces purpose or goal clauses. The verb following كي takes the subjunctive mood (منصوب). Arabic has multiple purpose particles: كي, لكي, حتى, لـ, all requiring subjunctive verb forms. كي is the most explicit purpose marker in the language.

items.question: لم أذهب _____ لم أُدعَ (I didn't go since I wasn't invited)
items.explanation: The causal conjunction 'إذ' (since/as) provides background reasoning. It is more literary than لأنّ and appears frequently in formal Arabic writing and Quranic text. إذ can also function temporally (when/at the time that), making it a multi-purpose subordinator that requires context to interpret correctly.

items.question: ليس الأمر صعباً _____ سهلٌ جداً (It's not difficult, rather it's very easy)
items.explanation: The corrective conjunction 'بل' (rather/but rather) negates the preceding statement and replaces it with a correction. It is stronger than لكن because it completely overrides the first clause. بل is classified as حرف إضراب (particle of cancellation) and is essential in Arabic rhetoric for making emphatic corrections and clarifications.

items.question: سأذهب _____ كان الطقس سيئاً (I will go even if the weather is bad)
items.explanation: The concessive conditional 'حتى لو' (even if) combines concession with hypothetical condition. لو introduces unlikely or hypothetical conditions, and حتى adds the concessive 'even' meaning. This compound conjunction expresses determination despite adverse conditions, frequently used in motivational and argumentative contexts in Arabic.

name: Vocative Style (أسلوب النداء)
description: Calling or addressing someone using vocative particles

items.question: _____ محمد، تعال هنا (O Muhammad, come here)
items.explanation: The vocative particle 'يا' is the most common way to address someone in Arabic. It is equivalent to 'O' or 'Hey' in English. يا is used before proper nouns, titles, and descriptions. It is so universal that it appears in virtually every Arabic text from the Quran to modern conversation, making it indispensable.

items.question: _____ الطلاب، انتبهوا (O students, pay attention)
items.explanation: The vocative form 'أيها' is used before definite nouns with ال (the). You cannot say يا الطلاب directly; instead Arabic requires أيها before masculine definite nouns and أيتها before feminine ones. This intermediary particle bridges the vocative يا with definite nouns, a unique feature of Arabic vocative grammar.

items.question: يا _____ العزيز (Dear doctor)
items.explanation: After يا, the addressed noun (المنادى) typically appears without the definite article and in the nominative case if it's a single word (مفرد علم). Professional titles like دكتور, أستاذ, مهندس follow this pattern. The title remains in its base form, reflecting the vocative case rules for proper nouns and single-word appellations.

items.question: يا _____ الباب (O opener of the door)
items.explanation: When the vocative noun is in إضافة (construct state), like فاتح الباب (opener of the door), the first element takes no tanwin and stays in the construct form. This is called المنادى المضاف. The active participle فاتح (opener) here addresses someone by their action, a rhetorical device common in Arabic literature and prayer.

items.question: _____ المعلمات، شكراً لكنّ (O female teachers, thank you)
items.explanation: The feminine vocative form 'أيتها' is required before feminine definite nouns. While أيها addresses masculine definite nouns, أيتها addresses feminine ones. This gender distinction in the vocative intermediary particle reflects Arabic's systematic gender agreement that extends even to particles of address.

items.question: يا _____ (O my Lord)
items.explanation: In the vocative with first-person possession, the ي (my) is often dropped in classical style: يا ربِّ instead of يا ربّي. The kasra on the last letter indicates the dropped possessive ي. This shortened form is extremely common in prayers and supplications, found throughout the Quran and Islamic devotional literature.

items.question: يا طالباً _____ (O diligent student)
items.explanation: When the vocative noun is indefinite and modified (نكرة غير مقصودة), both the noun and adjective take tanwin fatha (accusative). يا طالباً مجتهداً addresses any diligent student, not a specific one. This contrasts with يا طالبُ المجتهدُ addressing a specific known student, showing Arabic's precision in definiteness.

items.question: _____ بني، كن شجاعاً (O my son, be brave)
items.explanation: The expression 'يا بني' (O my son) uses يا with the إضافة form بُنَيّ (my little son), a diminutive of ابن. This intimate vocative is used in advice-giving contexts, famously in Quranic passages like Luqman's advice to his son. The diminutive adds endearment and tenderness to the address.

items.question: يا _____ الكريم (O generous one)
items.explanation: When calling someone by a descriptive adjective with ال, use أيها + adjective: يا أيها الكريم. The adjective الكريم functions as a noun (substantivized adjective) in this vocative construction. This construction is elevated and respectful, often used in formal address and religious contexts.

items.question: يا مَن _____ هنا (O you who are standing here)
items.explanation: The construction 'يا مَن' (O whoever/O you who) combines the vocative with the relative pronoun مَن. The verb following مَن is typically in the present tense (مضارع). This construction addresses people by their current action or state, creating dynamic descriptions used in speeches and public announcements.

items.question: يا _____ (O Allah)
items.explanation: The name 'الله' (Allah/God) is the one exception to the rule that يا cannot precede a noun with ال. You say يا الله directly without أيها. This unique exception reflects the supreme status of the divine name in Arabic. The expression يا الله is both a prayer invocation and an exclamation of amazement in everyday speech.

items.question: يا _____ اثنين (O you two)
items.explanation: When addressing a dual (two people), Arabic uses أيها الاثنان or just يا اثنان. The vocative system accommodates all number forms: singular, dual, and plural. Dual vocative is less common in modern Arabic but remains important in classical texts and formal address, reflecting Arabic's unique grammatical dual category.

items.question: _____ للعجب! (Oh, how amazing!)
items.explanation: The exclamatory particle 'وا' is used for lamentation and exclamation, functioning as a vocative of emotion. واأسفاه (Oh, what sorrow!) and واعجباه (Oh, how amazing!) are classic examples. This is called ندبة (lamentation vocative), a special subcategory of Arabic address used to express intense emotional reactions.

items.question: يا أبا _____ (O father of Ali)
items.explanation: In vocative إضافة with familial titles, the first element changes form: أب becomes أبا, أخ becomes أخا. The second element (عليٍّ) takes genitive case (kasra) as the مضاف إليه. This كنية (patronymic) vocative is a distinguished form of address in Arab culture, using familial relationships as honorific titles.

items.question: يا ليت _____ يعود (Oh, if only youth would return)
items.explanation: The construction 'يا ليت' combines the vocative with the wish particle ليت, creating an exclamatory wish. ليت governs the accusative case on its subject (الشبابَ). This is a famous Arabic literary expression of nostalgia. يا here is not truly vocative but exclamatory, adding emotional intensity to the wish statement.

name: Time Expressions (التعبيرات الزمنية)
description: Expressing time, duration, and temporal relationships

items.question: الاجتماع _____ الساعة الثالثة (The meeting is at three o'clock)
items.explanation: The preposition 'في' (at/in) is used with clock times in Arabic: في الساعة الثالثة (at three o'clock). Arabic uses ordinal numbers for hours: الأولى (1st), الثانية (2nd), etc. This differs from English which uses cardinal numbers. The في + الساعة construction is the standard way to state specific times.

items.question: سأسافر _____ الصيف (I will travel in the summer)
items.explanation: The preposition 'في' is also used with seasons: في الصيف (in summer), في الشتاء (in winter). Arabic has four seasons: الربيع (spring), الصيف (summer), الخريف (autumn), الشتاء (winter). Seasonal expressions with في indicate the general time period within which an action occurs.

items.question: درست _____ ساعتين (I studied for two hours)
items.explanation: The duration expression 'لمدة' (for the duration of) specifies how long an action lasts. It is followed by a time measurement: لمدة ساعتين (for two hours), لمدة أسبوع (for a week). Arabic distinguishes between في (point in time) and لمدة (duration), similar to English 'at/in' versus 'for' distinction.

items.question: أسكن هنا _____ خمس سنوات (I've lived here for five years)
items.explanation: The temporal preposition 'منذ' (since/for) indicates duration from a past starting point to now. منذ خمس سنوات means 'for five years' (continuing to present). Arabic uses منذ for both 'since' (a point) and 'for' (a duration), while English distinguishes between them. The context clarifies which meaning applies.

items.question: وصلت _____ ساعة (I arrived an hour ago)
items.explanation: The temporal adverb 'قبل' (before/ago) positions an event before the current time. قبل ساعة means 'an hour ago.' Arabic uses قبل for both 'before' (relative to another event) and 'ago' (relative to now). Its opposite بعد (after) works identically in marking post-event timing.

items.question: سنبدأ _____ عشر دقائق (We will start in ten minutes)
items.explanation: The temporal preposition 'بعد' (after/in) indicates future timing relative to now. بعد عشر دقائق means 'in ten minutes' (from now). In time expressions, بعد can reference future events from the present moment or sequence events relative to each other. This versatility makes it essential for scheduling and narration.

items.question: أستيقظ _____ كل يوم (I wake up early every day)
items.explanation: The time adverb 'باكراً' (early) with كل يوم (every day) describes habitual timing. Arabic expresses 'every' using كل + singular noun: كل يوم (every day), كل أسبوع (every week), كل شهر (every month). The combination of a time adverb with كل creates precise habitual time expressions.

items.question: وُلدت _____ عام ١٩٩٠ (I was born in the year 1990)
items.explanation: Years in Arabic use 'في عام' or 'في سنة' (in the year). Arabic numerals (ironically called 'Arabic' in English) are written left-to-right: ١٩٩٠. The word عام and سنة both mean 'year' but عام is more formal. Dates in Arabic follow the pattern: في عام + number, في شهر + month name.

items.question: أذهب إلى العمل _____ الثامنة صباحاً (I go to work at eight in the morning)
items.explanation: Complete time expressions in Arabic combine في الساعة (at the hour) with صباحاً (in the morning) or مساءً (in the evening). Arabic uses صباحاً (morning), ظهراً (noon), عصراً (afternoon), مساءً (evening), ليلاً (night) as time-of-day markers, each taking the accusative adverbial ending.

items.question: _____ أن تأتي اتصل بي (Before you come, call me)
items.explanation: The conjunction 'قبل أن' (before) introduces a temporal clause with the subjunctive verb form. The أن after قبل triggers the subjunctive mood (منصوب) on the following verb. This is a key grammatical rule: temporal prepositions + أن require subjunctive verbs, distinguishing them from indicative main clauses.

items.question: سأنتظرك _____ الساعة الخامسة (I'll wait for you until five o'clock)
items.explanation: The temporal limit 'حتى' (until) marks the endpoint of a duration. حتى الساعة الخامسة sets a clear temporal boundary. When حتى is followed by a noun (like الساعة), it functions as a preposition taking the genitive. When followed by a verb, it takes the subjunctive, showing Arabic's case-sensitive temporal system.

items.question: نلتقي يوم _____ (We meet on Friday)
items.explanation: Days of the week in Arabic take the definite article: الأحد (Sunday), الاثنين (Monday), الثلاثاء (Tuesday), الأربعاء (Wednesday), الخميس (Thursday), الجمعة (Friday), السبت (Saturday). They are used with يوم (day of) in formal register or alone in casual speech.

items.question: _____ وصلت بدأ المطر (When I arrived, the rain started)
items.explanation: The temporal conjunction 'عندما' (when) introduces simultaneous or immediately sequential events. It combines عند (at the time of) with ما (what/that). Arabic has several 'when' words: عندما (general when), حين/حينما (at the time when), لما (when, with completed action), each with slightly different temporal nuances and formality levels.

items.question: لم أره _____ سنوات (I haven't seen him for years)
items.explanation: With negative constructions, 'منذ' (for/since) emphasizes the duration of absence. لم أره منذ سنوات means the non-seeing has persisted for years. Arabic expresses this present perfect continuous negative naturally with لم + past negation + منذ + duration, creating a construction equivalent to English 'haven't...for.'

items.question: يبدأ الدرس _____ (The lesson starts soon)
items.explanation: The temporal adverb 'قريباً' (soon/shortly) indicates near-future timing. It derives from قريب (near/close). Arabic temporal adverbs taking tanwin fatha include: قريباً (soon), لاحقاً (later), سابقاً (previously), حالياً (currently), مؤخراً (recently). Each precisely positions events on the time axis relative to the present moment.

name: Noun Patterns (أوزان الأسماء)
description: Arabic morphological patterns for deriving nouns

items.question: _____ هو من يكتب (A writer is someone who writes)
items.explanation: The pattern فاعِل (faa'il) creates active participles indicating the doer of an action. كاتب (writer) from كَتَبَ (to write) follows this pattern. This is one of the most productive noun patterns in Arabic, generating agent nouns from virtually any verb: عامل (worker), طالب (student), سائق (driver).

items.question: الرسالة _____ بالأمس (The letter was written yesterday)
items.explanation: The pattern مفعول (maf'uul) creates passive participles indicating the recipient of an action. مكتوب (written) from كَتَبَ follows this pattern, with feminine تاء مربوطة added for the feminine noun رسالة. This pattern is essential for describing states resulting from completed actions across all Arabic verb forms.

items.question: هذا _____ جميل (This is a beautiful mosque)
items.explanation: The pattern مَفعِل (maf'il) creates nouns of place indicating where an action occurs. مسجد (mosque = place of prostration) from سَجَدَ (to prostrate). Other examples: مكتب (office/desk), مطبخ (kitchen), مدرسة (school). This pattern systematically derives locations from the verbs associated with activities performed there.

items.question: _____ الباب بالمفتاح (Open the door with the key)
items.explanation: While مِفتاح (key) follows the مِفعال pattern for instruments, the blank here requires the imperative verb افتح (open). The مِفعال/مِفعَل pattern creates instrument nouns: مفتاح (key), مقص (scissors), منشار (saw). These patterns show how Arabic derives tool names from the verbs describing their function.

items.question: _____ الصلاة واجبة (Performing prayer is obligatory)
items.explanation: The pattern إفعالة (if'aala) is the verbal noun (مصدر) of Form IV verbs. إقامة (performing/establishing) from أقامَ (to establish). Each Arabic verb form has a specific مصدر pattern: Form I is irregular, Form II تفعيل, Form III مُفاعَلة, Form IV إفعال. Mastering these patterns is key to advanced vocabulary.

items.question: هذا الولد _____ جداً (This boy is very playful)
items.explanation: The intensified pattern فَعول (fa'uul) indicates someone who does something excessively. لَعوب (very playful) from لَعِبَ (to play). Other intensity patterns include فَعّال (very active doer) and فَعِيل (deeply characterized). These patterns add semantic depth beyond what simple adjectives can convey in English.

items.question: يعمل في _____ كبيرة (He works in a big factory)
items.explanation: The place noun pattern مَفعَل (maf'al) creates location nouns from verbs. مصنع (factory) from صَنَعَ (to manufacture). The slight vowel difference between مَفعَل and مَفعِل both produce place nouns but follow different phonological rules based on the verb's vowel pattern. Both are highly productive in modern Arabic.

items.question: _____ اللغة العربية ممتعة (Studying the Arabic language is fun)
items.explanation: The verbal noun pattern فِعالة (fi'aala) is common for Form I verbs related to professions and activities. دراسة (studying) from دَرَسَ (to study). Other examples: كتابة (writing), قراءة (reading), زراعة (farming). These مصدر forms function as English gerunds or infinitives, serving as subjects or objects.

items.question: الطفل _____ في نومه (The child is peaceful in his sleep)
items.explanation: The active participle pattern فاعِل from the verb هَدَأَ (to be calm) gives هادئ (calm/peaceful). Active participles of intransitive verbs describe states rather than agents: نائم (sleeping), جالس (sitting), واقف (standing). They function as adjectives in Arabic, agreeing in gender, number, and definiteness with their nouns.

items.question: هذا _____ مفيد جداً (This dictionary is very useful)
items.explanation: The pattern مُفعَل (muf'al) from Form IV creates nouns indicating results or products. معجم (dictionary) from أعجَمَ (to make non-Arabic/to dot letters). Arabic morphological patterns allow speakers to predict a word's meaning from its pattern and root. This systematic derivation is unique to Semitic languages.

items.question: _____ هذا المشروع كبيرة (The cost of this project is large)
items.explanation: The verbal noun pattern تَفعِلة (taf'ila) comes from Form II verbs. تكلفة (cost) from كَلَّفَ (to cost). Form II verbal nouns follow تفعيل for most verbs, but some use تفعِلة instead, especially when the last root letter is weak. Knowing which sub-pattern applies requires familiarity with specific verbs.

items.question: عمل _____ بين البلدين (A cooperation between the two countries)
items.explanation: The pattern تَفاعُل (tafaa'ul) is the verbal noun of Form VI, indicating mutual/reciprocal action. تعاون (cooperation) from تعاوَنَ (to cooperate). Form VI inherently implies reciprocity: تبادل (exchange), تواصل (communication), تفاهم (mutual understanding). This pattern is essential for diplomatic and social vocabulary.

items.question: _____ الجديد مريح جداً (The new furniture is very comfortable)
items.explanation: The collective noun pattern فَعال (fa'aal) represents groups or categories. الأثاث (furniture) is a collective noun with no singular form. Arabic has many collective nouns: أمتعة (belongings), أسلحة (weapons). Collective nouns take feminine singular agreement for verbs and adjectives, a rule that often surprises English-speaking learners.

items.question: هذا _____ من أبي (This is a gift from my father)
items.explanation: The unit noun pattern فَعيلة (fa'iila) here gives هدية (gift) from the root ه-د-ي (to guide/give). Arabic derives related words from the same root with different patterns: هداية (guidance), هادٍ (guide), إهداء (dedication). Understanding root-and-pattern morphology unlocks vocabulary systematically rather than through rote memorization.

items.question: _____ العلمي يتطلب صبراً (Scientific research requires patience)
items.explanation: The basic verbal noun pattern فَعل (fa'l) is the simplest مصدر form for many Form I verbs. البحث (research/searching) from بَحَثَ (to search). Form I verbal nouns are the most irregular category in Arabic morphology with dozens of possible patterns (فَعل، فِعل، فُعل، فَعَل، فِعال, etc.), requiring memorization for each verb.

name: Verb Moods (إعراب الفعل المضارع)
description: Indicative, subjunctive, and jussive moods of imperfect verbs

items.question: الطالب _____ كل يوم (The student studies every day)
items.explanation: The indicative mood (المرفوع) is the default mood for present tense verbs, marked by damma (ُ) on the final letter. يدرسُ (he studies) shows the standard indicative ending. The indicative is used for statements of fact, habitual actions, and ongoing events. No particle precedes the verb in this mood.

items.question: أريد _____ اللغة العربية (I want to learn Arabic)
items.explanation: The subjunctive mood (المنصوب) is triggered by أن (to/that), marked by fatha (َ) on the final letter. أن أتعلمَ (to learn) shows the subjunctive ending. Key subjunctive triggers: أن (that), لن (will not), كي/لكي (in order to), حتى (until/so that). These particles nasb the following verb without exception.

items.question: لم _____ إلى المدرسة أمس (He did not go to school yesterday)
items.explanation: The jussive mood (المجزوم) is triggered by لم (did not), marked by sukoon (ْ) on the final letter. لم يذهبْ negates past actions using a present-form verb. Key jussive triggers: لم (did not), لا الناهية (don't), لمّا (not yet), conditional particles. The jussive is the most restricted mood in Arabic.

items.question: لن _____ عن الحلم (I will never give up the dream)
items.explanation: After لن (will not), the verb takes subjunctive mood. For verbs ending in alif maqsoura (ى), the subjunctive is shown by a hidden fatha (فتحة مقدرة) since the ى cannot take visible vowel marks. لن أتخلّى demonstrates this phonological constraint unique to weak-final verbs in the subjunctive mood.

items.question: لا _____ في الامتحان (Don't cheat on the exam)
items.explanation: The prohibitive لا (don't) triggers the jussive mood (مجزوم). لا تغشّْ is a negative command. Arabic distinguishes between لا الناهية (prohibitive, with jussive) and لا النافية (negating, no mood change). This homograph لا has completely different grammatical effects based on context, a subtlety that challenges learners.

items.question: يجب أن _____ الواجب (You must do the homework)
items.explanation: After يجب أن (must), the verb takes subjunctive (منصوب) because أن is the operative particle. يجب alone means 'it is necessary' and أن introduces the action that is necessary. This أن + subjunctive construction is Arabic's primary infinitive equivalent, used after all verbs of wanting, needing, and obligation.

items.question: ادرسْ كي _____ (Study in order to succeed)
items.explanation: The purpose particle كي (in order to) triggers the subjunctive mood. تنجحَ shows the fatha ending. Arabic has several purpose particles all requiring subjunctive: كي, لكي, حتى, لِـ. They all express goals or purposes but vary in formality. كي is the most explicit purpose marker and is common in educational and motivational contexts.

items.question: إن _____ ستجدْ (If you search, you will find)
items.explanation: The conditional particle إن (if) puts the condition verb in jussive mood (مجزوم). إن تبحثْ تجدْ — both the condition and answer verbs take jussive. This is the hallmark of Arabic conditional sentences with جازم particles. إن differs from إذا in that إن takes jussive mood while إذا does not affect verb mood.

items.question: لمّا _____ بعد (He hasn't arrived yet)
items.explanation: The particle لمّا (not yet) triggers jussive mood and indicates an action expected but not yet completed. لمّا يصلْ means 'he hasn't arrived yet' with the implication he will. Unlike لم which simply negates the past, لمّا implies continuity into the present and expectation of future completion, adding temporal nuance.

items.question: المعلمون _____ كل يوم (The teachers teach every day)
items.explanation: For the five special verb forms (الأفعال الخمسة), the indicative is marked by نون (ن) at the end: يدرِّسون (they teach). The five forms are: يفعلان, تفعلان, يفعلون, تفعلون, تفعلين. Their indicative marker is ن, their subjunctive and jussive drop the ن, making mood visible through presence/absence of this suffix.

items.question: لن _____ عن المحاولة (They will not stop trying)
items.explanation: After لن, the five verb forms (الأفعال الخمسة) drop the final ن to show subjunctive: يتوقفون becomes يتوقفوا. This ن-deletion is the visible subjunctive marker for plural/dual verbs. The same deletion occurs after all subjunctive particles (أن, لن, كي, حتى), making it a consistent and predictable morphological rule.

items.question: لم _____ من الامتحان (They did not finish the exam)
items.explanation: After لم, the five verb forms drop ن for jussive: ينتهون becomes لم ينتهوا. Both subjunctive and jussive cause ن-deletion in the five forms, making them identical in appearance for these conjugations. Only context and the governing particle distinguish subjunctive from jussive in these forms.

items.question: _____ الله يرحمك (May God have mercy on you)
items.explanation: Suppressed أن can trigger subjunctive mood in prayers and wishes. The full form would be أسأل أن يرحمَك الله, but Arabic often drops أن in formulaic expressions. This implicit subjunctive appears in blessings, curses, and prayers — a pragmatic use where the mood particle is understood from cultural context rather than stated explicitly.

items.question: من _____ يجدْ (Whoever seeks, finds)
items.explanation: The conditional relative من (whoever) can trigger jussive in both clauses: من يسعَ يجدْ. For hollow verbs like سعى, the jussive truncates the final weak letter, yielding يسعَ instead of يسعى. This interaction between mood and weak verb morphology creates some of the most complex conjugation patterns in Arabic grammar.

items.question: مهما _____ فلن أغيّر رأيي (Whatever you say, I won't change my mind)
items.explanation: The conditional particle مهما (whatever/no matter what) triggers jussive mood. تقولُ becomes تقلْ in jussive — hollow verbs lose their middle vowel letter in jussive mood. This is one of Arabic's most dramatic morphological changes: قال→يقول→يقلْ, showing how three consonant roots interact with mood markers in hollow verb conjugation.

name: Object Pronouns (ضمائر المفعول به)
description: Attached pronouns functioning as direct objects

items.question: المعلم علّم_____ الدرس (The teacher taught us the lesson)
items.explanation: The attached pronoun ـنا (us) functions as a direct object suffix on the verb. Arabic attaches object pronouns directly to verbs: علّمنا (taught us). This is fundamentally different from English where object pronouns are separate words. The suffix ـنا serves as both 'us' (object) and 'our' (possessive), with context disambiguating.

items.question: رأيت_____ في السوق (I saw him in the market)
items.explanation: The attached pronoun ـه (him) is the third person masculine singular object suffix. رأيتُه (I saw him) shows the pronoun attached directly after the verb's subject marker تُ (I). Arabic object pronouns follow a strict order when multiple suffixes attach: verb + subject marker + object pronoun, creating compact verb phrases.

items.question: الكتاب قرأت_____ بسرعة (The book — I read it quickly)
items.explanation: When a noun is fronted for emphasis (topicalization), Arabic uses a resumptive pronoun: الكتاب قرأتُه. The pronoun ـه refers back to الكتاب. This construction is called الاشتغال (preoccupation) where the verb is 'preoccupied' with its pronoun object while the actual noun sits in front for topic-comment structure.

items.question: أعطاني المعلم_____ (The teacher gave me it [the book])
items.explanation: Verbs taking two objects (like أعطى - to give) can attach two pronoun suffixes. أعطانيه = he gave + me + it. The indirect object (recipient) comes first, then the direct object. Arabic can stack multiple pronoun suffixes on a single verb, creating very compact expressions that would require several words in English.

items.question: هل سمعت_____ ؟ (Did you hear her?)
items.explanation: The feminine object pronoun ـها (her) attaches to the verb: سمعتَها (you heard her). Arabic strictly maintains gender distinction in all pronoun forms. The ها suffix serves for both 'her' (object) and 'her/its' (feminine possessive). Verb + pronoun combinations are the backbone of Arabic conversational fluency.

items.question: سأزور_____ غداً (I will visit them tomorrow)
items.explanation: The plural masculine object pronoun ـهم (them) attaches to future verbs: سأزورهم (I will visit them). In the future construction سـ + verb, pronouns attach to the verb itself, not to سـ. The ـهم suffix refers to masculine plural or mixed-gender groups, following Arabic's default masculine rule for mixed groups.

items.question: أخبرت_____ بالخبر (I told you [fem.] the news)
items.explanation: Arabic distinguishes gender in second person pronouns: ـكَ (you, masc.) vs. ـكِ (you, fem.). أخبرتُكِ (I told you [female]) shows the feminine address form. This gender distinction in 'you' pronouns has no English equivalent and must be consciously maintained in Arabic conversation based on the addressee's gender.

items.question: الأستاذ ساعد_____ كثيراً (The professor helped me a lot)
items.explanation: The first person singular object pronoun ـني (me) uses the form with ن to prevent confusion: ساعدني (he helped me). This ني form (with protective ن) is specific to verb objects, while the possessive form is just ي: كتابي (my book). This distinction between ـني (object) and ـي (possessive) is crucial for correct Arabic usage.

items.question: هل فهمت_____ ؟ (Did you [pl.] understand me?)
items.explanation: When a plural verb ending in و takes an object pronoun, a protective ن is inserted: فهمتموني (you all understood me). The breakdown is فهم + تم + و + ني. This ن insertion (called نون الوقاية - protective nun) prevents ambiguity between verb conjugation markers and pronoun suffixes in complex verb forms.

items.question: لا ت_____ (Don't [fem.] leave me)
items.explanation: In the feminine singular negative imperative, the verb form is تتركي + ني: لا تتركيني (don't leave me). The ي before ني marks the feminine 'you' conjugation. Combining mood markers, gender conjugation, and object pronouns on a single verb shows Arabic's impressive morphological density: four grammatical features in one word.

items.question: أحبّ_____ كثيراً (I love you [masc.] very much)
items.explanation: The masculine singular object 'you' ـكَ attaches directly: أحبُّكَ (I love you). The doubled consonant بّ maintains its full pronunciation even with the suffix. Arabic love expressions use direct object pronouns rather than prepositional phrases, unlike many languages. أحبّك is one of the most frequently used verb+pronoun combinations in Arabic.

items.question: الطبيب فحص_____ (The doctor examined them [fem.])
items.explanation: The feminine plural object pronoun ـهنّ (them, female) is used when referring to an all-female group: فحصهنّ. Arabic maintains the masculine/feminine distinction in all plural forms: هم (them, masc.), هنّ (them, fem.). This six-way pronoun distinction (masc/fem × sing/dual/plural) reflects Arabic's elaborate person-gender-number system.

items.question: سألت_____ سؤالاً (I asked you two a question)
items.explanation: Arabic has unique dual pronouns: ـكما (you two), ـهما (them two). سألتكما (I asked you both) uses the dual address form. The dual is a grammatical number category between singular and plural, exclusive to Arabic among major world languages. It applies to nouns, verbs, adjectives, and pronouns systematically.

items.question: إيّا_____ نعبد (You alone we worship)
items.explanation: The detached object pronoun إيّاكَ (You alone) is used for emphasis by fronting the object. This famous Quranic phrase إيّاكَ نعبد (You alone we worship) uses the emphatic separated form. Arabic has both attached (suffix) and detached (إيّا + suffix) object pronoun forms, with the detached form exclusively for emphasis and topicalization.

items.question: هل أُعجب_____ الفيلم؟ (Did the movie impress you [masc. pl.]?)
items.explanation: The masculine plural 'you' object suffix ـكم appears on verbs addressing groups: أعجبكم (impressed you all). In this sentence, الفيلم is the subject (the movie) and ـكم is the object (you all). Arabic verb أعجب follows the pattern where the thing causing admiration is the grammatical subject, opposite to English 'you liked the movie.'

name: Numbers & Counting (الأعداد)
description: Arabic number system and agreement rules

items.question: في الصف _____ طلاب (In the class there are five students)
items.explanation: Arabic numbers 3-10 show reverse gender agreement: they take the OPPOSITE gender of the counted noun. طلاب (students, masc.) requires the feminine number خمسة. This counter-intuitive rule is one of Arabic's most famous grammar challenges. The counted noun (تمييز) after 3-10 is always in genitive plural form.

items.question: اشتريت _____ كتب (I bought three books)
items.explanation: The number ثلاثة (three, feminine form) is used because كتب (books) is masculine (singular: كتاب). Numbers 3-10 take feminine form with masculine nouns and masculine form with feminine nouns. The counted noun كتب appears in genitive plural after the number, following the obligatory number-noun construction pattern.

items.question: في البيت _____ غرف (The house has four rooms)
items.explanation: The number أربع (four, masculine form) is used because غرف (rooms) is feminine (singular: غرفة). Since the counted noun is feminine, the number takes the masculine (unmarked) form. This reverse agreement system exists because original Arabic number words had inherent gender that was preserved despite seeming contradictory.

items.question: حضر _____ طالباً (Eleven students attended)
items.explanation: Numbers 11-19 are compound: أحدَ عشرَ (eleven) with masculine counted nouns (طالب). The first part أحد agrees in gender with the noun, and عشر also agrees. Both parts are مبني على الفتح (fixed on fatha). The counted noun after 11-99 appears as singular accusative (تمييز منصوب مفرد), unlike 3-10 which take plural genitive.

items.question: في المدرسة _____ معلمة (The school has twelve female teachers)
items.explanation: For twelve with feminine nouns, Arabic uses اثنتا عشرة. Both parts take feminine form to agree with معلمة. The number 12 is unique because both its parts agree directly (not inversely) with the counted noun's gender. اثنتا is dual feminine, and عشرة is the feminine عشر. This is an exception to the reverse agreement rule of 3-10.

items.question: _____ كتاباً في المكتبة (Twenty books in the library)
items.explanation: The tens (20, 30...90) in Arabic don't change for gender: عشرون (twenty) stays the same regardless of the noun's gender. These are called عقود (decades) and behave like sound masculine plurals, declining as عشرون (nominative) and عشرين (accusative/genitive). The counted noun is singular accusative: كتاباً.

items.question: استغرقت الرحلة _____ يوماً (The trip took thirty days)
items.explanation: After a verb, ثلاثين takes the accusative form (ending in ين instead of ون). The tens decline like sound masculine plurals: ثلاثون (nominative), ثلاثين (accusative/genitive). The counted noun يوماً is singular accusative (تمييز), not plural as you might expect. This singular after large numbers is a distinctive Arabic grammar rule.

items.question: في المكتبة _____ كتاباً (In the library there are a hundred books)
items.explanation: The number مئة (one hundred) is followed by the counted noun in genitive singular: مئة كتابٍ. Unlike 3-10 (genitive plural) and 11-99 (accusative singular), hundreds take genitive singular. This three-tier system (3-10: gen. plural, 11-99: acc. singular, 100+: gen. singular) is unique to Arabic counting grammar.

items.question: الكتاب يحتوي على _____ صفحة (The book contains two hundred pages)
items.explanation: Two hundred is expressed as a dual: مئتان (nominative) or مئتين (genitive/accusative). In إضافة with صفحة, it becomes مئتي (dropping the ن): مئتي صفحة. The dual in إضافة loses its final ن, a rule that applies to all duals and sound masculine plurals when they become the first term of a possessive construction.

items.question: _____ طالبٍ نجحوا (A thousand students passed)
items.explanation: The number ألف (thousand) is followed by genitive singular: ألف طالبٍ (a thousand students). Despite referring to many students, the counted noun is singular genitive. ألف behaves like مئة in this respect. Thousands pluralize irregularly: ألف, ألفان (2000), ثلاثة آلاف (3000), using the broken plural آلاف.

items.question: الفصل _____ من الكتاب (The third chapter of the book)
items.explanation: Ordinal numbers (الأعداد الترتيبية) follow the فاعِل pattern: ثالث (third), رابع (fourth), خامس (fifth). Unlike cardinal numbers, ordinals AGREE directly in gender with their noun: الفصل الثالث (masc.), الصفحة الثالثة (fem.). This direct agreement contrasts with the inverse agreement of cardinal numbers 3-10.

items.question: هذا هو اليوم _____ (This is the first day)
items.explanation: The ordinal 'first' in Arabic is الأوّل (masculine) and الأولى (feminine), derived from a different root than واحد (one). Arabic 'first' and 'second' are irregular ordinals that don't follow the فاعل pattern. الأول from the root أ-و-ل and الثاني from ث-ن-ي are memorized separately from the regular pattern.

items.question: عندي _____ أقلام (I have ten pens)
items.explanation: The number عشرة (ten, feminine form) is used with the masculine noun أقلام (pens, singular: قلم). Numbers 3-10 maintain reverse gender agreement: masculine noun → feminine number. The counted noun أقلام is genitive plural, completing the 3-10 counting pattern. Ten is the upper boundary of this reverse-agreement system.

items.question: عمري _____ سنة (I am twenty-five years old)
items.explanation: Compound numbers (21-99) put the unit before the ten: خمس وعشرون (five and twenty = twenty-five). The unit follows reverse agreement (خمس for feminine سنة), while the ten عشرون doesn't change for gender. The counted noun سنة is singular accusative after the full compound number. Arabic compound numbers read units-first, unlike English.

items.question: جاء _____ من الطلاب (Half of the students came)
items.explanation: Fractions in Arabic include نصف (half), ثلث (third), ربع (quarter), خمس (fifth). They follow the فُعل pattern for basic fractions. In إضافة with the counted noun: نصف الطلاب (half of the students). Arabic fractions function as regular nouns and take all the normal noun grammar: case, definiteness, and number agreement.

name: Colors & Defect Adjectives (الصفات على وزن أفعل)
description: Adjectives following the af'al pattern for colors and defects

items.question: اشتريت قميصاً _____ (I bought a red shirt)
items.explanation: Colors in Arabic follow the أفعل pattern for masculine: أحمر (red), أزرق (blue), أخضر (green). Since قميص (shirt) is masculine, the color takes the masculine أفعل form. These adjectives are called صفة مشبهة (resemblance adjective) and have a unique declension pattern called ممنوع من الصرف (diptote — no tanwin).

items.question: السيارة _____ جميلة (The white car is beautiful)
items.explanation: The feminine form of color adjectives follows the فعلاء pattern: بيضاء (white, fem.) from أبيض (white, masc.). السيارة is feminine, requiring feminine agreement. The فعلاء pattern for feminine colors is unique to this adjective class. Other examples: حمراء (red), زرقاء (blue), سوداء (black). Both أفعل and فعلاء are diptotes.

items.question: العيون _____ جذابة (Blue eyes are attractive)
items.explanation: When modifying a broken plural noun like عيون (eyes, plural of عين), color adjectives can take either feminine singular or broken plural form. الزرقاء (feminine singular) is used because broken plurals of non-human nouns are treated as feminine singular in grammar. This rule simplifies agreement with irregular plurals significantly.

items.question: الرجل _____ طويل (The bald man is tall)
items.explanation: Physical defects and traits follow the same أفعل/فعلاء pattern as colors. أصلع (bald, masc.) follows أفعل. This pattern covers: أعرج (lame), أعور (one-eyed), أحدب (hunchbacked). Arabic groups colors and physical characteristics together grammatically because they share the same morphological template and declension behavior.

items.question: الغرفة _____ (The room is dark/black)
items.explanation: The feminine color سوداء (black/dark) from أسود demonstrates the فعلاء pattern with the root س-و-د. Since غرفة is feminine, the color takes feminine form. The و in the root appears in the فعلاء form but is hidden in أفعل (أسود). Understanding how root letters interact with patterns is essential for Arabic morphological literacy.

items.question: لديه شعر _____ (He has black hair)
items.explanation: With masculine nouns like شعر (hair), the masculine أفعل form أسود is used. Hair in Arabic is grammatically masculine singular even when referring to all of one's hair. Color adjectives in the أفعل form are diptote (ممنوع من الصرف): they take no tanwin and their genitive marker is fatha instead of kasra.

items.question: الأشجار _____ في الربيع (The trees are green in spring)
items.explanation: The plural الأشجار (trees) takes feminine singular agreement because it is a broken plural of a non-human noun (شجرة). Therefore خضراء (green, fem. sing.) is correct. This rule — broken plurals of non-humans taking feminine singular agreement — is one of the most important agreement rules in Arabic grammar, simplifying what could be complex plural agreement.

items.question: اللون _____ يدل على الهدوء (The blue color indicates calmness)
items.explanation: With the masculine noun لون (color), the masculine form الأزرق (blue) is used. Arabic has rich vocabulary for color symbolism: أزرق for calmness, أبيض for purity, أخضر for growth, أسود for strength or grief. The definite article ال on both noun and adjective shows the standard attributive adjective construction requiring matching definiteness.

items.question: المرأة _____ تحتاج مساعدة (The blind woman needs help)
items.explanation: The feminine of أعمى (blind) is عمياء, following the فعلاء pattern. Physical defect adjectives like أعمى/عمياء (blind), أصم/صمّاء (deaf), أبكم/بكماء (mute) follow identical morphological patterns to colors. Arabic grammar treats these attributive categories as a unified class called باب أفعل الصفة.

items.question: الجدران _____ في المنزل (The walls are yellow in the house)
items.explanation: الجدران (walls, plural of جدار) takes feminine singular agreement as a broken plural of a non-human noun, requiring صفراء (yellow, fem.). The Arabic color system covers: أبيض (white), أسود (black), أحمر (red), أزرق (blue), أخضر (green), أصفر (yellow), each with its masculine, feminine, and plural forms.

items.question: يلبس نظارات _____  (He wears dark glasses)
items.explanation: نظارات (glasses) is a sound feminine plural, taking feminine agreement: سوداء. While sound feminine plurals normally take feminine plural adjective forms, color adjectives in the أفعل/فعلاء pattern have no regular feminine plural. Instead, the feminine singular فعلاء or the broken plural فُعل are used for plural agreement.

items.question: الكتب _____ على الرف (The brown books are on the shelf)
items.explanation: Some modern colors borrowed from other languages don't follow the أفعل/فعلاء pattern. بُني (brown) follows regular adjective agreement: بُنية (fem.), بُنيون (masc. pl.). These non-pattern colors include بُني (brown), بُرتقالي (orange), وردي (pink), بنفسجي (purple). They are called ألوان غير قياسية (non-standard colors).

items.question: السماء _____ اليوم (The sky is gray today)
items.explanation: رمادي (gray) is another non-pattern color that follows regular adjective declension: رمادية (fem.). Modern Arabic has expanded its color vocabulary beyond the classical أفعل/فعلاء system. These newer color words decline like regular adjectives with full case marking and tanwin, distinguishing them from the classical diptote color adjectives.

items.question: الولد _____ لا يسمع جيداً (The deaf boy doesn't hear well)
items.explanation: The physical defect adjective أصم (deaf, masc.) follows the أفعل pattern. For the masculine noun ولد (boy), the masculine form is required. The full paradigm: أصم (masc. sing.), صمّاء (fem. sing.), صُمّ (plural). Physical defect adjectives have important social sensitivity in modern Arabic, with euphemistic alternatives increasingly preferred.

items.question: اشترت فستاناً _____ للحفلة (She bought a green dress for the party)
items.explanation: فستان (dress) is grammatically masculine in Arabic despite being women's clothing, requiring the masculine color أخضر (green). Grammatical gender in Arabic is determined by the word's form, not the object's association. This distinction between grammatical gender and natural gender catches many learners off guard. The indefinite أخضر takes no tanwin (diptote).

name: Word Order (ترتيب الجملة)
description: Flexible word order patterns in Arabic sentences

items.question: _____ الولد التفاحة (The boy ate the apple)
items.explanation: The default Arabic sentence order is VSO (Verb-Subject-Object): أكل الولد التفاحة. This verb-first structure is called الجملة الفعلية (verbal sentence). Arabic strongly prefers verb-initial order in narrative and descriptive text, distinguishing it from English's rigid SVO pattern. The verb carries tense and person information.

items.question: الطالب _____ الدرس (The student understood the lesson)
items.explanation: When the subject comes first, it creates a جملة اسمية (nominal sentence) with a verbal predicate: الطالب فهمَ. The fronted subject الطالب becomes the مبتدأ (topic) and the verbal clause فهم الدرس becomes the خبر (comment). This SVO order emphasizes the subject and is common in conversation and contrastive contexts.

items.question: في المكتبة _____ كثيرة (In the library there are many books)
items.explanation: Prepositional phrases can front the sentence for emphasis or as setting: في المكتبة كتبٌ كثيرة. Here في المكتبة is a fronted خبر (predicate) and كتبٌ is a delayed مبتدأ (subject). This inverted structure is natural in Arabic for introducing new information or establishing location before revealing what exists there.

items.question: التفاحةَ _____ الولد (The apple, the boy ate [it])
items.explanation: Fronting the object (OSV) for emphasis requires a resumptive pronoun: التفاحةَ أكلها الولد. The pronoun ها on the verb refers back to التفاحة. This construction is called الاشتغال and is used when the speaker wants to emphasize what was acted upon. The fronted object takes accusative case (فتحة).

items.question: _____ الولدُ والبنتُ (The boy and the girl came)
items.explanation: When the verb precedes a compound subject, it agrees with the FIRST element only: جاء الولدُ والبنتُ. The verb جاء is masculine singular matching الولد. This is a key VSO rule in Arabic: pre-subject verbs take singular agreement regardless of how many subjects follow. Post-subject verbs would take dual/plural agreement.

items.question: هنا _____ أبي (Here my father works)
items.explanation: Fronting the adverb هنا (here) creates emphasis on location: هنا يعمل أبي. Arabic allows flexible adverb placement: initial (هنا يعمل), medial (يعمل هنا أبي), or final (يعمل أبي هنا), each with different pragmatic effects. Initial placement highlights the location as the most important information in the sentence.

items.question: الكتاب الذي _____ ممتع (The book that I read is enjoyable)
items.explanation: In relative clauses with الذي (which/that), a resumptive pronoun is required on the verb: الكتاب الذي قرأتُه (the book which I read-it). Arabic relative clauses always require this pronoun reference back to the head noun, unlike English where 'which I read' works without 'it.' This is obligatory, not optional.

items.question: ما _____ إلا الحقيقة (I said nothing but the truth)
items.explanation: The exceptive construction ما...إلا (nothing but/only) uses standard VSO order within a negated clause: ما قلتُ إلا الحقيقة.
