items.explanation: Form VI verb تَفاوَتَ means 'to vary/fluctuate,' suggesting mutual difference among items. Past tense feminine: تَفاوَتَت (with الأسعار — broken plural → feminine). Form VI here captures the idea that prices differ from each other — a reciprocal comparison. Economic and financial Arabic regularly uses Form VI verbs for describing market dynamics where multiple factors interact.

items.question: الجيران _____ الهدايا في العيد (The neighbors exchanged gifts during the holiday)
items.explanation: Form VI verb تَهادى means 'to exchange gifts,' from هَدِيَّة (gift). Past tense plural: تَهادَوا (weak final radical appears as واو in plural). Gift exchange is a reciprocal action par excellence — each person gives and receives. Arabic cultural traditions of exchanging gifts during Eid and other occasions make this Form VI verb culturally significant and frequently used.

items.question: المتهمان _____ التهم أمام القاضي (The two accused threw accusations at each other before the judge)
items.explanation: Form VI verb تَشاتَمَ means 'to exchange insults,' from شَتَمَ (to insult). Past tense dual: تَشاتَما (alif ending for dual subject — two accused). The dual form is grammatically required when exactly two subjects perform the action. Form VI captures mutual verbal attacks. Arabic grammar's precise dual number combined with Form VI's reciprocal meaning creates an exact description of the courtroom scene.

items.question: الحضارات القديمة _____ عبر طريق الحرير (Ancient civilizations interacted via the Silk Road)
items.explanation: Form VI verb تَواصَلَ means 'to communicate/interact with each other,' from واصَلَ (Form III: to continue/connect with). Past tense feminine: تَواصَلَت. The masdar تَواصُل (communication/networking) has become incredibly important in modern Arabic, especially in technology (التَواصُل الاجتماعي = social media/networking). This shows how classical Forms serve modern concepts.

items.question: _____ الطلاب مع المعلم حول المشروع (The students consulted with the teacher about the project)
items.explanation: Form VI verb تَشاوَرَ means 'to consult with each other,' from شاوَرَ (Form III: to consult). Past tense: تَشاوَرَ. Consultation (شورى/تَشاوُر) is a fundamental concept in Arabic-Islamic culture and governance. The Quranic concept of شورى (consultation) comes from the same root. Form VI emphasizes that consultation is a mutual process — all parties contribute their views and listen to others.

name: Verb Form VII (اِنْفَعَلَ)
description: Passive/reflexive meaning expressing submission to an action

items.question: الباب _____ بسبب الرياح القوية (The door opened because of the strong wind)
items.explanation: Form VII (اِنفَعَلَ) creates the passive/reflexive meaning of Form I. فَتَحَ (to open something) → اِنفَتَحَ (to become opened/open by itself). The prefix اِنـ indicates that the action happened to the subject without an external agent — the door opened on its own due to wind. Form VII cannot take a direct object, making it inherently intransitive. This is a fundamental pattern for describing spontaneous events.

items.question: الزجاج _____ عندما سقط على الأرض (The glass broke when it fell on the ground)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنكَسَرَ means 'to break (intransitive)/become broken,' from كَسَرَ (to break something). This is the classic Form VII example: the glass broke on its own — no agent broke it. The اِنـ prefix removes the agent from the action. In Arabic, using Form VII instead of passive Form I (كُسِرَ) emphasizes the spontaneous nature of the event rather than an implied agent.

items.question: الضوء _____ في الغرفة فجأة (The light went out in the room suddenly)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنطَفَأَ means 'to go out/be extinguished,' from طَفَأَ (to extinguish). Past tense: اِنطَفَأَ. The hamza at the end is maintained. Form VII is particularly common with verbs describing physical changes of state — things breaking, opening, closing, turning off. The light went out on its own (perhaps a power cut), emphasizing the absence of human agency.

items.question: الجنود _____ من المعركة (The soldiers withdrew from the battle)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنسَحَبَ means 'to withdraw/retreat,' from سَحَبَ (to pull/drag). Past tense plural: اِنسَحَبوا. The meaning shift: 'to pull' → 'to pull oneself away' = 'to withdraw.' Form VII creates reflexive meanings where the subject performs the action on itself. Military vocabulary extensively uses this verb. The masdar اِنسِحاب (withdrawal) follows the Form VII masdar pattern اِنفِعال.

items.question: الطعام _____ بسرعة في هذا الجو الحار (The food spoiled quickly in this hot weather)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنفَسَدَ means 'to become spoiled/corrupted,' from فَسَدَ (to be corrupt). The Form VII adds the nuance of spontaneous change — the food spoiled on its own due to conditions, not because someone deliberately ruined it. This distinction between deliberate and spontaneous action is one of the most important contributions Form VII makes to Arabic's expressive precision.

items.question: الحبل _____ تحت الضغط الشديد (The rope snapped under severe pressure)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنقَطَعَ means 'to be cut/snap (by itself),' from قَطَعَ (to cut). Past tense: اِنقَطَعَ. The rope wasn't cut by someone — it snapped due to pressure. Form VII is perfect for describing mechanical/physical failures and natural breakdowns. The masdar اِنقِطاع (interruption/disconnection) is common in modern Arabic for power outages, communication breakdowns, etc.

items.question: المطر _____ في المساء (The rain stopped in the evening)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنقَطَعَ also means 'to cease/stop' — rain stopping naturally without human intervention. The same Form VII verb can have multiple contextual meanings: اِنقَطَعَ الحبل (the rope snapped) vs. اِنقَطَعَ المطر (the rain stopped). What unites them is the concept of interruption/cessation happening spontaneously. Context determines the specific English translation while the Arabic Form carries a consistent core meaning.

items.question: الثلج _____ في فصل الربيع (The snow melted in spring)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنصَهَرَ means 'to melt (by itself)/fuse,' from صَهَرَ (to melt something). The snow melted naturally due to seasonal warming — no agent melted it. While ذابَ (Form I) also means 'to melt,' اِنصَهَرَ specifically emphasizes the process of melting as a transformation, often used for metals fusing. Form VII captures the scientific/natural process of state change.

items.question: الأطفال _____ في اللعب ولم يسمعوا أمهم (The children were absorbed in play and didn't hear their mother)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنهَمَكَ means 'to be engrossed/absorbed in,' conveying complete immersion in an activity. Past tense plural: اِنهَمَكوا. Form VII here creates a reflexive meaning — the children plunged themselves into play. This psychological/emotional use of Form VII extends beyond physical actions to mental states, showing the form's versatility in describing both physical and psychological experiences.

items.question: السفينة _____ في البحر (The ship sank in the sea)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنغَمَرَ means 'to be submerged/sunk,' from غَمَرَ (to flood/submerge). Past tense feminine: اِنغَمَرَت (agreeing with السفينة). While غَرِقَ also means 'to drown/sink,' اِنغَمَرَ emphasizes the submersion process. Form VII verbs typically cannot begin a sentence in Arabic — they require their subject to precede them or follow them, but don't appear in sentence-initial position.

items.question: الباب _____ بهدوء خلف الضيوف (The door closed quietly behind the guests)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنغَلَقَ means 'to close by itself,' from غَلَقَ (to close/lock). Past tense: اِنغَلَقَ. Compare: أَغلَقَ (Form IV: someone closed it) vs. اِنغَلَقَ (Form VII: it closed on its own). This pair beautifully illustrates the causative (IV) vs. reflexive (VII) contrast. The door closed by itself — perhaps a self-closing mechanism or wind — with no human agent specified.

items.question: الثوب _____ بعد الغسيل (The fabric shrank after washing)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنكَمَشَ means 'to shrink/contract,' from كَمَشَ (to grapple/clutch). Past tense: اِنكَمَشَ. The fabric shrank on its own as a result of washing — a spontaneous physical change. The masdar اِنكِماش (contraction/shrinkage) is used in economics (economic contraction/recession) as well as in physical sciences, demonstrating Form VII's versatility across domains.

items.question: جزء من المبنى _____ بسبب الزلزال (Part of the building collapsed because of the earthquake)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنهارَ means 'to collapse/crumble,' from هارَ (to demolish). Past tense: اِنهارَ (hollow root with alif). The building collapsed due to natural forces — the earthquake, not deliberate demolition. The masdar اِنهِيار (collapse) is used for buildings, economies, and even emotional breakdowns (اِنهِيار عَصَبي = nervous breakdown). Form VII with hollow roots shows vowel adjustments in the middle radical.

items.question: الحشرات _____ إلى الضوء في الليل (The insects were attracted to the light at night)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنجَذَبَ means 'to be attracted/drawn toward,' from جَذَبَ (to pull/attract). Past tense feminine: اِنجَذَبَت (with الحشرات — broken plural → feminine). The insects were passively attracted — the light drew them. Form VII perfectly captures this involuntary attraction. The verb is also used figuratively: اِنجَذَبَ إلى الفكرة (was attracted to the idea), extending from physical to intellectual attraction.

items.question: الجليد _____ تحت أقدام المتزلجين (The ice cracked under the skaters' feet)
items.explanation: Form VII verb اِنشَقَّ means 'to split/crack open,' from شَقَّ (to split). Past tense: اِنشَقَّ (doubled root letter with shadda). The ice cracked on its own under pressure — no one deliberately split it. Form VII with doubled (geminate) roots: the second and third radicals are the same letter, spelled with shadda. The masdar اِنشِقاق (splitting/schism) is used politically for party splits and ideological divisions.

name: Verb Form VIII (اِفْتَعَلَ)
description: Reflexive with infixed -ta- expressing self-directed action

items.question: _____ الطالب لامتحان اللغة العربية (The student prepared for the Arabic language exam)
items.explanation: Form VIII (اِفتَعَلَ) often expresses reflexive actions or effort. اِستَعَدَّ means 'to prepare oneself,' from the root ع-د-د (readiness). The تـ infix after the first radical is Form VIII's hallmark. However, note: اِستَعَدَّ is actually Form X (اِستَفعَلَ — seeking readiness). A true Form VIII example with this root would be اِعتَدَّ. This distinction between Forms VIII and X is crucial for accurate verb identification.

items.question: الشعب _____ على القرار الظالم (The people protested against the unjust decision)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِحتَجَّ means 'to protest/object,' from حَجَّ (to argue/make pilgrimage). Past tense: اِحتَجَّ (doubled final radical with shadda). The تـ infix creates an intensive reflexive meaning — the people made a strong argument against something. The masdar اِحتِجاج (protest) is one of the most common words in Arabic news media. Form VIII with doubled roots requires careful pronunciation of the shadda.

items.question: المسافر _____ عن التأخير (The traveler apologized for the delay)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِعتَذَرَ means 'to apologize,' from عَذَرَ (to excuse). The reflexive meaning: 'to excuse oneself' = 'to apologize.' Past tense follows اِفتَعَلَ with the characteristic تـ infix. The masdar اِعتِذار (apology) follows the Form VIII masdar pattern اِفتِعال. This verb is essential for polite Arabic conversation and formal communication. Apology formulas are important cultural expressions in Arabic-speaking societies.

items.question: الشركة _____ موظفين جدداً (The company hired new employees)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِختارَ means 'to choose/select,' from خارَ/خَيَرَ (to be good). Past tense feminine: اِختارَت. With hollow roots (middle radical is واو or ياء), Form VIII shows vowel adjustments — the ياء appears as alif in past tense. The masdar اِختِيار (choice/selection) is extremely common. Form VIII hollow verbs are important to master because many high-frequency verbs follow this pattern.

items.question: _____ الخبر في جميع أنحاء المدينة (The news spread throughout the city)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِنتَشَرَ means 'to spread (intransitive),' from نَشَرَ (to publish/spread). Past tense: اِنتَشَرَ. Note: when the first radical is ن, the Form VIII تـ infix assimilates — we might expect اِنْتَشَرَ but it's written normally. The news spread on its own — Form VIII here suggests spontaneous spreading. The masdar اِنتِشار (spread/proliferation) is used in medical, media, and social contexts.

items.question: العالم _____ أن النظرية صحيحة (The scientist believed that the theory was correct)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِعتَقَدَ means 'to believe/hold a conviction,' from عَقَدَ (to tie/knot). The metaphorical extension: 'to tie a knot in one's heart/mind' = 'to hold a firm belief.' Past tense: اِعتَقَدَ. The masdar اِعتِقاد (belief/conviction) and the related noun عَقيدة (creed/doctrine) share the root ع-ق-د. Form VIII often creates verbs expressing mental states and internal processes.

items.question: المتسابقون _____ في سباق الماراثون (The contestants participated in the marathon race)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِشتَرَكَ means 'to participate/subscribe,' from شَرَكَ (to share). Past tense plural: اِشتَرَكوا. The reflexive meaning: 'to share oneself in' = 'to participate.' The masdar اِشتِراك (subscription/participation) is used for magazine subscriptions, event participation, and shared services. Form VIII verbs often express voluntary self-involvement in activities.

items.question: الشرطة _____ اللص أمس (The police arrested the thief yesterday)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِعتَقَلَ means 'to arrest/detain,' from عَقَلَ (to bind/restrain). Past tense feminine: اِعتَقَلَت (agreeing with الشرطة — feminine noun). The meaning extension: 'to bind/restrain someone' → 'to arrest.' The masdar اِعتِقال (arrest/detention) is among the most common legal terms in Arabic news. Form VIII frequently creates verbs used in official, legal, and administrative contexts.

items.question: _____ بأخلاقك العالية (I am proud of your high morals)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِفتَخَرَ means 'to be proud/boast,' from فَخَرَ (to boast). Present tense first person: أَفتَخِرُ. Note: the hamza of the اِنـ prefix drops in present tense, replaced by the subject prefix (أَـ). Form VIII present tense pattern: يَفتَعِلُ → أَفتَعِلُ for first person. This is a common pattern across all Form VIII verbs and must be internalized for fluent conjugation.

items.question: الاجتماع _____ الساعة العاشرة صباحاً (The meeting started at ten o'clock in the morning)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِبتَدَأَ means 'to begin/start,' from بَدَأَ (to begin). Both Form I and Form VIII carry similar meanings, but Form VIII adds a nuance of initiative or formal commencement. Past tense: اِبتَدَأَ (hamza-final root). The masdar اِبتِداء (beginning/commencement) is used in formal phrases like مِن الاِبتِداء (from the beginning). The term المدرسة الاِبتِدائية (primary school) derives from this root.

items.question: العميل _____ على ارتفاع الأسعار (The customer complained about the high prices)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِشتَكى means 'to complain,' from شَكا (to complain). With weak final radical (واو/ياء), past tense ends in alif maqṣūra: اِشتَكى. Form VIII with defective roots requires attention to the final vowel changes across conjugations. While شَكا (Form I) also means 'to complain,' اِشتَكى often implies a more formal or intense complaint, especially in legal contexts (filing a complaint).

items.question: الأمم المتحدة _____ بحقوق الإنسان (The United Nations is concerned with human rights)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِهتَمَّ means 'to be interested in/care about,' from هَمَّ (to concern/worry). Present tense third person feminine: تَهتَمُّ (with shadda on final radical). The reflexive meaning: 'to concern oneself with.' The masdar اِهتِمام (interest/concern/attention) is one of the most frequently used abstract nouns in Arabic. Form VIII with doubled roots shows the shadda clearly in all conjugations.

items.question: الكاتب _____ من قصص الأطفال (The writer drew inspiration from children's stories)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِقتَبَسَ means 'to quote/draw inspiration from,' from قَبَسَ (to take a firebrand/ember). The metaphorical extension: 'to take a spark of knowledge/inspiration.' Past tense: اِقتَبَسَ. The masdar اِقتِباس means 'quotation/citation' — essential in academic and literary Arabic. This verb beautifully illustrates how Arabic extends concrete meanings (taking fire) to abstract ones (taking knowledge) through verb forms.

items.question: الحكومة _____ ضرائب جديدة على المواطنين (The government imposed new taxes on the citizens)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِفتَرَضَ means 'to assume/suppose/impose,' from فَرَضَ (to impose/obligate). Past tense feminine: اِفتَرَضَت. While فَرَضَ (Form I) means 'to impose directly,' اِفتَرَضَ (Form VIII) can mean both 'to impose' and 'to assume/suppose.' The masdar اِفتِراض (assumption/hypothesis) is critical in academic and scientific Arabic. This dual meaning requires context to disambiguate.

items.question: الطفل _____ وراء الشجرة أثناء اللعب (The child hid behind the tree during play)
items.explanation: Form VIII verb اِختَبَأَ means 'to hide oneself,' from خَبَأَ (to hide something). The reflexive meaning is clear: 'to hide something' → 'to hide oneself.' Past tense: اِختَبَأَ (hamza-final root). Form VIII reflexive verbs are among the most intuitive to understand because the self-directed meaning is transparent. The child actively hid himself — Form VIII shows voluntary self-action, distinguishing it from Form VII's spontaneous/involuntary character.

name: Verb Form IX (اِفْعَلَّ)
description: Colors and physical characteristics with doubled final radical

items.question: _____ وجه الطفل عندما شعر بالخجل (The child's face reddened when he felt shy)
items.explanation: Form IX (اِفعَلَّ) is uniquely used for colors and physical defects in Arabic. اِحمَرَّ means 'to become red/to redden,' derived from the root ح-م-ر (redness). This form is distinguished by the doubling (shadda) on the final radical and the initial hamza with kasra. It describes an involuntary change in color or physical state, making it one of the most specialized verb forms in Arabic morphology.

items.question: _____ الورق في فصل الخريف (The leaves yellowed in autumn)
items.explanation: اِصفَرَّ is Form IX from the root ص-ف-ر (yellowness). Form IX verbs are intransitive and describe acquiring a color or physical characteristic. Unlike most other verb forms, Form IX has a very narrow semantic range — limited almost exclusively to colors and defects. This makes it one of the easiest forms to identify, though it is relatively rare in everyday conversation.

items.question: _____ شعر جدي بسبب كبر سنه (My grandfather's hair turned white because of his old age)
items.explanation: اِبيَضَّ means 'to become white/to whiten,' from the root ب-ي-ض. This Form IX verb demonstrates the pattern اِفعَلَّ perfectly. Notice how the weak middle radical (ي) appears in its full form. Form IX is always intransitive — you cannot 'whiten something' using this form. To express a transitive meaning, you would use Form II (بَيَّضَ — to whiten/bleach something).

items.question: _____ السماء قبل هبوب العاصفة (The sky darkened before the storm hit)
items.explanation: اِسوَدَّ means 'to become black/dark,' from the root س-و-د. This Form IX verb follows the pattern اِفعَلَّ with the weak middle radical و appearing clearly. The shadda on the final دّ is characteristic of Form IX. Arabic uses color verbs metaphorically too — اِسوَدَّ وجهه (his face blackened) means he was humiliated, a common idiomatic expression.

items.question: _____ العشب بعد سقوط المطر (The grass turned green after the rain fell)
items.explanation: اِخضَرَّ means 'to become green,' from the root خ-ض-ر. Form IX verbs like this describe the natural process of acquiring a color. Notice that Arabic distinguishes between the adjective أخضر (green — Form IV pattern for adjectives of color) and the verb اِخضَرَّ (to become green — Form IX). This verb-adjective relationship between Forms IV and IX is a key morphological pattern.

items.question: _____ لون البحر عند الغروب (The color of the sea became blue at sunset)
items.explanation: اِزرَقَّ means 'to become blue,' from the root ز-ر-ق. Form IX verbs are morphologically distinctive because they always have a doubled final consonant (shadda) and begin with اِ (hamzat al-wasl with kasra). In modern Arabic, Form IX is relatively uncommon in active speech — speakers often prefer the phrase صار أزرق (became blue) instead, but the form remains important in literary and Classical Arabic.

items.question: _____ الجرح بعد عدة أيام (The wound reddened after several days)
items.explanation: Here اِحمَرَّ is used in a medical context, showing that Form IX extends beyond simple color description to physical states involving color change. The verb describes the reddening of a wound — a physiological process. This usage highlights how Form IX bridges the gap between pure color terminology and descriptions of physical conditions, making it valuable in scientific and medical Arabic texts.

items.question: _____ الحديد بعد تعرضه للماء والهواء (The iron rusted after being exposed to water and air)
items.explanation: اِصدَأَّ means 'to rust/become rusty,' from the root ص-د-أ. This Form IX verb demonstrates the form's secondary usage beyond pure colors — it also covers physical defects and deterioration. The hamza (أ) at the end creates a slightly irregular conjugation pattern. Form IX verbs of defect describe involuntary physical changes, whether in living beings or inanimate objects.

items.question: _____ وجهها _____ عندما سمعت الخبر السعيد (Her face brightened when she heard the happy news)
items.explanation: While not strictly a color verb, اِشرَقَّ (to brighten/radiate) follows the Form IX pattern and describes a visual change in appearance. From the root ش-ر-ق (east/sunrise/radiance), this verb extends Form IX's meaning from literal color to luminosity. It's often used figuratively — وجهه اِشرَقَّ (his face lit up) describes happiness. This shows how Form IX, though narrow, has some metaphorical flexibility.

items.question: لم يكن الحائط أبيض بل _____ بسبب الرطوبة (The wall wasn't white but rather turned yellow due to humidity)
items.explanation: This sentence demonstrates Form IX in a contrastive construction using بل (but rather). اِصفَرَّ describes the gradual yellowing of a wall — showing Form IX's use for inanimate objects undergoing color change. The negation لم with the jussive يكن sets up the contrast. This pattern (negation + بل + Form IX) is common in descriptive Arabic prose.

items.question: _____ الأوراق ثم سقطت من الشجرة (The leaves turned brown then fell from the tree)
items.explanation: اِسمَرَّ means 'to become brown/tanned,' from the root س-م-ر. This Form IX verb describes a natural process — leaves browning before they fall. The sequential construction using ثم (then) indicates two consecutive events. Form IX is essential in nature descriptions because it captures involuntary color transformations that English typically expresses with 'turn + color' or 'become + color.'

items.question: كان الطقس حارًا جدًا فـ_____ جلده (The weather was very hot so his skin tanned/darkened)
items.explanation: Here اِسمَرَّ describes sun tanning, from the root س-م-ر (brownness). The فـ prefix (fa-) indicates consequence — 'so/therefore.' This sentence shows Form IX in a cause-and-effect structure: حار جدًا (very hot) → اِسمَرَّ (tanned). Note how Arabic uses a single Form IX verb where English needs a multi-word phrase ('got a tan' or 'his skin darkened'), demonstrating Arabic's morphological efficiency.

items.question: بعد المرض _____ لونه وأصبح شاحبًا (After the illness his color paled and he became pallid)
items.explanation: In Arabic, pallor is described as 'yellowing' (اِصفَرَّ) rather than 'paling' as in English. This cultural-linguistic difference is important for learners. The coordinating conjunction و connects two descriptions of the same state, and أصبح (became) adds a synonym. Form IX here serves a medical/descriptive function, showing how Arabic color verbs carry different cultural associations than their English counterparts.

items.question: عند الفجر _____ الأفق ثم بدأ النهار (At dawn the horizon reddened then the day began)
items.explanation: This poetic usage of اِحمَرَّ describes the red sky at dawn. Form IX verbs frequently appear in literary and descriptive Arabic, particularly in nature writing and poetry. The temporal phrase عند الفجر (at dawn) sets the scene, and ثم (then) provides sequence. Arabic literature extensively uses Form IX for atmospheric and landscape description, making this form essential for reading classical and modern Arabic prose.

items.question: _____ عينه اليسرى بسبب الحساسية (His left eye reddened because of allergies)
items.explanation: This item demonstrates Form IX with the feminine past tense suffix ـت because عين (eye) is feminine in Arabic. The verb اِحمَرَّت shows how Form IX conjugates — the shadda on the final radical is maintained, and the feminine marker is simply appended. The causal phrase بسبب (because of) introduces the reason. Remembering noun gender agreement with Form IX verbs is crucial for correct conjugation.

name: Verb Form X (اِسْتَفْعَلَ)
description: Seeking or considering — request and deeming meanings

items.question: _____ المعلم من الطلاب أن ينتبهوا (The teacher requested from the students to pay attention)
items.explanation: Form X (اِستَفعَلَ) typically conveys seeking, requesting, or considering something to have a quality. While طَلَبَ (Form I) means simply 'to request,' اِستَطلَبَ emphasizes the act of seeking or soliciting. The prefix اِستـ is the hallmark of Form X. This form is one of the most productive in Arabic, generating hundreds of commonly used verbs from basic roots.

items.question: _____ الباحث المعلومات من مصادر مختلفة (The researcher extracted information from different sources)
items.explanation: اِستَخرَجَ means 'to extract/derive,' from the root خ-ر-ج (going out). Compare the three related verbs: خَرَجَ (Form I — to go out), أخرَجَ (Form IV — to take out/produce), اِستَخرَجَ (Form X — to extract/derive). Form X adds the nuance of deliberate effort to obtain something. This three-way comparison illustrates how Arabic verb forms create systematic semantic relationships from a single root.

items.question: هل _____ الضيوف رأيك في الموضوع؟ (Did the guests ask for your opinion on the topic?)
items.explanation: اِستَشارَ means 'to consult/seek counsel,' from the root ش-و-ر (consultation). This is a hollow Form X verb (with weak middle radical و that disappears in conjugation). Compare: أشارَ (Form IV — to indicate/point), شاوَرَ (Form III — to consult mutually), اِستَشارَ (Form X — to seek advice). The form perfectly illustrates Form X's 'seeking' meaning: literally 'to seek شورى (consultation).'

items.question: _____ العامل إذنًا للخروج مبكرًا (The worker asked permission to leave early)
items.explanation: اِستَأذَنَ means 'to ask permission,' from the root أ-ذ-ن (permission). This Form X verb literally means 'to seek إذن (permission).' The hamza at the beginning of the root creates a double hamza situation (اِستَأ), which is a common spelling challenge. Compare: أذِنَ (to permit), اِستَأذَنَ (to seek permission). This clearly shows Form X's requestive function.

items.question: _____ الطبيب أن يرتاح المريض في البيت (The doctor deemed it preferable that the patient rest at home)
items.explanation: اِستَحسَنَ means 'to consider good/approve,' from the root ح-س-ن (goodness). This illustrates Form X's second major meaning: deeming/considering something to have a quality. Compare the four forms: حَسُنَ (I — to be good), حَسَّنَ (II — to improve), أحسَنَ (IV — to do well), اِستَحسَنَ (X — to find good/approve). Form X adds a subjective judgment dimension.

items.question: _____ الجيش للمعركة القادمة (The army prepared for the upcoming battle)
items.explanation: اِستَعَدَّ means 'to prepare oneself,' from the root ع-د-د (readiness/counting). This is one of the most common Form X verbs in modern Arabic. Note the doubled final radical (shadda on دّ). Compare: عَدَّ (I — to count), أعَدَّ (IV — to prepare something), اِستَعَدَّ (X — to prepare oneself). Form X here adds reflexivity — the subject prepares themselves rather than preparing something else.

items.question: _____ المدير الموظف الجديد في القسم (The manager employed the new employee in the department)
items.explanation: اِستَخدَمَ means 'to employ/use,' from the root خ-د-م (service). This extremely common Form X verb literally means 'to seek service from.' In modern Arabic, it's used for both employing people and using objects/tools. The semantic shift from 'seeking service' to 'using' shows how Form X meanings evolve over time while maintaining their core concept.

items.question: _____ الشعب _____ من الحكومة تغيير السياسة (The people demanded from the government a policy change)
items.explanation: اِستَلزَمَ means 'to necessitate/demand,' from the root ل-ز-م (necessity). Compare: لَزِمَ (I — to be necessary), ألزَمَ (IV — to obligate someone), اِستَلزَمَ (X — to necessitate/require). Form X here carries the meaning of 'seeking necessity' — demanding that something become obligatory. This verb is extremely common in formal and academic Arabic discourse.

items.question: _____ الطفل أمه عندما ضاع في السوق (The child called for help from his mother when he got lost in the market)
items.explanation: اِستَغاثَ means 'to call for help/seek rescue,' from the root غ-و-ث (rescue). This hollow Form X verb (with weak و) shows the 'seeking' meaning clearly: literally 'to seek غَوث (rescue).' Compare: أغاثَ (IV — to rescue someone), اِستَغاثَ (X — to call for rescue). The hollow root makes the conjugation irregular — the و disappears and an alif appears instead.

items.question: _____ العالم أن النظرية صحيحة بعد التجارب (The scientist concluded that the theory was correct after the experiments)
items.explanation: اِستَنتَجَ means 'to conclude/deduce,' from the root ن-ت-ج (production/result). This Form X verb illustrates the meaning of 'seeking to arrive at a result' — hence 'to deduce.' Compare: نَتَجَ (I — to result), أنتَجَ (IV — to produce), اِستَنتَجَ (X — to deduce). This is essential vocabulary in academic and scientific Arabic, appearing frequently in research papers and intellectual discourse.

items.question: _____ المسافر عن موعد الرحلة في المطار (The traveler inquired about the flight time at the airport)
items.explanation: اِستَفسَرَ means 'to inquire/ask for clarification,' from the root ف-س-ر (explanation). Form X here means 'to seek تفسير (explanation).' This verb is commonly used in formal contexts — at offices, airports, and institutions. Compare: فَسَّرَ (II — to explain/interpret), اِستَفسَرَ (X — to seek explanation). The difference between asking (سأل) and formally inquiring (اِستَفسَرَ) reflects Arabic's register sensitivity.

items.question: _____ الشركة عمالًا جددًا للمشروع الكبير (The company recruited new workers for the large project)
items.explanation: اِستَقطَبَ means 'to attract/recruit/polarize,' from the root ق-ط-ب (pole/axis). This Form X verb literally means 'to seek to draw toward a pole.' In modern usage, it has acquired the meanings of recruiting (employees), attracting (investment), and polarizing (opinions). This semantic extension from the classical 'seeking' meaning to modern business/political usage shows how Form X remains productive in contemporary Arabic.

items.question: _____ القاضي شهادة الشهود قبل إصدار الحكم (The judge heard the witnesses' testimony before issuing the verdict)
items.explanation: اِستَمَعَ means 'to listen attentively,' from the root س-م-ع (hearing). This is a crucial distinction in Arabic: سَمِعَ (I — to hear, involuntary) vs. اِستَمَعَ (X — to listen carefully, deliberate). Form X here adds intentionality — 'seeking to hear' becomes 'active listening.' This semantic nuance parallels English hear vs. listen, but Arabic encodes it morphologically through verb form rather than using separate roots.

items.question: _____ الحكومة مبلغًا كبيرًا في مشاريع البنية التحتية (The government invested a large sum in infrastructure projects)
items.explanation: اِستَثمَرَ means 'to invest,' from the root ث-م-ر (fruit/yield). Literally 'to seek fruit/yield,' this Form X verb perfectly captures the concept of investment — putting in resources to obtain returns. Compare: أثمَرَ (IV — to bear fruit), اِستَثمَرَ (X — to seek fruit/invest). This verb is ubiquitous in financial and economic Arabic. The noun اِستِثمار (investment) is one of the most common Form X verbal nouns (masdars).

items.question: _____ المريض بسرعة بعد العملية الجراحية (The patient recovered quickly after the surgery)
items.explanation: Although عادَ (I — to return) and أعادَ (IV — to return/repeat something) exist, اِستعادَ means 'to recover/regain.' This hollow Form X verb (root ع-و-د) shows the 'seeking' meaning: literally 'seeking to return to a former state.' In medical contexts, it means recovery. In other contexts, اِستَعادَ الذكريات means 'to recall memories.' The hollow conjugation (و→ا) is consistent across Form X hollow verbs.

name: Hollow Verbs (الأفعال الجوفاء)
description: Verbs with weak middle radical (و or ي) that changes form

items.question: _____ الأب لابنه قصة جميلة قبل النوم (The father told his son a beautiful story before sleep)
items.explanation: قالَ (to say/tell) is a classic hollow verb from the root ق-و-ل. In hollow verbs (أفعال جوفاء), the middle radical is a weak letter (و or ي) that undergoes changes during conjugation. In the past tense, the و becomes ا (alif): قَوَلَ → قالَ. In the present tense, it becomes يَقولُ. Understanding these transformations is key to mastering a large portion of Arabic verbs, as hollow verbs are extremely common.

items.question: _____ الرجل إلى بيته بعد يوم طويل في العمل (The man returned to his house after a long day at work)
items.explanation: عادَ (to return) is a hollow verb from the root ع-و-د. The original form عَوَدَ loses its middle radical و in the past tense, becoming عادَ. In the present tense, it becomes يَعودُ (the و returns). The command form is عُد (return!). This pattern — و→ا in past, و stays in present — applies to all واوي (waw-type) hollow verbs and is one of the most fundamental conjugation patterns in Arabic.

items.question: _____ الطائر فوق المدينة لساعات طويلة (The bird flew over the city for long hours)
items.explanation: طارَ (to fly) is a hollow verb from the root ط-ي-ر. This is a يائي (ya-type) hollow verb where the middle radical ي becomes ا in the past tense. In the present tense, it becomes يَطيرُ (the ي returns). The distinction between واوي (waw-type) and يائي (ya-type) hollow verbs affects vowel patterns: waw-type has ضمة in present (يَقولُ), while ya-type has كسرة (يَطيرُ).

items.question: _____ الولد في البحر طوال فصل الصيف (The boy swam in the sea throughout the summer season)
items.explanation: عامَ (to swim/float) is a hollow verb from the root ع-و-م. Like other واوي hollow verbs, the و becomes ا in the past tense. However, note that سَبَحَ (to swim) is a regular/sound verb with a similar meaning. Arabic often has both hollow and sound verbs expressing similar concepts. The choice depends on register and regional variety — عامَ is more literary while سَبَحَ is more colloquial.

items.question: _____ الزائرون في المتحف لمدة ساعتين (The visitors wandered in the museum for two hours)
items.explanation: جالوا is the plural past tense of جالَ (to wander/tour), from the root ج-و-ل. When a hollow verb conjugates in the plural past tense, the alif that replaced و disappears and the original pattern re-emerges as a short vowel: جالَ → جالوا (not جاالوا). This contraction is essential to learn. Compare: قالَ→قالوا, عادَ→عادوا, نامَ→ناموا.

items.question: لم _____ الطفل جيدًا بسبب الضوضاء (The child didn't sleep well because of the noise)
items.explanation: يَنَم is the jussive (مجزوم) form of نامَ (to sleep), root ن-و-م. After لم (did not), the verb takes the jussive mood. For hollow verbs, the jussive removes the long vowel: يَنامُ (present indicative) → يَنَم (jussive). This is a critical rule: hollow verbs in the jussive lose their middle radical entirely. Compare: يَقولُ→يَقُل, يَعودُ→يَعُد, يَزورُ→يَزُر.

items.question: _____ الأولاد ساحة المدرسة خلال الاستراحة (The children visited the school yard during the break)
items.explanation: زاروا is the plural past of زارَ (to visit), root ز-و-ر. This common hollow verb follows the standard واوي pattern: past زارَ (و→ا), present يَزورُ (و returns with ضمة). The plural past زاروا shows the typical contraction. زيارة (visit, noun) is the masdar of this verb. Knowing the verb-masdar relationship helps: زارَ→زيارة, عادَ→عَودة, قالَ→قَول.

items.question: _____ الصياد سمكة كبيرة في النهر (The fisherman caught a large fish in the river)
items.explanation: صادَ (to hunt/catch) is a يائي hollow verb from the root ص-ي-د. The ي becomes ا in the past tense (صَيَدَ→صادَ) and returns as يَصيدُ in the present. The noun صَيد means 'hunting/catch.' يائي hollow verbs have كسرة in present tense (يَصيدُ, يَطيرُ, يَسيرُ) vs. واوي verbs which have ضمة (يَقولُ, يَزورُ, يَعودُ). This vowel difference helps identify the root.

items.question: _____ السيارة في الشارع الرئيسي (The car drove/went on the main street)
items.explanation: سارَت is the feminine past of سارَ (to walk/drive/go), root س-ي-ر. The تاء التأنيث (feminine marker ت) is added directly after the modified stem. Since سيارة (car) is feminine, the verb must agree: سارَت (not سارَ). This demonstrates hollow verb conjugation with gender agreement — a common area where learners make errors. The present is تَسيرُ (she/it drives).

items.question: لن _____ أخي من السفر قبل نهاية الشهر (My brother won't return from the trip before the end of the month)
items.explanation: يَعودَ is the subjunctive (منصوب) form of يَعودُ after لن (will not). In the subjunctive, hollow verbs keep their long vowel but change the final vowel: يَعودُ (indicative with ضمة) → يَعودَ (subjunctive with فتحة). This differs from the jussive where the long vowel disappears: يَعُد. The three moods of hollow verbs — indicative, subjunctive, jussive — each handle the middle radical differently.

items.question: _____ الماء من الصنبور طوال الليل (The water leaked/flowed from the faucet all night)
items.explanation: سالَ (to flow/leak) is a يائي hollow verb from the root س-ي-ل. The present is يَسيلُ. This verb demonstrates how يائي hollow verbs work with inanimate subjects — Arabic doesn't change the verb form for life/non-life, only for gender and number. The masdar is سَيَلان (flowing), which uses a different pattern than واوي hollow verb masdars. Learning both masdar patterns is essential.

items.question: _____ في الاجتماع حوالي ثلاثين شخصًا (About thirty people were present at the meeting)
items.explanation: كانَ (to be/exist) is the most important hollow verb in Arabic, root ك-و-ن. It's used as an auxiliary verb for past tense constructions (كانَ يَدرُسُ — he was studying) and in the كانَ وأخواتها (kana and its sisters) grammatical framework. In the present, it becomes يَكونُ, and the jussive is يَكُن (used after لم: لم يَكُن — he wasn't). Mastering كانَ's conjugation is fundamental to Arabic grammar.

items.question: _____ ! هذا ليس صحيحًا (Say [to a man]! This is not correct)
items.explanation: قُل is the masculine singular imperative of قالَ (to say). Hollow verbs form the imperative by removing the prefix and keeping the shortened stem: يَقولُ → يَقُل (jussive) → قُل (imperative). The ضمة on the single consonant قُ comes from the original واوي nature of the verb. Compare: نَم (sleep!), عُد (return!), زُر (visit!), سِر (walk!) — the vowel depends on whether it's واوي (ضمة) or يائي (كسرة).

items.question: _____ للبنت أن تختار هديتها بنفسها (The girl was allowed to choose her gift herself)
items.explanation: جازَ (to be permissible/allowed) is a واوي hollow verb from the root ج-و-ز. It's commonly used in legal, religious, and formal Arabic: يَجوزُ (it is permissible), لا يَجوزُ (it is not permissible). The impersonal usage — where the verb doesn't have a specific human subject — is very common. Compare: جازَ (I — to be allowed), أجازَ (IV — to permit), تَجاوَزَ (VI — to exceed/overlook).

items.question: ما _____ الطلاب على الأسئلة الصعبة (The students didn't answer the difficult questions)
items.explanation: أجابوا is the plural past of أجابَ (to answer), root ج-و-ب. Note that أجابَ is Form IV of a hollow verb — the combination of Form IV prefix أ with a hollow root creates unique patterns. In the plural: أجابَ→أجابوا. The present is يُجيبُ (not يُجوبُ — the واو transforms to ياء in Form IV hollow verbs). ما here functions as a negation particle. These double complexities (form + hollow) challenge even advanced learners.

name: Defective Verbs (الأفعال الناقصة)
description: Verbs with weak final radical that drops in certain forms

items.question: _____ المحامي إلى المحكمة صباحًا (The lawyer came to the court in the morning)
items.explanation: أتى (to come) is a defective verb (فعل ناقص) with a weak final radical (ي or و that appears as ى in writing). The final ى is actually an alif maqsura representing the original ي. In the present, it becomes يأتي (the ي appears clearly). Defective verbs are characterized by changes in their final radical during conjugation — it can appear as ى, ي, or disappear entirely depending on the form.

items.question: _____ الشرطي على الحادثة بعد دقائق (The policeman arrived at the accident after minutes)
items.explanation: أتى is used here for arriving/coming to a location. Defective verbs ending in ى (alif maqsura) like أتى, مَشى, رَمى have a distinct conjugation pattern. In the plural past: مَشى→مَشَوا (the ى transforms to وا). In the dual: مَشَيا. In the feminine: مَشَت (the ى drops). These transformations of the final radical are the hallmark of defective verb conjugation and must be memorized.

items.question: _____ القاضي بالعدل بين الناس (The judge ruled with justice among the people)
items.explanation: قَضى (to judge/rule/spend time) is a defective verb from the root ق-ض-ي. The final ي appears as ى (alif maqsura) in the past tense singular masculine. In the present tense: يَقضي (the ي returns clearly). This verb has multiple important meanings: قَضى الحُكم (passed judgment), قَضى وقتًا (spent time), قَضى على (eliminated). Context determines the meaning — a common feature of Arabic verbs.

items.question: _____ الأطفال في الحديقة حتى المساء (The children played in the park until evening)
items.explanation: جَرَوا is the plural past tense of جَرى (to run), root ج-ر-ي. When defective verbs conjugate in the plural masculine past, the final ى/ي transforms to وا: جَرى→جَرَوا, مَشى→مَشَوا, بَكى→بَكَوا. The original ي completely disappears and is replaced by the plural marker. This pattern is consistent across all defective verbs and is different from how sound verbs form their plural past.

items.question: _____ الطفل عندما فقد لعبته المفضلة (The child cried when he lost his favorite toy)
items.explanation: بَكى (to cry) is a defective verb from the root ب-ك-ي. Present tense: يَبكي. The causative Form IV أبكى means 'to make someone cry.' This illustrates an important pattern with defective verbs — when they enter other forms, the weak final radical ي continues to cause changes. Form IV أبكى also ends in ى (alif maqsura) because the original ي transforms identically across forms.

items.question: _____ المعلمة الدرس بشرح واضح (The teacher [f] finished the lesson with a clear explanation)
items.explanation: أنهَت is the feminine past of أنهى (to finish/complete), root ن-ه-ي. When a defective verb takes the feminine suffix ـت, the final ى drops entirely: أنهى→أنهَت (not أنهَيَت). This silent deletion is a key conjugation rule. Compare with sound verbs where the ت simply attaches: كَتَبَ→كَتَبَت. The feminine form of defective verbs is always shorter than expected, which can confuse learners.

items.question: _____ المسلمون الصلاة في المسجد (The Muslims prayed the prayer in the mosque)
items.explanation: صَلَّوا is the plural past of صَلّى (to pray), root ص-ل-و. This is a واوي defective verb — the final radical is و (which appears as ى in the singular past). The plural past replaces ى with وا: صَلّى→صَلَّوا. Notice the shadda remains on the ل because صَلّى is Form II (فَعَّلَ) of a defective verb — combining Form II doubling with defective verb changes creates a complex but predictable pattern.

items.question: _____ الشاعر قصيدة عن الوطن (The poet recited a poem about the homeland)
items.explanation: تَلا (to recite/read aloud) is a واوي defective verb from the root ت-ل-و. Unlike يائي defective verbs (which end in ى/ي), واوي defective verbs have a distinctly different present tense vowel pattern: يَتلو (with و) vs. يَقضي (with ي). In the past singular, both types look similar (ending in ا or ى), but the present tense reveals the original final radical.

items.question: لم _____ الخبر حتى اتصل بنا صديقنا (We didn't know the news until our friend called us)
items.explanation: نَدرِ is the jussive first-person plural of دَرى (to know/be aware), root د-ر-ي. After لم, defective verbs in the jussive lose their final radical: يَدري→يَدرِ→(with لم)→لم يَدرِ, نَدري→نَدرِ→لم نَدرِ. The كسرة on the final consonant remains as a trace of the deleted ي. This jussive pattern applies to all يائي defective verbs: لم يَمشِ, لم يَجرِ, لم يَبكِ.

items.question: _____ أن تدرس العلوم أم الآداب؟ (Do you intend to study sciences or humanities?)
items.explanation: تَنوي is the present tense of نَوى (to intend), root ن-و-ي. This verb has both و and ي as weak letters in the root (doubled weak), creating complex conjugation patterns. The present tense uses ي for the final radical: يَنوي. The past uses ى: نَوى. The masdar is نِيَّة (intention — with shadda showing assimilation). Double-weak verbs are among the most challenging in Arabic morphology.

items.question: _____ العاملة شكوى ضد المدير (The worker [f] filed a complaint against the manager)
items.explanation: شَكَت is the feminine past of شَكا (to complain), root ش-ك-و. This واوي defective verb shows the typical feminine past pattern where the final radical drops: شَكا→شَكَت. Present tense: يَشكو (واوي pattern with و). The alif at the end of شَكا (not ى) indicates this is a واوي root — a useful spelling clue. Compare: يائي roots write ى (بَكى, مَشى) while واوي roots write ا (دَعا, شَكا).

items.question: _____ الرجل صديقه إلى حفل العشاء (The man invited his friend to the dinner party)
items.explanation: دَعا (to invite/call/pray) is a واوي defective verb from the root د-ع-و. It has multiple important meanings: دَعا إلى (invited to), دَعا لـ (prayed for), دَعا على (cursed). The present tense يَدعو shows the و clearly. Notice the alif (not ى) at the end signals واوي origin. The noun دَعوة (invitation/prayer) and دُعاء (supplication) are derived from this root — showing how defective verb roots generate common vocabulary.

items.question: هل _____ بنتائج الامتحان؟ (Are you satisfied with the exam results?)
items.explanation: رَضيتَ is the second-person past of رَضِيَ (to be satisfied/pleased), root ر-ض-ي. When conjugating defective verbs with connected pronouns, the ي reappears: رَضِيَ→رَضيتَ (you were satisfied). This contrasts with the third-person where ي becomes ى or disappears. The full past conjugation is: رَضِيَ, رَضِيَت, رَضيتَ, رَضيتُ, رَضُوا. Note how the weak radical behaves differently with each pronoun.

items.question: _____ الطبيب المريض من المرض الخطير (The doctor cured the patient from the dangerous disease)
items.explanation: شَفى (to cure/heal) is a يائي defective verb from the root ش-ف-ي. The masdar is شِفاء (healing/cure). This verb is often used in the passive: شُفِيَ (he was cured). The related adjective شافٍ means 'curative/healing' — notice how the tanwin replaces the final ي in the indefinite form. Defective verb active participles like شافٍ follow a special declension pattern called المنقوص (the deficient noun).

items.question: لن _____ عن حقوقنا مهما حدث (We will never give up our rights no matter what happens)
items.explanation: نَتَخَلّى is the first-person plural subjunctive of تَخَلّى (to abandon/give up), root خ-ل-و. This is Form V of a واوي defective verb — combining Form V (تَفَعَّلَ) with a defective root. After لن (will not), the subjunctive is used. For defective verbs, the subjunctive keeps the final radical but with فتحة: يَتَخَلّى (indicative with ضمة) → يَتَخَلّى (subjunctive with فتحة — written identically but pronounced differently). Context distinguishes them.

name: Doubled Verbs (الأفعال المضعّفة)
description: Verbs where the second and third radicals are identical

items.question: _____ الولد الكرة بقوة نحو المرمى (The boy kicked the ball hard toward the goal)
items.explanation: شَدَّ (to pull/kick/tighten) is a doubled verb (فعل مُضَعَّف) from the root ش-د-د, where the second and third radicals are identical. In doubled verbs, the two identical consonants merge with a shadda (ّ) in most forms: شَدَدَ→شَدَّ. However, when a voweled suffix is added, they separate: شَدَدتُ (I pulled). This merger/separation pattern is the defining characteristic of doubled verbs.

items.question: _____ المعلم على أهمية القراءة اليومية (The teacher emphasized the importance of daily reading)
items.explanation: While شَدَّ is Form I, شَدَّدَ is Form II (فَعَّلَ) of the doubled root ش-د-د. In Form II doubled verbs, all three radicals are visible because Form II already doubles the middle radical, creating a word with three identical consonants grouped as two syllables: شَدْ-دَدَ→شَدَّدَ. This makes Form II doubled verbs distinctive. Similarly, حَدَّدَ (to determine) from ح-د-د and رَدَّدَ (to repeat) from ر-د-د.

items.question: _____ الطبيب يد المريض من الكسر (The doctor extended/straightened the patient's hand from the fracture)
items.explanation: مَدَّ (to extend/stretch) is a doubled verb from the root م-د-د. The key conjugation rule for doubled verbs: before consonant-initial suffixes, the doubled letters separate — مَدَدتُ (I extended), مَدَدنا (we extended). Before vowel-initial suffixes, they stay merged — مَدّا (they two extended), مَدّوا (they extended). This separation/merger pattern creates two distinct paradigms within the same verb's conjugation.

items.question: _____ الأم خيط الصوف لتصنع سترة لطفلها (The mother wound the wool thread to make a sweater for her child)
items.explanation: لَفَّت is the feminine past of لَفَّ (to wrap/wind), root ل-ف-ف. With the feminine suffix ت (consonant-initial), the doubled letters stay merged: لَفَّ+ت→لَفَّت (not لَفَفَت). This contrasts with لَفَفتُ (I wrapped) where the pronoun suffix forces separation. The rule is phonological: Arabic avoids three consecutive consonants, so CCC clusters cause separation while CC+CV stays merged.

items.question: لم _____ الاجتماع إلى نتيجة واضحة (The meeting didn't lead to a clear result)
items.explanation: يُؤَدِّ is the jussive of أدّى (to lead to/perform), which combines Form IV (أفعَلَ) with a doubled-defective root (أ-د-ي/و). After لم, doubled-defective verbs lose both the final vowel and shorten the doubled consonant: يُؤَدّي→يُؤَدِّ. This type of verb — doubled AND defective — is among the most challenging in Arabic because two sets of weak-verb rules apply simultaneously.

items.question: _____ الطالب الأسئلة بسرعة كبيرة (The student counted the questions very quickly)
items.explanation: عَدَّ (to count) is a doubled verb from the root ع-د-د. Present tense: يَعُدُّ (he counts — note the ضمة pattern). The noun عَدَد (number) is the masdar of this verb with the radicals separated. Compare عَدَّ (count), أعَدَّ (prepare), اِعتَدَّ (consider), اِستَعَدَّ (prepare oneself) — all from the same root, showing how doubled roots create families of related verbs across different forms.

items.question: _____ الجمهور بحرارة بعد العرض المسرحي (The audience clapped warmly after the theatrical performance)
items.explanation: Although صَفَّقَ looks like a doubled verb, it's actually Form II (فَعَّلَ) from the root ص-ف-ق (not a doubled root). True doubled verbs have identical second and third radicals (like ر-د-د, م-د-د). In Form II, the middle radical is doubled with shadda, which can look similar but follows different conjugation rules. Distinguishing between true doubled verbs and Form II of sound verbs is an important analytical skill.

items.question: _____ الدواء صحة المريض بشكل ملحوظ (The medicine improved the patient's health noticeably)
items.explanation: حَسَّنَ (to improve/make better) is Form II of the root ح-س-ن — a sound verb, not a doubled verb. However, حَسّ (to feel/sense), root ح-س-س, IS a doubled verb. Learners commonly confuse these. The test: if removing the shadda reveals two different consonants (حَسَنَ → ح-س-ن), it's Form II of a sound verb. If both consonants are identical (حَسَسَ → ح-س-س), it's a true doubled verb. This distinction affects conjugation patterns.

items.question: _____ الطالب يديه قبل الأكل (The student washed his hands before eating)
items.explanation: This is a contrast question — غَسَلَ (to wash) is a regular sound verb (root غ-س-ل), while مَسَّ (to touch, root م-س-س) and شَمَّ (to smell, root ش-م-م) are doubled verbs. The question tests whether learners can identify the meaning correctly despite the distractor doubled verbs. Sound verbs like غَسَلَ follow standard conjugation with no merger/separation, making them simpler than their doubled counterparts.

items.question: _____ الجرس عند نهاية الحصة (The bell rang at the end of the class period)
items.explanation: رَنَّ (to ring/chime) is a doubled verb from the root ر-ن-ن. The present tense يَرِنُّ shows the كسرة pattern (like يَمِلُّ from مَلَّ). Doubled verbs in the present can take either ضمة or كسرة as the base vowel, and this must be learned for each verb — there's no consistent rule. Compare: يَعُدُّ (ضمة — to count), يَرِنُّ (كسرة — to ring), يَمُرُّ (ضمة — to pass).

items.question: هل _____ الأخبار السيئة أمك؟ (Did the bad news upset your mother?)
items.explanation: ضَرَّت is the feminine past of ضَرَّ (to harm/upset), root ض-ر-ر. With the feminine suffix ـت, the shadda is preserved: ضَرَّ+ت→ضَرَّت. The antonym is نَفَعَ (to benefit — a sound verb). In Arabic, many doubled verbs describe emotional states: سَرَّ (to delight), ضَرَّ (to harm), هَمَّ (to concern), أحَبَّ (Form IV — to love). This semantic cluster makes doubled verbs especially important for expressing feelings.

items.question: _____ سنتين على خطبتهما ثم تزوجا (Two years passed since their engagement then they married)
items.explanation: مَرَّت is the feminine past of مَرَّ (to pass), root م-ر-ر. سنتين (two years) is feminine, requiring the feminine verb. مَرَّ is one of the most common doubled verbs — مَرَّ يَمُرُّ مُرورًا (to pass). The masdar مُرور (passing/traffic) is used daily. Note the ضمة pattern in the present (يَمُرُّ). The time expression 'X passed since' is مَرَّ + time period + على + event — a very useful construction in Arabic.

items.question: _____ الطالب من الدراسة المستمرة (The student got bored from continuous studying)
items.explanation: مَلَّ (to get bored/fed up) is a doubled verb from the root م-ل-ل. Present: يَمَلُّ (with فتحة — unusual, as most doubled verbs take ضمة or كسرة). The preposition من follows to indicate the source of boredom. The adjective مَلول (easily bored) and the masdar مَلَل (boredom) are common derivatives. Doubled verbs generate nouns by separating the radicals: مَلَّ→مَلَل, ضَرَّ→ضَرَر, حَبَّ→حُبّ.

items.question: _____ الجندي على الهرب من الأسر (The soldier was determined to escape from captivity)
items.explanation: أصَرَّ (to insist/be determined) is Form IV (أفعَلَ) of the doubled root ص-ر-ر. Form IV doubled verbs follow a specific pattern: أ + فعّ → أفَعَّ. The present tense is يُصِرُّ (with ضمة prefix and كسرة base — typical Form IV pattern). أصَرَّ على (to insist on) requires the preposition على. The combination of Form IV morphology with doubled verb rules requires managing two sets of conjugation patterns simultaneously.

items.question: _____ صحة المريض بعد تناول الدواء الجديد (The patient's health improved after taking the new medicine)
items.explanation: تَحَسَّنَت is the feminine past of تَحَسَّنَ (Form V — to improve, intransitive), root ح-س-ن. While this appears doubled (double سّ), it's actually Form V of a sound root — the shadda is from Form V's تَفَعَّلَ pattern, not from root doubling. The feminine ت is added because صحة (health) is feminine. Compare: حَسَّنَ (II — improved something), تَحَسَّنَ (V — improved by itself). This reflexive relationship between Forms II and V is systematic.

name: Hamzated Verbs (الأفعال المهموزة)
description: Verbs containing hamza as one of their root letters

items.question: _____ الطالب القرآن بصوت جميل (The student read the Quran with a beautiful voice)
items.explanation: قَرَأَ (to read) is a hamzated verb (فعل مهموز) with hamza as the third radical (ق-ر-أ). When conjugating hamzated verbs, the hamza can change its 'seat' (كرسي) depending on surrounding vowels. In قَرَأَ, the hamza sits on alif because it follows a fatha. In forms like قَرَأتُ (I read), the hamza remains on alif. Understanding hamza seat rules is essential for correct Arabic spelling and pronunciation.

items.question: _____ الضيوف إلى الحفلة في الموعد المحدد (The guests came to the party at the scheduled time)
items.explanation: جَاءَ (to come) has hamza as its third radical (ج-ي-أ) and is also a hollow verb, making it doubly irregular. The hamza in جَاءَ sits on alif because it's preceded by a fatha (the long 'aa' sound). When adding suffixes, the hamza seat changes: جَاءُوا (they came) — hamza sits alone. This verb is extremely common in Arabic and mastering its conjugation patterns is crucial for fluency.

items.question: _____ الإمام المؤمنين في صلاة الجمعة (The imam led the believers in Friday prayer)
items.explanation: أَمَّ (to lead in prayer) has hamza as its first radical (أ-م-م) and is also a doubled verb. When hamza is the first radical, it typically appears as أ with a fatha (أَمَّ), إ with a kasra (إِمام), or أُ with a damma (أُمّة). This combination of being both hamzated and doubled makes verbs like أَمَّ particularly challenging. The noun إمام (imam/leader) derives from the same root.

items.question: _____ الطبيب المريض بالتعافي السريع (The doctor informed the patient of quick recovery)
items.explanation: أَنبَأَ (to inform/announce) is Form IV (أَفعَلَ) of the hamzated root ن-ب-أ. When hamza appears as the third radical in Form IV, it follows the standard hamza seat rules: أَنبَأَ→أُنبِئَ (passive)→يُنبِئُ (imperfect). The related noun نَبَأ (news/report) and نَبِيّ (prophet — one who receives news) both come from this root. Form IV hamzated verbs are common in formal and media Arabic.

items.question: _____ الطفل أمه عن موعد العشاء (The child asked his mother about dinner time)
items.explanation: سَأَلَ (to ask) has hamza as its second radical (س-أ-ل). When hamza is the middle radical, its seat depends on surrounding vowels. In سَأَلَ, hamza sits on alif after fatha. In يَسأَلُ (he asks), it stays on alif. But in سُئِلَ (passive: was asked), the hamza sits on yaa-seat because kasra is the strongest vowel. This vowel-hierarchy rule (kasra>damma>fatha>sukun) governs all hamza seat decisions.

items.question: الأم _____ أطفالها كل يوم بالمدرسة (The mother takes her children every day to school)
items.explanation: أَخَذَ (to take) is a hamzated verb with hamza as the first radical (أ-خ-ذ). In the imperfect, the initial hamza drops: يَأخُذُ→تَأخُذُ (she takes). Uniquely, the imperative of أَخَذَ drops the hamza entirely: خُذْ (take!) instead of the expected *اُأخُذْ. This hamza-dropping is irregular and specific to a few high-frequency verbs. The مصدر (verbal noun) is أَخْذ.

items.question: _____ في كلامه ولم يقل الحقيقة (He erred in his speech and did not tell the truth)
items.explanation: أَخطَأَ (to make a mistake) is Form IV of the root خ-ط-أ, where hamza is the third radical. When Form IV has a final hamza, conjugation requires careful attention: أَخطَأَ→يُخطِئُ (imperfect, hamza on yaa-seat after kasra)→أَخطِئْ (jussive). The related words خَطَأ (error) and خَطِيئة (sin/major error) show how the root's hamza appears in different noun patterns. Form IV hamzated verbs are very productive in Modern Standard Arabic.

items.question: المعلمة _____ الدرس بقصة ممتعة (The teacher began the lesson with an enjoyable story)
items.explanation: بَدَأَ (to begin) has hamza as its third radical (ب-د-أ). When adding the feminine suffix تْ, the hamza remains: بَدَأَتْ. However, with other suffixes the hamza seat changes: بَدَأُوا (they began — hamza alone after waw). The related nouns بِدَاية (beginning) and مُبتَدِئ (beginner) show pattern derivation. Note the difference between بَدَأَ (Form I: to begin) and أَبدَأَ (Form IV: to initiate/start something).

items.question: _____ الشاعر قصيدته أمام الجمهور (The poet recited his poem before the audience)
items.explanation: أَنشَدَ (to recite poetry) is Form IV of ن-ش-د. While not hamzated in its root, the Form IV prefix أَ adds a hamza. This is different from truly hamzated verbs where hamza is part of the root. Compare: أَنشَدَ (Form IV, hamza is prefix) vs. أَخَذَ (hamza is first radical). Understanding this distinction helps learners avoid confusing prefixed hamza with radical hamza — a common error that affects conjugation patterns.

items.question: هل _____ الرسالة التي أُرسلت إليك؟ (Did you read the message that was sent to you?)
items.explanation: قَرَأتَ (you read, past) shows how hamzated verbs conjugate with consonant-initial suffixes. The hamza in قَرَأَ stays on alif when adding تَ: قَرَأتَ. Compare this with رَأَيتَ from رَأَى (to see), where the hamza also appears on alif. The hamza seat is determined by the strongest adjacent vowel using the hierarchy: kasra → damma → fatha → sukun. Here, both surrounding vowels are fatha, so hamza sits on alif.

items.question: _____ الشركة مشروعاً جديداً هذا العام (The company established a new project this year)
items.explanation: أَنشَأَ (to establish/create) is Form IV of the root ن-ش-أ, where hamza is the third radical. With the feminine marker: أَنشَأَتْ. This verb is extremely common in formal Arabic: إنشاء (creation/establishment) is its مصدر. The passive is أُنشِئَ (was established) — note how the hamza seat changes to yaa because of the preceding kasra. Modern Standard Arabic uses this verb extensively in business and administrative contexts.

items.question: الباحث _____ عن تأثير التكنولوجيا على التعليم (The researcher is investigating the impact of technology on education)
items.explanation: While يَسأَلُ (he asks) would also fit contextually, يَبحَثُ (he researches) is the correct answer meaning 'to investigate/research.' Notice يَسأَلُ as a hamzated imperfect: the hamza sits on alif after fatha. In contrast, يَبحَثُ is a regular Form I verb. The distinction matters: سَأَلَ implies direct questioning of a person, while بَحَثَ implies systematic investigation. The مصدر بَحْث (research) is ubiquitous in academic Arabic.

items.question: _____ الولد بالكأس فانكسرت (The boy bumped into the cup and it broke)
items.explanation: أَطاحَ (to knock over/topple) is Form IV of ط-و-ح with a hollow middle radical. While not a classic hamzated verb, contrasting it with truly hamzated verbs reinforces learning. True hamzated verbs like أَكَلَ (to eat, أ-ك-ل) have hamza in the root itself. Form IV's initial أ is a pattern prefix, not a radical. This distinction is critical: أَكَلَ→يَأكُلُ (hamza stays) vs. أَطاحَ→يُطِيحُ (no hamza in imperfect).

items.question: _____ الرجل برأيه دون تردد (The man expressed his opinion without hesitation)
items.explanation: أَدلَى (to express/cast — as in أَدلَى بصوته to cast a vote) is Form IV of د-ل-و/ي. While focusing on hamzated verbs, it's valuable to contrast with verbs where the initial أ is merely Form IV's prefix. In truly hamzated-first verbs like أَمَرَ (to command, أ-م-ر), the hamza persists in all forms: يَأمُرُ, أُمِرَ. This persistence test distinguishes root hamza from pattern hamza.

items.question: _____ الأب ابنه بالاجتهاد في دراسته (The father commanded his son to be diligent in his studies)
items.explanation: أَمَرَ (to command/order) is a hamzated-first verb from the root أ-م-ر. Its imperative is irregular: مُرْ (command!) — the initial hamza drops entirely, making it one of the shortest Arabic imperatives. The imperfect is يَأمُرُ (the hamza persists, confirming it's radical, not prefixed). Related nouns: أَمْر (order/matter), أَمِير (prince/commander), مَأمُور (subordinate). This root is foundational in Arabic — أُولُو الأَمْر (those in authority) appears frequently in religious and legal texts.

name: Passive Participle (اسم المفعول)
description: Adjectives and nouns formed from verbs indicating the receiver of an action

items.question: الكتاب _____ على الطاولة منذ أسبوع (The book has been placed on the table for a week)
items.explanation: مَوضُوع (placed/subject) is the passive participle (اسم المفعول) of وَضَعَ (to place). For Form I triliteral verbs, the passive participle follows the pattern مَفعُول: و-ض-ع→مَوضُوع. The passive participle describes the entity receiving the action — here, the book that was placed. Compare with the active participle وَاضِع (one who places, pattern فَاعِل). This مَفعُول pattern is one of the most productive in Arabic word formation.

items.question: هذا المطعم _____ بأطباقه الشرقية اللذيذة (This restaurant is known for its delicious Eastern dishes)
items.explanation: مَعرُوف (known/famous) is the passive participle of عَرَفَ (to know), following the مَفعُول pattern: ع-ر-ف→مَعرُوف. It describes the entity that is known (the restaurant). In Arabic, passive participles frequently function as adjectives: مَعرُوف (known), مَشهُور (famous), مَكتُوب (written). The word مَعرُوف also means 'a favor/good deed' in Islamic terminology, showing how passive participles can develop specialized meanings beyond their literal derivation.

items.question: الرسالة _____ بخط واضح وجميل (The letter is written in clear and beautiful handwriting)
items.explanation: مَكتُوبة is the feminine form of the passive participle مَكتُوب (written), from كَتَبَ (to write). The feminine تاء مربوطة (-ة) is added because رسالة (letter/message) is grammatically feminine. Passive participles agree in gender and number with the noun they describe: مَكتُوب (masc.), مَكتُوبة (fem.), مَكتُوبُون (masc. pl.), مَكتُوبَات (fem. pl.). This agreement system parallels adjective agreement rules throughout Arabic.

items.question: الباب _____ لجميع الزوار (The door is open to all visitors)
items.explanation: مَفتُوح (opened/open) is the passive participle of فَتَحَ (to open). The مَفعُول pattern consistently produces passive participles from Form I verbs: فَتَحَ→مَفتُوح, كَسَرَ→مَكسُور (broken), شَغَلَ→مَشغُول (busy/occupied). Note that مِفتَاح (key) follows a different pattern — it's an instrument noun (اسم الآلة) meaning 'the tool for opening.' This demonstrates how different patterns applied to the same root create different semantic relationships.

items.question: التقرير _____ من قبل اللجنة المختصة (The report is approved by the competent committee)
items.explanation: مُعتَمَد (approved/accredited) is the passive participle of the Form VIII verb اِعتَمَدَ (to approve/rely on). For augmented forms (Forms II-X), the passive participle is formed by replacing the imperfect prefix with مُ and changing the last vowel to fatha: يَعتَمِدُ→مُعتَمَد. This مُ- prefix + fatha-before-last pattern applies uniformly to all augmented forms: مُستَخدَم (Form X), مُنتَظَر (Form VIII), مُتَّفَق (Form VIII).

items.question: هذا العمل _____ في جميع أنحاء العالم (This work is prohibited in all parts of the world)
items.explanation: مَمنُوع (prohibited/forbidden) is the passive participle of مَنَعَ (to prevent/forbid). This is one of the most commonly seen passive participles on signs and in regulations: مَمنُوع التدخين (Smoking prohibited), مَمنُوع الدخول (Entry forbidden). The مَفعُول pattern for Form I verbs is extremely reliable — virtually any Form I transitive verb can form its passive participle this way: صَنَعَ→مَصنُوع (manufactured), طَبَعَ→مَطبُوع (printed).

items.question: النتائج _____ على الموقع الإلكتروني (The results are published on the website)
items.explanation: مَنشُورة (published, fem.) is the passive participle of نَشَرَ (to publish/spread). Since نتائج (results) is a broken plural of feminine nouns, the adjective can take either feminine singular or plural agreement. Here, feminine singular مَنشُورة is used — this is standard when the broken plural refers to non-human entities. Compare with the active participle مُنتَشِرة (widespread, Form VIII active participle), which has a different meaning.

items.question: الطعام _____ بعناية من قبل الطباخ الماهر (The food is prepared carefully by the skilled chef)
items.explanation: مُحَضَّر (prepared) is the passive participle of the Form II verb حَضَّرَ (to prepare). For Form II verbs (فَعَّلَ), the passive participle follows the pattern مُفَعَّل: حَضَّرَ→مُحَضَّر, دَرَّسَ→مُدَرَّس (taught), صَوَّرَ→مُصَوَّر (photographed). The مُ- prefix with fatha before the last radical distinguishes Form II passive participles from their active counterparts: مُحَضِّر (preparer, active) vs. مُحَضَّر (prepared, passive).

items.question: القرار _____ من المحكمة العليا (The decision is issued by the Supreme Court)
items.explanation: صَادِر is actually the active participle of صَدَرَ (to issue/emanate) — an intransitive verb. For intransitive verbs, the فَاعِل pattern functions like a passive participle because the subject both performs and receives the state: القرار صَادِر (the decision is issued/has emanated). This is a subtle but important distinction: truly transitive verbs use مَفعُول for passive meaning, while intransitive verbs use فَاعِل to describe the state. This is a common source of confusion for learners.

items.question: هذا المرض _____ في المنطقة الاستوائية (This disease is widespread in the tropical region)
items.explanation: مُنتَشِر (widespread) is actually the active participle of Form VIII اِنتَشَرَ (to spread, intransitive). Like صَادِر, intransitive verbs use their active participle to describe the resulting state. Compare: نَشَرَ→مَنشُور (published — transitive, passive participle) vs. اِنتَشَرَ→مُنتَشِر (widespread — intransitive, active participle functioning as a state description). This demonstrates how Arabic uses different patterns for transitive vs. intransitive meaning from the same root.

items.question: هذا المبنى _____ للهدم قريباً (This building is scheduled for demolition soon)
items.explanation: مُقَرَّر (decided/scheduled) is the passive participle of the Form II verb قَرَّرَ (to decide/schedule). The مُفَعَّل pattern clearly marks it as passive. In academic Arabic, مُقَرَّر also means 'curriculum/course.' The active participle مُقَرِّر means 'rapporteur/one who decides.' Note how the vowel before the last radical distinguishes active (kasra: مُقَرِّر) from passive (fatha: مُقَرَّر) in Form II — this single vowel difference is the key diagnostic.

items.question: الجائزة _____ لأفضل باحث في العلوم (The prize is designated for the best researcher in science)
items.explanation: مُخَصَّصة (designated/allocated, fem.) is the passive participle of Form II خَصَّصَ (to designate/allocate), with feminine ة for agreement with جائزة. The full paradigm: مُخَصَّص (masc. sg.), مُخَصَّصة (fem. sg.), مُخَصَّصُون (masc. pl.), مُخَصَّصَات (fem. pl.). Note that خَاصّة (special/private) is from the Form I root خَصَّ, a related but different word. Arabic's derivational system creates these semantic networks from shared roots.

items.question: الكلمة _____ من اللغة الفارسية (The word is borrowed from the Persian language)
items.explanation: مُستَعَارة (borrowed, fem.) is the passive participle of Form X اِستَعَارَ (to borrow). Form X passive participles follow the pattern مُستَفعَل: اِستَعَارَ→مُستَعَار, اِستَخدَمَ→مُستَخدَم (used), اِستَورَدَ→مُستَورَد (imported). The prefix مُستَ- is distinctive to Form X. In linguistic terminology, كلمة مُستَعَارة means 'loanword.' Arabic has borrowed extensively from Persian, Turkish, and other languages throughout its history.

items.question: الطالب _____ بنتائجه الممتازة (The student is pleased with his excellent results)
items.explanation: مَسرُور (pleased/happy) is the passive participle of سَرَّ (to please/delight) from the doubled root س-ر-ر. With doubled verbs, the مَفعُول pattern becomes مَفعُول with the doubled letter: س-ر-ر→مَسرُور (literally: 'one who has been pleased'). Other examples: ضَرَّ→مَضرُور (harmed), مَسَّ→مَمسُوس (touched). The passive participle here functions as an adjective describing the student's emotional state — a very common use.

items.question: هذا الموضوع _____ في الاجتماع القادم (This topic is to be discussed in the next meeting)
items.explanation: مُنَاقَش (to be discussed) is the passive participle of Form III نَاقَشَ (to discuss). Form III passive participles follow the pattern مُفَاعَل: نَاقَشَ→مُنَاقَش, قَابَلَ→مُقَابَل (met/interviewed), عَالَجَ→مُعَالَج (treated). The active counterpart is مُنَاقِش (one who discusses/thesis examiner). Remember: for all augmented forms (II-X), passive = مُ prefix + fatha before final radical; active = مُ prefix + kasra before final radical.

name: Active Participle (اسم الفاعل)
description: Adjectives and nouns indicating the doer of an action

items.question: الطالب _____ يحتاج إلى مساعدة المعلم (The failing student needs the teacher's help)
items.explanation: الرَّاسِب (the failing one) is the active participle (اسم الفاعل) of رَسَبَ (to fail an exam). For Form I triliteral verbs, the active participle follows the pattern فَاعِل: ر-س-ب→رَاسِب. The active participle identifies the doer of the action. Like adjectives, it takes the definite article: رَاسِب (failing) → الرَّاسِب (the failing one). Compare with the antonym نَاجِح (passing/successful) from نَجَحَ (to succeed) — same pattern.

items.question: هذا الكتاب _____ لكل طالب عربي (This book is useful for every Arabic student)
items.explanation: مُفِيد (useful/beneficial) is the active participle of Form IV أَفَادَ (to benefit/be useful). For augmented forms (II-X), the active participle is formed by replacing the imperfect prefix with مُ and keeping the kasra before the last radical: يُفِيدُ→مُفِيد. This pattern applies to all augmented forms: مُعَلِّم (Form II teacher), مُسَاعِد (Form III helper), مُمتَاز (Form VIII excellent). Compare active مُفِيد vs. passive مُفَاد (conveyed).

items.question: الشرطي _____ المرور عند التقاطع (The officer directing traffic at the intersection)
items.explanation: مُنَظِّم (organizer/one who organizes) is the active participle of Form II نَظَّمَ (to organize/regulate). Form II active participles follow مُفَعِّل: نَظَّمَ→مُنَظِّم. The shadda (ّ) on the middle radical is preserved. Compare: مُعَلِّم (teacher, Form II of عَلَّمَ), مُدَرِّس (teacher/lecturer, Form II of دَرَّسَ), مُصَوِّر (photographer, Form II of صَوَّرَ). The kasra before the final radical marks it as active; fatha would make it passive: مُنَظَّم (organized).

items.question: _____ يعرف كل شيء عن الحادثة (The witness knows everything about the incident)
items.explanation: الشَّاهِد (the witness) is the active participle of شَهِدَ (to witness), following the فَاعِل pattern. Arabic active participles often lexicalize as nouns: شَاهِد (witness), كَاتِب (writer/clerk), حَاكِم (ruler/judge). Note the semantic differences in this root: شَاهِد (witness, Form I), مُشَاهِد (viewer/spectator, Form III), شَهِيد (martyr — an intensive form). Understanding these distinctions shows how Arabic builds rich vocabulary networks from single roots.

items.question: هذا الفيلم _____ جداً ولا أستطيع مشاهدته (This movie is very scary and I cannot watch it)
items.explanation: مُخِيف (scary/frightening) is the active participle of Form IV أَخَافَ (to frighten). Compare with خَائِف (afraid/scared) — the active participle of Form I خَافَ (to fear). This pair demonstrates a crucial distinction: مُخِيف = 'causing fear' (the movie frightens), خَائِف = 'feeling fear' (a person is afraid). Form IV often makes Form I verbs causative: خَافَ (to fear) → أَخَافَ (to cause fear). The active participles maintain this causative relationship.

items.question: المدير _____ لجميع الموظفين في الشركة (The manager is responsible for all employees in the company)
items.explanation: Interestingly, مَسؤُول (responsible/official) is actually the passive participle of سَأَلَ (to ask) — literally 'one who is asked/questioned.' It has evolved to mean 'responsible' because a responsible person is one who can be questioned about their duties. This demonstrates how passive participles can develop active-seeming meanings through semantic drift. The active participle سَائِل means 'one who asks/questioner.' This distinction between grammatical form and practical meaning is vital for advanced learners.

items.question: _____ أن يصل الطبيب في أي لحظة (It is expected that the doctor will arrive at any moment)
items.explanation: مِنَ المُتَوَقَّع (it is expected) uses the passive participle of Form V تَوَقَّعَ (to expect). Note: مُتَوَقَّع (expected, passive) vs. مُتَوَقِّع (one who expects, active). The difference is one vowel: fatha (passive) vs. kasra (active) before the final radical. This minimal pair illustrates the systematic relationship between active and passive participles in augmented forms. The expression مِنَ المُتَوَقَّع أَنْ... (it is expected that...) is standard in news Arabic.

items.question: الجندي _____ عن وطنه بشجاعة (The soldier defending his homeland bravely)
items.explanation: المُدَافِع (the defender) is the active participle of Form III دَافَعَ (to defend). Form III active participles follow مُفَاعِل: دَافَعَ→مُدَافِع, حَاوَلَ→مُحَاوِل (one who tries), سَاعَدَ→مُسَاعِد (helper). Note that الدَّافِع (the motive/driver) is the Form I active participle of دَفَعَ (to push/pay), with a completely different meaning. Same root, different forms, different participles, different meanings — this showcases Arabic's derivational power.

items.question: أصبحت المدينة _____ بالسياح في الصيف (The city has become crowded with tourists in summer)
items.explanation: مُزدَحِمة (crowded, fem.) is the active participle of Form VIII اِزدَحَمَ (to be crowded). Form VIII active participles follow مُفتَعِل: اِزدَحَمَ→مُزدَحِم. Note the د in مُزدَحِم — Form VIII sometimes changes the تاء of اِفتَعَلَ to د after certain letters (ز here triggers the change: اِزتَحَمَ→اِزدَحَمَ). This phonological assimilation (إدغام) is a unique feature of Form VIII. The active participle preserves this assimilated form.

items.question: المسافر _____ إلى بلده بعد غياب طويل (The traveler returning to his country after a long absence)
items.explanation: العَائِد (the one returning) is the active participle of عَادَ (to return), a hollow verb (ع-و-د). For hollow verbs, the فَاعِل pattern shows the و/ي as a long vowel: عَادَ→عَائِد (the waw appears before the hamza seat). Other hollow verb participles: قَالَ→قَائِل (speaker), زَارَ→زَائِر (visitor), نَامَ→نَائِم (sleeper).
