The hamza in عَائِد is actually an orthographic seat for the original waw — a spelling convention unique to hollow verb participles.

items.question: المحامي _____ المتهم أمام المحكمة (The lawyer representing the accused before the court)
items.explanation: المُمَثِّل (representative/actor) is the active participle of Form II مَثَّلَ (to represent/act). Form II active participles maintain the doubled middle radical: مُمَثِّل. This word has multiple meanings depending on context: مُمَثِّل (representative in court), مُمَثِّل (actor in film/theater), مُمَثِّل تجاري (commercial representative). The passive مُمَثَّل means 'represented.' Context determines meaning — a fundamental principle of Arabic vocabulary.

items.question: الماء _____ من الصنبور بغزارة (The water flowing from the faucet abundantly)
items.explanation: الجَارِي (the flowing one) is the active participle of جَرَى (to flow/run), a defective verb (ج-ر-ي). For defective verbs ending in ي, the active participle pattern فَاعِل becomes فَاعِي→فَاعٍ (in indefinite) or الفَاعِي (in definite, with the ي restored): جَرَى→جَارٍ/الجَارِي. Other examples: مَشَى→مَاشٍ/المَاشِي (walker), قَضَى→قَاضٍ/القَاضِي (judge). The ي drops in indefinite accusative/genitive cases — a key morphological feature of these participles.

items.question: الطبيب _____ للعملية الجراحية (The doctor performing the surgery)
items.explanation: المُجرِي (the one performing/conducting) is the active participle of Form IV أَجرَى (to conduct/carry out). Form IV active participles follow مُفعِل: أَجرَى→مُجرٍ/المُجرِي. When the root ends in ي/و (defective), the active participle behaves like other defective nouns — ي drops in indefinite: مُجرٍ, but is restored with ال: المُجرِي. Compare with the Form I جَرَى→جَارٍ (flowing) — different form, different meaning, same root. This shows how form number changes both meaning and participle pattern.

items.question: الباحث _____ في مجال الذكاء الاصطناعي (The researcher specializing in artificial intelligence)
items.explanation: المُتَخَصِّص (the specialist) is the active participle of Form V تَخَصَّصَ (to specialize). Form V active participles follow مُتَفَعِّل: تَخَصَّصَ→مُتَخَصِّص. The shadda and kasra combination is distinctive. Related forms from the root خ-ص-ص: خَاصّ (special, Form I), مُخَصَّص (allocated, Form II passive participle), تَخَصُّص (specialization, Form V مصدر), اِختَصَاص (specialty, Form VIII مصدر). This root family demonstrates Arabic's ability to generate dozens of related words systematically.

items.question: النار _____ في الغابة منذ ثلاثة أيام (The fire has been burning in the forest for three days)
items.explanation: مُشتَعِلة (burning/blazing, fem.) is the active participle of Form VIII اِشتَعَلَ (to ignite/blaze). Form VIII active participles: مُفتَعِل→مُشتَعِل. The feminine ة agrees with نار (fire, feminine). Active participles can indicate ongoing states — مُشتَعِلة describes a fire currently burning. This 'present state' usage is one of the most common functions of active participles in Arabic, effectively replacing present continuous tense constructions found in English.

name: Elative Form (صيغة التفضيل)
description: The أَفعَل pattern used for comparatives and superlatives

items.question: القاهرة _____ مدينة في أفريقيا (Cairo is the largest city in Africa)
items.explanation: أَكبَر (bigger/biggest) is the elative form (صيغة التفضيل) of كَبِير (big), following the أَفعَل pattern: ك-ب-ر→أَكبَر. When used as a superlative, the elative takes the definite article or is in an إضافة construction: أَكبَر مدينة (the biggest city). Compare: هو أَكبَر مني (he is bigger than me — comparative) vs. هو الأَكبَر (he is the biggest — superlative). The feminine elative is كُبرَى (greatest, fem.), not أَكبَرة.

items.question: هذا الامتحان _____ من الامتحان السابق (This exam is easier than the previous exam)
items.explanation: أَسهَل (easier) is the elative of سَهل (easy): س-هـ-ل→أَسهَل. The comparative construction in Arabic uses أَفعَل + مِن: أَسهَل مِن (easier than). Unlike English, Arabic doesn't distinguish between 'easier' and 'easiest' in form — context and syntax determine the meaning. With مِن it's comparative; with ال or إضافة it's superlative. Note: the antonym أَصعَب (harder) from صَعب follows the same pattern perfectly.

items.question: العسل _____ من السكر (Honey is sweeter than sugar)
items.explanation: أَحلَى (sweeter) is the elative of حُلو (sweet). When the third radical is و or ي (defective root), the elative pattern أَفعَل becomes أَفعَى: ح-ل-و→أَحلَى (not *أَحلَو). Other defective elatives: أَغلَى (more expensive, from غَالٍ), أَعلَى (higher, from عَالٍ), أَدنَى (lower/closer, from دَانٍ). The alif maqsura (ى) at the end is characteristic of all defective-root elatives and never changes regardless of case.

items.question: _____ شيء في الحياة هو الصحة (The most important thing in life is health)
items.explanation: أَهَمّ (most important) is the elative of مُهِمّ/هَامّ (important), from the doubled root هـ-م-م. With doubled roots, the elative أَفعَل pattern merges the identical radicals: هـ-م-م→أَهَمّ (not *أَهمَم). The shadda on the final letter indicates the merger. Other doubled-root elatives: أَقَلّ (less/least, from قَلِيل, root ق-ل-ل), أَحَبّ (more beloved, from حَبِيب, root ح-ب-ب), أَشَدّ (more intense, from شَدِيد, root ش-د-د).

items.question: المحيط الهادئ هو _____ محيطات العالم (The Pacific Ocean is the largest of the world's oceans)
items.explanation: أَكبَر محيطات العالم uses the superlative إضافة construction: elative + genitive noun. This is one of three superlative structures: 1) أَكبَر محيطات (the largest of the oceans — إضافة), 2) المحيط الأَكبَر (the largest ocean — with ال), 3) أَكبَر محيط (the largest ocean — indefinite إضافة). Each has slightly different emphasis. The إضافة form (أَفعَل + definite plural) is most common for 'superlative among a group.'

items.question: الشتاء في موسكو _____ من الشتاء في القاهرة (Winter in Moscow is colder than winter in Cairo)
items.explanation: أَبرَد (colder) is the elative of بَارِد (cold): ب-ر-د→أَبرَد. The comparative construction أَفعَل مِن is remarkably consistent in Arabic — unlike English, which uses both '-er than' and 'more... than' depending on syllable count, Arabic always uses أَفعَل مِن regardless of the adjective. However, for adjectives that cannot form the أَفعَل pattern (like colors or physical defects), Arabic uses أَكثَر + مصدر: أَكثَر اِحمِراراً (more reddened).

items.question: الدول _____ في العالم تقع في أفريقيا (The poorest countries in the world are in Africa)
items.explanation: الأَفقَر (the poorest) uses the elative with the definite article for superlative meaning. When modifying a plural noun, the elative can remain singular: الدول الأَفقَر (the poorest countries). This is because the elative (أَفعَل) is technically a noun of invariable form — it doesn't change for number or gender in many constructions. However, the feminine form فُعلَى exists: الدولة الفُقرَى. The masculine plural pattern for elatives is practically never used in Modern Standard Arabic.

items.question: _____ الأمور أن نبقى متحدين (The most important thing is that we remain united)
items.explanation: أَهَمّ الأمور uses the إضافة superlative: elative + definite noun. This construction makes the elative inherently definite through إضافة. A subtle distinction: أَهَمّ الأمور (the most important of matters) vs. مِن أَهَمّ الأمور (among the most important matters — using مِن to indicate 'one of the most'). The مِن construction is very common in Arabic rhetoric and avoids claiming absolute supremacy, adding nuance to formal writing and speech.

items.question: هذه القصة _____ مما توقعت (This story is stranger than what I expected)
items.explanation: أَغرَب (stranger/more strange) is the elative of غَرِيب (strange): غ-ر-ب→أَغرَب. Note the construction أَفعَل + مِمَّا (مِن + ما): أَغرَب مِمَّا توقعت (stranger than what I expected). The مِمَّا construction is essential for comparisons with clauses rather than nouns. Similarly: أَسهَل مِمَّا ظننت (easier than what I thought), أَجمَل مِمَّا تصورت (more beautiful than what I imagined). This comparative clause pattern is ubiquitous in Arabic prose.

items.question: النيل _____ أنهار العالم (The Nile is the longest river in the world)
items.explanation: أَطوَل (longest) from طَوِيل (long/tall): ط-و-ل→أَطوَل. In أَطوَل أنهار العالم, the إضافة makes it superlative. Note the root has و as its second radical (hollow), but the elative pattern handles it smoothly: the medial و becomes part of the أَفعَل template without irregularity. This contrasts with hollow verb conjugation, where the middle radical causes significant changes. The elative pattern is remarkably stable across different root types.

items.question: كلما تعلمت _____, كلما أدركت جهلي (The more I learned, the more I realized my ignorance)
items.explanation: أَكثَر (more) is the elative of كَثِير (many/much). The كُلَّما...كُلَّما (the more...the more) construction uses the elative to express proportional relationships. أَكثَر is one of the most versatile elatives: أَكثَر الناس (most people), أَكثَر مِن (more than), أَكثَر فأَكثَر (more and more). It's also used as an adverb: يعمل أَكثَر (he works more). This multi-functional usage makes أَكثَر one of the highest-frequency words in Arabic.

items.question: الوضع الآن _____ مما كان عليه (The situation now is worse than it was)
items.explanation: أَسوَأ (worse/worst) is the elative of سَيِّئ (bad), from the root س-و-أ. This root is both hollow (middle و) and hamzated (final أ), but the elative pattern absorbs these irregularities: أَسوَأ. The hamza sits on alif because the preceding vowel is fatha. In Quran and classical texts, you'll also encounter the form أَسوَأُ (nominative) and أَسوَإِ (genitive) — the hamza seat changes with case endings. Modern Standard Arabic generally avoids visible case endings, simplifying usage.

items.question: اللغة العربية _____ اللغات السامية انتشاراً (Arabic is the most widespread of the Semitic languages)
items.explanation: أَكثَر اللغات انتشاراً uses a special construction: elative + إضافة + تمييز (specification). The تمييز اِنتِشاراً (in terms of spread) specifies what dimension the comparison applies to. This pattern (أَفعَل + noun + تمييز) is used when the adjective cannot directly form an elative: since اِنتِشار is a مصدر, you say أَكثَر انتشاراً (most widespread) rather than *أَنشَر. The تمييز is always indefinite accusative (منصوب).

items.question: هي _____ طالبة في الصف (She is the smartest student in the class)
items.explanation: أَذكَى (smartest) is the elative of ذَكِيّ (smart), from the root ذ-ك-و/ي. Defective roots produce elatives ending in ألف مقصورة: أَذكَى. For feminine superlatives, Arabic has two options: 1) هي أَذكَى طالبة (invariable elative — most common), 2) هي الذُّكرَى (using فُعلَى feminine — rare/classical). In modern Arabic, option 1 dominates. The elative أَفعَل remains masculine in form even when describing feminine nouns in many constructions.

items.question: على _____ تقدير, سيستغرق المشروع ستة أشهر (At the very least, the project will take six months)
items.explanation: أَقَلّ (least/minimum) is the elative of قَلِيل (few/little), from the doubled root ق-ل-ل. The expression على أَقَلّ تقدير (at the very least) is a fixed idiomatic phrase. Other fixed expressions with elatives: على الأَقَلّ (at least), في أَقرَب وقت (as soon as possible), بأَفضَل طريقة (in the best way). These formulaic expressions are essential for formal Arabic writing. Note that doubled-root elatives always show the shadda: أَقَلّ, أَحَبّ, أَهَمّ.

name: Root System (نظام الجذور)
description: Understanding Arabic's trilateral root system and word derivation

items.question: كلمة 'مَكتَبة' مشتقة من الجذر _____ (The word 'library' is derived from the root _____)
items.explanation: مَكتَبة (library) derives from the root ك-ت-ب (related to writing). The pattern مَفعَلة indicates a place noun (اسم مكان): the place where writing happens. From this same root: كَتَبَ (to write), كِتَاب (book), كَاتِب (writer), مَكتُوب (written), مَكتَب (desk/office), كُتُب (books), كِتَابة (writing). This demonstrates Arabic's root system — typically three consonants carry core meaning, and patterns (أَوزان) create related words with predictable meanings.

items.question: 'مُعَلِّم' و'تَعلِيم' و'عِلم' كلها من الجذر _____ (muʿallim, taʿlīm, and ʿilm are all from the root _____)
items.explanation: The root ع-ل-م (related to knowledge/knowing) generates a vast family: عَلِمَ (to know), عِلم (knowledge/science), عَالِم (scholar/scientist), مَعلُومة (information), تَعلِيم (education), مُعَلِّم (teacher), تَعَلُّم (learning), مُتَعَلِّم (learner), عَلَامة (sign/mark), مَعلَم (landmark). Each word follows a specific morphological pattern that adds systematic meaning to the root. This predictability is why understanding the root system accelerates Arabic vocabulary acquisition dramatically.

items.question: كلمة 'اِستِقلال' مأخوذة من الجذر _____ (The word 'independence' is taken from the root _____)
items.explanation: اِستِقلال (independence) comes from Form X اِستَقَلَّ (to become independent) of the root ق-ل-ل. The semantic journey: قَلَّ (Form I: to be few/little) → اِستَقَلَّ (Form X: to consider oneself sufficient/independent). Form X often means 'to consider oneself [quality]' or 'to seek [action].' The مصدر اِستِقلال gained its political meaning in the 20th century. Related: قَلِيل (few), أَقَلّ (less), إقلال (reduction). Understanding root derivation helps decode unfamiliar words by connecting them to known roots.

items.question: الجذر ع-م-ل يعطينا كلمة _____ بمعنى 'factory' (The root ʿ-m-l gives us the word _____ meaning 'factory')
items.explanation: مَعمَل (factory/laboratory) follows the مَفعَل place noun pattern from ع-م-ل (related to work/doing). Place nouns (اسم المكان) use مَفعَل or مَفعِل patterns: the place where the root action occurs. Compare: كَتَبَ→مَكتَب (office), طَبَخَ→مَطبَخ (kitchen), دَخَلَ→مَدخَل (entrance), خَرَجَ→مَخرَج (exit). Other ع-م-ل words: عَمَل (work), عَامِل (worker), عَمَلِيّة (operation/process), مَعمُول (something made — passive participle).

items.question: من الجذر ح-ك-م نحصل على _____ بمعنى 'wisdom' (From the root ḥ-k-m we get _____ meaning 'wisdom')
items.explanation: حِكمة (wisdom) from ح-ك-م follows the فِعلة pattern. This root is extraordinarily productive: حَكَمَ (to judge/rule), حُكم (judgment/ruling), حَاكِم (ruler/governor), مَحكَمة (court), مُحَكَّم (arbitrator), حَكِيم (wise person/doctor), إِحكَام (perfection/mastery), تَحَكُّم (control), حُكُومة (government). Notice how the core concept 'judgment/wisdom' threads through all derivatives. Arabic dictionaries are organized by root, making this system practical as well as linguistic.

items.question: كلمة 'مُستَشفَى' على وزن _____ (The word 'hospital' follows the pattern _____)
items.explanation: مُستَشفَى (hospital) follows the مُستَفعَل pattern — the passive participle/place noun of Form X. From the root ش-ف-ي (related to healing): اِستَشفَى (to seek healing) → مُستَشفَى (place where healing is sought). The مُستَفعَل pattern for Form X can indicate a place or a passive state. Compare: اِستَقبَلَ→مُستَقبَل (future — 'what is faced/received'), اِستَودَعَ→مُستَودَع (warehouse — 'where things are deposited'). Recognizing patterns helps decode new vocabulary instantly.

items.question: من الجذر س-ف-ر نحصل على _____ بمعنى 'embassy' (From the root s-f-r we get _____ meaning 'embassy')
items.explanation: سِفَارة (embassy) from the root س-ف-ر (related to travel/mediation) follows the فِعَالة pattern, which often indicates a profession or institution. From this root: سَفَرَ (to travel), سَفَر (journey), سَفِير (ambassador), سِفَارة (embassy), مُسَافِر (traveler), تَسفِير (deportation), جَوَاز سَفَر (passport — literally 'travel permit'). The semantic connection: ambassadors were originally those who traveled between nations. This root shows how Arabic words trace logical paths from concrete to abstract meanings.

items.question: كلمة 'مُرَاسِل' من الجذر _____ (The word 'correspondent' is from the root _____)
items.explanation: مُرَاسِل (correspondent) is the active participle of Form III رَاسَلَ (to correspond with), from ر-س-ل (related to sending). The root family: رَسَلَ/أَرسَلَ (to send), رِسَالة (message/letter), رَسُول (messenger/apostle), إِرسَال (sending/transmission), مُرسِل (sender), مُرسَل (sent), مُرَاسَلة (correspondence), رَاسِل (sender in classical). The Form III مُفَاعِل pattern indicates reciprocal action — a correspondent exchanges messages mutually. This root is one of the most frequently used in media Arabic.

items.question: من الجذر د-ر-س نحصل على _____ بمعنى 'school' (From the root d-r-s we get _____ meaning 'school')
items.explanation: مَدرَسة (school) follows the مَفعَلة place noun pattern from د-ر-س (related to studying). The full derivation family: دَرَسَ (to study), دَرس (lesson), دِرَاسة (study/research), مُدَرِّس (teacher, Form II active participle), مَدرَسَة (school — place of study), مَدرَسِيّ (academic/scholastic — nisba adjective). The pattern مَفعَلة specifically marks 'the place where X happens': مَزرَعة (farm, from زَرَعَ to plant), مَصنَعة (factory, from صَنَعَ to make).

items.question: الجذر ج-م-ع يتضمن معنى _____ (The root j-m-ʿ contains the meaning of _____)
items.explanation: The root ج-م-ع centers on the concept of 'gathering/collecting.' Its derivatives span: جَمَعَ (to gather), جَمع (collection/plural), جَامِع (mosque — where people gather), جَامِعة (university — comprehensive gathering), مَجمُوعة (group/collection), مُجتَمَع (society — Form VIII: people gathering together), إجمَاع (consensus), جَمِيع (all/everyone), اِجتِمَاع (meeting). This root beautifully illustrates how one concept radiates into dozens of related words through Arabic's systematic morphology.

items.question: كلمة 'تَارِيخ' مرتبطة بالجذر _____ (The word 'history/date' is related to the root _____)
items.explanation: تَارِيخ (history/date) comes from the root أ-ر-خ. The تَفعِيل pattern (here تَأرِيخ→تَارِيخ with simplification) is a Form II مصدر. Related: أَرَّخَ (to date/chronicle, Form II), مُؤَرِّخ (historian, Form II active participle), تَأرِيخِيّ (historical, nisba adjective). Identifying roots can be challenging when the word has undergone phonological changes — تَارِيخ doesn't obviously show أ-ر-خ. Learning to strip away pattern letters (م, ت, ا, ن, etc.) to find the root is a critical skill for Arabic literacy.

items.question: من الجذر ن-ظ-ر نحصل على _____ بمعنى 'theory' (From the root n-ẓ-r we get _____ meaning 'theory')
items.explanation: نَظَرِيّة (theory) from ن-ظ-ر (related to looking/viewing) is formed by adding the nisba suffix ـِيّة to نَظَر (view/consideration). The semantic logic: a theory is a 'way of viewing/considering' something. Other derivatives: نَظَرَ (to look), نَظَر (view/gaze), مَنظَر (scene/view — place noun), نَاظِر (viewer/principal), مَنظُور (perspective — passive participle), نَظَّارة (eyeglasses — instrument), مُنَاظَرة (debate — Form III مصدر: 'looking at each other's views'). This root shows Arabic's remarkable capacity for systematic word creation.

items.question: كلمة 'اِقتِصَاد' على وزن _____ (The word 'economy' follows the pattern _____)
items.explanation: اِقتِصَاد (economy) follows the Form VIII مصدر pattern اِفتِعَال from the root ق-ص-د (related to intention/direction). The base verb قَصَدَ means 'to intend/head toward,' and Form VIII اِقتَصَدَ means 'to economize/be moderate' — literally 'to direct oneself toward moderation.' Recognizing مصدر patterns is essential: Form II تَفعِيل, Form III مُفَاعَلة/فِعَال, Form IV إِفعَال, Form V تَفَعُّل, Form VI تَفَاعُل, Form VII اِنفِعَال, Form VIII اِفتِعَال, Form X اِستِفعَال.

items.question: من الجذر ح-ر-ر نحصل على _____ بمعنى 'freedom' (From the root ḥ-r-r we get _____ meaning 'freedom')
items.explanation: حُرِّيّة (freedom) from the doubled root ح-ر-ر is formed: حُرّ (free) + ـِيّة (abstract noun suffix). This root is rich: حَرَّ (to be hot — the original meaning), حُرّ (free — metaphor: 'burning with independence'), حَرَارة (heat/temperature), تَحرِير (liberation/editing), مُحَرِّر (liberator/editor), تَحَرُّر (emancipation), حَرِيق (fire). The semantic journey from 'heat' to 'freedom' reflects how Arabic roots develop metaphorical extensions over centuries while maintaining core consonantal identity.

items.question: كلمة 'اِستِفهام' من الجذر _____ بمعنى 'understanding' (The word 'interrogation' is from the root _____ meaning 'understanding')
items.explanation: اِستِفهَام (interrogation/questioning) is the Form X مصدر of اِستَفهَمَ (to seek to understand) from the root ف-هـ-م (related to understanding). Form X often means 'to seek/request the root action': فَهِمَ (to understand) → اِستَفهَمَ (to seek understanding = to ask/interrogate). علامة الاِستِفهام means 'question mark.' Related: فَهم (understanding), فَهِيم (perceptive), مَفهُوم (concept — passive participle used as noun), تَفَاهُم (mutual understanding — Form VI). This root family is essential for academic Arabic.

name: Direct Object (المفعول به)
description: Using the accusative direct object in Arabic sentences

items.question: قَرَأَ الطَّالِبُ _____ (The student read the book)
items.explanation: The direct object (المفعول به) takes the accusative case (منصوب) in Arabic. Here الكِتَابَ ends with fatḥa (-a) because it receives the action of reading directly. The subject الطَّالِبُ is in nominative (مرفوع) with ḍamma. This accusative marking on the direct object is fundamental — whenever a verb acts upon something, that object must be منصوب. Compare: الكِتَابُ (nominative, subject) vs الكِتَابَ (accusative, object).

items.question: شَرِبَتِ الطِّفلَةُ _____ (The girl drank the milk)
items.explanation: الحَليبَ is the direct object (مفعول به) of the verb شَرِبَ (to drink), so it must be in the accusative case with fatḥa. The subject الطِّفلَةُ takes nominative case (ḍamma). In Arabic, the direct object always follows the verb-subject structure and takes the accusative ending. Even when word order shifts for emphasis, the case ending tells you which noun is the object. This case system is what allows Arabic's flexible word order.

items.question: كَتَبَ المُعَلِّمُ _____ عَلَى السَّبُّورَةِ (The teacher wrote the lesson on the board)
items.explanation: الدَّرسَ is in the accusative (منصوب) as the direct object of كَتَبَ. The teacher (المُعَلِّمُ) is the subject performing the action, and the lesson (الدَّرسَ) is what was written. Notice the prepositional phrase عَلَى السَّبُّورَةِ (on the board) — the noun after a preposition takes genitive case (مجرور), but the direct object takes accusative. Distinguishing these cases based on grammatical function is essential for correct Arabic.

items.question: أَكَلَ الوَلَدُ _____ لَذيذَةً (The boy ate a delicious apple)
items.explanation: تُفَّاحَةً (an apple) is an indefinite direct object in the accusative case. Indefinite accusative nouns take tanwīn fatḥa (-an), written as تُفَّاحَةً. The adjective لَذيذَةً also takes accusative tanwīn to agree with the noun it modifies. This pattern — indefinite مفعول به with tanwīn fatḥa — is very common in Arabic. Compare definite التُّفَّاحَةَ (with fatḥa, no tanwīn) vs indefinite تُفَّاحَةً (with tanwīn fatḥa).

items.question: فَتَحَ الرَّجُلُ _____ (The man opened the door)
items.explanation: البَابَ is the direct object of فَتَحَ (to open) and takes the accusative case ending -a. Transitive verbs (أفعال متعدية) in Arabic require a direct object to complete their meaning. Some verbs are intransitive (لازمة) and don't take objects. فَتَحَ is transitive — it needs to specify what was opened. The ability to identify transitive vs intransitive verbs helps you know when to expect a مفعول به in a sentence.

items.question: شَاهَدَ الأَطفَالُ _____ الجَديدَ (The children watched the new film)
items.explanation: الفيلمَ is the definite direct object in the accusative case. The adjective الجَديدَ also takes accusative to agree with the object it describes. In Arabic, adjectives must match their nouns in case, gender, number, and definiteness. So a definite accusative noun gets a definite accusative adjective: الفيلمَ الجَديدَ. This noun-adjective agreement in case is one of the hallmarks of correct Arabic grammar and applies consistently.

items.question: سَمِعتُ _____ جَميلًا (I heard a beautiful voice)
items.explanation: صَوتًا is an indefinite accusative direct object marked with tanwīn fatḥa (-an). The verb سَمِعَ (to hear) is transitive and the 'tā' at the end indicates first person (I). The adjective جَميلًا (beautiful) agrees in case (accusative) and indefiniteness with صَوتًا. Notice how tanwīn fatḥa appears as an alif at the end: صَوتًا. This 'alif of tanwīn' is a spelling convention for most masculine accusative indefinite nouns.

items.question: زَارَ أَبي _____ أَمسِ (My father visited the museum yesterday)
items.explanation: المَتحَفَ is the direct object of زَارَ (to visit) in the accusative case. أَبي (my father) is the subject — possessive suffixes make case endings invisible but the grammatical role remains. أَمسِ (yesterday) is a time adverb (ظرف زمان). Note that Arabic verbs in the past tense are conjugated for the subject even when the subject is stated explicitly. In this sentence, زَارَ is third person masculine singular, matching أَبي.

items.question: يَدرُسُ الطُّلَّابُ _____ كُلَّ يَومٍ (The students study Arabic every day)
items.explanation: العَرَبيَّةَ is the direct object of يَدرُسُ (to study, present tense). Even in the present tense, the direct object takes accusative. كُلَّ يَومٍ (every day) functions as a time adverb — كُلَّ is in accusative as an adverbial expression (ظرف), and يَومٍ takes genitive because it's مُضاف إليه. This sentence demonstrates how multiple accusative elements can coexist: the direct object العَرَبيَّةَ and the adverbial accusative كُلَّ.

items.question: أَحَبَّتِ الأُمُّ _____ كَثيرًا (The mother loved her children very much)
items.explanation: أَولَادَها (her children) is the direct object in accusative. The possessive suffix -ها (her) is attached, and the base noun takes fatḥa to indicate accusative case: أَولَادَ + ها. When possessive suffixes are attached, the tanwīn disappears and the case is shown only by the vowel before the suffix. كَثيرًا (a lot) functions as an adverb in the accusative — this is a common pattern where an indefinite accusative adjective serves as an adverb of manner.

items.question: لَم أَفهَم _____ (I did not understand the question)
items.explanation: السُّؤالَ is the direct object of أَفهَم (understand). The particle لَم negates the present tense verb and puts it in the jussive mood (مجزوم), but this doesn't affect the case of the direct object — it remains accusative. The verb أَفهَمُ becomes أَفهَمْ after لَم (the final vowel is dropped for jussive), but the مفعول به always stays منصوب regardless of the verb's mood. This consistency makes case assignment predictable.

items.question: سَيَشتَري عَلِيٌّ _____ جَديدًا (Ali will buy a new car)
items.explanation: سَيَّارَةً is an indefinite feminine direct object with tanwīn fatḥa. Note that tā' marbūṭa (ة) at the end of feminine nouns doesn't take the extra alif that masculine nouns do — you write سَيَّارَةً not سَيَّارَةًا. The prefix سَ on the verb indicates near future. The adjective جَديدًا takes masculine form because تَمييز (specification) follows certain agreement rules, but here جَديدًا agrees with the feminine noun — actually, in this context it should be جَديدَةً (feminine) to agree properly.

items.question: أَعطاني المُديرُ _____ (The manager gave me a chance)
items.explanation: أَعطَى (to give) takes two objects: the first object is the pronoun ني- (me), and the second object is فُرصَةً (a chance). Verbs that take two direct objects (أفعال متعدية لمفعولين) are a special category in Arabic grammar. Both objects are in the accusative case. Common double-object verbs include: أَعطَى (give), مَنَحَ (grant), سَأَلَ (ask), عَلَّمَ (teach), أَخبَرَ (inform). This doubled accusative pattern is characteristic of Arabic.

items.question: وَجَدتُ _____ مَفتوحًا (I found the window open)
items.explanation: وَجَدَ (to find) can take two objects when it means 'to find something in a state.' النَّافِذَةَ is the first direct object and مَفتوحًا (open) is the second object (حال or مفعول به ثانٍ depending on analysis). Both are in accusative case. This construction — verb + object + state/attribute — is common with verbs of perception and cognition: وَجَدَ, رَأَى, ظَنَّ, حَسِبَ, عَلِمَ. It provides rich descriptive sentences in Arabic.

items.question: لَن أَنسَى _____ أَبَدًا (I will never forget this day)
items.explanation: هَذا اليَومَ (this day) is the direct object of أَنسَى (to forget). The demonstrative هَذا is invariable (doesn't show case), but the noun اليَومَ that follows it takes the accusative. لَن + present tense creates future negation and puts the verb in subjunctive mood (منصوب), but again the object's case doesn't change. أَبَدًا (ever/never) is an adverbial accusative emphasizing perpetuity. The combination of لَن with أَبَدًا creates emphatic permanent negation.

name: Purpose Object (المفعول لأجله)
description: Expressing reason and purpose using the accusative of cause

items.question: جَاءَ الطَّالِبُ _____ لِلعِلمِ (The student came seeking knowledge)
items.explanation: طَلَبًا is a مفعول لأجله (object of cause/purpose) — it explains WHY the student came. It takes the indefinite accusative with tanwīn fatḥa. The مفعول لأجله must be: (1) a verbal noun (مصدر), (2) indicating a reason/motive for the main verb's action, (3) sharing the same subject and time as the main verb. طَلَب is the masdar of طَلَبَ (to seek). This elegant construction lets Arabic express purpose concisely without conjunctions.

items.question: وَقَفَ النّاسُ _____ لِلبَطَلِ (The people stood up in respect for the hero)
items.explanation: احتِرامًا (out of respect) is the مفعول لأجله explaining why the people stood. It's the masdar of احتَرَمَ (Form VIII: to respect). The tanwīn fatḥa marks the indefinite accusative. Note the preposition لِلبَطَلِ (for the hero) — the ل here specifies the beneficiary. The مفعول لأجله can sometimes be replaced by a prepositional phrase: وَقَفوا مِن أَجلِ الاحتِرامِ, but the accusative form is more concise and literary.

items.question: سَافَرَ أَبي _____ (My father traveled seeking a livelihood)
items.explanation: طَلَبًا لِلرِّزقِ (seeking livelihood) combines the مفعول لأجله with a prepositional phrase specifying what is sought. The masdar طَلَب takes accusative tanwīn, and لِلرِّزقِ adds the specific purpose. Arabic commonly uses this pattern to express motivation elegantly. Alternative constructions exist: مِن أَجلِ طَلَبِ الرِّزقِ or لِكَي يَطلُبَ الرِّزقَ, but the مفعول لأجله form is the most eloquent and frequently appears in formal and literary Arabic.

items.question: بَكَتِ الأُمُّ _____ (The mother cried out of joy)
items.explanation: فَرَحًا (out of joy) is a مفعول لأجله showing the emotional cause of crying. فَرَح is the masdar of فَرِحَ (to be happy). This sentence beautifully illustrates how the مفعول لأجله can create surprising or paradoxical meanings — crying from joy, not sadness. The accusative of cause is powerful for conveying complex emotions and motivations. Similar examples: بَكَى حُزنًا (cried from sadness), صَرَخَ خَوفًا (screamed from fear), ضَحِكَ سُرورًا (laughed from delight).

items.question: يَعمَلُ الفَلَّاحُ كُلَّ يَومٍ _____ (The farmer works every day hoping for harvest)
items.explanation: أَمَلًا (hoping/in hope) is the مفعول لأجله, with في الحَصَادِ (of harvest) specifying the hope. The masdar أَمَل from أَمَلَ (to hope) takes accusative tanwīn. The prepositional phrase في الحَصَادِ uses في (in/of) followed by a genitive noun. This construction efficiently expresses both the reason for action and its specific goal. The مفعول لأجله typically comes after the main verb and any direct objects.

items.question: هَرَبَ اللِّصُّ _____ مِنَ الشُّرطَةِ (The thief fled in fear of the police)
items.explanation: خَوفًا (out of fear) is the مفعول لأجله explaining the thief's motive for fleeing. خَوف is the masdar of خَافَ (to fear) — a hollow verb. مِنَ الشُّرطَةِ (from the police) specifies the source of fear. The مفعول لأجله here answers 'why did he flee?' — خَوفًا مِنَ الشُّرطَةِ. This construction is extremely common in Arabic storytelling, news reporting, and everyday speech for succinctly explaining motivations behind actions.

items.question: جَلَسَ الطِّفلُ بِجانِبِ أُمِّهِ _____ (The child sat beside his mother seeking safety)
items.explanation: طَلَبًا لِلأَمَانِ (seeking safety) is a مفعول لأجله with a prepositional complement. بِجانِبِ أُمِّهِ (beside his mother) is a ظرف مكان (adverb of place) using the preposition بِ + جانِب (side) + possessive أُمِّهِ. This sentence requires analyzing multiple grammatical elements: the verb جَلَسَ (intransitive), the locative phrase, and the purpose expression. Each element follows its own case rules, demonstrating Arabic's layered case system.

items.question: صَامَ المُسلِمُ _____ لِله (The Muslim fasted in obedience to God)
items.explanation: طَاعَةً (in obedience) is the مفعول لأجله, and لِلَّهِ (to God) specifies the direction of obedience. طَاعَة is the masdar of أَطَاعَ (Form IV: to obey). Important: when the مفعول لأجله becomes definite (has ال or is in an idāfa), it usually takes a preposition instead of the accusative: صَامَ لِلطَّاعَةِ or صَامَ مِن طَاعَةِ اللهِ. The bare accusative form (without preposition) works best when the masdar is indefinite.

items.question: تَبَرَّعَ الغَنيُّ _____ (The rich man donated seeking reward)
items.explanation: ابتِغاءً (seeking/desiring) is a مفعول لأجله from Form VIII ابتَغَى (to seek/desire). لِلأَجرِ (reward) completes the meaning. The verb تَبَرَّعَ (Form V: to donate voluntarily) is itself derived from broad/wide (وَسِعَ). This sentence showcases Arabic's morphological richness — verb Form V (تَفَعَّلَ) for the main action, Form VIII masdar (افتِعال) for the purpose. Understanding these patterns makes complex Arabic texts much more accessible.

items.question: رَكَضَ الوَلَدُ _____ لِلحافِلَةِ (The boy ran for fear of missing the bus)
items.explanation: خَشيَةً (out of fear/apprehension) is the مفعول لأجله. خَشيَة is the masdar of خَشِيَ (to fear/apprehend) — a more literary synonym of خَافَ. The subtle difference: خَوف is general fear, while خَشيَة implies reverent fear or concern about a consequence. لِلحافِلَةِ here means 'of missing the bus' (idiomatically). Arabic masdar choices convey nuanced emotional tones — using خَشيَةً instead of خَوفًا suggests thoughtful concern rather than raw fear.

items.question: ذَهَبَتْ إِلى الطَّبيبِ _____ (She went to the doctor seeking treatment)
items.explanation: طَلَبًا لِلعِلاجِ (seeking treatment) is the مفعول لأجله with its complement. Note ذَهَبَتْ — the تْ ending indicates a feminine subject (she). The subject is implicit (هِيَ) — Arabic allows subject omission when the verb conjugation makes it clear. The destination إِلى الطَّبيبِ uses the preposition إِلى (to). This sentence has three layers: action (went), destination (to the doctor), and purpose (seeking treatment), each with its own grammatical construction.

items.question: أَسرَعَ السَّائِقُ _____ في الوُصولِ (The driver hurried desiring to arrive on time)
items.explanation: رَغبَةً (out of desire) is the مفعول لأجله from رَغِبَ (to desire/wish). في الوُصولِ (in arriving) uses the masdar الوُصول after في to express what is desired. The verb أَسرَعَ (Form IV: to hurry) shows how Form IV adds intensity — the base سَرُعَ means to be fast, while أَسرَعَ means to actively hurry. When the مفعول لأجله is followed by في + masdar, it creates a natural purpose clause that flows well in Arabic composition.

items.question: سَكَتَ الطَّالِبُ _____ لِلمُعَلِّمِ (The student was silent out of respect for the teacher)
items.explanation: إِجلالًا (out of reverence/esteem) is a مفعول لأجله. إِجلال is the masdar of أَجَلَّ (Form IV: to revere). The prepositional phrase لِلمُعَلِّمِ specifies toward whom the reverence is directed. سَكَتَ (to be silent) is intransitive — it has no direct object, which makes the مفعول لأجله the primary complement explaining the silence. This shows that the مفعول لأجله works with both transitive and intransitive verbs, adding purpose to any action.

items.question: تَعَلَّمَ اللُّغَةَ العَرَبيَّةَ _____ (He learned Arabic out of love for the culture)
items.explanation: حُبًّا (out of love) is the مفعول لأجله with shadda on the final consonant before tanwīn. Note that this sentence has BOTH a direct object (اللُّغَةَ العَرَبيَّةَ — what was learned) and a مفعول لأجله (حُبًّا — why it was learned). Multiple accusative elements can coexist: the مفعول به answers 'what?' while the مفعول لأجله answers 'why?'. The verb تَعَلَّمَ (Form V: to learn) takes اللُّغَةَ as its direct object.

items.question: دافَعَ الجُندِيُّ عَن وَطَنِهِ _____ (The soldier defended his homeland out of loyalty)
items.explanation: وَفاءً (out of loyalty/faithfulness) is the مفعول لأجله from وَفَى (to be faithful). دافَعَ (Form III: to defend) takes عَن (about/for) to indicate what is defended — عَن وَطَنِهِ (his homeland). This sentence shows the مفعول لأجله at its most powerful: transforming a simple statement of action into a statement of character and motivation. In Arabic rhetoric (البلاغة), such constructions are valued for their eloquence and emotional impact.

name: Adverb of Time/Place (المفعول فيه)
description: Using accusative nouns to express when and where actions occur

items.question: سَافَرَ أَخي _____ المَاضي (My brother traveled last month)
items.explanation: الشَّهرَ is a ظرف زمان (adverb of time), technically called المفعول فيه — 'the object in which' the action took place. Time nouns used adverbially take the accusative case. الشَّهرَ المَاضي means 'last month' — both the noun and its adjective are accusative. Common time adverbs: يَومًا (one day), لَيلَةً (one night), سَاعَةً (for an hour), صَبَاحًا (in the morning). This accusative time construction is essential for Arabic temporal expressions.

items.question: جَلَسَ الطِّفلُ _____ الشَّجَرَةِ (The child sat under the tree)
items.explanation: تَحتَ (under/beneath) is a ظرف مكان (adverb of place) — a spatial المفعول فيه. It's permanently in the accusative case (مبني على الفتح). The noun after it (الشَّجَرَةِ) takes genitive case because it's مُضاف إليه. Common place adverbs: فَوقَ (above), أَمَامَ (in front), وَرَاءَ (behind), بَينَ (between), حَولَ (around). These spatial nouns are essential building blocks for describing locations and positions in Arabic.

items.question: عَمِلَ في المَصنَعِ _____ سَنَواتٍ (He worked in the factory for years)
items.explanation: عِدَّةَ (several) is in the accusative as part of a ظرف زمان construction: عِدَّةَ سَنَواتٍ (several years). عِدَّة is the مُضاف taking accusative, and سَنَواتٍ is the مُضاف إليه in genitive. This pattern — accusative number/quantity + genitive noun — is how Arabic expresses duration. في المَصنَعِ (in the factory) is a separate prepositional phrase. The temporal accusative answers 'how long?' while the prepositional phrase answers 'where?'

items.question: وَصَلتُ _____ الظُّهرِ (I arrived before noon)
items.explanation: قَبلَ (before) is a ظرف زمان in the accusative case. When followed by a noun (الظُّهرِ — noon, in genitive), it forms a time expression. قَبلَ and بَعدَ (after) are among the most frequently used temporal adverbs. They're always accusative when used as adverbs: جِئتُ قَبلَ المَوعِدِ (I came before the appointment), سَأَذهَبُ بَعدَ الغَدَاءِ (I'll go after lunch). Note: if cut off from their مُضاف إليه, they take ḍamma: مِن قَبلُ (from before).

items.question: يَنَامُ الطِّفلُ _____ بَاكِرًا (The child sleeps every night early)
items.explanation: كُلَّ لَيلَةٍ (every night) is a ظرف زمان in the accusative. كُلَّ takes accusative as an adverb of time, and لَيلَةٍ is genitive as مُضاف إليه. بَاكِرًا (early) is a حال (state/manner adverb) also in accusative. This sentence has two accusative elements serving different functions: كُلَّ لَيلَةٍ (when?) and بَاكِرًا (how?). Arabic's case system distinguishes these roles clearly even though both are accusative.

items.question: وَقَفَ _____ البَابِ يَنتَظِرُ (He stood in front of the door waiting)
items.explanation: أَمَامَ (in front of) is a ظرف مكان (spatial adverb) in the accusative. Like تَحتَ, فَوقَ, and وَرَاءَ, it's inherently accusative when used adverbially. البَابِ (the door) follows in genitive as مُضاف إليه. يَنتَظِرُ (waiting) is a حال جملة فعلية — a verbal sentence functioning as a circumstantial expression describing the subject's state while standing. This layering of ظرف مكان + حال creates vivid scene descriptions.

items.question: سَأَزورُكَ _____ القَادِمِ (I will visit you next week)
items.explanation: الأُسبوعَ القَادِمَ (next week) is a ظرف زمان — the definite time noun takes accusative to express when the visit will happen. The adjective القَادِمَ (next/coming) agrees in case (accusative), definiteness, gender, and number. The verb سَأَزورُ uses سَ for near future, and the attached pronoun كَ (you, masculine) is the direct object. Time nouns used adverbially without a preposition take accusative: اليَومَ (today), غَدًا (tomorrow), أَمسِ (yesterday).

items.question: لَعِبَ الأَولَادُ _____ السَّاعَاتِ (The children played for hours)
items.explanation: طِوَالَ (throughout/for the duration of) is a ظرف زمان meaning 'during the entirety of.' السَّاعَاتِ (the hours) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. طِوَال is related to طَويل (long) but functions specifically as a temporal adverb of duration. Similar duration words: خِلَالَ (during), أَثنَاءَ (during/while), مُدَّةَ (for the duration of). These words provide precise temporal framing and are invaluable for narration and describing events in Arabic.

items.question: جَلَسنَا _____ الطَّاوِلَةِ (We sat around the table)
items.explanation: حَولَ (around) is a ظرف مكان in the accusative. The الطَّاوِلَةِ (the table) follows in genitive. جَلَسنَا uses the نَا suffix for 'we'. Arabic spatial adverbs create precise location descriptions without prepositions — the accusative case itself functions like a preposition. Compare: حَولَ (around), بَينَ (between), وَسَطَ (in the middle of) — all accusative, all followed by genitive. This system is more compact than English prepositional phrases.

items.question: زُرتُ جَدَّتي _____ العيدِ (I visited my grandmother during the holiday)
items.explanation: أَيَّامَ (during the days of) is a ظرف زمان in accusative. العيدِ (the holiday) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. أَيَّام is the plural of يَوم (day). In ظرف constructions, you can use singular or plural time nouns: يَومَ الجُمعَةِ (on Friday), لَيلَةَ العيدِ (on the night of the holiday), صَبَاحَ الاثنَينِ (Monday morning). Each takes accusative when used as a temporal adverb, creating precise time references.

items.question: مَشَينَا _____ النَّهرِ (We walked alongside the river)
items.explanation: بِمُحَاذَاةِ (alongside/parallel to) uses the preposition بِ with the masdar مُحَاذاة (running parallel). After prepositions, nouns take genitive (مجرور). النَّهرِ is also genitive as مُضاف إليه of مُحَاذاة. Not all spatial expressions are pure ظروف — some use prepositions instead. Compare: أَمَامَ البَيتِ (in front of, accusative ظرف) vs بِجانِبِ البَيتِ (beside, prepositional). Both locate position, but their grammar differs.

items.question: اِستَيقَظتُ _____ الفَجرِ (I woke up at dawn)
items.explanation: عِندَ functions as both a temporal and spatial adverb. Here عِندَ الفَجرِ means 'at (the time of) dawn.' الفَجرِ (dawn) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. عِندَ is versatile: عِندَ البَابِ (at the door — spatial), عِندَ المَغرِبِ (at sunset — temporal), عِندي كِتَابٌ (I have a book — possessive). It's one of Arabic's most multifunctional ظروف, always in accusative when used adverbially. Understanding عِندَ's range unlocks many common expressions.

items.question: دَرَسَ _____ اللَّيلِ (He studied during the night)
items.explanation: أَثنَاءَ (during/while) is a ظرف زمان in accusative, indicating the time period during which studying occurred. اللَّيلِ (the night) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. أَثنَاء is the plural of ثَنِيّ (fold/interval) — literally 'in the folds of time.' Similar words: خِلَالَ (during, more formal), فِي أَثنَاءِ (during, with preposition — then أَثنَاءِ is genitive). The bare accusative form without في is more literary and concise.

items.question: وَضَعتُ الكُتُبَ _____ الرَّفِّ (I placed the books on top of the shelf)
items.explanation: فَوقَ (above/on top of) is a ظرف مكان in accusative. الرَّفِّ (the shelf) is genitive — note the shadda indicating the doubled final consonant. This sentence also has a مفعول به: الكُتُبَ (the books, accusative). So there are two accusative elements: the direct object (الكُتُبَ) and the spatial adverb (فَوقَ). Arabic keeps these distinct through their position: the مفعول به follows the verb directly, while the ظرف مكان specifies location.

items.question: سَنَلتَقي _____ الغَدِ (We will meet the day after tomorrow)
items.explanation: بَعدَ (after) is a ظرف زمان in accusative. بَعدَ الغَدِ literally means 'after tomorrow' = the day after tomorrow. Compare: قَبلَ (before) and بَعدَ (after) — both accusative as ظروف, both followed by genitive مُضاف إليه. Important: when these words are cut from their مُضاف إليه (used absolutely), they take ḍamma instead: مِن قَبلُ (from before), مِن بَعدُ (from after). This special behavior is one of Arabic grammar's distinctive features.

name: Apposition (البدل)
description: Replacing or clarifying nouns through grammatical apposition

items.question: جَاءَ الخَليفَةُ _____ (The caliph, Omar, came)
items.explanation: عُمَرُ is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ (complete apposition) — it completely replaces or identifies الخَليفَةُ. The بدل must match the مُبدَل مِنه (the word it replaces) in grammatical case. Since الخَليفَةُ is nominative (subject), عُمَرُ also takes nominative. عُمَر is a diptote (ممنوع من الصرف) — it takes ḍamma for nominative and fatḥa for both accusative and genitive, never tanwīn. This type of بدل is the most common, fully identifying the preceding noun.

items.question: أَعجَبَتني المَدينَةُ _____ (I liked the city's streets — lit: the city, its streets impressed me)
items.explanation: شَوَارِعُها (its streets) is بدل بَعض مِن كُلّ (partial apposition) — it refers to a PART of المَدينَةُ. The بدل must match the case of the original noun: المَدينَةُ is nominative, so شَوَارِعُ is also nominative. The possessive suffix ها (her/its) connects it back to المَدينَةُ. This type of بدل is essential for specifying which aspect of something you mean: أَحبَبتُ الكِتَابَ أُسلوبَهُ (I loved the book — its style).

items.question: زُرتُ مِصرَ _____ (I visited Egypt, Cairo specifically)
items.explanation: القَاهِرَةَ is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ — it specifies which part of مِصرَ was visited. مِصرَ is accusative (direct object of زُرتُ), so القَاهِرَةَ matches with accusative. Note: مِصر is a diptote — it takes fatḥa for both accusative and genitive, never tanwīn. This بدل can also be analyzed as بدل بعض from the country perspective. Context determines interpretation, but the case matching rule applies either way.

items.question: قَابَلتُ الدُّكتورَ _____ (I met Dr. Ahmad)
items.explanation: أَحمَدَ is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ (complete apposition) identifying الدُّكتورَ. Since الدُّكتورَ is accusative (object of قَابَلتُ), أَحمَدَ takes accusative too. أَحمَد is a diptote (ممنوع من الصرف) because it follows the pattern أَفعَل — it takes fatḥa in both accusative and genitive. In professional Arabic, title + name constructions always use بدل: الرَّئيسُ مُحَمَّدٌ, الوَزيرَةُ فَاطِمَةُ, الأُستاذُ عَلِيٌّ.

items.question: سَمِعتُ الخَبَرَ خَبَرَ _____ (I heard the news, the news of victory)
items.explanation: Here خَبَرَ الفَوزِ is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ from الخَبَرَ — it repeats and specifies the news. خَبَرَ matches the accusative case of الخَبَرَ. الفَوزِ (victory) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. This repetition-clarification pattern (الخَبَرَ خَبَرَ الفَوزِ) is common in Arabic for emphasis and specification. It's a rhetorical device: first mention the general, then clarify with the specific. This creates dramatic tension and clarity simultaneously.

items.question: مَرَرتُ بِالوَزيرِ _____ (I passed by the minister, Khalid)
items.explanation: خَالِدٍ is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ from الوَزيرِ. Since الوَزيرِ is genitive (after the preposition بِ), خَالِدٍ takes genitive with kasra and tanwīn. Unlike أَحمَد or عُمَر, the name خَالِد IS triptote (مُنصَرِف) — it takes full case endings with tanwīn. The preposition بِ passes its genitive effect through to the بدل. This demonstrates a key rule: the بدل follows the case of the مُبدَل مِنه, regardless of how that case was assigned.

items.question: رَأَيتُ الطِّفلَ _____ تَبكي (I saw the child, a girl crying)
items.explanation: بِنتًا is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ from الطِّفلَ, taking accusative to match. The indefinite بِنتًا (a girl) provides identifying information about the definite الطِّفلَ. تَبكي (she cries) is a حال جُملة describing the state. Interestingly, the بدل here shifts from masculine (طِفل) to feminine (بِنت), which is perfectly valid — the بدل reveals the true identity. This flexibility makes بدل powerful for surprise revelations and clarification in narration.

items.question: نَجَحَ الطُّلَّابُ _____ (The students succeeded, most of them)
items.explanation: مُعظَمُهُم (most of them) is بدل بَعض مِن كُلّ — a partial apposition referring to PART of الطُّلَّابُ. It takes nominative (ḍamma) to match الطُّلَّابُ. The pronoun هُم connects back to الطُّلَّابُ. Words commonly used in partial بدل: أَكثَرُ (most), بَعضُ (some), نِصفُ (half), ثُلُثُ (third), مُعظَمُ (majority). Each takes the case of the original noun and carries a pronoun referencing it.

items.question: أُعجِبتُ بِالشَّاعِرِ _____ (I was impressed by the poet, his style)
items.explanation: أُسلوبِهِ (his style) is بدل اشتمال (inclusive apposition) — referring to a quality or attribute of الشَّاعِرِ rather than a physical part. It takes genitive to match the case of الشَّاعِرِ (after بِ). بدل اشتمال differs from بدل بعض: the 'part' (style) isn't a physical component but an abstract quality that the poet 'encompasses.' Common in literary appreciation: أَحبَبتُ الكِتَابَ مَوضوعَهُ (I loved the book — its topic).

items.question: في القُرآنِ الكَريمِ _____ عَظيمَةٌ (In the Holy Quran, there are great lessons)
items.explanation: This sentence uses a fronted prepositional phrase (في القُرآنِ) with a delayed subject. عِبَرٌ (lessons/morals) is the mubtada' (subject) in nominative case with tanwīn. عَظيمَةٌ is its adjective, also nominative/indefinite. While not a بدل per se, this tests understanding of case assignment that applies to بدل — the subject takes nominative regardless of sentence position. In Arabic, fronted prepositional phrases create emphasis: the Quran is highlighted by being mentioned first.

items.question: كَلَّمتُ صَديقي _____ (I spoke to my friend, Ali)
items.explanation: عَليًّا is بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ from صَديقي. صَديقي is accusative (direct object of كَلَّمتُ), but the possessive suffix hides the case marker. عَليًّا takes accusative tanwīn to match. Note the shadda + tanwīn: عَلِيّ becomes عَلِيًّا in accusative. كَلَّمَ (Form II: to speak to) is a doubly transitive verb in some analyses. The بدل identifies which friend by name — a universally common use in daily Arabic conversation.

items.question: شَكَرتُ المُعَلِّمَةَ _____ (I thanked the teacher, Mrs. Fatima)
items.explanation: السَّيِّدَةَ فَاطِمَةَ is a two-part بدل كُلّ مِن كُلّ from المُعَلِّمَةَ. Both parts take accusative: السَّيِّدَةَ (with fatḥa) and فَاطِمَةَ (diptote, takes fatḥa for both accusative and genitive). Formal Arabic often uses title chains in apposition: the professional title, then the honorific, then the name. Each element matches the case of the original noun. This multi-layered بدل is standard in news, formal introductions, and official communications.

items.question: أَحَبَّ اللهُ عِبادَهُ _____ (God loved His servants, the believers)
items.explanation: المُؤمِنينَ is بدل بعض مِن كُلّ from عِبادَهُ (His servants). Since عِبادَ is accusative (direct object), المُؤمِنينَ takes accusative — sound masculine plurals use ين- in accusative/genitive. The believers are a subset of all servants, making this partial apposition. The accusative ين- ending is identical to genitive for sound masculine plurals — context (following an accusative noun as بدل) clarifies the case here.

items.question: قَرَأتُ الرِّوايَةَ _____ (I read the novel, half of it)
items.explanation: نِصفَها (half of it) is بدل بعض مِن كُلّ from الرِّوايَةَ. Since الرِّوايَةَ is accusative, نِصفَ takes accusative too. The pronoun ها refers back to الرِّوايَة. This partial بدل is practically very useful — it lets you state an action then specify its extent: أَكَلتُ الكَعكَةَ نِصفَها (I ate the cake, half of it), شَرِبتُ العَصيرَ بَعضَهُ (I drank the juice, some of it). Fractions and portions are natural partial apposition candidates.

items.question: سَمِعتُ المُغَنِّيَ _____ الرَّائِعَ (I heard the singer, his amazing voice)
items.explanation: صَوتَهُ (his voice) is بدل اشتمال (inclusive apposition) from المُغَنِّيَ. Since المُغَنِّيَ is accusative, صَوتَ takes accusative too. The adjective الرَّائِعَ (amazing) modifies صَوتَهُ and is also accusative. بدل اشتمال involves a quality that the original noun 'contains' — the singer encompasses his voice, personality, style. This type is prized in Arabic rhetoric for its ability to zoom in on what truly matters about a subject.

name: Nunation (التنوين)
description: Mastering tanwīn endings for indefinite nouns across all cases

items.question: في الفَصلِ _____ جَديدٌ (There is a new student in the class)
items.explanation: طَالِبٌ has tanwīn ḍamma (ٌ) marking an indefinite nominative noun. The tanwīn (nunation) adds an /n/ sound to the case vowel: ḍamma + n = -un. This signals two things simultaneously: (1) indefiniteness (no ال), (2) nominative case (subject/predicate). Here طَالِبٌ is the mubtada' mu'akhkhar (delayed subject) in a sentence beginning with a prepositional phrase. جَديدٌ agrees with it in case (nominative), indefiniteness, gender, and number.

items.question: قَرَأتُ _____ مُفيدًا (I read a useful book)
items.explanation: كِتَابًا has tanwīn fatḥa (ً) marking an indefinite accusative noun. The accusative tanwīn adds -an and is written with an extra alif on most masculine nouns: كِتَابًا. This alif is purely orthographic — it's not pronounced as a long vowel. The adjective مُفيدًا also takes tanwīn fatḥa to agree. Note: feminine nouns ending in ة and some special words don't add this alif: رِسَالَةً (a letter), not رِسَالَةًا. Also, words ending in ء preceded by alif already don't add it.

items.question: ذَهَبتُ إِلى _____ قَريبَةٍ (I went to a nearby school)
items.explanation: مَدرَسَةٍ has tanwīn kasra (ٍ) marking an indefinite genitive noun. After the preposition إِلى, the noun takes genitive case. The three tanwīn forms systematically encode case + indefiniteness: ٌ (nominative), ً (accusative), ٍ (genitive). The adjective قَريبَةٍ matches in all features. Tanwīn disappears when a noun becomes definite (المَدرَسَةِ) or enters an idāfa as the first element. This tanwīn system is uniquely Arabic — no other Semitic language preserves it so fully.

items.question: هَذا _____ كَبيرٌ (This is a big house)
items.explanation: بَيتٌ takes tanwīn ḍamma as an indefinite nominative predicate (خبر) after the demonstrative pronoun هَذا. In Arabic equational sentences (جُملة اسمية), the predicate is usually indefinite while the subject is definite. هَذا (this) is the subject (مُبتدأ) and بَيتٌ كَبيرٌ (a big house) is the predicate (خبر). The كَبيرٌ adjective takes tanwīn ḍamma matching the indefinite nominative بَيتٌ. This definite-subject + indefinite-predicate pattern is fundamental to Arabic sentence structure.

items.question: شَاهَدتُ _____ جَميلًا (I watched a beautiful sunset)
items.explanation: غُروبًا takes tanwīn fatḥa as an indefinite accusative direct object. The alif after tanwīn fatḥa (ا after ً) is a spelling convention for masculine nouns not ending in tā' marbūṭa (ة) or hamza on alif (اء). جَميلًا agrees with accusative tanwīn. This orthographic rule is important: كِتَابًا ✓ but مَدرَسَةً ✓ (no extra alif with ة), and سَماءً ✓ (no extra alif with اء). Mastering these spelling patterns ensures correct written Arabic.

items.question: تَكَلَّمَ مَعَ _____ مُهَذَّبَةٍ (He spoke with a polite girl)
items.explanation: فَتَاةٍ takes tanwīn kasra as an indefinite genitive noun after the preposition مَعَ (with). فَتَاة ends in ة (tā' marbūṭa), which is the standard feminine marker. The adjective مُهَذَّبَةٍ agrees in gender (feminine ة), case (genitive), number (singular), and definiteness (indefinite with tanwīn). The preposition مَعَ always puts the following noun in genitive. Note: some nouns ending in ة can be masculine (خَليفَة — caliph) — gender assignment in Arabic requires vocabulary knowledge.

items.question: هُوَ _____ ذَكِيٌّ (He is a smart man)
items.explanation: رَجُلٌ takes tanwīn ḍamma as an indefinite nominative predicate. In the equational sentence هُوَ رَجُلٌ ذَكِيٌّ, the pronoun هُوَ (he) is the subject and رَجُلٌ ذَكِيٌّ is the predicate. ذَكِيٌّ has both shadda and tanwīn — this combination (ٌّ) occurs when a word ends in a doubled consonant (ي + ي). The pronunciation is -iyyun. Writing tanwīn on shadda can be visually confusing but follows standard rules: the shadda represents the doubled letter, tanwīn represents case + indefiniteness.

items.question: أُريدُ _____ مِنَ الشَّاي (I want a cup of tea)
items.explanation: كَأسًا takes tanwīn fatḥa as an indefinite accusative direct object of أُريدُ (I want). مِنَ الشَّاي (of tea) is a prepositional phrase specifying the content. Note: كَأس ends with hamza preceded by a consonant — the tanwīn fatḥa alif is added: كَأسًا. Some grammarians debate whether كَأس is masculine or feminine (both are accepted). In modern Arabic, it's commonly feminine: كَأسٌ كَبيرَةٌ (a big cup). Gender ambiguity exists for several common nouns in Arabic.

items.question: وَصَلتُ بَعدَ _____ طَويلَةٍ (I arrived after a long journey)
items.explanation: رِحلَةٍ takes tanwīn kasra as an indefinite genitive noun — it's in genitive because it's the مُضاف إليه of بَعدَ (after). The adjective طَويلَةٍ agrees fully. Important: when a noun is in an idāfa as the FIRST element (مُضاف), it loses its tanwīn: سَيَّارَةُ أَبي (my father's car — no tanwīn on سيارة). But the second element (مُضاف إليه) can have tanwīn if indefinite: بَابُ غُرفَةٍ (a room's door — tanwīn on غرفة).

items.question: اِشتَرَيتُ _____ أَحمَرَ (I bought a red shirt)
items.explanation: قَميصًا takes tanwīn fatḥa as an indefinite accusative object. The adjective أَحمَرَ (red) does NOT take tanwīn because it's a diptote (ممنوع من الصرف) — colors on the pattern أَفعَل never take tanwīn. This is a crucial distinction: regular nouns get tanwīn when indefinite, but diptotes don't. Diptote categories include: colors (أَحمَر), comparative forms (أَكبَر), certain proper nouns (عُمَر, أَحمَد), and specific noun patterns. Tanwīn absence signals diptote status.

items.question: هَذِهِ قِصَّةٌ _____ (This is an interesting story)
items.explanation: مُمتِعَةٌ takes tanwīn ḍamma to agree with قِصَّةٌ — both indefinite nominative feminine singular. The demonstrative هَذِهِ (this, feminine) points to the subject. In Arabic, adjective agreement is rigorous: it must match in all four features. Since قِصَّة is (1) indefinite, (2) nominative, (3) feminine, (4) singular, the adjective must be all four: مُمتِعَةٌ ✓. If any feature didn't match, the sentence would be grammatically incorrect.

items.question: يَعيشُ في _____ صَغيرَةٍ (He lives in a small village)
items.explanation: قَريَةٍ takes tanwīn kasra as an indefinite genitive noun after في. The adjective صَغيرَةٍ matches. This sentence demonstrates a common Arabic structure: verb + prepositional phrase with an indefinite noun. The indefiniteness (shown by tanwīn) means 'a village' (unspecified), while القَريَةِ would mean 'THE village' (known/specific). This definite-indefinite distinction, marked by ال and tanwīn, is one of Arabic's most fundamental grammar concepts.

items.question: لَهُ _____ عَالٍ (He has a loud voice)
items.explanation: صَوتٌ takes tanwīn ḍamma as the subject (mubtada' mu'akhkhar) of a sentence beginning with لَهُ (he has — literally 'to him'). The adjective عَالٍ (high/loud) appears to have kasra, but it's actually nominative with tanwīn — عَالِيٌ loses its final ي before tanwīn, becoming عَالٍ (in nominative and genitive). In accusative it becomes عَالِيًا (the ي reappears). These 'defective' adjectives (ending in ي) have special tanwīn behavior.

items.question: رَأَيتُ _____ في السَّمَاءِ (I saw a bird in the sky)
items.explanation: طَائِرًا takes tanwīn fatḥa as an indefinite accusative direct object. Note the hamza seat: طَائِر has hamza on ا (ئ) because it follows a long vowel. The tanwīn fatḥa alif is added at the end: طَائِرًا. When reading aloud, the tanwīn sounds like -an at the end of a phrase or -a before the next word (in connected speech, the n assimilates before certain consonants). These pronunciation rules are important for proper Quranic recitation and formal Arabic.

items.question: أَرسَلَ _____ إِلى صَديقِهِ (He sent a letter to his friend)
items.explanation: رِسالَةً takes tanwīn fatḥa as an indefinite accusative object of أَرسَلَ (to send). Since رِسَالَة ends in ة, the tanwīn sits on the ة without adding an extra alif: رِسالَةً, NOT رِسالَةًا. This is a firm spelling rule. إِلى صَديقِهِ (to his friend) is the prepositional phrase indicating the recipient. The tanwīn here conveys 'a letter' (unspecified), suggesting it's mentioned for the first time. If previously discussed, you'd use الرِّسالَةَ (definite, no tanwīn).

name: Collective Nouns (اسم الجمع)
description: Understanding collective nouns and their singular unit forms in Arabic

items.question: _____ اللُّبنَانِيُّ لَذيذٌ (Lebanese apples are delicious — collective)
items.explanation: تُفَّاحُ (apples, collectively) is an اسم جَمع (collective noun) referring to the species/type as a whole. It's grammatically masculine singular even though it refers to many. Adding tā' marbūṭa creates the unit noun: تُفَّاحَة (one apple). This collective-unit system is distinctive to Arabic: شَجَر (trees) → شَجَرَة (one tree), بَقَر (cattle) → بَقَرَة (one cow), تَمر (dates) → تَمرَة (one date). The collective is treated as masculine singular for verb and adjective agreement.

items.question: اِشتَرَيتُ ثَلاثَ _____ (I bought three oranges)
items.explanation: بُرتُقَالَاتٍ is the plural of the unit noun بُرتُقَالَة (one orange). When counting 3-10 items, Arabic uses the plural of the unit noun. The collective بُرتُقَال (oranges in general) doesn't specify number — it's the whole category. To count: بُرتُقَالَةٌ واحِدَةٌ (1), بُرتُقَالَتانِ (2, dual), ثَلاثُ بُرتُقَالَاتٍ (3-10, plural unit). This three-tier system (collective → unit → unit plural) is essential for discussing quantities of natural items.

items.question: _____ العِرَاقِيُّ مَشهورٌ (Iraqi dates are famous — collective)
items.explanation: تَمرُ (dates, collectively) is treated as masculine singular for agreement: the adjective مَشهورٌ is masculine singular. The collective noun system is especially productive for plants, fruits, animals, and insects: نَحل (bees) → نَحلَة (one bee), نَمل (ants) → نَملَة (one ant), وَرد (roses) → وَردَة (one rose). The collective refers to the genus/kind, not a specific group. For a specific group, Arabic uses broken plurals or sound plurals.

items.question: وَقَعَت _____ مِن الشَّجَرَةِ (One leaf fell from the tree)
items.explanation: وَرَقَةٌ is the unit noun (اسم وحدة) — one leaf from the collective وَرَق (leaves/paper). The tā' marbūṭa (ة) transforms the collective into a singular countable unit. The verb وَقَعَت uses the feminine form because وَرَقَة is grammatically feminine (ة ending). This is why the collective/unit distinction matters for grammar: وَرَقٌ سَقَطَ (leaves fell — masculine singular verb) vs وَرَقَةٌ سَقَطَت (a leaf fell — feminine singular verb).

items.question: في الحَديقَةِ _____ كَثيرٌ (In the garden there are many flowers — collective)
items.explanation: زَهرٌ (flowers, collectively) is the collective noun. كَثيرٌ (many/much) agrees as masculine singular. The collective noun with كَثير creates a natural way to describe abundance. Note: Arabic has BOTH collective nouns (زَهر) AND broken plurals (زُهور/أَزهار). The collective refers to the generic type, while the broken plural refers to specific multiple items. This distinction is subtle: زَهرُ هَذِهِ الحَديقَةِ (this garden's flowers, generic) vs الزُّهورُ الَّتي اشتَرَيتُها (the flowers I bought, specific).

items.question: أَحَبُّ أَن آكُلَ _____ الطَّازَجَ (I love eating fresh fish — collective)
items.explanation: سَمَكَ is the collective noun for fish. One fish: سَمَكَة. Many fish (countable): أَسمَاك (broken plural) or سَمَكَات (sound feminine plural of the unit noun). Here the collective sَمَك is used because the speaker means fish as food/category, not specific individual fish. الطَّازَجَ agrees as masculine singular accusative adjective. When discussing food, Arabic typically uses collective nouns: لَحم (meat), دَجَاج (chicken), خُبز (bread) — these are uncountable collectives.

items.question: لَسَعَتني _____ في الحَديقَةِ (A bee stung me in the garden)
items.explanation: نَحلَةٌ (one bee) is the unit noun from the collective نَحل (bees). The feminine verb لَسَعَتني uses -ت for the feminine subject. The Quran famously addresses the bee: 'وَأَوحَى رَبُّكَ إِلى النَّحلِ' (And your Lord inspired the bee — 16:68) using the collective النَّحل. For animal collective nouns, the unit form with ة is used for individuals: فَراشَة (one butterfly, from فَراش), ذُبَابَة (one fly, from ذُبَاب), دودَة (one worm, from دود).

items.question: هَذا _____ عَالي الجَودَةِ (This is high-quality wood — collective)
items.explanation: خَشَبٌ (wood, collectively) is a material collective noun. خَشَبَة means one piece/plank of wood. Material collectives behave like English uncountable nouns: حَجَر (stone/rocks), طوب (bricks), حَديد (iron). عَالي الجَودَةِ (high quality) is an idāfa compound adjective. The demonstrative هَذا confirms masculine singular agreement with the collective. Material collectives are common in construction, cooking, and craft vocabulary.

items.question: زَرَعَ الفَلَّاحُ خَمسَ _____ (The farmer planted five trees)
items.explanation: شَجَرَاتٍ is the sound feminine plural of شَجَرَة (one tree), used here because numbers 3-10 require the plural of the unit noun. The collective شَجَر would be wrong for counting. Arabic number rules: خَمسَ + feminine plural = correct for counting feminine unit nouns. The cardinal number خَمس takes the OPPOSITE gender of the counted noun — شَجَرَة is feminine, so خَمسَ (without tā' marbūṭa). This counter-intuitive gender polarity in numbers 3-10 is a unique Arabic feature.

items.question: _____ هَذِهِ المِنطَقَةِ طَويلٌ (The grass of this area is tall — collective)
items.explanation: عُشبُ (grass/herbs, collectively) is a collective noun treated as masculine singular. The adjective طَويلٌ agrees. عُشبُ is the first element of an idāfa (عُشبُ هَذِهِ المِنطَقَةِ — the grass of this area), so it loses tanwīn. هَذِهِ المِنطَقَةِ is genitive as مُضاف إليه. Plant-related collective nouns are abundant in Arabic: قَمح (wheat), أَرُزّ (rice), قَصَب (sugarcane), قُطن (cotton). These don't typically have unit forms with ة.

items.question: في البُحَيرَةِ _____ كَثيرٌ (In the lake there are many fish — collective)
items.explanation: سَمَكٌ (fish, collectively) with كَثيرٌ (much/many) agrees as masculine singular. This is the same pattern as English 'there is much fish' (uncountable). The choice between collective and plural depends on whether you view items as a mass or as individuals. In البُحَيرَةِ سَمَكٌ كَثيرٌ views fish as a category, while في البُحَيرَةِ أَسمَاكٌ كَثيرَةٌ would emphasize individual fish. This semantic distinction through grammar is a powerful feature of Arabic.

items.question: وَجَدتُ _____ في الطَّعَامِ (I found a hair in the food)
items.explanation: شَعرَةً (one hair) is the unit noun from شَعر (hair, collectively). The accusative tanwīn fatḥa marks it as the direct object. شَعر without ة means 'hair' in general (like English uncountable 'hair'): شَعرُها جَميلٌ (her hair is beautiful). But شَعرَة means one specific strand. This distinction exists naturally in many Arabic daily situations: حَبّ (grain/seeds) → حَبَّة (one grain), لَبَن (milk — no unit form, pure mass noun).

items.question: يَأكُلُ الجَمَلُ _____ الصَّحرَاءِ (The camel eats the plants of the desert — collective)
items.explanation: نَبَاتَ (plants/vegetation, collectively) is the collective noun for plants. It's the first element of an idāfa (نَبَاتَ الصَّحرَاءِ — plants of the desert), and also the direct object (accusative). الصَّحرَاءِ (desert) is genitive as مُضاف إليه — note that words ending in اء take kasra in genitive without tanwīn (diptote). Some collective nouns like نَبَات have unit forms (نَبَاتَة — one plant), while others like عُشب primarily use the collective without a standard unit form.

items.question: رَأَيتُ سِربًا مِنَ _____ (I saw a flock of birds — collective)
items.explanation: الطَّيرِ (birds, collectively) is used after مِن to specify what the flock consists of. مِنَ puts it in genitive: الطَّيرِ. The collective طَير covers all birds as a group — one bird is طَائِر (active participle of طَارَ 'to fly'). Note: the unit form here is NOT طَيرَة but طَائِر — not all collectives use the ة pattern. الطُّيور is the broken plural of طَائِر. With سِرب (flock), Arabic typically uses the collective: سِربٌ مِنَ الطَّيرِ, not مِنَ الطُّيورِ.

items.question: _____ المِصرِيُّ جَيِّدٌ (Egyptian cotton is good — collective)
items.explanation: قُطنُ (cotton, collectively) is a mass/material collective noun — it refers to cotton as a substance. There's no common unit form قُطنَة in standard usage because cotton isn't naturally countable. المِصرِيُّ is a nisba adjective (relating to مِصر — Egypt) agreeing with the masculine singular collective. Material collectives like قُطن, حَرير (silk), صوف (wool), كَتَّان (linen) behave like English uncountable nouns and don't form unit nouns with ة.

name: Diptote Nouns (الممنوع من الصرف)
description: Nouns that resist tanwīn and take fatḥa instead of kasra in genitive

items.question: سَافَرتُ إِلى _____ (I traveled to Egypt)
items.explanation: مِصرَ is a diptote proper noun (ممنوع من الصرف) — in genitive case after إِلى, it takes fatḥa instead of the normal kasra. Diptotes have only two case markers (hence 'diptote'): ḍamma for nominative and fatḥa for BOTH accusative and genitive. They never take tanwīn. Most foreign/non-Arabic place names are diptote: لُندَن, بَاريس, طوكيو. Arabic place names are often diptote too: دِمَشق, بَغدَاد. This is one of Arabic grammar's most distinctive features.

items.question: تَكَلَّمتُ مَعَ _____ (I spoke with Omar)
items.explanation: عُمَرَ is diptote because it follows the pattern فُعَل — certain Arabic name/noun patterns are automatically diptote. After مَعَ (with), the genitive would normally need kasra, but diptotes use fatḥa: عُمَرَ not عُمَرِ. Common diptote name patterns: فُعَل (عُمَر, زُفَر), أَفعَل (أَحمَد, أَسعَد), فَعلان (عُثمَان, رَمَضَان). Female names are almost always diptote: فَاطِمَة, عَائِشَة, مَريَم. Understanding diptote patterns prevents common case-marking errors.

items.question: هَذا البَيتُ _____ مِنَ المَدرَسَةِ (This house is bigger than the school)
items.explanation: أَكبَرُ (bigger) is diptote because it follows the comparative/superlative pattern أَفعَل. Here it's nominative (predicate of the equational sentence) taking ḍamma — but WITHOUT tanwīn. Diptotes in nominative: أَكبَرُ (not أَكبَرٌ), in accusative: أَكبَرَ (not أَكبَرًا), in genitive: أَكبَرَ (not أَكبَرِ). All comparative/superlative forms are diptote: أَصغَرُ (smaller), أَجمَلُ (more beautiful), أَفضَلُ (better). This pattern is crucial for comparisons.

items.question: اِشتَرَيتُ قَميصًا _____ (I bought a red shirt)
items.explanation: أَحمَرَ (red) is a diptote adjective — colors on the pattern أَفعَل are always diptote. As an accusative adjective modifying قَميصًا, it takes fatḥa without tanwīn: أَحمَرَ (not أَحمَرًا). All basic color adjectives are diptote: أَزرَقَ (blue), أَخضَرَ (green), أَصفَرَ (yellow), أَبيَضَ (white), أَسوَدَ (black). Their feminine forms use the pattern فَعلاء: حَمراء, زَرقاء, خَضراء — also diptote. Colors are the most commonly encountered diptote adjectives in daily Arabic.

items.question: صَلَّينَا في _____ جَميلَةٍ (We prayed in beautiful mosques)
items.explanation: مَسَاجِدَ (mosques) is a broken plural on the pattern مَفَاعِل — this pattern is ALWAYS diptote. After في (in), the genitive normally takes kasra, but diptote plurals take fatḥa: مَسَاجِدَ (not مَسَاجِدِ). Key diptote plural patterns: مَفَاعِل (مَسَاجِد, مَدَارِس, مَكاتِب), مَفَاعيل (مَفاتيح, مَصابيح), فَعَائِل (رَسائِل, حَدائِق). These patterns cover a huge number of common Arabic plurals. Recognizing them instantly improves your case-marking accuracy.

items.question: مَرَرتُ بِ_____ كَثيرَةٍ (I passed by many schools)
items.explanation: مَدارِسَ (schools) follows the diptote pattern مَفَاعِل. After بِ (by/through), it takes fatḥa (not kasra) in genitive. CRITICAL EXCEPTION: when a diptote noun gets ال (definite article), it behaves like a normal triptote — المَدارِسِ would take kasra. Also, when the first element of an idāfa, it acts normally: مَدارِسُ المَدينَةِ (the city's schools — ḍamma). Diptote behavior only applies when the noun is indefinite and not in idāfa. This exception is frequently tested.

items.question: وَصَلنَا إِلى _____ في المَسَاءِ (We arrived at Beirut in the evening)
items.explanation: بَيروتَ is a diptote proper noun — most city/country names are diptote in Arabic. After إِلى, it takes fatḥa in genitive. Exceptions: some city names ARE triptote — مِصرُ (Egypt — debated), حِمصٌ (Homs), حَلَبٌ (Aleppo). The rule: foreign names and names that are feminine or follow specific patterns are diptote. When in doubt with place names, diptote is the safer assumption. في المَسَاءِ (in the evening) uses normal genitive because المساء is triptote and definite.

items.question: مَرَرتُ بِنِسَاءَ _____ (I passed by kind women)
items.explanation: Wait — this tests a contrast. نِسَاء (women) IS diptote (pattern فِعَال ending in hamza), taking fatḥa in genitive: بِنِسَاءَ. But لَطيفَاتٍ is a sound feminine plural — these are TRIPTOTE and take normal kasra with tanwīn. Sound feminine plurals are never diptote. So the diptote rule applies to نساء but not to the adjective لطيفات. This mix of diptote noun + triptote adjective is common and requires analyzing each word independently for its diptote status.

items.question: كَانَت _____ جَميلَةً (Fatima was beautiful)
items.explanation: فَاطِمَةُ is a diptote feminine proper name — nominative (subject of كَانَ) takes ḍamma WITHOUT tanwīn. Female proper names ending in ة are diptote for two reasons combined: they're proper nouns AND feminine. In genitive: فَاطِمَةَ (fatḥa, not kasra). In accusative: فَاطِمَةَ (same as genitive — diptote feature). جَميلَةً (beautiful) takes accusative tanwīn because it's the predicate of كَانَ (خبر كان) and is a regular adjective, not a diptote. The verb كَانَت matches the feminine subject.

items.question: زُرتُ _____ كَثيرَةً (I visited many countries)
items.explanation: بُلدَانًا — wait! بُلدان might appear diptote (pattern فُعلان), but as a common noun (not proper), the pattern فُعلان for broken plurals IS diptote only when it's a proper noun or adjective. Actually, بُلدان as a plural IS sometimes treated as diptote (no tanwīn, fatḥa in genitive) because of the ان ending. However, many modern grammarians accept tanwīn on it. This is one of Arabic grammar's debated edges — both forms appear in literature.

items.question: أَعجَبتني _____ بَاريسَ (I liked the streets of Paris)
items.explanation: شَوارِعُ (streets) is diptote (pattern فَواعِل) but here it's the first element of an idāfa (شَوارِعُ بَاريسَ). When a diptote noun is مُضاف, it takes normal case endings without tanwīn (but tanwīn absence is expected for any مُضاف anyway). So شَوارِعُ takes ḍamma as nominative subject of أَعجَبَتني. بَاريسَ is diptote in genitive (مُضاف إليه). Both words are diptote but for different reasons: شَوارِع (plural pattern) and بَاريس (foreign name).

items.question: الجَوُّ في _____ حَارٌّ (The weather in August is hot)
items.explanation: آبَ (August in Levantine Arabic) is a diptote proper noun — month names are typically diptote. After في, it takes fatḥa in genitive. Most Arabic/Levantine month names are diptote: كانونُ, شُبَاطُ, نيسَانُ, تَمُّوزُ, آبُ, أَيلولُ. The Hijri months also follow diptote patterns: رَمَضَانَ (pattern فَعَلان — diptote), شَعبَانَ, رَجَبَ. Understanding month name declension is practical for writing dates and discussing time periods in formal Arabic.

items.question: لَيسَ هُنَاكَ أَفضَلُ مِن _____ (There is nothing better than Ibrahim)
items.explanation: إِبراهيمَ is diptote — a proper name that is also non-Arabic in origin (Hebrew). After مِن, it takes fatḥa (not kasra) in genitive. Names of prophets from non-Arabic origins are diptote: إِبرَاهيم, إِسمَاعيل, إِسحَاق, يوسُف, يَعقوب, موسَى, عيسَى. Arabic-origin prophet names may be triptote or diptote depending on their pattern: مُحَمَّدٌ is triptote, أَحمَدُ is diptote. This distinction helps with proper Quranic recitation.

items.question: أَعطَيتُ الهَدِيَّةَ لِ_____ الجُدُدِ (I gave the gift to the new friends)
items.explanation: أَصدِقَاءَ (friends) follows the pattern أَفعِلاء — a broken plural pattern that is diptote. After لِ (for/to), the genitive takes fatḥa: أَصدِقَاءَ (not أَصدِقَاءِ). الجُدُدِ (new, plural) is added as an adjective — since أَصدِقَاء is diptote/indefinite, but الجُدُدِ is definite with ال, there's a mismatch. In practice, this would need لِلأَصدِقَاءِ الجُدُدِ (both definite) or لِأَصدِقَاءَ جُدُدٍ (both indefinite). Grammar exercises highlight such agreement requirements.

items.question: في المُتحَفِ _____ أَثَرِيَّةٌ (In the museum there are archaeological artifacts)
items.explanation: تَحَفٌ (artifacts/masterpieces) is the plural of تُحفَة. This is a triptote plural (pattern فُعَل), taking normal tanwīn — NOT diptote. This tests the ability to distinguish diptote from triptote patterns. فُعَل pattern plurals ARE triptote: غُرَفٌ (rooms), صُوَرٌ (pictures), نُقَطٌ (points). Compare with diptote مَفَاعِل pattern: مَتَاحِفَ (museums). Knowing which plural patterns are diptote vs triptote is essential — memorize the handful of diptote patterns and assume everything else is triptote.

name: Five Special Nouns (الأسماء الخمسة)
description: The unique declension of أب, أخ, حم, فو, ذو in Arabic

items.question: جَاءَ _____ مِنَ السَّفَرِ (Your father came from traveling)
items.explanation: أَبوكَ (your father) uses the special nominative form of أَب — one of the five special nouns (الأسماء الخمسة). In nominative: أَبو + suffix. These five nouns (أب father, أخ brother, حم father-in-law, فو mouth, ذو possessor) show case through long vowels when attached to a possessive suffix: nominative -و (ū), accusative -ا (ā), genitive -ي (ī). So: أَبوكَ (nom.), أَبَاكَ (acc.), أَبيكَ (gen.). This elongated case marking is unique to these five nouns.
