items.question: رَأَيتُ _____ في السُّوقِ (I saw your brother in the market)
items.explanation: أَخَاكَ (your brother) uses the special accusative form of أَخ — with long ā before the suffix. As the direct object of رَأَيتُ, أخ takes the accusative marker: أَخَا + كَ. Compare the three cases: أَخوكَ (nominative — your brother came), أَخَاكَ (accusative — I saw your brother), أَخيكَ (genitive — I went to your brother's house). This vowel-based case system only works when these nouns have a possessive suffix — without suffixes, they use short endings: أَبٌ, أَخٌ.

items.question: تَكَلَّمتُ مَعَ _____ (I spoke with your father-in-law)
items.explanation: حَميكِ (your father-in-law) uses the genitive form of حَم with the feminine possessive suffix كِ (your, feminine — since a woman's father-in-law is meant). After مَعَ (with), genitive case applies: حَمِي + كِ. حَم (father-in-law/relative by marriage) is less commonly encountered than أَب or أَخ but follows identical rules. The five nouns' special declension only applies when: (1) they're singular, (2) followed by a possessive suffix OTHER than ي (my). With ي: أَبي stays as أَبي in all cases.

items.question: هَذا رَجُلٌ _____ خُلُقٍ حَسَنٍ (This is a man of good character)
items.explanation: ذو (possessor of) takes nominative form here as part of the predicate. ذو is unique among the five nouns because it's NEVER followed by a pronoun suffix — only by a noun (idāfa). It declines: ذو (nom.), ذَا (acc.), ذي (gen.), without any possessive suffix. خُلُقٍ حَسَنٍ (good character) is the مُضاف إليه. ذو is extremely useful: ذو مَالٍ (wealthy — possessor of money), ذو عِلمٍ (knowledgeable), ذَاتُ (feminine form). The Quran uses ذو frequently.

items.question: أَغلِقْ _____ (Close your mouth!)
items.explanation: فَاكَ (your mouth) uses the accusative form of فو — the fifth special noun. As the direct object of the imperative أَغلِقْ, it takes accusative: فَا + كَ. فو (mouth) becomes: فوكَ (nom.), فَاكَ (acc.), فيكَ (gen.). In modern Arabic, فَم (mouth) is more commonly used in daily speech, but فو appears in classical texts and set phrases. The imperative أَغلِقْ has sukūn on the final consonant — imperative verbs end in sukūn in the singular masculine form.

items.question: _____ مُهَندِسٌ نَاجِحٌ (Her brother is a successful engineer)
items.explanation: أَخوهَا (her brother) is nominative — subject of the equational sentence — so أَخ takes the long vowel و (ū). The possessive suffix هَا (her) triggers the five-noun special declension. مُهَندِسٌ نَاجِحٌ (a successful engineer) is the predicate, both nominative with tanwīn. Without a suffix, أَخ would be أَخٌ (normal triptote). The special declension only activates with suffixes other than ي: أَخي (my brother) stays the same in all three cases.

items.question: سَافَرَ مَعَ _____ إِلى أُورُوبَّا (He traveled with his father to Europe)
items.explanation: أَبيهِ (his father) uses the genitive form of أَب after مَعَ (with). The genitive marker is long ī: أَبِي + هِ. Note the suffix هِ (his) — this is the masculine genitive/accusative possessive pronoun. أُورُوبَّا (Europe) is a diptote foreign name, genitive after إِلى. All five conditions for the special declension are met: (1) singular, (2) مُضاف to a suffix, (3) that suffix is not ي, (4) the noun is one of the five, (5) it's not in diminutive form.

items.question: إِنَّ _____ كَريمٌ جِدًّا (Indeed your father is very generous)
items.explanation: أَبَاكَ (your father) is accusative because إِنَّ puts its subject (اسم إنّ) in the accusative case. So أَب takes the long ā vowel: أَبَا + كَ. This inna-sisters rule overrides the default nominative for subjects. كَريمٌ (generous) is the predicate of إِنَّ (خبر إنّ) and remains nominative. جِدًّا (very) is an absolute object/adverb intensifier. This sentence tests two concepts: five-noun declension AND إِنَّ's effect on case.

items.question: هَلْ رَأَيتَ _____ اليَومَ؟ (Did you see your brother today?)
items.explanation: أَخَاكَ is accusative (direct object of رَأَيتَ) — the long ā marks accusative case. هَلْ is a yes/no question particle that doesn't change the grammar of the sentence. اليَومَ (today) is a ظرف زمان in accusative. The question particle هَلْ or أَ simply converts a statement to a question without affecting case — رَأَيتُ أَخَاكَ (I saw your brother) → هَلْ رَأَيتَ أَخَاكَ (Did you see your brother?). Questions are formed easily in Arabic through particles.

items.question: ذَهَبتُ إِلى بَيتِ _____ (I went to your brother's house)
items.explanation: أَخيكَ (your brother) is genitive because it's the مُضاف إليه of بَيتِ (house). The genitive form uses long ī: أَخِي + كَ. This creates a chain of idāfas: بَيتِ أَخيكَ (your brother's house). Multiple idāfa chains are common in Arabic: مَكتَبُ أَبيكَ (your father's office), سَيَّارَةُ أَخيهَا (her brother's car). Each مُضاف takes its case from its role in the sentence; each مُضاف إليه is automatically genitive.

items.question: لا يَعرِفُ _____ مَن هُوَ (He doesn't know who his father-in-law is)
items.explanation: حَمَاهُ (his father-in-law) is accusative — direct object of يَعرِفُ — taking the long ā: حَمَا + هُ. لا before a present tense verb simply negates it without changing the mood. مَن هُوَ (who he is) is an interrogative clause serving as a second object or clarification. حَم is the rarest of the five special nouns in modern usage, as حَمو or even حَماة (mother-in-law) are more commonly heard in dialects. But the grammatical pattern remains important for classical Arabic.

items.question: كَانَ _____ طَبيبًا (His father was a doctor)
items.explanation: أَبوهُ is nominative — it's the subject of كَانَ (اسم كان), which stays nominative. The long و marks nominative: أَبُو + هُ. طَبيبًا is the predicate of كَانَ (خبر كان) in accusative. This tests the interaction between كَانَ and the five nouns: كَانَ doesn't change the subject's case (still nominative), but the predicate becomes accusative. Compare: أَبوهُ طَبيبٌ (his father is a doctor — both nominative) vs كَانَ أَبوهُ طَبيبًا (his father was a doctor — nom. + acc.).

items.question: هَذِهِ السَّيِّدَةُ _____ عِلمٍ واسِعٍ (This lady is of vast knowledge)
items.explanation: ذَاتُ is the feminine form of ذو, used because السَّيِّدَةُ (the lady) is feminine. It takes nominative case (ḍamma) as part of the predicate. ذَات declines: ذَاتُ (nom.), ذَاتَ (acc.), ذَاتِ (gen.). Unlike ذو, ذَات can also take pronoun suffixes: ذَاتُها (herself — used reflexively). عِلمٍ واسِعٍ (vast knowledge) is the مُضاف إليه chain. ذو/ذَات + noun is the elegant Arabic way to express 'possessing a quality': ذو مَالٍ (wealthy), ذَاتُ جَمَالٍ (beautiful).

items.question: لَعَلَّ _____ سَيَأتي غَدًا (Perhaps your father will come tomorrow)
items.explanation: أَبَاكَ uses accusative because لَعَلَّ (perhaps/maybe) is one of the إِنَّ sisters — it puts its subject in the accusative case. So أَب takes ā: أَبَا + كَ. سَيَأتي (will come) is the predicate. All six إِنَّ sisters (إِنَّ, أَنَّ, كَأَنَّ, لَكِنَّ, لَيتَ, لَعَلَّ) put the five nouns in accusative with the long ā vowel. This interaction between two grammar topics (inna sisters + five nouns) is a common exam question in Arabic grammar courses.

items.question: تَقَدَّمَ الرَّجُلُ _____ المَالِ لِلمَشروعِ (The man of wealth stepped forward for the project)
items.explanation: ذو is nominative here — it's a صِفة (adjective/descriptor) for الرَّجُلُ, which is the subject (nominative). As a descriptor, it must match in case: الرَّجُلُ ذو → both nominative. المَالِ (wealth) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. لِلمَشروعِ (for the project) uses لِ + genitive. ذو is unique among Arabic nouns because it functions purely as a linking device — it has no independent meaning but connects its possessor to the quality. Arabic grammarians call it اسم ملازم للإضافة (a noun inherently bound to idāfa).

name: Verbal Sentences (الجملة الفعلية)
description: Structure and word order variations in Arabic verbal sentences

items.question: _____ الطُّلَّابُ الدَّرسَ (The students understood the lesson)
items.explanation: In a verbal sentence (الجملة الفعلية), when the verb comes BEFORE the subject, it is always SINGULAR — regardless of the subject's number. الطُّلَّابُ is plural, but the preceding verb فَهِمَ stays singular masculine. This is a fundamental rule: فَهِمَ الطُّلَّابُ (the students understood) — singular verb. Compare: الطُّلَّابُ فَهِموا (starting with subject — now the verb DOES match in number). This verb-subject agreement rule in VSO order is one of Arabic's most distinctive features.

items.question: _____ البَنَاتُ إِلى المَدرَسَةِ (The girls went to school)
items.explanation: ذَهَبَت (she went) — when the verb precedes a feminine plural subject, it takes feminine SINGULAR form, not feminine plural. So ذَهَبَت البَنَاتُ (the girls went) uses third person feminine singular. If the sentence were البَنَاتُ ذَهَبنَ (girls-first order), the verb would match the plural. This gender agreement IS required (feminine subject → feminine verb), but number agreement is not (plural subject → singular verb in V-S order). Gender yes, number no — in verb-subject order.

items.question: يَكتُبُ المُعَلِّمُ _____ عَلى السَّبُّورَةِ (The teacher writes the lesson on the board)
items.explanation: الدَّرسَ is the direct object (مفعول به) in accusative case. The verbal sentence structure here is: verb (يَكتُبُ) + subject (المُعَلِّمُ) + object (الدَّرسَ) + adverbial (عَلى السَّبُّورَةِ). This V-S-O order is the default/neutral word order in Arabic. Each position has a specific case: verb (conjugated), subject (nominative), object (accusative), prepositional phrases (genitive after preposition). Understanding this default order helps identify deviations used for emphasis.

items.question: لَم _____ الطَّالِبُ الإِجابَةَ (The student did not know the answer)
items.explanation: لَم puts the present tense verb into jussive mood (مجزوم), changing the meaning to past negation. يَعرِفُ becomes يَعرِفْ — the final ḍamma changes to sukūn. لَم + مضارع مجزوم = past negation. The verb stays singular because it precedes the plural subject (if plural). لَم is the most common way to negate the past in modern Arabic — more common than مَا + past tense in everyday usage. The jussive mood affects ONLY the final vowel of the verb.

items.question: سَ_____ الأَولَادُ في الحَديقَةِ غَدًا (The children will play in the park tomorrow)
items.explanation: سَيَلعَبُ — the prefix سَ marks near future. The verb stays masculine singular even though الأَولَادُ is plural, because the verb precedes the subject. If reversed: الأَولَادُ سَيَلعَبونَ (they will play). غَدًا (tomorrow) is an adverb in accusative. في الحَديقَةِ is a locative prepositional phrase. Future verbal sentences maintain the same V-S-O order and the same singular-verb-before-plural-subject rule. The rule applies to past, present, and future tenses equally.

items.question: أَكَلَ الوَلَدُ الطَّعَامَ _____ (The boy ate the food hungrily)
items.explanation: جَائِعًا (hungry/hungrily) is a حال (circumstantial expression) — it describes the state of the subject WHILE performing the action. The حال is always accusative and typically indefinite. The verbal sentence structure is: verb + subject + object + حال. The حال answers 'in what state/condition?' — جاء مُبتَسِمًا (he came smiling), عَادَ مُتعَبًا (he returned tired), نَامَ هَادِئًا (he slept calmly). This accusative حال is one of Arabic's most elegant grammatical features.

items.question: _____ القِطَّةُ الحَليبَ (The cat drank the milk)
items.explanation: شَرِبَتِ uses feminine singular because القِطَّةُ (the cat) is a feminine noun. The kasra on تِ is a helping vowel before the next consonant (ال). When verb precedes subject: gender agreement is REQUIRED but number agreement is not. So for a feminine subject, the verb must be feminine — شَرِبَتِ not شَرِبَ. For a feminine plural subject like القِطَطُ: شَرِبَتِ القِطَطُ (same feminine singular verb). Gender is always marked; number only in subject-first order.

items.question: قَد _____ العُمَّالُ مِن بِنَاءِ المَبنَى (The workers have finished building the building)
items.explanation: قَد + past tense = emphasis/confirmation that something has happened. انتَهَى stays singular (despite العُمَّالُ being plural) because the verb precedes the subject. قَد has two functions: with past tense it means 'indeed/already' (emphasis), with present tense it means 'maybe/sometimes' (possibility). مِن بِنَاءِ (from building) uses the masdar بِنَاء after مِن. المَبنَى (the building — passive participle used as noun) is the مُضاف إليه of بِنَاء.

items.question: لَن _____ المُوَظَّفونَ اليَومَ (The employees will not work today)
items.explanation: لَن negates the future and puts the verb in subjunctive mood (منصوب): يَعمَلُ → يَعمَلَ. The verb stays singular before a plural subject. لَن signals definitive future negation — 'will never/not.' The subjunctive changes the final vowel: ḍamma → fatḥa for sound verbs. For the five special verb forms (يَعمَلونَ, etc.), the subjunctive drops the نون: لَن يَعمَلوا (if SVO order). Compare: لَم يَعمَلْ (past negation, jussive) vs لَن يَعمَلَ (future negation, subjunctive).

items.question: _____ المَطَرُ مُنذُ الصَّبَاحِ (The rain has been falling since morning)
items.explanation: يَهطِلُ (it falls/rains) — المَطَرُ is masculine, so the verb takes masculine form يَ- prefix. مُنذُ (since) is followed by a genitive noun. This sentence uses the present tense with مُنذُ to express an ongoing action from a past point — similar to English present perfect continuous. Arabic doesn't have a separate verb form for this; context and time markers (مُنذُ, مُنذُ أَن) create the continuous meaning. The verb preceding المَطَرُ is singular, as expected in VSO order.

items.question: إِنَّمَا _____ المُعَلِّمَةُ الحَقيقَةَ (The teacher only told the truth)
items.explanation: إِنَّمَا (only/merely) is a restrictive particle that doesn't change the verb's case or mood — the sentence remains a verbal sentence with verb-subject order. قَالَتِ uses feminine form to match المُعَلِّمَةُ. The Quran frequently uses إِنَّمَا for restriction: إِنَّمَا يَخشَى اللهَ مِن عِبَادِهِ العُلَمَاءُ (Only the scholars among His servants fear God). إِنَّمَا doesn't change grammar — it adds meaning. The verb is still conjugated normally for the verbal sentence.

items.question: أَينَ _____ الأَطفَالُ؟ (Where did the children go?)
items.explanation: أَينَ (where?) is an interrogative adverb that begins a question without changing the verb's form. ذَهَبَ stays singular before الأَطفَالُ (the children) — standard VSO rule. Question words in Arabic don't trigger any special verb forms — unlike English inversion (where did they go?). Arabic simply places the question word first: أَينَ ذَهَبَ الأَطفَالُ? = Where + verb + subject. Other question words follow the same pattern: مَتَى جَاءَ? (when?), كَيفَ وَصَلَ? (how?), لِمَاذا فَعَلَ? (why?)

items.question: _____ الطَّالِبَتَانِ لِلاِمتِحَانِ (The two female students prepared for the exam)
items.explanation: اِستَعَدَّتِ — feminine singular verb before the dual subject الطَّالِبَتَانِ. Even though the subject is dual, the preceding verb stays singular (matching gender only). If the order were reversed: الطَّالِبَتَانِ اِستَعَدَّتَا (dual verb ending تَا). The verb اِستَعَدَّ (Form X: to prepare) shows the pattern اِستَفعَلَ. لِلاِمتِحَانِ uses لِ (for) + genitive. The dual subject ending -انِ indicates nominative case; in accusative/genitive it would be الطَّالِبَتَينِ.

items.question: مَا _____ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الاجتِمَاعِ (No one left the meeting)
items.explanation: مَا + past tense negates the past: مَا خَرَجَ = 'did not leave.' أَحَدٌ (anyone/no one — in negative context) is the subject taking nominative with tanwīn. مِنَ الاجتِمَاعِ (from the meeting) is a prepositional phrase. مَا for past negation is more classical/formal than لَم + jussive, but both are correct. In Quranic and literary Arabic, مَا is preferred. The verbal sentence here has a simple structure: negation particle + verb + indefinite subject + prepositional phrase.

items.question: هَل _____ الضُّيوفُ بَعدُ؟ (Have the guests arrived yet?)
items.explanation: هَل + verb-subject order: وَصَلَ stays singular before الضُّيوفُ (the guests, plural). بَعدُ (yet — with ḍamma because it's cut from its مُضاف إليه) completes the question. هَل is a yes/no question particle — it expects a نَعَم (yes) or لا (no) answer. Compare with أَ (another yes/no particle): أَوَصَلَ الضُّيوفُ؟ The verb-before-plural-subject singular agreement rule applies in questions just as in statements — question particles don't override it.

name: Nominal Sentences (الجملة الاسمية)
description: Structure and variations of Arabic sentences beginning with a noun

items.question: الطَّقسُ _____ اليَومَ (The weather is nice today)
items.explanation: In a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية), the structure is: subject (مُبتدأ) + predicate (خبر). الطَّقسُ is the مُبتدأ (nominative with ḍamma), and جَميلٌ is the خبر (also nominative with tanwīn ḍamma). The خبر is typically indefinite when the مُبتدأ is definite — this definite-indefinite contrast is how Arabic identifies subject from predicate in equational sentences. Without a verb, Arabic uses this structure for 'is/are' statements — no Arabic word for 'is' is needed.

items.question: _____ نَشيطونَ في العَمَلِ (The workers are active at work)
items.explanation: العُمَّالُ is the مُبتدأ (subject) of a nominal sentence — definite and nominative. نَشيطونَ (active, masculine plural) is the خبر, also nominative but indefinite. When a nominal sentence starts with the subject, the predicate (if a verb or verb phrase) must agree in BOTH gender AND number: العُمَّالُ يَعمَلونَ (they work), البَنَاتُ يَدرُسنَ (the girls study). This contrasts with verbal sentences where only gender agreement applies.

items.question: المَكتَبَةُ _____ الجَامِعَةِ (The library is next to the university)
items.explanation: بِجَانِبِ الجَامِعَةِ (next to the university) is a شبه جملة (semi-sentence / prepositional phrase) functioning as the خبر. The خبر of a nominal sentence can be: (1) a single word: البَيتُ كَبيرٌ, (2) a prepositional phrase: الكِتَابُ عَلى الطَّاوِلَةِ, (3) a verbal sentence: الطِّفلُ يَلعَبُ, (4) a nominal sentence: البَيتُ حَديقَتُهُ واسِعَةٌ. This flexibility makes the خبر one of Arabic's most versatile grammatical elements.

items.question: الحَياةُ _____ (Life is short)
items.explanation: قَصيرَةٌ is the خبر (predicate) — indefinite nominative with tanwīn, matching the feminine مُبتدأ الحَياةُ in gender. The خبر must agree with the مُبتدأ in gender and number when it's an adjective. قَصيرَة is feminine (ة ending) to agree with حَياة. If the خبر were definite (القَصيرَةُ), it would be interpreted as an adjective modifying الحياة, not a predicate — creating a noun phrase, not a sentence. This indefinite-خبر rule is critical for sentence formation.

items.question: هُناكَ _____ في الحَديقَةِ (There are children in the garden)
items.explanation: هُنَاكَ (there is/are) introduces an existential sentence — a special type of nominal sentence. The noun after هُنَاكَ is the مُبتدأ مُؤَخَّر (delayed subject) and takes nominative case: أَطفَالٌ. في الحَديقَةِ is the خبر مُقَدَّم (fronted predicate). This fronting of the predicate is required when the subject is indefinite — Arabic usually requires the مُبتدأ to be definite, so fronting the خبر allows indefinite subjects. Compare: أَطفَالٌ في الحَديقَةِ (awkward) vs في الحَديقَةِ أَطفَالٌ (correct).

items.question: الطُّلَّابُ _____ لِلاِمتِحَانِ (The students are preparing for the exam)
items.explanation: يَستَعِدُّونَ (they are preparing) is a verbal sentence serving as the خبر of the nominal sentence. Since الطُّلَّابُ is the مُبتدأ (subject-first order), the verb in the خبر MUST agree in number: يَستَعِدُّونَ (plural, not singular يَستَعِدُّ). This is the key difference from verbal sentences: SVO order requires full agreement, VSO requires only gender agreement. This rule is frequently tested and is one of the most important Arabic grammar principles to master.

items.question: البَيتُ _____ واسِعَةٌ (The house, its garden is spacious)
items.explanation: حَديقَتُهُ واسِعَةٌ is a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية) functioning as the خبر of the larger sentence. This 'predicate as a sentence' structure is called خبر جملة اسمية. حَديقَتُهُ is nominative (مُبتدأ of the inner sentence), واسِعَةٌ is its خبر. The pronoun هُ (his/its) links back to البَيتُ. This nested-sentence predicate is elegant Arabic: الرَّجُلُ أَخلَاقُهُ حَسَنَةٌ (the man — his manners are good), القَاهِرَةُ شَوارِعُها مُزدَحِمَةٌ (Cairo — its streets are crowded).

items.question: لِ_____ حَقٌّ في التَّعليمِ (Every child has a right to education)
items.explanation: لِكُلِّ طِفلٍ (for every child) is a prepositional phrase serving as a fronted خبر. After لِ (for/to), كُلِّ takes genitive (kasra). طِفلٍ is genitive as مُضاف إليه. حَقٌّ (a right) is the delayed مُبتدأ — it's indefinite, which requires the خبر to be fronted. في التَّعليمِ (in education) further specifies the right. This fronted-predicate structure with لِ is extremely common for expressing possession: لِلطَّالِبِ كِتَابٌ (the student has a book).

items.question: أَنتُم _____ في هَذا المَوضوعِ (You are right about this subject)
items.explanation: مُحِقُّونَ (right/correct, masculine plural) is the خبر agreeing with أَنتُم (you, masculine plural) in gender, number, and case (nominative). Personal pronouns as مُبتدأ trigger full agreement in the خبر: أَنَا مُجتَهِدٌ (I am hardworking), هُم سُعَدَاءُ (they are happy), هِيَ ذَكِيَّةٌ (she is smart). في هَذا المَوضوعِ is a prepositional qualifier. Sound masculine plural nominative ending is -ونَ (مُحقُّونَ); accusative/genitive would be -ينَ (مُحقِّينَ).

items.question: العِلمُ _____ (Knowledge is light)
items.explanation: نورٌ (light) is the خبر — indefinite nominative as required. العِلمُ نورٌ is a quintessential Arabic proverb demonstrating the simplest nominal sentence: definite subject + indefinite predicate. This type of metaphorical equation is central to Arabic rhetoric: الصَّبرُ مِفتاحُ الفَرَجِ (patience is the key to relief), الكِتابُ خَيرُ جَليسٍ (a book is the best companion). These pithy nominal sentences are the backbone of Arabic proverbs, which learners should collect and memorize.

items.question: السَّمَاءُ _____ اللَّيلَةَ (The sky is clear tonight)
items.explanation: صَافِيَةٌ (clear, feminine) is the خبر matching السَّمَاءُ in gender. اللَّيلَةَ (tonight) is a ظرف زمان in accusative. صَافِيَة is a feminine active participle from صَفَا (to be clear) — active participles commonly serve as predicates in nominal sentences, describing current states: النَّهرُ جارٍ (the river is flowing), الشَّمسُ سَاطِعَةٌ (the sun is shining), الطَّعامُ جاهِزٌ (the food is ready). These participial predicates imply ongoing states.

items.question: كُلُّ _____ مَسؤولٌ عَن أَفعَالِهِ (Every person is responsible for their actions)
items.explanation: إِنسَانٍ is genitive as the مُضاف إليه of كُلُّ. كُلُّ (every) functions as the مُبتدأ taking nominative (ḍamma). مَسؤولٌ (responsible) is the خبر. عَن أَفعَالِهِ (for his actions) is a prepositional complement. كُلُّ is always definite in meaning (referring to all members), so it can serve as مُبتدأ followed by an indefinite خبر. This sentence structure — كُلُّ + مُضاف إليه + خبر — is extremely common for expressing universal truths and general principles in Arabic.

items.question: المُسلِمونَ _____ (Muslims are brothers)
items.explanation: إِخوَةٌ (brothers/siblings) is the خبر — indefinite nominative. المُسلِمونَ is the مُبتدأ (sound masculine plural, nominative -ونَ). This is a well-known hadith (prophetic saying) that perfectly illustrates the nominal sentence. Note: إِخوَة is the broken plural of أَخ (brother) for the meaning 'brethren/brothers in faith,' while إِخوان is another plural of أَخ used for close friends/brotherhood. The خبر doesn't need to match the subject in number when it's a different noun, only when it's an adjective.

items.question: _____ الطَّعامُ عَلى المَائِدَةِ (The food is on the table — fronted predicate)
items.explanation: When the blank is at the beginning and the answer is a prepositional phrase, this tests خبر مُقَدَّم (fronted predicate). عَلى المَائِدَةِ is the predicate moved to the front, and الطَّعامُ is the مُبتدأ مُؤَخَّر (delayed subject). Both orders are correct: الطَّعامُ عَلى المَائِدَةِ or عَلى المَائِدَةِ الطَّعامُ. Fronting the predicate is obligatory when the subject is indefinite. It's optional for stylistic emphasis when the subject is definite.

items.question: أُمِّي هِيَ _____ في حَياتي (My mother is the most important person in my life)
items.explanation: هِيَ here is a ضَمير فَصل (separating pronoun) — it separates the مُبتدأ from the خبر and allows the خبر to be definite. Without هِيَ, أُمِّي الأَهَمُّ would be a noun phrase (my most important mother), not a sentence. The ضَمير فَصل enables definite-subject + definite-predicate sentences. الأَهَمُّ (the most important) takes nominative (ḍamma) as the خبر. This separating pronoun is a sophisticated Arabic mechanism used in the Quran: أُولَئِكَ هُمُ المُفلِحونَ (those are the successful ones).

name: Occupations (المهن والوظائف)
description: Vocabulary and grammar for describing jobs and professions in Arabic

items.question: أَبي _____ في المُستَشفَى (My father is a doctor at the hospital)
items.explanation: طَبيبٌ (a doctor) is the خبر (predicate) in this nominal sentence, taking nominative case with tanwīn. أَبي (my father) is the مُبتدأ. In Arabic, occupations are stated without an article when predicative: هُوَ مُهَندِسٌ (he is an engineer), هِيَ مُعَلِّمَةٌ (she is a teacher). The hospital المُستَشفَى uses the preposition في (in/at). Occupation nouns follow standard Arabic patterns and have masculine/feminine pairs.

items.question: أُختي _____ في مَدرَسَةٍ ابتِدائِيَّةٍ (My sister is a teacher at an elementary school)
items.explanation: مُعَلِّمَةٌ (female teacher) uses the feminine form (ة-ending) because the subject أُختي (my sister) is female. Arabic systematically creates feminine occupation forms: مُعَلِّم → مُعَلِّمَة (teacher), مُهَندِس → مُهَندِسَة (engineer), طَبيب → طَبيبَة (doctor), مُحَامٍ → مُحَامِيَة (lawyer). ابتِدائِيَّة is a nisba adjective from ابتِدَاء (beginning). Gender matching in occupations is mandatory in Arabic — using masculine for a female is grammatically incorrect.

items.question: يَعمَلُ كَ_____ في شَرِكَةٍ كَبيرَةٍ (He works as an engineer at a large company)
items.explanation: After the preposition كَ (as/like), the noun takes genitive case: مُهَندِسٍ with tanwīn kasra. The verb يَعمَلُ (he works) introduces the occupation. Arabic uses كَ + occupation or بِصِفَةِ + occupation to express 'as a [job].' شَرِكَة كَبيرَة (a large company) shows adjective agreement — both indefinite genitive feminine. مُهَندِس comes from هَندَسَة (engineering/geometry), which itself derives from the Persian word for measurement. Many professional terms in Arabic have rich etymologies.

items.question: أُريدُ أَن أَصبِحَ _____ في المُستَقبَلِ (I want to become a pilot in the future)
items.explanation: طَيَّارًا (a pilot) is the predicate of أَصبِحَ (to become — one of كَانَ's sisters), which takes accusative: خبر أَصبَحَ is منصوب. The verb أَصبِحَ is in subjunctive mood after أَن. طَيَّار comes from the root ط-ي-ر (to fly) with the intensive pattern فَعَّال indicating someone who does something professionally/habitually: طَبَّاخ (cook), نَجَّار (carpenter), حَدَّاد (blacksmith), خَبَّاز (baker). The فَعَّال pattern is one of Arabic's most productive occupation patterns.

items.question: _____ يُدافِعُ عَن المُتَّهَمِ (The lawyer defends the accused)
items.explanation: المُحامي (the lawyer) is the subject in nominative case. It's a defective noun (اسم منقوص) — the final ي drops before tanwīn in nominative/genitive: مُحامٍ (a lawyer), but stays with ال: المُحامي. يُدافِعُ عَن (defends, from) — the verb Form III indicates interaction/doing something with/for someone. المُتَّهَمِ (the accused) is a passive participle from Form VIII اتَّهَمَ (to accuse). Legal occupation vocabulary is important for formal Arabic proficiency.

items.question: تَعمَلُ أُمّي _____ في البَنكِ (My mother works as an accountant at the bank)
items.explanation: مُحاسِبَةً (as an accountant, feminine) is a حال (circumstantial accusative) or a second predicate in accusative — describing her capacity while working. مُحاسِبَة comes from حِسَاب (accounting/calculation), using the active participle of Form III حاسَبَ (to hold accountable/calculate). في البَنكِ (at the bank) is a locative phrase. Arabic uses active participles extensively for occupations: مُحاسِب (accountant), مُراسِل (correspondent), مُدَرِّس (instructor), مُمَرِّض (nurse).

items.question: الصَّحَفِيُّ _____ الأَخبَارَ كُلَّ يَومٍ (The journalist writes the news every day)
items.explanation: يَكتُبُ (he writes, present tense) — the journalist الصَّحَفِيُّ is the مُبتدأ, and يَكتُبُ الأَخبَارَ is the verbal خبر. Since the sentence starts with the subject (SVO order), the verb must agree in number (singular here). صَحَفِيّ is a nisba adjective from صَحيفَة (newspaper), used as an occupation noun. كُلَّ يَومٍ (every day) is an adverbial accusative. Media-related occupations: مُذيعٌ (broadcaster), مُصَوِّرٌ (photographer), مُحَرِّرٌ (editor).

items.question: أَخي _____ ماهِرٌ (My brother is a skilled carpenter)
items.explanation: نَجَّارٌ (carpenter) is the خبر in nominative with tanwīn. ماهِرٌ (skilled) is an additional adjective also nominative. The فَعَّال pattern for tradespeople: نَجَّار (carpenter, from نَجَرَ to carve), حَدَّاد (blacksmith, from حَديد iron), خَيَّاط (tailor, from خَاطَ to sew), بَنَّاء (builder, from بَنَى to build), رَسَّام (artist/painter, from رَسَمَ to draw). This pattern suggests intensive/habitual action and is Arabic's main way to form craft/trade occupation nouns.

items.question: ذَهَبتُ إِلى _____ لِإِصلاحِ سَيَّارَتي (I went to the mechanic to fix my car)
items.explanation: المِيكانيكيِّ (the mechanic) takes genitive after إِلى. This is a borrowed word with a nisba suffix يّ- added: مِيكانيكيّ. Many modern occupations use borrowed words: بْروفِيسور (professor), تِكنولوجيّ (technologist). لِإِصلاحِ (to fix) uses لِ + masdar. سَيَّارَتي (my car) — the ي possessive suffix. Arabic adapts foreign occupation words by adding Arabic morphological features like nisba and tanwīn, integrating them into the case system.

items.question: المُمَرِّضَةُ تُساعِدُ _____ في المُستَشفَى (The nurse helps the patients at the hospital)
items.explanation: المَرضَى (the patients/sick people) is the direct object in accusative. It's a defective noun — words ending in alif maqṣūra (ى) don't show case endings visibly. المُمَرِّضَةُ (female nurse) derives from مَرَّضَ (Form II: to nurse/tend to the sick), which comes from مَرِضَ (to be sick). The active participle مُمَرِّض/مُمَرِّضَة shows the doer. تُساعِدُ (she helps, Form III) uses the feminine prefix تُ-. This sentence connects the occupation to its function elegantly.

items.question: يَحلُمُ ابني بِأَن يَكونَ _____ (My son dreams of being an astronaut)
items.explanation: رَائِدَ فَضَاءٍ (astronaut — literally 'space pioneer') is the predicate of يَكونَ in accusative. يَكونَ is subjunctive after أَن, and its predicate (خبر كان) takes accusative. رَائِد is an active participle meaning 'pioneer/leader' from رَادَ (to explore). فَضَاءٍ (space) is the مُضاف إليه in genitive. Arabic creates compound occupation titles: رَائِدُ فَضَاءٍ (astronaut), طَبيبُ أَسنَانٍ (dentist), مُهَندِسُ بَرمَجِيَّاتٍ (software engineer).

items.question: كَانَ جَدّي _____ في القَريَةِ (My grandfather was a farmer in the village)
items.explanation: فَلَّاحًا (a farmer) is the predicate of كَانَ — خبر كان is always accusative. فَلَّاح follows the فَعَّال pattern from فَلَحَ (to cultivate/succeed). في القَريَةِ (in the village) is locative. جَدّي (my grandfather) is اسم كان in nominative. Arabic agricultural occupations: فَلَّاح (farmer), رَاعٍ (shepherd — defective noun), صَيَّاد (fisherman/hunter), بُستانِيّ (gardener). كَانَ + occupation expresses past profession, useful for family history narratives.

items.question: _____ يَطبُخُ طَعامًا لَذيذًا (The chef cooks delicious food)
items.explanation: الطَّبَّاخُ (the chef/cook) is the subject in nominative. طَبَّاخ is another فَعَّال pattern from طَبَخَ (to cook). يَطبُخُ (he cooks) agrees in gender and number since this is SVO order. طَعامًا لَذيذًا (delicious food) — both accusative with tanwīn as direct object + adjective. Food service occupations: طَبَّاخ (cook), نادِل/خادِم (waiter), حَلوَانِيّ (confectioner). The فَعَّال pattern implies professional-level habitual action vs occasional cooking.

items.question: صَديقَتي تَعمَلُ _____ في المَطارِ (My friend works as a translator at the airport)
items.explanation: مُتَرجِمَةً (female translator) is accusative — functioning as a حال describing her work role. مُتَرجِم comes from تَرجَمَ (a quadriliteral verb: to translate), with active participle pattern مُفَعلِل. في المَطارِ (at the airport) — مَطار follows the pattern مَفعَل (place noun) from طَارَ (to fly). Language-related occupations: مُتَرجِم (translator), مُفَسِّر (interpreter/exegete), لُغَوِيّ (linguist), كاتِب (writer). These are relevant to language learners.

items.question: إِنَّ _____ مَسؤولٌ عَن سَلامَةِ المَبنَى (Indeed the engineer is responsible for the building's safety)
items.explanation: المُهَندِسَ is accusative because إِنَّ puts its subject (اسم إنّ) in the accusative case. مَسؤولٌ (responsible) is the خبر of إِنَّ in nominative. سَلامَة (safety) is a masdar from سَلِمَ. This sentence demonstrates how إِنَّ affects case marking of occupation nouns — a critical grammar point. The مُهَندِس pattern (مُفَعلِل from quadriliteral هَندَسَ) is also used for: مُدَرِّس (from دَرَّسَ Form II), مُصَمِّم (designer, from صَمَّمَ).

name: Weather & Nature (الطقس والطبيعة)
description: Arabic vocabulary and structures for describing weather and natural phenomena

items.question: الطَّقسُ _____ اليَومَ (The weather is hot today)
items.explanation: حَارٌّ (hot) is the خبر in nominative with tanwīn. The shadda on the final ر indicates a doubled consonant. Arabic weather descriptions use nominal sentences: الجَوُّ بَارِدٌ (the atmosphere is cold), الطَّقسُ مُعتَدِلٌ (the weather is moderate), الحَرَارَةُ مُرتَفِعَةٌ (the temperature is high). اليَومَ (today) is an adverbial accusative. Weather vocabulary is essential for daily conversation and news comprehension in Arabic.

items.question: تُمطِرُ السَّماءُ _____ غَزيرًا (The sky is raining heavily)
items.explanation: مَطَرًا is a مفعول مطلق (absolute/cognate object) — the verb تُمطِرُ and the noun مَطَر share the same root م-ط-ر. The مفعول مطلق is always accusative and reinforces/specifies the verb's action. غَزيرًا (heavy/abundant) modifies مَطَرًا and is also accusative. This construction is similar to English 'rained a heavy rain.' The feminine verb تُمطِرُ matches السَّماءُ (the sky, feminine). Arabic weather verbs: أَمطَرَت (it rained), أَثلَجَت (it snowed), أَبرَقَت (it lightninged).

items.question: في الشِّتاءِ تَكونُ اللَّيَالي _____ (In winter the nights are long)
items.explanation: طَويلَةً (long, feminine) is the predicate of تَكونُ (to be — كَانَ in present tense), which requires accusative for its خبر. اللَّيَالي (the nights) is the subject of تَكونُ in nominative — it's the broken plural of لَيلَة. الشِّتاءِ (winter) is genitive after في. Arabic seasons: الرَّبيع (spring), الصَّيف (summer), الخَريف (autumn), الشِّتَاء (winter). Season names are used with في for temporal reference: في الصَّيفِ (in summer).

items.question: _____ الرِّيَاحُ بِشِدَّةٍ هَذا المَساءَ (The winds are blowing strongly this evening)
items.explanation: تَهُبُّ (it/she blows) uses the feminine prefix تَ- because الرِّيَاحُ (winds) is a broken plural — broken plurals of non-human nouns take FEMININE SINGULAR verb agreement. This is a fundamental Arabic rule: الأَشجَارُ تَنمو (the trees grow), الكُتُبُ وَصَلَت (the books arrived), الجِبَالُ تَبدو جَميلَةً (the mountains look beautiful). بِشِدَّةٍ (with force/strongly) uses بِ + masdar as an adverb of manner. هَذا المَسَاءَ is an adverbial accusative.

items.question: الشَّمسُ _____ مِن خَلفِ الغُيومِ (The sun appeared from behind the clouds)
items.explanation: ظَهَرَت uses feminine past tense because الشَّمسُ (the sun) is feminine in Arabic. Note: SVO order here means the verb could also agree in number, but since الشَّمس is singular, singular agreement is expected either way. مِن خَلفِ (from behind) combines the preposition مِن with the ظرف مكان خَلف. الغُيومِ (the clouds) is genitive — plural of غَيمَة (a cloud). Celestial vocabulary: الشَّمس (sun, feminine), القَمَر (moon, masculine), النُّجوم (stars).

items.question: دَرَجَةُ الحَرارَةِ _____ في الصَّيفِ (The temperature rises in summer)
items.explanation: تَرتَفِعُ (it rises, feminine) — Form VIII اِرتَفَعَ from رَفَعَ (to raise). The feminine verb matches دَرَجَة (degree, feminine). دَرَجَةُ الحَرارَةِ (temperature — literally 'degree of heat') is an idāfa construction. Arabic uses specific verbs for temperature changes: اِرتَفَعَت (rose), اِنخَفَضَت (dropped), اِستَقَرَّت (stabilized). في الصَّيفِ (in summer) uses في + season name. Weather reports in Arabic use this formal structure extensively.

items.question: الأَشجَارُ _____ أَورَاقَها في الخَريفِ (The trees shed their leaves in autumn)
items.explanation: تَفقِدُ (she/it loses) — feminine singular for the broken plural الأَشجَار (trees, non-human plural = feminine singular agreement). أَورَاقَها (its/their leaves) is the direct object — أَوراق is the broken plural of وَرَقَة, with the feminine possessive suffix ها referring back to الأَشجَار. This rule — non-human plurals take feminine singular — applies to verbs, adjectives, and pronouns universally in Arabic and is one of the most important agreement rules to master.

items.question: سَيَكونُ الطَّقسُ _____ غَدًا (The weather will be cloudy tomorrow)
items.explanation: غَائِمًا (cloudy) is the predicate of سَيَكونُ (will be) in accusative — خبر كان is always منصوب. غَائِم is an active participle from غَامَ (to be cloudy). Arabic weather adjectives: غَائِم (cloudy), صَافٍ (clear — defective), مُشمِس (sunny), مَاطِر (rainy), عَاصِف (stormy), ضَبَابيّ (foggy). These participles describe weather states. غَدًا (tomorrow) is an adverbial accusative — note the alif with tanwīn fatḥa.

items.question: البَحرُ _____ وَهَادِئٌ (The sea is blue and calm)
items.explanation: أَزرَقُ (blue) is a diptote adjective (pattern أَفعَل for colors) — nominative without tanwīn as the خبر. وَهَادِئٌ (and calm) is coordinated with وَ. Note the case contrast: أَزرَقُ has no tanwīn (diptote) while هَادِئٌ has tanwīn (triptote). Colors in Arabic are diptote and have distinct masculine/feminine forms: أَزرَق/زَرقاء, أَخضَر/خَضراء, أَحمَر/حَمراء. Nature vocabulary linked to sea: مَوجٌ (waves), شَاطِئٌ (shore), مَدٌّ وَجَزرٌ (tide).

items.question: سَقَطَ _____ كَثيفٌ عَلى الجِبالِ (Heavy snow fell on the mountains)
items.explanation: ثَلجٌ (snow) is the subject (فاعل) in nominative after the verb سَقَطَ (it fell). كَثيفٌ (thick/heavy) is its adjective, also nominative. عَلى الجِبالِ (on the mountains) — الجِبال is the broken plural of جَبَل. In verbal sentences (VSO), the subject after the verb takes nominative case. Precipitation vocabulary: ثَلج (snow), بَرَد (hail), جَليد (ice/frost), نَدَى (dew). Arabic uses سَقَطَ (fell) for all types of precipitation.

items.question: يَزدَادُ جَمَالُ الحَديقَةِ في _____ (The garden's beauty increases in spring)
items.explanation: الرَّبيعِ (spring) is genitive after في. يَزدَادُ (increases, Form VIII) from زَادَ (to increase). جَمَالُ الحَديقَةِ (the garden's beauty) is an idāfa — جَمَال nominative as subject, الحَديقَة genitive as مُضاف إليه. الرَّبيع derives from the root ر-ب-ع (related to 'four' — the fourth season in some old reckonings). Nature-beauty vocabulary: زُهور (flowers), خُضرَة (greenery), عُشب (grass), نَسيم (breeze).

items.question: رَأَينا _____ جَميلًا بَعدَ المَطَرِ (We saw a beautiful rainbow after the rain)
items.explanation: قَوسَ قُزَحَ (rainbow — literally 'bow of Quzaḥ') is the direct object in accusative. It's an idāfa compound: قَوس (bow, accusative) + قُزَح (proper name of a pre-Islamic deity/angel of clouds, genitive — diptote). جَميلًا (beautiful) is accusative adjective. بَعدَ المَطَرِ (after the rain) is a ظرف زمان. Arabic compound nature terms: كُسوف (solar eclipse), خُسوف (lunar eclipse), شَلَّال (waterfall), بُركان (volcano). These terms combine poetic Arabic roots with scientific precision.

items.question: تَسقُطُ _____ مِن الأَشجَارِ في الخَريفِ (The leaves fall from trees in autumn)
items.explanation: الأَوراقُ (the leaves) is the subject (فاعل) coming after the verb تَسقُطُ (they/it fall). In VSO order, the verb is feminine singular (تَسقُطُ — non-human plural treated as feminine singular). The subject is nominative. مِن الأَشجَارِ (from the trees) is a prepositional phrase. في الخَريفِ (in autumn) is temporal. الخَريف comes from خَرَفَ (to harvest fruit), reflecting the agricultural origins of Arabic season names.

items.question: اللَّيلَةَ السَّماءُ _____ بِالنُّجومِ (Tonight the sky is filled with stars)
items.explanation: مَليئَةٌ (full/filled, feminine) is the خبر in nominative. السَّماءُ (the sky) is the مُبتدأ. بِالنُّجومِ (with stars) uses بِ + genitive. اللَّيلَةَ (tonight) is a fronted adverbial accusative providing temporal context. النُّجوم is the broken plural of نَجم (star). Arabic night-sky vocabulary: نَجم (star), كَوكَب (planet), مَجَرَّة (galaxy), بَدر (full moon), هِلال (crescent — also the Islamic symbol). مَليء follows the pattern فَعيل and pairs with بِ for 'full of.'

items.question: تَتَساقَطُ قَطَرَاتُ _____ عَلى النَّافِذَةِ (Raindrops are falling on the window)
items.explanation: المَطَرِ (the rain) is genitive as مُضاف إليه of قَطَرَات (drops). قَطَرَاتُ is a sound feminine plural in nominative (subject). تَتَساقَطُ (they fall one by one, Form VI) — this pattern indicates gradual/reciprocal action, perfect for describing raindrops. عَلى النَّافِذَةِ (on the window) uses عَلى + genitive. Form VI تَساقَطَ vs سَقَطَ: the base verb means 'fell,' while Form VI adds the nuance of 'falling gradually/repeatedly.' Arabic verb forms create poetic precision in nature descriptions.

name: Food & Restaurant (الطعام والمطعم)
description: Arabic vocabulary for ordering food, describing meals, and restaurant dialogue

items.question: أُريدُ _____ مِنَ القَهوَةِ مِن فَضلِكَ (I want a cup of coffee please)
items.explanation: فِنجَانًا (a cup) is the direct object of أُريدُ (I want), taking accusative with tanwīn fatḥa. مِنَ القَهوَةِ (of coffee) specifies the content using مِن + genitive. مِن فَضلِكَ (please — literally 'from your kindness') is a polite phrase. فِنجَان is originally from Persian and entered Arabic with full declension. Restaurant ordering uses أُريدُ or أَرغَبُ في (I would like). Common beverage words: شَاي (tea), عَصير (juice), مَاء (water), حَليب (milk).

items.question: هَل عِندَكُم _____ بِدونِ لَحمٍ؟ (Do you have dishes without meat?)
items.explanation: أَطبَاقٌ (dishes/plates) is the مُبتدأ مُؤَخَّر (delayed subject) in nominative — the sentence begins with عِندَكُم (you have — fronted خبر). هَل introduces a yes/no question. بِدونِ لَحمٍ (without meat) — بِدون is an indeclinable preposition meaning 'without.' أَطبَاق is the broken plural of طَبَق. Arabic restaurant phrases: القَائِمَة مِن فَضلِكَ (the menu please), مَا هِيَ الأَطبَاقُ المُتَاحَةُ؟ (what dishes are available?). Food vocabulary is essential for travel.

items.question: الطَّعَامُ في هَذا المَطعَمِ _____ جِدًّا (The food at this restaurant is delicious)
items.explanation: لَذيذٌ (delicious) is the خبر in nominative. الطَّعَامُ (the food) is the مُبتدأ. في هَذا المَطعَمِ (in this restaurant) — المَطعَم (restaurant) is a place noun (اسم مكان) from طَعِمَ (to taste/eat), pattern مَفعَل. جِدًّا (very) is an adverbial intensifier in accusative. Arabic taste adjectives: لَذيذ (delicious), شَهِيّ (appetizing), حَارّ (spicy), مَالِح (salty), حُلو (sweet), مُرّ (bitter), حَامِض (sour). These follow standard adjective patterns.

items.question: أَطلُبُ _____ مَشوِيًّا مَعَ الأُرزِّ (I'll order grilled chicken with rice)
items.explanation: دَجَاجًا (chicken) is the direct object of أَطلُبُ (I order), taking accusative. دَجَاج is a collective noun (اسم جمع) — one chicken is دَجَاجَة. مَشوِيًّا (grilled) is an adjective in accusative agreeing with دَجَاجًا. مَعَ الأُرزِّ (with rice) — الأُرز (rice) is genitive after مَعَ. Cooking methods vocabulary: مَشوِيّ (grilled), مَقلِيّ (fried), مَسلوق (boiled), مَحشِيّ (stuffed), مَطهُوّ/مَطبوخ (cooked). These passive participles describe food preparation styles.

items.question: الحِسابَ مِن _____ (The bill please — literally 'the bill, from your favor')
items.explanation: فَضلِكَ (your kindness/favor) is genitive after مِن. الحِسَابَ (the bill/check) stands alone as a request — implied verb أُريدُ or أَعطِني is dropped for brevity. فَضل means 'grace/kindness/favor.' This is the standard polite restaurant closing phrase. Other useful restaurant phrases: هَل يُمكِنُني الدَّفعُ بِالبِطاقَةِ؟ (Can I pay by card?), أَينَ الحَمَّامُ؟ (Where is the restroom?), شُكرًا عَلى الخِدمَةِ (Thank you for the service).

items.question: أُفَضِّلُ _____ بِدونِ سُكَّرٍ (I prefer tea without sugar)
items.explanation: الشَّايَ (the tea) is the direct object of أُفَضِّلُ (I prefer, Form II), taking accusative. Arabic uses the definite article with generic references to types of food/drink: أُحِبُّ الشَّايَ (I like tea in general). بِدونِ سُكَّرٍ (without sugar) — سُكَّر has shadda on the كّ. أُفَضِّلُ is Form II (intensive), from فَضَلَ (to be superior). Beverage modifications: بِالحَليبِ (with milk), ساخِن (hot), بَارِد (cold), مَعَ ثَلجٍ (with ice).

items.question: هَل هَذا الطَّبَقُ _____ لِلأَطفَالِ؟ (Is this dish suitable for children?)
items.explanation: مُنَاسِبٌ (suitable/appropriate) is the خبر in nominative — predicating هَذا الطَّبَقُ (this dish). لِلأَطفَالِ (for children) uses لِ + genitive. مُنَاسِب is an active participle from Form III نَاسَبَ (to suit/be appropriate for). هَل makes this a yes/no question. Useful restaurant questions: هَل فيهِ مُكَسَّرَاتٌ؟ (does it contain nuts?), هَل هُوَ حَلالٌ؟ (is it halal?), مَا المُكَوِّنَاتُ؟ (what are the ingredients?). These are practical for dietary restrictions.

items.question: سَأَتَناوَلُ _____ مَعَ سَلَطَةٍ (I will have fish with a salad)
items.explanation: سَمَكًا (fish) is the indefinite accusative direct object of سَأَتَناوَلُ (I will eat/have, Form VI). مَعَ سَلَطَةٍ (with a salad) — سَلَطَة is borrowed from Turkish/Italian. تَناوَلَ (Form VI) is more formal than أَكَلَ (to eat) — used in restaurants and formal dining. Future prefix سَ indicates near future. سَمَك is a collective noun (one fish = سَمَكَة). Seafood vocabulary: رُوبيان (shrimp), كَابوريا (crab), مَحار (oysters). Arabic restaurant language often blends formal verbs with casual context.

items.question: الخُبزُ العَرَبيُّ _____ مَعَ كُلِّ وَجبَةٍ (Arab bread is served with every meal)
items.explanation: يُقَدَّمُ (is served/presented) is passive voice (مبني للمجهول) of Form II قَدَّمَ (to present/serve). The pattern يُفَعَّلُ converts active to passive. الخُبزُ (bread) is the نائب فاعل (deputy subject) in nominative. مَعَ كُلِّ وَجبَةٍ (with every meal) — وَجبَة (meal) is مُضاف إليه. Food serving verbs in passive: يُطهَى (is cooked), يُقلَى (is fried), يُشوَى (is grilled). Passive voice is common in recipe instructions and menu descriptions.

items.question: طَلَبنَا _____ أَصنَافٍ مِنَ الحَلَوَيَاتِ (We ordered three types of desserts)
items.explanation: ثَلَاثَةَ (three) is accusative — direct object of طَلَبنَا. It takes tā' marbūṭa because أَصنَاف (types) is masculine — Arabic numbers 3-10 take the OPPOSITE gender of the counted noun (gender polarity). أَصنَافٍ is genitive plural as تمييز العدد (number specification). الحَلَوَيَات (desserts/sweets) is sound feminine plural from حَلوَى (sweet/dessert). Arabic desserts: بَقلاوَة (baklava), كُنافَة (kunafa), مَهَلَّبِيَّة (pudding). Number-gender polarity is crucial for ordering quantities.

items.question: هَل تُريدُ أَن تُجَرِّبَ هَذا _____؟ (Do you want to try this dish?)
items.explanation: الطَّبَقَ is accusative as the direct object of تُجَرِّبَ (to try, Form II, subjunctive after أَن). هَذا (this) is a demonstrative modifying الطَّبَقَ. تُريدُ (you want) + أَن + subjunctive is a standard Arabic construction for 'want to do.' In restaurant contexts: هَل تُريدُ أَن تَطلُبَ المَزيدَ؟ (want to order more?), هَل تُريدُ أَن تَغيُرَ الطَّلَبَ؟ (want to change the order?). The subjunctive after أَن changes the final vowel from ḍamma to fatḥa.

items.question: الطَّعامُ المِصريُّ _____ بِالتَّوابِلِ (Egyptian food is known for its spices)
items.explanation: مَعروفٌ (known/famous) is a passive participle from عَرَفَ (to know), functioning as the خبر in nominative. بِالتَّوابِلِ (for its spices) uses بِ + genitive. التَّوابِل (spices) is the broken plural of تَابِل. Arabic food culture vocabulary: تَوابِل (spices), بُهارات (seasonings), زَيتون (olives), زَيت (oil). Passive participles as predicates are very common in food descriptions: مَحشِيّ (stuffed), مَشهور (famous), مُفَضَّل (preferred).

items.question: اِطلُب لي _____ مَاءٍ مِن فَضلِكَ (Order a bottle of water for me please)
items.explanation: زُجاجَةَ (a bottle) is accusative — direct object of the imperative اِطلُب (order!). مَاءٍ (water) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. لي (for me) places the beneficiary. The imperative اِطلُب comes from طَلَبَ (to request) — imperative of Form I verbs with hamzat waṣl. Container words used with food: زُجاجَة (bottle), كَأس (cup/glass), صَحن (plate), وِعاء (container), عُلبَة (can/box). These are essential for specifying quantities when ordering.

items.question: لا أَستَطيعُ أَن آكُلَ الطَّعامَ _____ (I cannot eat spicy food)
items.explanation: الحَارَّ (the hot/spicy) is an adjective modifying الطَّعَامَ — it must agree in case (accusative), gender (masculine), number (singular), and definiteness (definite with ال). Both are accusative: الطَّعامَ الحَارَّ. لا أَستَطيعُ (I cannot) + أَن + subjunctive = inability expression. Dietary restriction phrases: لَدَيَّ حَسَاسِيَّةٌ مِن... (I have an allergy to...), أَنَا نَبَاتِيٌّ (I am vegetarian), أَنَا صَائِمٌ (I am fasting).

items.question: الوَجبَةُ كَانَت _____ فِعلًا (The meal was truly excellent)
items.explanation: مُمتَازَةً (excellent, feminine) is the predicate of كَانَت — خبر كان takes accusative. The ت on كَانَت marks past tense feminine to agree with الوَجبَةُ (the meal, feminine). فِعلًا (truly/indeed) is an adverbial accusative intensifier. مُمتَاز is an active participle from Form VIII اِمتَازَ (to be distinguished/excellent). Quality descriptions for food: مُمتَاز (excellent), رَائِع (wonderful), عَادِيّ (ordinary), سَيِّئ (bad). Restaurant review language transfers directly from classroom Arabic.

name: Health & Body (الصحة والجسم)
description: Medical and health vocabulary with Arabic grammatical structures

items.question: أَشعُرُ بِ_____ في رَأسي (I feel a pain in my head)
items.explanation: أَلَمٍ (a pain) is genitive after the preposition بِ. أَشعُرُ بِ (I feel — Form I with بِ for sensation) is the standard way to express physical feelings. في رَأسي (in my head) locates the pain — رَأس (head) with possessive ي. Body pain expressions follow this pattern: أَشعُرُ بِأَلَمٍ في... (I feel pain in...). Body parts: رَأس (head), بَطن (stomach), ظَهر (back), صَدر (chest), حَلق (throat). This sentence pattern is essential for doctor visits.

items.question: يَجِبُ أَن تَذهَبَ إِلى _____ (You must go to the doctor)
items.explanation: الطَّبيبِ (the doctor) is genitive after إِلى (to). يَجِبُ أَن (it is necessary that/must) + subjunctive is a standard obligation structure. تَذهَبَ is subjunctive (fatḥa ending) after أَن. Medical specialist vocabulary: طَبيبُ أَسنانٍ (dentist), طَبيبُ عُيونٍ (eye doctor), طَبيبُ أَطفالٍ (pediatrician), جَرَّاح (surgeon), طَبيبٌ نَفسِيٌّ (psychiatrist). يَجِبُ is impersonal — it doesn't conjugate for the subject.

items.question: المَريضُ يَحتاجُ إِلى _____ (The patient needs rest)
items.explanation: رَاحَةٍ (rest) is genitive after إِلى. يَحتاجُ إِلى (needs — Form VIII with إِلى) is the standard expression for medical needs. المَريضُ (the patient) is a nominative subject — from مَرِضَ (to be sick), on the pattern فَعيل for ailment states. Medical instruction verbs: يَحتاجُ إِلى (needs), يَجِبُ أَن (must), يُنصَحُ بِ (is advised to), يُمنَعُ مِن (is prohibited from). رَاحَة (rest) is the masdar of رَاحَ (to rest/go).

items.question: تَنَاوَلْ هَذا _____ ثَلَاثَ مَرَّاتٍ يَومِيًّا (Take this medicine three times daily)
items.explanation: الدَّوَاءَ (the medicine) is accusative — direct object of the imperative تَنَاوَلْ (take!/consume!). هَذا is a demonstrative adjective. ثَلَاثَ مَرَّاتٍ (three times) — مَرَّات is sound feminine plural of مَرَّة (time/instance), genitive after the number. يَومِيًّا (daily) is an adverb with nisba ending. Medical instructions: تَنَاوَلْ (take — for medicine), اِشرَبْ (drink), اِستَعمِلْ (use), ضَعْ (apply — for cream). Imperative forms are essential in medical Arabic.

items.question: _____ مُرتَفِعَةٌ وَيَجِبُ خَفضُها (The temperature is high and must be lowered)
items.explanation: الحَرارَةُ (the temperature/fever) is the مُبتدأ in nominative. مُرتَفِعَةٌ (high/elevated, feminine) is the خبر. خَفضُها (lowering it) — خَفض is the masdar of خَفَضَ (to lower), and ها is the possessive pronoun. يَجِبُ خَفضُها (it must be lowered) uses the impersonal يَجِبُ + masdar construction. Medical condition vocabulary: حُمَّى (fever), ضَغط (pressure), سُكَّر (blood sugar), تَحلِيل (test/analysis). Arabic medical terminology often uses masdars for precise clinical expression.

items.question: كَسَرَ الوَلَدُ _____ أَثنَاءَ اللَّعِبِ (The boy broke his arm during play)
items.explanation: ذِراعَهُ (his arm) is accusative — direct object of كَسَرَ (he broke). The possessive هُ (his) is attached. أَثنَاءَ (during) is a ظرف زمان in accusative. اللَّعِبِ (play/playing) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. Upper body parts: ذِراع (arm), يَد (hand), كَتِف (shoulder), مِرفَق (elbow), إِصبَع (finger), مِعصَم (wrist). Injury vocabulary: كَسر (fracture), جُرح (wound), رَضَّة (bruise), اِلتِوَاء (sprain). These commonly appear in emergency situations.

items.question: يَجِبُ غَسلُ _____ قَبلَ الأَكلِ (Hands must be washed before eating)
items.explanation: اليَدَينِ (the two hands) is genitive — it's the مُضاف إليه of the masdar غَسل (washing). The dual form in genitive/accusative uses ين-: اليَدَينِ, while nominative uses ان-: اليَدَانِ. قَبلَ الأَكلِ (before eating) — قَبلَ is accusative as ظرف, الأَكل is genitive masdar. Hygiene vocabulary: غَسل (washing), تَنظيف (cleaning), تَعقيم (sterilization), صَابون (soap). The dual form is particularly relevant for body parts that come in pairs.

items.question: أُعاني مِن _____ مُزمِنٍ في الظَّهرِ (I suffer from chronic back pain)
items.explanation: أَلَمٍ (a pain) takes genitive after مِن. أُعاني مِن (I suffer from, Form III) is the standard expression for chronic conditions. مُزمِنٍ (chronic) is an adjective agreeing with أَلَم in case (genitive), gender (masculine), and definiteness (indefinite). في الظَّهرِ (in the back) locates the pain. Medical condition adjectives: مُزمِن (chronic), حَادّ (acute), مُتَقَطِّع (intermittent), خَفيف (mild), شَديد (severe). These are used with أَلَم to describe pain precisely.

items.question: أَجرَى الطَّبيبُ _____ لِلمَريضِ (The doctor performed surgery on the patient)
items.explanation: عَمَلِيَّةً (an operation/surgery) is accusative — direct object of أَجرَى (he performed/conducted, Form IV). لِلمَريضِ (for the patient) uses لِ + genitive. عَمَلِيَّة is derived from عَمَل (work) with the nisba suffix + tā' marbūṭa, creating an abstract/procedural noun. Medical procedures: عَمَلِيَّة جِراحِيَّة (surgical operation), فَحص (examination), تَحلِيل (analysis), أَشِعَّة (X-ray), تَصوير بِالرَّنين (MRI). أَجرَى (Form IV) is the standard verb for 'to conduct/perform' medical procedures.

items.question: وَصَفَ الطَّبيبُ _____ لِعِلاجِ الزُّكامِ (The doctor prescribed medicine for treating the cold)
items.explanation: دَوَاءً (medicine) is accusative — direct object of وَصَفَ (he prescribed/described). لِعِلاجِ (for treating) uses لِ + masdar عِلاج (treatment). الزُّكامِ (the cold/flu) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. وَصَفَ has two meanings: to describe and to prescribe (medical). Common illnesses: زُكام (cold), إِنفلوَنزا (flu), حَساسِيَّة (allergy), اِلتِهاب (inflammation), عَدوَى (infection). Medical Arabic is important for healthcare workers and patients in Arabic-speaking countries.

items.question: عَلَيكَ أَن تَشرَبَ _____ كَثيرًا (You should drink a lot of water)
items.explanation: مَاءً (water) is accusative — direct object of تَشرَبَ (drink, subjunctive after أَن). عَلَيكَ أَن (you should — literally 'upon you that') expresses medical advice. كَثيرًا (a lot) is an adverb in accusative. مَاء (water) has special spelling: in accusative, the hamza-alif combination makes it مَاءً (no extra alif because the word already ends in اء). Health advice verbs: اِشرَبْ (drink), كُلْ (eat), نَمْ (sleep), تَمَرَّنْ (exercise), اِستَرِحْ (rest). عَلَيكَ أَن is softer than يَجِبُ.

items.question: تَحسَّنَتْ _____ المَريضِ بَعدَ العِلاجِ (The patient's condition improved after treatment)
items.explanation: حَالَةُ (condition/state of) is nominative — it's the subject (فاعل) of تَحسَّنَتْ (improved, Form V feminine past). As the first element of an idāfa (حَالَةُ المَريضِ), it loses tanwīn. المَريضِ is genitive as مُضاف إليه. بَعدَ العِلاجِ (after treatment) — بَعدَ is ظرف زمان. تَحسَّنَ (Form V: to improve) from حَسُنَ (to be good). Medical progress verbs: تَحسَّنَ (improved), تَدَهوَرَ (deteriorated), اِستَقَرَّ (stabilized), تَعافَى (recovered).

items.question: لا تَنسَ أَن تَأخُذَ _____ في مَوعِدِها (Don't forget to take the medicine on time)
items.explanation: الأَدوِيَةَ (the medicines) is accusative — direct object of تَأخُذَ (take, subjunctive). أَدوِيَة is the broken plural of دَوَاء. لا تَنسَ (don't forget) — لا + jussive for negative command; the final alif of تَنسَى drops in jussive. في مَوعِدِها (on time/at their appointed time) — مَوعِد (appointment/time) with possessive ها referring to the medicines. Plural medical terms: أَدوِيَة (medicines), حُبوب (pills), كَبسولات (capsules), حُقَن (injections), ضَمَّادات (bandages).

items.question: يُعاني كَثيرٌ مِنَ النَّاسِ مِن _____ (Many people suffer from diabetes)
items.explanation: مَرَضِ السُّكَّرِيِّ (diabetes — literally 'sugar disease') is genitive after مِن. This is an idāfa: مَرَض (disease, genitive) + السُّكَّرِيّ (sugar-related, genitive as مُضاف إليه). كَثيرٌ مِنَ النَّاسِ (many people) — كَثير is the subject, مِنَ النَّاسِ specifies the group. Common diseases in Arabic: مَرَض السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetes), ضَغط الدَّم (blood pressure), الرَّبو (asthma), القَلب (heart disease). Arabic medical terminology often uses idāfa constructions.

items.question: الطَّبيبَةُ _____ المَرضَى بِعِنايَةٍ (The female doctor examines the patients with care)
items.explanation: تَفحَصُ (she examines) uses the feminine prefix تَ- to agree with الطَّبيبَةُ (feminine doctor). Since this is SVO order (subject first), full agreement is expected — and singular feminine matches. المَرضَى (the patients/sick ones) is the direct object — it's a defective noun not showing visible case endings. بِعِنايَةٍ (with care) uses بِ + masdar for manner. Medical examination vocabulary: فَحَصَ (examined), شَخَّصَ (diagnosed), عَالَجَ (treated), قَاسَ (measured). The feminine occupation form is standard in Arabic medical contexts.

name: Travel & Transport (السفر والمواصلات)
description: Essential Arabic for traveling, directions, and transportation

items.question: أُريدُ _____ إِلى القَاهِرَةِ (I want a ticket to Cairo)
items.explanation: تَذكِرَةً (a ticket) is accusative — direct object of أُريدُ. إِلى القَاهِرَةِ (to Cairo) — القَاهِرَة is genitive after إِلى. Travel booking vocabulary: تَذكِرَة ذَهاب (one-way ticket), تَذكِرَة ذَهاب وَعَودَة (round-trip ticket), حَجز (reservation), دَرَجَة أولَى (first class), دَرَجَة سِياحِيَّة (economy class). تَذكِرَة is from ذَكَّرَ (to remind) — originally a reminder/receipt. This practical vocabulary is essential for any traveler in Arabic-speaking countries.

items.question: مَتَى تُقلِعُ _____ ؟ (When does the plane take off?)
items.explanation: الطَّائِرَةُ (the plane/airplane) is nominative — subject of تُقلِعُ (takes off, Form IV). مَتَى (when?) is an interrogative adverb. الطَّائِرَة is literally 'the flying one' — feminine active participle of طَارَ (to fly). تُقلِعُ (Form IV أَقلَعَ: to take off/depart) uses the feminine prefix. Airport vocabulary: مَطَار (airport), إِقلاع (takeoff), هُبوط (landing), بَوَّابَة (gate), صَالَة المُغادَرَة (departure hall). Form IV verbs are common in transportation contexts.

items.question: أَينَ _____ الحافِلاتِ؟ (Where is the bus station?)
items.explanation: مَحَطَّةُ (station) is nominative — subject (مُبتدأ مُؤَخَّر) with أَينَ serving as the fronted خبر. الحافِلاتِ (buses) is genitive as مُضاف إليه — sound feminine plural in genitive takes kasra. مَحَطَّة is a place noun from حَطَّ (to set down/land). Transport stations: مَحَطَّة القِطارِ (train station), مَحَطَّة المِترو (metro station), مَوقِف الحافِلاتِ (bus stop — مَوقِف from وَقَفَ to stop). أَينَ questions are essential for navigating Arabic cities.

items.question: سَنَسَافِرُ _____ إِلى بَيروتَ (We will travel by car to Beirut)
items.explanation: بِالسَّيَّارَةِ (by car) uses بِ + definite noun in genitive to express means of transport. This is the standard Arabic way to say 'by [vehicle]': بِالطَّائِرَةِ (by plane), بِالقِطارِ (by train), بِالحافِلَةِ (by bus), بِالبَاخِرَةِ (by ship). سَنَسَافِرُ (we will travel) uses سَ for future + first person plural. بَيروتَ is diptote (genitive with fatḥa). Mode of transport vocabulary: سَيَّارَة (car), قِطَار (train), حَافِلَة (bus), تَاكسي (taxi), دَرَّاجَة (bicycle).

items.question: هَل يُمكِنُكَ أَن تَدُلَّني عَلى _____ إِلى المَتحَفِ؟ (Can you show me the way to the museum?)
items.explanation: الطَّريقِ (the road/way) is genitive after عَلى. تَدُلَّني (show/guide me) — Form I دَلَّ + ني (me), subjunctive after أَن (fatḥa on shadda). يُمكِنُكَ (is it possible for you — literally 'it is possible to you'). Navigation vocabulary: طَريق (road/way), شَارِع (street), تَقاطُع (intersection), إِشارَة مُرور (traffic light), دَوَّار (roundabout). Direction verbs: اِذهَبْ يَمينًا (go right), اِذهَبْ يَسارًا (go left), اِستَمِرَّ (continue straight).

items.question: الفُندُقُ _____ مِنَ المَطَارِ (The hotel is far from the airport)
items.explanation: بَعيدٌ (far) is the خبر in nominative. مِنَ المَطَارِ (from the airport) uses مِن + genitive for distance reference. الفُندُقُ (the hotel) is the مُبتدأ. Location adjectives with مِن: بَعيدٌ مِن (far from), قَريبٌ مِن (near to). Distance expressions: عَلى بُعدِ كيلومِتر (at a distance of a kilometer), يَبعُدُ عَن (is distant from). Accommodation vocabulary: فُندُق (hotel), غُرفَة (room), حَجز (reservation), اِستِقبال (reception), تَسجيل الدُّخولِ (check-in).

items.question: وَصَلنَا إِلى _____ في الوَقتِ المُحَدَّدِ (We arrived at the airport on time)
items.explanation: المَطَارِ (the airport) is genitive after إِلى. وَصَلنَا (we arrived) — نَا suffix marks first person plural. في الوَقتِ المُحَدَّدِ (on time — literally 'in the designated time') — المُحَدَّد is a passive participle from Form II حَدَّدَ (to determine/specify). مَطَار is a place noun (مَفعَل) from طَارَ (to fly). Airport procedures: تَسجيل/تَحقُّق (check-in), تَفتيش أَمني (security check), بَوَّابَة (gate), اِستِلام الأَمتِعَة (baggage claim).

items.question: سَنَستَقِلُّ _____ مِن هُنا إِلى وَسَطِ المَدينَةِ (We will take a taxi from here to downtown)
items.explanation: سَيَّارَةَ أُجرَةٍ (a taxi — literally 'hire car') is accusative as the direct object of سَنَستَقِلُّ (we will ride/take, Form X). It's an idāfa: سَيَّارَة (car, accusative) + أُجرَة (hire/fee, genitive). مِن هُنا (from here) → إِلى وَسَطِ المَدينَةِ (to the city center). اِستَقَلَّ (Form X: to ride/board) is formal — يَركَبُ is more colloquial. وَسَط (center/middle) is both a ظرف and a regular noun: في وَسَطِ (in the middle of).

items.question: يَجِبُ أَن نَمُرَّ عَبرَ _____ الأَمنِيِّ (We must pass through the security checkpoint)
items.explanation: نُقطَةِ التَّفتيشِ (the inspection/checkpoint point) is genitive — it's the مُضاف إليه of عَبرَ (through/across, actually عَبرَ here is a preposition taking genitive). Wait — عَبرَ functions as a preposition 'across/through,' taking genitive. التَّفتيشِ (inspection) is the مُضاف إليه of نُقطَة. الأَمنِيِّ (security-related, nisba adjective) modifies the whole compound. Airport security vocabulary: تَفتيش (inspection), جَواز سَفَر (passport), تَأشيرَة (visa), جُمرُك (customs).

items.question: القِطارُ يَتَأَخَّرُ عَن _____ بِعِشرينَ دَقيقَةً (The train is delayed by twenty minutes from its schedule)
items.explanation: مَوعِدِهِ (its schedule/appointment) is genitive after عَن. يَتَأَخَّرُ (it is delayed, Form V) from أَخَّرَ (to delay). بِعِشرينَ دَقيقَةً — عِشرين (twenty) is in genitive after بِ (takes ين- in all oblique cases), and دَقيقَةً (minute) is accusative as تَمييز العَدَد (number distinction — numbers 11-99 take singular accusative counted noun). Delay expressions: مُتَأَخِّر (delayed), في المَوعِدِ (on time), مُبَكِّر (early). Travel disruption vocabulary is practical for Arabic-speaking transit systems.

items.question: أَحتاجُ إِلى _____ غُرفَةٍ في الفُندُقِ (I need to book a room at the hotel)
items.explanation: حَجزِ (booking/reservation) is genitive after إِلى. It's also the first element of an idāfa: حَجزِ غُرفَةٍ (booking of a room). The double genitive — after إِلى and as مُضاف — means حَجز takes kasra. غُرفَةٍ (room) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. في الفُندُقِ (at the hotel) is locative. Accommodation verbs: حَجَزَ (to book), سَجَّلَ (to check in), غَادَرَ (to check out), أَلغَى (to cancel). The masdar حَجز after إِلى creates a need + action construction.

items.question: كَم تَبعُدُ المَسافَةُ مِن هُنا إِلى _____؟ (How far is it from here to the station?)
items.explanation: المَحَطَّةِ (the station) is genitive after إِلى. كَم (how much/many) is an interrogative. تَبعُدُ (it is distant/far) — from بَعُدَ. المَسافَة (the distance) is nominative as subject. مِن هُنا إِلى (from here to) creates a distance question. Direction/distance vocabulary: كَم كيلومِتر (how many kilometers), كَم ساعَة (how many hours), بِالتَّقريبِ (approximately), مَسافَة قَصيرَة (short distance), مَسافَة طَويلَة (long distance).

items.question: يُقلِعُ القِطارُ مِن _____ الثَّالِثِ (The train departs from platform three)
items.explanation: الرَّصيفِ (the platform) is genitive after مِن. الثَّالِثِ (the third) is an ordinal number adjective, agreeing in case (genitive), gender (masculine), and definiteness (definite). يُقلِعُ (departs, Form IV) — the same verb used for planes and trains. Ordinal numbers: الأَوَّل (first), الثَّاني (second), الثَّالِث (third)... up to العَاشِر (tenth). Station vocabulary: رَصيف (platform), سِكَّة (track/rail), قَاطِرَة (locomotive), عَرَبَة (carriage/car).

items.question: هَل هَذِهِ _____ تَذهَبُ إِلى المَدينَةِ القَديمَةِ؟ (Does this bus go to the old city?)
items.explanation: الحَافِلَةُ (the bus) is nominative as the مُبتدأ. هَذِهِ (this, feminine) correctly matches the feminine noun حَافِلَة. تَذهَبُ (it goes) uses the feminine prefix تَ-. إِلى المَدينَةِ القَديمَةِ (to the old city) — both noun and adjective are genitive, definite, feminine. Public transport vocabulary: حَافِلَة (bus), حَافِلَة صَغيرَة (minibus), ترَام (tram), مِترو (metro). Route questions: إِلى أَينَ تَذهَبُ؟ (where does it go?), كَم تَستَغرِقُ الرِّحلَةُ؟ (how long does the trip take?).

items.question: ضَاعَت _____ في المَطَارِ (My luggage was lost at the airport)
items.explanation: حَقيبَتي (my suitcase/luggage) is nominative — subject (فاعل) of ضَاعَت (was lost/got lost, feminine past). The ي possessive suffix hides the case ending but the grammatical role is still nominative. في المَطَارِ (at the airport) is locative. ضَاعَ (to be lost) is intransitive. Lost luggage vocabulary: حَقيبَة (suitcase), أَمتِعَة (luggage, plural of مَتاع), مَكتَب الأَمتِعَةِ المَفقودَةِ (lost baggage office), بَطاقَة الأَمتِعَة (luggage tag). This is a critical travel emergency scenario to practice in Arabic.

name: Technology (التكنولوجيا)
description: Modern technology vocabulary and grammar structures in Arabic

items.question: أَحتاجُ إِلى _____ جَديدٍ لِلعَمَلِ (I need a new computer for work)
items.explanation: حاسوبٍ (a computer) is genitive after إِلى. جَديدٍ (new) agrees in case (genitive), gender (masculine), and definiteness (indefinite). لِلعَمَلِ (for work) uses لِ + genitive. حاسوب comes from حَسَبَ (to calculate) — it literally means 'calculator.' Arabic technology terms often derive from classical roots: حاسوب (computer), طابِعَة (printer, from طَبَعَ to print), شاشَة (screen). Some regions prefer كُمبيوتر (borrowed from English).

items.question: هَل يُمكِنُكَ _____ هَذا المَلَفَّ؟ (Can you download this file?)
items.explanation: تَحميلُ (downloading) is a masdar (Form II of حَمَّلَ) functioning as the فاعل of يُمكِنُ (is possible). المَلَفّ (the file) is a passive participle from لَفَّ (to wrap/fold), used metaphorically for digital files. Arabic internet terminology blends native and borrowed words: تَحميل (download), رَفع (upload — literally 'lifting'), حَذف (delete — literally 'erasing'), نَسخ (copy — literally 'copying/transcribing'). يُمكِنُكَ + masdar is a polite ability expression.

items.question: الإِنتِرنِت _____ في بَعضِ المَناطِقِ (The internet is slow in some areas)
items.explanation: بَطيءٌ (slow) is the خبر in nominative. الإِنتِرنِت (the internet) is a borrowed word treated as masculine in Arabic. في بَعضِ المَناطِقِ (in some areas) — بَعض (some) is مُضاف, المَناطِق (areas/regions, broken plural of مِنطَقَة) is مُضاف إليه. Internet speed adjectives: بَطيء (slow), سَريع (fast), مُستَقِرّ (stable), مُنقَطِع (disconnected). Arabic handles borrowed tech terms by adding Arabic morphology: إِنتِرنِت gets ال for definiteness.

items.question: أَرسَلتُ _____ إِلكترونِيًّا إِلى المُديرِ (I sent an email to the manager)
items.explanation: بَريدًا (mail/email) is accusative — direct object of أَرسَلتُ (I sent). إِلكترونِيًّا (electronic) is a nisba adjective with borrowed root, agreeing in accusative. إِلى المُديرِ (to the manager) — المُدير is an active participle from أَدارَ (Form IV: to manage). Communication technology vocabulary: بَريد إِلكترونيّ (email), رِسالَة نَصِّيَّة (text message), مُكالَمَة مَرئِيَّة (video call), رِسالَة صَوتِيَّة (voice message). Arabic adapts foreign adjectives with nisba suffix.

items.question: يَجِبُ أَن تُحَدِّثَ _____ بِانتِظامٍ (You must update the software regularly)
items.explanation: البَرنامَجَ (the program/software) is accusative — direct object of تُحَدِّثَ (update, Form II subjunctive after أَن). تُحَدِّثَ comes from حَدَّثَ (to make new/update). بِانتِظامٍ (regularly) uses بِ + masdar. بَرنامَج is originally from Persian (بَرنامه) and forms a rare broken plural: بَرامِج (programs). Software terms: تَطبيق (app/application), نِظام تَشغيل (operating system), تَحديث (update), مُتَصَفِّح (browser — from تَصَفَّحَ Form V: to browse).

items.question: نَسيتُ _____ الخَاصَّةَ بِحِسابي (I forgot the password for my account)
items.explanation: كَلِمَةَ المُرورِ (the password — literally 'word of passage') is accusative as the direct object of نَسيتُ (I forgot). It's an idāfa: كَلِمَة (word, accusative) + المُرور (passage, genitive). الخَاصَّة (the specific/private) is an adjective modifying كَلِمَة and also accusative. بِحِسابي (for my account) — حِساب (account) with possessive ي. Security vocabulary: كَلِمَة مُرور (password), اِسم مُستَخدِم (username), تَوثيق (authentication), حِماية (protection).

items.question: _____ الذَّكِيَّةُ غَيَّرَت حَياتَنا (Smartphones changed our lives)
items.explanation: الهَواتِفُ (the phones) is nominative as the مُبتدأ. الذَّكِيَّةُ (smart, feminine singular) agrees with الهَواتِف — non-human broken plurals take feminine singular adjective agreement. غَيَّرَت (changed, Form II feminine past) also uses feminine singular for the same reason. حَياتَنا (our lives) is accusative direct object with possessive نا (our). هَواتِف is the broken plural of هاتِف (telephone — literally 'caller,' active participle from هَتَفَ to call out).

items.question: تَمَّ _____ التَّطبيقِ بِنَجاحٍ (The app was installed successfully)
items.explanation: تَثبيتُ (installation) is nominative — it's the فاعل of تَمَّ (was completed). تَمَّ + masdar is a passive-like construction meaning 'X was done.' التَّطبيقِ (the app) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. بِنَجاحٍ (successfully) uses بِ + masdar. تَثبيت is the masdar of Form II ثَبَّتَ (to install/fix), from ثَبَتَ (to be firm). App-related masdars: تَحميل (downloading), تَثبيت (installation), تَحديث (updating), حَذف (deletion). تَمَّ + masdar is extremely common in formal Arabic tech writing.

items.question: لا أَستَطيعُ _____ إِلى شَبَكَةِ الوَايفاي (I cannot connect to the WiFi network)
items.explanation: الاِتِّصالَ (the connection/connecting) is accusative — direct object of أَستَطيعُ (I can). اِتِّصال is the masdar of Form VIII اِتَّصَلَ (to connect). شَبَكَةِ (network) is genitive as مُضاف إليه after إِلى. الوَايفاي is a borrowed term. لا + present indicative = simple negation. Network vocabulary: شَبَكَة (network), إِشارَة (signal), اِتِّصال (connection), مَوجِّه (router — active participle of Form II وَجَّهَ to direct). Notice how شَبَكَة (net/web) comes from classical Arabic.

items.question: حَمَّلتُ _____ جَديدًا عَلى هاتِفي (I downloaded a new app on my phone)
items.explanation: تَطبيقًا (an application) is accusative — direct object of حَمَّلتُ (I downloaded, Form II). جَديدًا (new) agrees in accusative. عَلى هاتِفي (on my phone) — هاتِف with possessive ي. تَطبيق is the masdar of Form II طَبَّقَ (to apply), repurposed for 'app' in modern Arabic. حَمَّلَ (Form II: to load/download) vs حَمَلَ (Form I: to carry) — the intensive doubles the middle radical. App store vocabulary: تَطبيق مَجَّانيّ (free app), تَطبيق مَدفوع (paid app), تَقييم (rating).

items.question: يُمكِنُكَ حِفظُ _____ عَلى السَّحابَةِ (You can save the data on the cloud)
items.explanation: البَياناتِ (the data) is genitive — مُضاف إليه of حِفظ (saving). البَيانات is a sound feminine plural of بَيان (statement/datum), taking kasra in genitive. عَلى السَّحابَةِ (on the cloud) — سَحابَة means 'cloud' literally, now also used for cloud computing. حِفظ (saving/preserving) is the masdar of حَفِظَ (to save/memorize). Data vocabulary: بَيانات (data), مِلَفّ (file), مُجَلَّد (folder), نُسخَة اِحتِياطِيَّة (backup). Arabic elegantly repurposed سَحابَة for cloud technology.

items.question: الطَّابِعَةُ لا _____ بِشَكلٍ صَحيحٍ (The printer is not working properly)
items.explanation: تَعمَلُ (it/she works) uses the feminine prefix تَ- to match الطَّابِعَة (the printer, feminine). لا + present indicative = simple negation of current state. بِشَكلٍ صَحيحٍ (in a correct manner — properly) uses بِ + noun + adjective for adverbial meaning. الطَّابِعَة (printer) is an active participle of طَبَعَ (to print/stamp) with ة for instrument/machine nouns. Tech troubleshooting: لا تَعمَلُ (not working), مُعَطَّلَة (broken/out of order), بَطيئَة (slow), تَحتاجُ إِصلاحًا (needs repair).

items.question: شَحنُ _____ يَستَغرِقُ ساعَتَينِ (Charging the battery takes two hours)
items.explanation: البَطَّارِيَّةِ (the battery) is genitive as مُضاف إليه of شَحن (charging). شَحن is the masdar of شَحَنَ (to charge/load). يَستَغرِقُ (takes/requires — Form X) from غَرِقَ (to drown/sink). ساعَتَينِ (two hours) is accusative dual — the ين- ending for dual accusative/genitive. Battery vocabulary: شَحن (charging), بَطَّارِيَّة (battery — borrowed), شَاحِن (charger — active participle), مُنخَفِضَة (low). Time duration with يَستَغرِقُ is standard for tech contexts.

items.question: يَجِبُ _____ الجِهازَ قَبلَ الاِستِخدامِ (You must restart the device before use)
items.explanation: إِعادَةُ (restarting — literally 're-doing') is nominative as the فاعل of يَجِبُ (must). تَشغيلِ (operating) is genitive in an idāfa chain with إِعادَة. الجِهازَ (the device) is accusative — direct object of the implied action. قَبلَ الاِستِخدامِ (before use) is temporal. إِعادَة (re-doing) is the masdar of Form IV أَعادَ (to redo). تَشغيل (operating) is the masdar of Form II شَغَّلَ (to operate). Device verbs: تَشغيل (turning on), إِيقاف (turning off), إِعادَة تَشغيل (restart).

items.question: اِستَخدِمْ _____ لِحِمايَةِ بَياناتِكَ (Use encryption to protect your data)
items.explanation: التَّشفيرَ (the encryption) is accusative — direct object of the imperative اِستَخدِمْ (use! Form X). تَشفير is the masdar of Form II شَفَّرَ (to encrypt/encode), from شِفرَة (code/cipher). لِحِمايَةِ (for protecting) uses لِ + masdar. بَياناتِكَ (your data) is مُضاف إليه. Cybersecurity vocabulary: تَشفير (encryption), قِرصَنَة (hacking — from قُرصان pirate), فَيروس (virus — borrowed), جِدار ناريّ (firewall — literally 'fire wall'). Arabic creates elegant tech terms from classical roots.

name: Education & Learning (التعليم والتعلم)
description: Academic and educational vocabulary with Arabic grammar structures

items.question: يَدرُسُ _____ في الجَامِعَةِ (He studies medicine at the university)
items.explanation: الطِّبَّ (medicine) is accusative — direct object of يَدرُسُ (he studies). في الجَامِعَةِ (at the university) — الجَامِعَة (university) is an active participle-turned-noun from جَمَعَ (to gather), originally meaning 'the comprehensive/gathering [institution].' Academic subjects: الطِّبّ (medicine), الهَندَسَة (engineering), الحُقوق (law), الأَدَب (literature), العُلوم (sciences), الاقتِصاد (economics). Arabic uses the definite article with academic subjects when studying them generally: يَدرُسُ الطِّبَّ.

items.question: المُعَلِّمَةُ _____ الدَّرسَ لِلطُّلَّابِ (The teacher explains the lesson to the students)
items.explanation: تَشرَحُ (she explains) uses the feminine prefix تَ- to agree with المُعَلِّمَة (female teacher). الدَّرسَ (the lesson) is accusative direct object. لِلطُّلَّابِ (for/to the students) — الطُّلَّاب is the broken plural of طَالِب (student, active participle of طَلَبَ to seek). Education verbs: شَرَحَ (explained), عَلَّمَ (taught), دَرَسَ (studied), فَهِمَ (understood), حَفِظَ (memorized), اِمتَحَنَ (tested). SVO word order here requires full gender agreement on the verb.

items.question: نَجَحَ الطَّالِبُ في _____ النِّهائِيِّ (The student passed the final exam)
items.explanation: الاِمتِحانِ (the exam) is genitive after في. نَجَحَ في (passed/succeeded in) requires في + genitive — unlike English, Arabic doesn't directly transitize 'pass.' النِّهائِيِّ (final) is a nisba adjective agreeing in genitive, definite, masculine. اِمتِحان is the masdar of Form VIII اِمتَحَنَ (to test/examine). Exam vocabulary: اِمتِحان (exam), اِختِبار (test), نَتيجَة (result), عَلامَة/دَرَجَة (mark/grade), رُسوب (failure). نَجَحَ في is the standard Arabic expression for academic success.

items.question: يَجِبُ أَن تَقرَأَ _____ قَبلَ المُحاضَرَةِ (You must read the book before the lecture)
