Justice proverbs: كَما تَدينُ تُدانُ (as you judge, you will be judged), الظُّلمُ ظُلُماتٌ يَومَ القِيامَةِ (injustice is darkness on Judgment Day).

items.question: أَنا وَأَخي عَلى ابنِ _____ (My brother and I against my cousin — tribal solidarity proverb)
items.explanation: عَمّي (my paternal uncle) — possessive suffix ي on عَمّ. ابنِ عَمّي (my cousin — literally 'son of my uncle') is genitive after عَلى. The full proverb: أَنا وَأَخي عَلى ابنِ عَمّي، وَأَنا وَابنُ عَمّي عَلى الغَريبِ (I and my brother against my cousin; I and my cousin against the stranger). This describes tribal loyalty hierarchy. Kinship terms: عَمّ (paternal uncle), خَال (maternal uncle), ابن عَمّ (paternal cousin). This proverb encapsulates the traditional Arab social structure of concentric loyalty circles.

items.question: اِسأَل _____ قَبلَ أَن تَسأَلَ عَنِ الطَّريقِ (Ask an experienced person before you ask about the road)
items.explanation: مُجَرِّبًا (an experienced person) is accusative — direct object of the imperative اِسأَل (ask!). It's indefinite with tanwīn, meaning 'ask any experienced person.' قَبلَ أَن تَسأَلَ (before you ask — subjunctive after أَن). عَنِ الطَّريقِ (about the road). مُجَرِّب is an active participle of Form II جَرَّبَ (to experience/try). Experience proverbs: اِسأَل مُجَرِّبًا وَلا تَسأَل طَبيبًا (ask the experienced, not the doctor), التَّجرِبَةُ خَيرُ بُرهانٍ (experience is the best proof).

items.question: إِذا _____ الرِّياحُ فَاستَعِدَّ لِلأَمطَارِ (When the winds blow, prepare for the rains)
items.explanation: هَبَّتِ (they/it blew — feminine past with kasra for liaison) — feminine singular because الرِّيَاحُ (winds, broken plural → feminine singular agreement). إِذا takes past tense for future conditional meaning. فَاستَعِدَّ (then prepare! — فَ + imperative of Form X اِستَعَدَّ). لِلأَمطَارِ (for the rains — لِ + genitive). This proverb counsels preparedness: when warning signs appear, take action. Preparedness proverb: خُذ مِنَ الرَّخَاءِ لِلشِّدَّةِ (take from prosperity for hardship).

items.question: مَن _____ التَّاريخَ لا يُكَرِّرُ أَخطَاءَهُ (Who reads/studies history does not repeat its mistakes)
items.explanation: قَرَأَ (he read/studied) — past tense in مَن conditional clause expressing a timeless truth. التَّاريخَ (history) is accusative — direct object. لا يُكَرِّرُ (does not repeat, Form II) — present tense in the result clause. أَخطَاءَهُ (its mistakes — broken plural of خَطَأ + هُ possessive). Arabic intellectual proverbs: مَن لا ماضِيَ لَهُ لا مُستَقبَلَ لَهُ (who has no past has no future), التَّاريخُ يُعيدُ نَفسَهُ (history repeats itself). These reflect Arabic literary culture's profound engagement with historical memory.

name: Common Errors (الأخطاء الشائعة)
description: Frequently made mistakes by English speakers learning Arabic grammar

items.question: الكِتابُ _____ (The book is new — no verb 'is' needed)
items.explanation: جَديدٌ (new) is nominative — the خبر (predicate) in a nominal sentence. Arabic does NOT use a copula (verb 'to be') in present tense: الكِتَابُ جَديدٌ (The book [is] new). English speakers commonly add هُوَ or يَكونُ unnecessarily. The nominal sentence structure المُبتَدأ + الخَبَر directly links subject and predicate. Common error: inserting يَكونُ in present-tense descriptions. Correct patterns: الجَوُّ حَارٌّ (the weather is hot), البَيتُ كَبيرٌ (the house is big). Only use كَانَ for past tense.

items.question: ذَهَبَت _____ إِلى المَدرَسَةِ (The girls went to school — correct verb-subject agreement)
items.explanation: البَنَاتُ (the girls) is nominative — the فاعل (subject) coming after the verb. Common error: when the verb precedes a plural subject, it stays SINGULAR (ذَهَبَت not ذَهَبنَ). This is a key Arabic rule: verb-subject order = singular verb agreement. Only when subject precedes verb does the verb take plural form: البَنَاتُ ذَهَبنَ. English speakers expect plural verbs with plural subjects, but Arabic VSO structure demands: ذَهَبَ الأَولادُ (the boys went), ذَهَبَت البَنَاتُ (the girls went) — both with singular verbs.

items.question: هَذِهِ _____ جَميلَةٌ (This is a beautiful car — demonstrative with feminine noun)
items.explanation: سَيَّارَةٌ (a car) is indefinite nominative — the خبر after هَذِهِ (this, feminine demonstrative). Common error: adding ال to the noun after a demonstrative in a predicative sentence. When you mean 'this is a car,' the noun is indefinite: هَذِهِ سَيَّارَةٌ. But 'this car' (as subject) uses definite: هَذِهِ السَّيَّارَةُ. The distinction: هَذِهِ سَيَّارَةٌ (This is a car — جملة اسمية) vs هَذِهِ السَّيَّارَةُ جَميلَةٌ (This car is beautiful — demonstrative + defined noun as subject).

items.question: عِندي _____ إِخوَةٍ (I have three brothers — number-noun gender polarity)
items.explanation: ثَلاثَةُ (three — with tā' marbūṭa) because إِخوَة (brothers) is masculine: numbers 3-10 take the OPPOSITE gender of the counted noun (gender polarity). This is one of the most common errors for Arabic learners. With masculine nouns: ثَلاثَةُ رِجالٍ (three men), خَمسَةُ كُتُبٍ (five books). With feminine nouns: ثَلاثُ بَنَاتٍ (three girls), خَمسُ سَاعَاتٍ (five hours). The counted noun is always genitive plural after numbers 3-10.

items.question: الشَّمسُ _____ (The sun rose — sun is feminine in Arabic)
items.explanation: طَلَعَت (it rose — feminine) because الشَّمسُ (the sun) is feminine in Arabic. Common error: English speakers default to masculine verbs since 'sun' has no gender in English. Arabic nouns have inherent gender that must be memorized. Feminine nouns without تاء مربوطة: شَمس (sun), أَرض (earth), نَار (fire), حَرب (war), ريح (wind), يَد (hand), عَين (eye). Body parts that come in pairs are typically feminine. Always check a noun's gender as verb agreement depends on it.

items.question: هُوَ _____ مِن أَخيهِ (He is taller than his brother — comparative form error)
items.explanation: أَطوَلُ (taller) is the elative/comparative form (أَفعَل) of طَويل. مِن أَخيهِ (than his brother). Common error: using the regular adjective طَويل instead of the comparative أَطوَل, or adding أَكثَر before the adjective (English influence). Arabic comparatives use the أَفعَل pattern directly: كَبير → أَكبَرُ (bigger), جَميل → أَجمَلُ (more beautiful), ذَكِيّ → أَذكَى (smarter). The أَفعَل form is diptote (لا يُنوَّن — no tanwīn), and it never changes for gender or number in comparative use.

items.question: سَافَرتُ إِلى _____ (I traveled to France — diptote country name error)
items.explanation: فَرَنسَا (France) ends in alif maqṣūra — it's a diptote (مَمنوع مِنَ الصَّرفِ) foreign proper noun. After إِلى it takes fatḥa (not kasra) as genitive case: إِلى فَرَنسَا. Common error: adding kasra or tanwīn to diptote nouns. Diptote nouns get fatḥa instead of kasra in genitive, and never take tanwīn. Country names that are diptote: فَرَنسَا, أَلمَانيَا, بَريطَانيَا, إِيطَاليَا. Some countries take ال: العِرَاق, الأُردُنّ, المَغرِب. This is a major error source for learners.

items.question: الأَولادُ _____ في الحَديقَةِ (The boys are playing in the park — placing subject before verb)
items.explanation: يَلعَبونَ (they are playing — masculine plural) because الأَولادُ (the boys) precedes the verb, so the verb must agree in number AND gender. When subject comes FIRST (SVO): الأَولادُ يَلعَبونَ (boys play — plural verb). When verb comes FIRST (VSO): يَلعَبُ الأَولادُ (plays the boys — singular verb). Common error: using singular verb when subject precedes it. This agreement rule is one of the trickiest in Arabic grammar and a consistent source of mistakes for all learners.

items.question: قَرَأتُ _____ كُتُبٍ (I read many books — quantifier agreement error)
items.explanation: كَثيرًا مِن (a lot of) uses the accusative adverbial pattern + مِن + genitive. كُتُبٍ (books) is genitive after مِن. Common error: using كَثير as a direct adjective with a noun (*كُتُبٌ كَثيرَةٌ is correct but different — it means 'many books' as adjective). كَثيرًا مِنَ الكُتُبِ (many of the books — definite) vs كُتُبٌ كَثيرَةٌ (many books — adjective). Quantity expressions: كَثيرًا مِن (much/many of), قَليلًا مِن (a little of), بَعضَ (some of), مُعظَمَ (most of).

items.question: أُريدُ _____ أَذهَبَ (I want to go — subjunctive error)
items.explanation: أَن (to/that) followed by the subjunctive أَذهَبَ (fatḥa ending). Common error: omitting أَن and conjugating the second verb in indicative (*أُريدُ أَذهَبُ). In English, 'I want to go' puts 'to' before the infinitive. Arabic has no infinitive — it uses أَن + subjunctive. The verb after أَن changes the ending vowel: يَذهَبُ → أَن يَذهَبَ. Key subjunctive triggers: أَن (to), لَن (will not), كَي/لِكَي (in order to), حَتَّى (until/so that). Mastering أَن + subjunctive is essential for expressing desires, intentions, and purpose.

items.question: في _____ مَدينَةِ القَاهِرَةِ (In the city of Cairo — unnecessary definite article error)
items.explanation: مَدينَةِ (city of) is indefinite genitive — because the مُضاف (first part of idāfa) NEVER takes ال. Common error: adding ال to the مُضاف (*في المَدينَةِ القَاهِرَةِ). In idāfa, definiteness transfers from the مُضاف إليه: مَدينَةِ القَاهِرَةِ = 'the city of Cairo' (definite meaning, no ال on مَدينَة). Also wrong: adding tanwīn to مُضاف (*مَدينَةٍ القَاهِرَةِ). The مُضاف drops both ال and tanwīn. This idāfa rule is foundational and errors here instantly mark non-native speakers.

items.question: الكُتُبُ _____ عَلى الرَّفِّ (The books are on the shelf — inanimate plural agreement error)
items.explanation: مَوجودَةٌ (present/existing — feminine singular) because non-human plurals in Arabic take FEMININE SINGULAR agreement. الكُتُبُ (the books — broken plural) → مَوجودَة (not *مَوجودات or *مَوجودون). Common error: using plural adjectives with inanimate plural nouns. Correct: الكُتُبُ الجَديدَةُ (the new books — fem. sing. adj.), السَّيَّارَاتُ السَّريعَةُ (the fast cars). This rule applies to adjectives, verbs, and pronouns referring to non-human plurals: الأَشجَارُ كَبيرَةٌ (the trees are big).

items.question: لَم _____ إِلى المَدرَسَةِ أَمسِ (He did not go to school yesterday — jussive error)
items.explanation: يَذهَب (he goes — jussive mood with sukūn) after لَم. لَم negates past actions using present tense verb in JUSSIVE mood: يَذهَبُ → لَم يَذهَبْ (the final ḍamma drops to sukūn). Common errors: (1) using past tense after لَم (*لَم ذَهَبَ), (2) keeping indicative ending (*لَم يَذهَبُ), (3) using subjunctive (*لَم يَذهَبَ — that's for لَن). Jussive markers: لَم (did not), لَمَّا (not yet), لا + jussive (negative imperative), لِ (let/command). Each mood has distinct endings.

items.question: هَل أَنتَ مِن _____ ؟ (Are you from Egypt? — country with definite article error)
items.explanation: مِصرَ (Egypt) is diptote — it takes fatḥa in genitive (after مِن) and NEVER takes ال. Common error: adding ال to country names that don't require it. Countries WITHOUT ال: مِصر (Egypt), سوريَا (Syria), لُبنان (Lebanon), فِلَسطين (Palestine). Countries WITH ال: العِراق (Iraq), الأُردُنّ (Jordan), المَغرِب (Morocco), السُّودَان (Sudan). This distinction must be memorized — there's no reliable rule. مِصر is also diptote (مَمنوع مِنَ الصَّرف) as a proper noun/feminine place name.

items.question: أَعطَيتُ _____ الكِتَابَ (I gave him the book — attached pronoun error)
items.explanation: ـهُ (him — attached object pronoun) is directly suffixed to أَعطَى: أَعطَيتُهُ (I gave him). Common error: using the separate pronoun هُوَ (he) or adding لَهُ (to him) unnecessarily. Arabic verbs take direct attached pronouns as objects: كَتَبتُهُ (I wrote it), رَأَيتُها (I saw her), أَخبَرتُهُم (I told them). The attached pronouns are: ـنِي (me), ـكَ (you m.), ـكِ (you f.), ـهُ (him), ـها (her), ـنَا (us), ـكُم (you pl.), ـهُم (them). إِيَّاهُ is only used for emphasis or before أَن.

name: Advanced Negation (النفي المتقدم)
description: Complex negation patterns including لا النافية للجنس, ما الحجازية, and more

items.question: لا _____ في القاعَةِ (There is no student in the hall — لا النافية للجنس)
items.explanation: طَالِبَ (student — accusative without tanwīn) after لا النَّافِيَة لِلجِنسِ (لا that negates the entire genus/species). When لا negates a general class, its اسم (subject) is accusative and مَبنِيّ عَلى الفَتحِ (built on fatḥa — no tanwīn). This is different from لا + tanwīn (which would be لا طَالِبٌ — negating one instance). لا النَّافِيَة لِلجِنسِ works like إِنَّ: لا طَالِبَ = 'absolutely no student at all.' في القَاعَةِ (in the hall) is the خبر. This is equivalent to the emphatic English 'there is no student whatsoever.'

items.question: _____ لي بِهَذا الأَمرِ (I have nothing to do with this matter — ما الحجازية)
items.explanation: ما لِي شَأنٌ — here ما functions like لَيسَ (is not) in Hijazi Arabic dialect grammar adopted by Classical Arabic: ما + مُبتدأ + خبر. However, in the Hijazi usage (ما الحِجازِيَّة), ما acts like لَيسَ putting the خبر in accusative: ما هَذَا بَشَرًا (this is not a human, Quran 12:31). In Tamimi usage, ما doesn't affect case. بِهَذا (with this) — بِ is extra (حرف جر زائد). الأَمر (the matter). This is an advanced negation showing ما's versatile negation functions across different Arabic grammatical traditions.

items.question: لَن _____ عَن حَقّي (I will never give up my right — emphatic future negation)
items.explanation: أَتَنازَلَ (I give up — subjunctive with fatḥa) after لَن, which negates the future emphatically. لَن + subjunctive = 'will never/shall not.' The verb ending changes: أَتَنازَلُ (indicative) → أَتَنازَلَ (subjunctive). تَنَازَلَ (Form VI — mutual/assumed action: تَفَاعَلَ, here 'to cede/concede'). عَن حَقّي (about my right). Negation particles and their moods: لا يَفعَلُ (does not — indicative), لَم يَفعَلْ (did not — jussive), لَن يَفعَلَ (will not — subjunctive), لَمَّا يَفعَلْ (has not yet — jussive). Each requires a specific mood.

items.question: لَيسَ _____ سَهلًا (The exam is not easy — لَيسَ negation)
items.explanation: الاِمتِحانُ (the exam) is nominative — اسم لَيسَ (subject of لَيسَ 'is not'), which works like كَانَ: subject in nominative, predicate in accusative. سَهلًا (easy) is accusative — خبر لَيسَ. لَيسَ is a unique verb: it conjugates for person/gender/number but only expresses present-tense negation. Conjugation: لَستُ (I am not), لَستَ (you m. are not), لَستِ (you f.), لَيسَ (he/it is not), لَيسَت (she/it is not), لَسنا (we are not), لَستُم (you pl.), لَيسوا (they are not).

items.question: إِن لَم _____ فَسَوفَ أَذهَبُ وَحدي (If you don't come, I will go alone — conditional + negation)
items.explanation: تَأتِ (you come — jussive, with ي dropped) after لَم. The jussive of يَأتي/تَأتي drops the final ي: تَأتي → تَأتِ. إِن (if — conditional particle) + لَم + jussive = 'if you did not.' فَسَوفَ (then will — result clause). This shows two conditional structures interacting: إِن (conditional) + لَم (negation of past with jussive). Defective verbs (نَاقِص) ending in ي lose it in jussive: يَمشي → لَم يَمشِ, يَجري → لَم يَجرِ, يَبني → لَم يَبنِ. This is a common source of errors for intermediate learners.

items.question: ما كُنتُ _____ ذَلِكَ لَو عَلِمتُ (I would not have done that if I had known)
items.explanation: لِأَفعَلَ (to do — لام الجحود + subjunctive) after ما كَانَ/كُنتُ (was not). لام الجُحود (the لام of denial) is a special negation structure: ما كَانَ + لِ + subjunctive = 'would never have.' This is different from ordinary لِ (for/to). ما كُنتُ لِأَفعَلَ means 'I was never going to do / I would never have done.' لَو عَلِمتُ (if I had known) creates an unreal past conditional. This is an advanced negation pattern from Quranic/Classical Arabic used in formal Modern Standard Arabic for emphatic denial of past possibility.

items.question: لَمَّا _____ الطِّفلُ بَعدُ (The child has not walked yet — لَمَّا for 'not yet')
items.explanation: يَمشِ (he walks — jussive with dropped ي) after لَمَّا (not yet). لَمَّا works like لَم (negates past using jussive mood) but adds the meaning 'not yet' — implying the action may happen later. يَمشي → لَمَّا يَمشِ (has not yet walked). بَعدُ (yet — مَبنِيّ عَلى الضَّم because it lost its مُضاف إليه). Comparison: لَم يَمشِ (did not walk — completed negation) vs لَمَّا يَمشِ (has not yet walked — still possible). لَمَّا is common in both Classical and Modern Arabic for ongoing situations expected to change.

items.question: لا تَقُل _____ لَهُمَا أُفٍّ (Do not say to them even 'uff' — Quranic prohibition)
items.explanation: The Quranic verse (17:23): فَلا تَقُل لَهُمَا أُفٍّ (so do not say to the two of them 'uff'). لا (with jussive) creates a prohibition (نَهي): تَقولُ → لا تَقُلْ (don't say). فَ connects to the preceding context. لَهُمَا (to both of them — dual pronoun). أُفٍّ is an interjection expressing annoyance. This verse illustrates لا النَّاهِيَة (prohibitive لا) — different from لا النَّافِيَة (negating لا). Prohibitive لا + jussive = 'do not': لا تَفعَل (don't do), لا تَأكُل (don't eat), لا تَقُل (don't say).

items.question: ما _____ عِندَنا مِن شَيءٍ (We have absolutely nothing — emphatic negation with مِن)
items.explanation: بَقِيَ (remained) — past tense after ما (not). مِن شَيءٍ (of anything — مِن here is زائِدة 'extra/emphatic'). The مِن الزَّائِدَة adds emphasis to the negation: ما بَقِيَ شَيءٌ (nothing remained) → ما بَقِيَ مِن شَيءٍ (absolutely nothing remained at all). This extra مِن only occurs after negation and questions. Other examples: هَل عِندَكَ مِن كِتابٍ؟ (do you have any book at all?), ما جَاءَ مِن أَحَدٍ (absolutely no one came). This rhetorical emphasis creates stronger negation than simple ما + verb.

items.question: لا سَمَحَ _____ (God forbid! — formulaic negation)
items.explanation: اللهُ (God) is nominative — the فاعل (subject) of سَمَحَ (permitted/allowed). لا سَمَحَ اللهُ (God forbid! — literally 'May God not permit') is a formulaic prayer/wish using لا + past tense for a wish/prayer (لا الدُّعائِيَّة). This لا expresses a wish that something NOT happen — different from نَفي (negation) and نَهي (prohibition). Other formulaic wishes: لا قَدَّرَ اللهُ (may God not decree), لا حَولَ وَلا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ (there is no power except through God). These phrases are used daily in Arabic-speaking communities.

items.question: ما إِن وَصَلتُ _____ بَدَأَ المَطَرُ (No sooner had I arrived than the rain started)
items.explanation: حَتَّى (until/than) after ما إِن... = 'no sooner... than.' ما إِن is an emphatic temporal phrase: ما (not) + إِن (indeed) together create the meaning 'barely/no sooner.' وَصَلتُ (I arrived) is the first action. بَدَأَ المَطَرُ (the rain started) is the immediate second action. This is an advanced rhetorical structure (أُسلوب) showing how negation particles combine to create temporal meaning. Alternative structure: ما كَادَ... حَتَّى (hardly... when). These sophisticated temporal negations appear in literature and news.

items.question: لا _____ إِلَّا اللهَ (There is no god but God — لا النافية للجنس + exception)
items.explanation: إِلَهَ (god — accusative without tanwīn, مَبنِيّ عَلى الفَتحِ) after لا النَّافِيَة لِلجِنسِ. This is the first part of the Islamic declaration of faith (الشَّهادَة). إِلَّا (except) introduces the exception: اللهَ = إِلَّا اللهُ (except God — nominative as بَدَل from the position of اسم لا). The grammatical analysis of لا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللهُ has been debated by grammarians for centuries. The structure لا + noun (accusative no tanwīn) + إِلَّا + exception is a powerful Arabic construction combining absolute negation with exception.

items.question: غَيرُ _____ لَن يَحضُرَ (No one other than Ahmad will not attend — double negation confusion)
items.explanation: أَحمَدَ (Ahmad) is genitive (indicated by fatḥa because it's diptote — مَمنوع مِنَ الصَّرفِ as a proper noun on the أَفعَل pattern). غَيرُ (other than) is the مُضاف, أَحمَدَ is the مُضاف إليه. لَن يَحضُرَ (will not attend — لَن + subjunctive). غَير functions as an antonym/exception word: غَيرُ مُمكِنٍ (impossible), غَيرُ مَعقولٍ (unreasonable). أَحمَد is diptote because it follows the comparative أَفعَل pattern. Diptote proper names take fatḥa in genitive: مِن أَحمَدَ, إِلى عُمَرَ, مَعَ إِبراهيمَ.

items.question: بِلا _____ (Without a doubt / indisputably — بِلا + noun)
items.explanation: شَكٍّ (doubt — genitive with tanwīn) after بِلا (without). بِلا = بِ + لا, where لا is used as a negative prefix with nouns. The noun after بِلا takes genitive case (مَجرور). This is different from لا النَّافِيَة لِلجِنسِ which takes accusative. بِلا expressions: بِلا شَكٍّ (without doubt), بِلا سَبَبٍ (without reason), بِلا أَمَلٍ (without hope), بِلا حُدودٍ (without limits). Alternative constructions: بِدونِ شَكٍّ (without doubt), مِن دونِ شَكٍّ. The preposition بِ governs the genitive case on the noun.

items.question: لا _____ مِن المُحاوَلَةِ (There is no harm in trying — fixed expression)
items.explanation: بَأسَ (harm — accusative without tanwīn) after لا النَّافِيَة لِلجِنسِ. لا بَأسَ (no harm/it's okay) is one of the most common Arabic expressions. مِن المُحاوَلَةِ (in trying — Form III masdar). The اسم of لا النافية للجنس when it's a single indefinite noun (مُفرَد نَكِرَة) is مَبنِيّ عَلى الفتح (built on fatḥa, no tanwīn): لا بَأسَ, لا شَكَّ, لا رَيبَ, لا بُدَّ. But when it has an idāfa, it takes accusative with tanwīn: لا طَالِبَ عِلمٍ كَسولٌ. This distinction is crucial for advanced Arabic grammar.

name: Question Particles (أدوات الاستفهام)
description: Arabic interrogative particles and advanced question formation

items.question: _____ أَنتَ طَالِبٌ أَم مُعَلِّمٌ؟ (Are you a student or a teacher?)
items.explanation: أَ (the hamza of interrogation) is used for yes/no questions when followed by أَم (or) to offer alternatives. أَ...أَم creates 'either...or' questions: أَأَنتَ طَالِبٌ أَم مُوَظَّفٌ (are you a student or employee?). هَل cannot be used with أَم — this is a key distinction. أَ is the original Arabic interrogative particle from Classical Arabic. هَل is only for simple yes/no questions without alternatives. This أَم is called أَم المُتَّصِلَة (connected أَم), different from أَم المُنقَطِعَة (disconnected أَم meaning 'or rather').

items.question: _____ تَفعَلُ هُنا؟ (What are you doing here?)
items.explanation: مَاذا (what) asks about actions and things. ما and مَاذا both mean 'what' but have grammatical differences: ما + verb directly (ما تَفعَلُ), مَاذا + verb (مَاذا تَفعَلُ). مَاذا = ما + ذا (what is that which). هُنا (here) is a demonstrative adverb. Question word positions: Arabic question words go at the BEGINNING of the sentence, similar to English but unlike some languages. ما is more Classical, مَاذا more modern. ما can also be used in non-interrogative functions: ما الَّذي (that which), ما شَاءَ اللهُ (what God willed).

items.question: _____ ذَاكَرتَ لِلاِمتِحَانِ؟ (How did you study for the exam?)
items.explanation: كَيفَ (how) asks about manner/method. ذَاكَرتَ (you studied — Form III: ذَاكَرَ means 'to review/study'). لِلاِمتِحَانِ (for the exam — لِ + genitive). كَيفَ is مَبنِيّ عَلى الفَتحِ (indeclinable, built on fatḥa). It can function as: (1) interrogative: كَيفَ حَالُكَ (how are you?), (2) exclamatory: كَيفَ يَفعَلُ ذَلِكَ! (how can he do that!). كَيفَ + كَانَ: كَيفَ كَانَ الاِمتِحَانُ (how was the exam?). كَيفَ is one of the most versatile Arabic question words, used in greetings, inquiries, and rhetoric.

items.question: _____ سَتَسافِرُ إِلى لُبنَانَ؟ (When will you travel to Lebanon?)
items.explanation: مَتَى (when) asks about time. سَتَسافِرُ (you will travel — سَ + present tense for near future). إِلى لُبنَانَ (to Lebanon — diptote, fatḥa in genitive). مَتَى is مَبنِيّ عَلى السُّكونِ (indeclinable, built on sukūn). Time question words: مَتَى (when — general), أَيَّانَ (when — for future/serious matters, more formal), مُنذُ مَتَى (since when), حَتَّى مَتَى (until when). أَيَّانَ appears in Quranic language: أَيَّانَ يَومُ القِيامَةِ (when is the Day of Judgment?). مَتَى is universally used in all registers.

items.question: لِ_____ تَعَلَّمتَ العَرَبِيَّةَ؟ (Why did you learn Arabic?)
items.explanation: لِمَاذا (why — literally 'for what') = لِ (for) + مَاذا (what). تَعَلَّمتَ (you learned — Form V past tense). العَرَبِيَّةَ (Arabic — accusative, nisba adjective used as noun). لِمَاذا asks about reason/cause. Alternative 'why' words: لِمَ (contracted لِمَا — why), لِأَيِّ سَبَبٍ (for what reason — formal). Answering لِمَاذا: لِأَنَّ (because) + noun clause, لِكَي (in order to) + subjunctive, مِن أَجلِ (for the sake of). Arabic has multiple causal question forms reflecting different levels of formality.

items.question: _____ كِتَابٍ تُفَضِّلُ؟ (Which book do you prefer?)
items.explanation: أَيَّ (which — accusative) is the direct object of تُفَضِّلُ (you prefer — Form II). كِتَابٍ (book) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. أَيّ is the only interrogative that declines for case: أَيُّ (nominative), أَيَّ (accusative), أَيِّ (genitive). أَيُّ كِتَابٍ أَفضَلُ؟ (Which book is best? — nominative subject). أَيَّ كِتَابٍ تُريدُ؟ (Which book do you want? — accusative object). في أَيِّ مَدينَةٍ تَسكُنُ؟ (In which city do you live? — genitive after في). This case-changing property makes أَيّ unique among question particles.

items.question: _____ يَكونُ عَدَدُ السُّكَّانِ؟ (How many is the population?)
items.explanation: كَم (how many/how much) asks about quantity. يَكونُ (is — from كَانَ). عَدَدُ السُّكَّانِ (population count — idāfa). كَم has two types: (1) كَم الاِستِفهَامِيَّة (interrogative): كَم كِتَابًا قَرَأتَ؟ (how many books did you read?) — followed by تَمييز in accusative singular. (2) كَم الخَبَرِيَّة (informative/exclamatory): كَم كِتَابٍ قَرَأتُ! (so many books I've read!) — followed by genitive. The same word كَم changes its grammar based on whether it's asking or exclaiming.

items.question: _____ وَصَلتَ إِلى هَذَا الاِستِنتَاجِ؟ (How did you reach this conclusion?)
items.explanation: كَيفَ (how) asks about the method of reaching the conclusion. وَصَلتَ (you arrived/reached). إِلى هَذَا الاِستِنتَاجِ (to this conclusion — demonstrative + genitive). الاِستِنتَاج (conclusion/deduction — Form X masdar of اِستَنتَجَ). كَيفَ in academic/analytical context refers to process and methodology. Analytical questions: كَيفَ تَوَصَّلتَ (how did you arrive at), كَيفَ يُمكِنُ (how is it possible), كَيفَ نُفَسِّرُ (how do we explain). In formal Arabic, the how-question opens analytical discussions and research inquiries.

items.question: هَل مِن _____ يُسَاعِدُنَا؟ (Is there anyone to help us? — rhetorical question)
items.explanation: أَحَدٍ (anyone — genitive) after مِن الزَّائِدَة (emphatic مِن). هَل مِن أَحَدٍ = 'is there anyone at all?' The مِن here is extra (زائِدة), adding emphasis to the question. يُسَاعِدُنَا (helps us — with نَا object suffix). Without مِن: هَل أَحَدٌ يُسَاعِدُنَا (nominative). With emphatic مِن: هَل مِن أَحَدٍ (genitive). This مِن only appears in negative and interrogative contexts. Rhetorical questions: هَل مِن مُجيبٍ (is there anyone to respond?), هَل مِن مَزيدٍ (is there more?), هَل مِن سَبيلٍ (is there a way?).

items.question: أَلَيسَ _____ صَحيحًا؟ (Isn't this correct? — negative question expecting 'yes')
items.explanation: هَذَا (this — masculine singular demonstrative) is اسم لَيسَ in nominative. صَحيحًا (correct — accusative) is خبر لَيسَ. أَلَيسَ = أَ (interrogative hamza) + لَيسَ (is not). This creates a negative question expecting agreement: 'Isn't this correct? Yes, it is!' This rhetorical technique (الاِستِفهام الإِنكاري) uses negation to assert the opposite. Answering negative questions: بَلَى means 'yes, it IS' (confirming against the negation), while نَعَم after a negative question means 'yes, you're right, it isn't.' بَلَى vs نَعَم distinction is critical.

items.question: _____ تَجلِسُ في البَيتِ وَالطَّقسُ جَميلٌ؟ (How can you sit at home when the weather is beautiful? — أَ of reproach)
items.explanation: أَ (interrogative hamza — here used for reproach/denial, اِستِفهام إِنكاري) isn't a real question but a rebuke: 'How can you possibly sit at home?!' تَجلِسُ (you sit) is indicative. وَالطَّقسُ جَميلٌ (while the weather is beautiful — واو الحال + nominal clause). The hamza أَ has multiple rhetorical uses beyond simple questions: (1) اِستِفهام حَقيقي (genuine question), (2) إِنكاري (reproach), (3) تَقريري (confirmation), (4) تَعَجُّبي (wonder). Context determines which function applies. This versatility makes أَ a powerful rhetorical tool in Arabic.

items.question: مِمَّ _____ هَذِهِ الحَقيبَةُ؟ (What is this bag made of? — contracted question)
items.explanation: صُنِعَت (it was made — feminine passive) agrees with الحَقيبَةُ (the bag, feminine). مِمَّ = مِن + ما (from what?) — when ما is interrogative after a preposition, its alif is dropped and the letters merge: مِن + ما = مِمَّ, عَن + ما = عَمَّ, لِ + ما = لِمَ, في + ما = فيمَ, بِ + ما = بِمَ. These contractions are mandatory in formal Arabic, and using the uncontracted form (*مِن ما) is a grammatical error. This is a common area of confusion since relative ما keeps its alif: مِمَّا (from that which).

items.question: _____ الفَرقُ بَينَ هَذَينِ الكَلِمَتَينِ؟ (What is the difference between these two words?)
items.explanation: ما (what) asks about things/concepts. الفَرقُ (the difference — nominative as مُبتدأ). بَينَ هَذَينِ (between these two — dual demonstrative in genitive/accusative). الكَلِمَتَينِ (the two words — dual genitive after بَينَ). ما vs مَن: ما asks about non-human things, مَن asks about people. ما الفَرقُ (what is the difference), ما المَعنَى (what is the meaning), ما السَّبَبُ (what is the reason). مَن here would be wrong as we're asking about an abstract concept. Arabic maintains this human/non-human distinction consistently in its interrogatives.

items.question: بِ_____ تَسَافِرُ: بِالطَّائِرَةِ أَم بِالقِطَارِ؟ (By what means will you travel: by plane or train?)
items.explanation: بِمَاذا (by what/with what) = بِ + مَاذا. The preposition attaches to the question word. تُسَافِرُ (you travel). بِالطَّائِرَةِ (by plane) أَم (or) بِالقِطَارِ (by train). Questions with prepositions: بِمَاذا (with what), لِمَاذا (for what = why), إِلى أَينَ (to where), مِن أَينَ (from where), عَلى مَاذا (on what). The preposition always precedes the question word in Arabic, unlike English where it can be stranded at the end (*What did you travel by?). Arabic never strands prepositions.

items.question: أَيُّهُما _____ : القِراءَةُ أَم الكِتابَةُ؟ (Which of them is easier: reading or writing?)
items.explanation: أَسهَلُ (easier — elative/comparative, nominative) is the خبر of أَيُّهُما (which of the two — nominative as مُبتدأ). أَيُّهُما = أَيّ + هُمَا (dual pronoun suffix). القِراءَةُ أَم الكِتابَةُ (reading or writing — both nominative as clarifying apposition). أَسهَل follows the أَفعَل comparative pattern from سَهُلَ (to be easy). Comparative questions: أَيُّهُما أَفضَلُ (which is better), أَيُّهُم أَكبَرُ (which of them is bigger), أَيُّهُنَّ أَجمَلُ (which of them f. is more beautiful). The pronoun suffix on أَيّ specifies the group being compared.

name: Discourse Markers (علامات الخطاب)
description: Arabic connective words and phrases that structure speech and writing

items.question: _____ ، أَوَدُّ أَن أَشكُرَكُم (First of all, I would like to thank you)
items.explanation: بِدَايَةً (to begin with / first of all) is an adverbial accusative opening marker. أَوَدُّ أَن (I would like to — Form I) + أَشكُرَكُم (thank you — subjunctive after أَن + plural object كُم). Opening discourse markers: بِدَايَةً (to begin with), أَوَّلًا (firstly), في البِدَايَةِ (at the beginning), قَبلَ كُلِّ شَيءٍ (before everything). These markers organize speech and signal structure to the listener. Arabic formal speeches require clear signposting using these transition words more than English does.

items.question: الطَّقسُ جَميلٌ، _____ سَنَذهَبُ في نُزهَةٍ (The weather is nice, therefore we will go on an outing)
items.explanation: لِذَلِكَ (therefore/for that reason) = لِ (for) + ذَلِكَ (that). It connects cause and result. سَنَذهَبُ (we will go — سَ + present). في نُزهَةٍ (on an outing — genitive). Cause-result markers: لِذَلِكَ (therefore), لِهَذا (for this reason), مِن ثَمَّ (hence), بِالتَّالي (consequently), وَبِنَاءً عَلَى ذَلِكَ (based on that), إِذَن (then/so). These markers are essential for argumentative and academic writing in Arabic, showing logical connections between ideas.

items.question: أَنا أُحِبُّ القِراءَةَ، _____ أُفَضِّلُ الرِّوايَاتِ (I like reading, especially I prefer novels)
items.explanation: وَبِخَاصَّةٍ (and especially/particularly) = وَ + بِ + خَاصَّة (specialness — genitive after بِ). It specifies or narrows from general to particular. أُفَضِّلُ (I prefer — Form II). الرِّوَايَاتِ (novels — sound feminine plural accusative). Specification markers: بِخَاصَّةٍ / خَاصَّةً (especially), تَحديدًا (specifically), بِالأَخَصِّ (most particularly), لا سِيَّما (particularly — classical), وَعَلى وَجهِ الخُصوصِ (and in particular). These markers are crucial in academic and professional Arabic for narrowing scope.

items.question: الاِقتِصادُ يَنمو، _____ البَطالَةَ لا تَزالُ مُرتَفِعَةً (The economy is growing, however unemployment remains high)
items.explanation: إِلَّا أَنَّ (however/yet) introduces a contrast or exception to the preceding statement. البَطالَةَ (unemployment — accusative after أَنَّ). لا تَزالُ (still is — one of كَانَ's sisters indicating continuity). مُرتَفِعَةً (high — accusative as خبر لا تزال). Contrast/concession markers: إِلَّا أَنَّ (however), غَيرَ أَنَّ (but/yet), عَلى الرَّغمِ مِن (despite), مَعَ ذَلِكَ (nevertheless), بَيدَ أَنَّ (yet — formal). These function like English 'however/nonetheless' and are vital for balanced argumentation in Arabic.

items.question: _____ القَولِ، هَذَا المَشروعُ نَاجِحٌ (In short, this project is successful)
items.explanation: خُلَاصَةَ القَولِ (the summary of speech = in short) — خُلَاصَة is accusative as a مَفعول مُطلَق or ظرف-like expression. القَولِ (of speech/saying — genitive as مُضاف إليه). Summarizing markers: خُلَاصَةَ القَولِ (in short), بِإِيجَازٍ (briefly), بِاختِصَارٍ (in summary), مُجمَلَ القَولِ (the sum of speech), وَخِتامًا (and in conclusion). These summary markers signal the speaker/writer is wrapping up. Arabic academic writing particularly relies on these explicit transition markers to guide the reader.

items.question: أُحِبُّ السَّفَرَ. _____ ، أُحِبُّ التَّعَرُّفَ عَلى ثَقافَاتٍ جَديدَةٍ (I love travel. Moreover, I love getting to know new cultures)
items.explanation: عِلاوَةً عَلى ذَلِكَ (moreover/in addition to that) — عِلاوَة is accusative adverbial. عَلى ذَلِكَ (on top of that). التَّعَرُّفَ (getting to know — Form V masdar, accusative after أُحِبُّ). عَلى ثَقافَاتٍ (about cultures — genitive after عَلى). جَديدَةٍ (new — genitive adjective). Addition markers: عِلاوَةً عَلى ذَلِكَ (moreover), بِالإِضَافَةِ إِلى (in addition to), فَضلًا عَن (not to mention), كَذَلِكَ (likewise), أَيضًا (also). These additive connectors build cumulative arguments in Arabic.

items.question: _____ رَأيي، يَجِبُ أَن نَتَّخِذَ قَرارًا سَريعًا (In my opinion, we must make a quick decision)
items.explanation: في رَأيي (in my opinion) — في + رَأي (opinion, genitive) + ي (my). يَجِبُ أَن (it is necessary that — impersonal + subjunctive). نَتَّخِذَ (we make/take — Form VIII subjunctive). قَرارًا سَريعًا (a quick decision — both accusative). Opinion markers: في رَأيي (in my opinion), مِن وِجهَةِ نَظَري (from my point of view), أَعتَقِدُ أَنَّ (I believe that), أَرَى أَنَّ (I see that), في اعتِقادي (in my belief). Arabic academic style frequently uses these hedging markers to attribute opinions and maintain objectivity.

items.question: _____ يَتَعَلَّقُ بِالمَوضوعِ الثَّاني (As for what concerns the second topic)
items.explanation: فيمَا يَتَعَلَّقُ بِ (regarding/as far as... is concerned) — فيمَا = في + ما (in what). يَتَعَلَّقُ بِ (is related to — Form V). الموضوعِ الثَّاني (the second topic — both genitive). Topic-shift markers: فيمَا يَتَعَلَّقُ بِ (regarding), بِالنِّسبَةِ لِ (as for/regarding), أَمَّا بِالنِّسبَةِ لِ (as for), فيمَا يَخُصُّ (concerning). أَمَّا... فَ is the classical topic-fronting structure: أَمَّا التَّعليمُ فَهُوَ مُهِمٌّ (as for education, it is important). These markers organize multi-topic discussions.

items.question: لَقَد نَجَحنا _____ الصُّعوبَاتِ (We succeeded despite the difficulties)
items.explanation: رَغمَ (despite/in spite of) is a ظرف (adverb) taking a genitive مُضاف إليه: الصُّعوبَاتِ (the difficulties — sound feminine plural genitive). لَقَد (indeed — emphatic past marker). نَجَحنا (we succeeded). Concession markers: رَغمَ (despite), بِالرَّغمِ مِن (in spite of), عَلى الرَّغمِ مِن (despite), مَعَ أَنَّ (even though), رَغمَ أَنَّ (despite that). The difference: رَغمَ + noun (رَغمَ الصُّعوبَاتِ), رَغمَ أَنَّ + clause (رَغمَ أَنَّنا واجَهنا صُعوبَاتٍ). Mastering concessive markers elevates Arabic writing significantly.

items.question: يَجِبُ أَن نَقرَأَ كَثيرًا. _____ الحِكمَةِ، القِراءَةُ تُغَذّي العَقلَ (We must read a lot. The wisdom behind it is that reading nourishes the mind)
items.explanation: وَجهُ الحِكمَةِ (the face/aspect of wisdom = the wisdom behind it) — وَجه (face/aspect, nominative) + الحِكمَة (wisdom, genitive). The القِراءَةُ (reading) is مُبتدأ. تُغَذّي (nourishes — Form II feminine) العَقلَ (the mind, accusative). Explanation markers: وَجهُ الحِكمَةِ (the wisdom is), السَّبَبُ في ذَلِكَ (the reason for that), ما يُفَسِّرُ ذَلِكَ (what explains that), يَعودُ ذَلِكَ إِلى (that goes back to). These explanatory discourse markers help elaborate on claims and are common in instruction and persuasive Arabic texts.

items.question: _____ ناحِيَةٍ أُخرَى، هُنَاكَ فَوائِدُ كَثيرَةٌ (On the other hand, there are many benefits)
items.explanation: مِن نَاحِيَةٍ أُخرَى (from another side/aspect = on the other hand) — نَاحِيَة (side/aspect — genitive after مِن). أُخرَى (another — diptote feminine adjective). هُنَاكَ (there are). فَوائِدُ (benefits — broken plural of فَائِدَة, nominative). كَثيرَةٌ (many — feminine singular for non-human plural). Two-sided argument markers: مِن نَاحِيَةٍ... وَمِن نَاحِيَةٍ أُخرَى (on one hand... on the other), مِن جِهَةٍ... وَمِن جِهَةٍ أُخرَى (from one side... from another). These paired markers create balanced discussions.

items.question: هَذا المَقالُ مُفيدٌ، _____ أَنَّهُ طَويلٌ قَليلًا (This article is useful, except that it is a bit long)
items.explanation: غَيرَ أَنَّهُ (except that it — غَير is accusative here as مُستَثنى or ظرف). أَنَّهُ (that it) — أَنَّ + هُ pronoun. طَويلٌ (long — nominative as خبر أَنَّ). قَليلًا (a little — adverbial accusative). Qualification/reservation markers: غَيرَ أَنَّ (except that — formal), إِلَّا أَنَّ (but/however), سِوَى أَنَّ (except that), عَدَا (except/besides). These markers introduce minor reservations or qualifications to an otherwise positive statement. They are softer than لَكِنَّ (but) and more sophisticated in academic register.

items.question: _____ سَبيلِ المِثالِ، يُمكِنُ أَن نَذكُرَ ما يَلي (For example, we can mention the following)
items.explanation: عَلى سَبيلِ المِثالِ (by way of example = for example) — سَبيل (way/path) is genitive after عَلى, المِثَال (the example) is genitive as مُضاف إليه. يُمكِنُ أَن (it is possible to — impersonal + subjunctive). نَذكُرَ (we mention — subjunctive). ما يَلي (what follows — ما relative + يَلي present). Exemplification markers: عَلَى سَبيلِ المِثالِ (for example), مَثَلًا (for instance), كَ (like/such as), نَحو (approximately/such as), مِن ذَلِكَ (among that), كَأَن (as if/such as). Academic Arabic heavily uses these to support arguments with evidence.

items.question: أَمَّا السُّؤَالُ الثَّالِثُ _____ سَنُناقِشُهُ لاحِقًا (As for the third question, we will discuss it later)
items.explanation: فَ (then/so — correlative فَ after أَمَّا) is MANDATORY. أَمَّا... فَ is one of Arabic's most important discourse structures: أَمَّا introduces the topic, فَ introduces the comment. This is called التَّفصيل بَعدَ الإِجمال (detailing after generalizing). سَنُنَاقِشُهُ (we will discuss it — سَ + present + هُ object). لاحِقًا (later — adverbial accusative). The فَ after أَمَّا is not optional — omitting it is a serious grammatical error. أَمَّا...فَ structure: أَمَّا أَنا فَأُحِبُّ القِراءَةَ (as for me, I love reading).

items.question: _____ وَلَيسَ آخِرًا، أَشكُرُ فَريقَ العَمَلِ (Last but not least, I thank the work team)
items.explanation: أَخيرًا وَلَيسَ آخِرًا (lastly and not last = last but not least) — أَخيرًا (lastly — adverbial accusative from أَخير 'last'). وَلَيسَ آخِرًا (and not least/last — لَيسَ negation). أَشكُرُ (I thank). فَريقَ العَمَلِ (the work team — idāfa, accusative). Closing markers: أَخيرًا (finally), خِتَامًا (in conclusion), وَفي الخِتَامِ (and in closing), وَآخِرُ ما أَقولُهُ (the last thing I say). Arabic speeches and essays require explicit closing markers. The symmetric opening-body-closing structure is emphasized in Arabic rhetoric (عِلم البَلاغَة).

name: Register & Formality (السجل واللغة الرسمية)
description: Understanding different levels of formality and register in Arabic

items.question: _____ اللهِ الرَّحمَنِ الرَّحيمِ (In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful — formal opening)
items.explanation: بِسمِ (in the name of) is a special contracted form: بِ + اسم → بِسمِ (the alif of اسم drops when attached to بِ with genitive). اللهِ (God — genitive as مُضاف إليه). الرَّحمَنِ الرَّحيمِ (the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful — both genitive adjectives/appellations). This بَسمَلَة (invocation) opens virtually all formal Arabic documents, letters, speeches, and books. It is the most recognizable formal opening in Arabic. Using بِاسمِ instead of بِسمِ is less common but grammatically valid — بِسمِ is the established formula.

items.question: أَرجو _____ طَلَبي بِعَينِ الاِعتِبارِ (I request that you take my request into consideration — formal register)
items.explanation: أَخذَ (taking — masdar of أَخَذَ) is the direct object of أَرجو (I request/hope). Using the masdar instead of أَن + subjunctive is MORE formal: أَرجو أَخذَ (I request the taking) vs أَرجو أَن تَأخُذوا (I request that you take). طَلَبي (my request — accusative as مُضاف إليه of أَخذ). بِعَينِ الاِعتِبارِ (into consideration — literally 'with the eye of consideration'). Formal register prefers: masdars over verb clauses, passive over active, impersonal constructions over personal ones, يُرجَى over أَرجو.

items.question: تَفَضَّلوا بِ_____ (Please have a seat — polite imperative)
items.explanation: الجُلوسِ (sitting — masdar of جَلَسَ, genitive after بِ). تَفَضَّلوا بِ (please do/kindly — Form V imperative + بِ + masdar) is the elegant formal way to make requests. تَفَضَّلوا is more polite than the direct imperative اِجلِسوا (sit!). Politeness levels: (1) اِجلِس (sit! — direct/informal), (2) مِن فَضلِكَ اِجلِس (please sit — moderate), (3) تَفَضَّل بِالجُلوسِ (kindly have a seat — formal), (4) أَرجو التَّفَضُّلَ بِالجُلوسِ (I request you kindly sit — very formal). Arabic has well-defined politeness hierarchies.

items.question: يُعَدُّ هَذَا المَشروعُ _____ أَهَمِّ المُبادَرَاتِ (This project is considered one of the most important initiatives — formal passive)
items.explanation: مِن أَهَمِّ (one of the most important — مِن partitive + elative, genitive). يُعَدُّ (is considered — passive of عَدَّ, Form I of counted/reckoned) is the formal passive construction. هَذَا المَشروعُ (this project — nominative as نَائِب فاعِل). المُبادَرَاتِ (the initiatives — sound feminine plural genitive). Formal evaluation verbs: يُعَدُّ (is considered), يُعتَبَرُ (is regarded), يُصَنَّفُ (is classified), يُوصَفُ بِ (is described as). Academic Arabic heavily uses passive forms for objectivity: يُلاحَظُ أَنَّ (it is noted that), يُتَوَقَّعُ أَن (it is expected that).

items.question: _____ لَكُم جَزيلَ الشُّكرِ (We extend to you our deepest thanks — very formal gratitude)
items.explanation: نَتَقَدَّمُ (we advance/extend — Form V) is the formal way to express gratitude: نَتَقَدَّمُ بِجَزيلِ الشُّكرِ (we extend deep thanks). لَكُم (to you plural). جَزيلَ (deep/copious — accusative, مُضاف) الشُّكرِ (of thanks — genitive). Gratitude levels: شُكرًا (thanks — casual), أَشكُرُكَ (I thank you — neutral), أَتَقَدَّمُ بِالشُّكرِ (I extend thanks — formal), نَتَقَدَّمُ بِوَافِرِ الشُّكرِ وَالتَّقدِيرِ (we extend abundant thanks and appreciation — very formal). Arabic formal register requires 'performative' verbs that describe the act of thanking.

items.question: لا يَخفَى _____ أَنَّ الوَضعَ الرَّاهِنَ يَتَطَلَّبُ تَغييرًا (It is not hidden from you that the current situation requires change — formal academic)
items.explanation: لا يَخفَى عَلَيكُم (it is not hidden from you = you're well aware) — an impersonal negative construction for formal settings. يَخفَى (is hidden — Form I defective verb from خ-ف-ي). عَلَيكُم (from you). أَنَّ الوَضعَ (that the situation — accusative after أَنَّ). الرَّاهِنَ (the current — active participle adjective). يَتَطَلَّبُ (requires — Form V). تَغييرًا (change — accusative). Formal awareness phrases: لا يَخفَى عَلَيكُم (it's not hidden from you), كَمَا تَعلَمونَ (as you know), مِنَ المَعلومِ (it is known). These soften assertions by attributing knowledge to the audience.

items.question: تُحيطُكُم _____ عِلمًا بِأَنَّ المَوعِدَ قَد تَغَيَّرَ (We inform you that the appointment has changed — official notification register)
items.explanation: الإِدارَةُ (the administration — nominative as فاعِل). تُحيطُكُم... عِلمًا (informs you — literally 'surrounds you with knowledge') is the standard official notification formula. بِأَنَّ (that — بِ + أَنَّ for content). المَوعِدَ (the appointment — accusative after أَنَّ). قَد تَغَيَّرَ (has changed — قَد + past = completed action, Form V). Official notification formulas: يُحيطُكُم عِلمًا (informs you), يُفيدُكُم عِلمًا (notifies you), يُنهي إِلَيكُم (conveys to you). These impersonal administrative phrases are the backbone of Arabic bureaucratic communication.

items.question: هَلَّا _____ لي بِرَأيِكَ في المَوضوعِ? (Would you kindly share your opinion on the topic? — polite request)
items.explanation: أَدلَيتَ (you shared/gave — Form IV past of أَدلَى بِ 'to cast/give/share') after هَلَّا (would you/why don't you — هَل + لا in a polite request). هَلَّا + past tense is a polite request/suggestion, softer than the imperative. بِرَأيِكَ (with your opinion — بِ + رَأي genitive + كَ possessive). في المَوضوعِ (on the topic). Polite request forms (escalating formality): أَخبِرني (tell me), هَل يُمكِنُكَ (can you), هَلَّا أَخبَرتَني (would you tell me), أَرجو مِنكَ التَّفَضُّلَ بِ (I request you kindly to). هَلَّا is an elegant softener unique to Arabic.

items.question: مُلَخَّصُ _____ أَنَّ النَّتائِجَ إِيجَابِيَّةٌ (The summary of the matter is that the results are positive — academic register)
items.explanation: الأَمرِ (the matter — genitive as مُضاف إليه). مُلَخَّصُ (summary — passive participle of Form II لَخَّصَ, nominative as مُبتدأ). أَنَّ + clause is the خبر. النَّتائِجَ (the results — accusative after أَنَّ). إِيجَابِيَّةٌ (positive — nisba adjective + tā' marbūṭa, nominative as خبر أَنَّ). Academic summary phrases: مُلَخَّصُ الأَمرِ (the summary), حَاصِلُ القَولِ (the gist), خُلاصَةُ البَحثِ (the research conclusion). Arabic academic writing uses these noun-heavy constructions rather than verbal expressions: 'the summary is that' rather than 'to summarize.'

items.question: نَودُّ _____ سِيادَتَكُم بِأَنَّ المُهلَةَ قَد اِنتَهَت (We wish to inform Your Excellency that the deadline has expired — official register)
items.explanation: إِفادَةَ (informing — Form IV masdar, accusative as direct object of نَوَدُّ). سِيادَتَكُم (Your Excellency — accusative). بِأَنَّ (that). المُهلَةَ (the deadline — accusative after أَنَّ). قَدِ اِنتَهَت (has expired — Form VIII feminine past). Using the masdar إِفادَة instead of أَن نُفيدَ (that we inform) is MORE formal — official Arabic strongly prefers nominal/masdar constructions over verbal ones. Register difference: نُريدُ أَن نُخبِرَكُم (informal), نَوَدُّ إِفادَتَكُم (formal), يُشرِّفُنا إِفادَةُ سِيادَتِكُم (very formal).

items.question: جَزَاكَ اللهُ _____ (May God reward you well — Islamic formal thanks)
items.explanation: خَيرًا (with good — accusative as مَفعول بِهِ ثَانٍ, second direct object of جَزَى which takes two objects). جَزَاكَ (He rewarded you — past tense used as دُعَاء/prayer). اللهُ (God — nominative as فاعل). This is the most common Islamic thank-you expression, used across all registers from casual to highly formal. More elaborate versions: جَزَاكُمُ اللهُ خَيرَ الجَزَاءِ (may God reward you the best of rewards). Religiously inflected formulas pervade Arabic formal expression, even in secular contexts, since they carry cultural weight beyond religious meaning.

items.question: يُؤسِفُنا _____ أَنَّ طَلَبَكُم قَد رُفِضَ (We regret to inform you that your request has been rejected — formal refusal)
items.explanation: إِبلاغُكُم (informing you — Form IV masdar + كُم object, nominative as فاعل of يُؤسِفُنا). يُؤسِفُنا (it saddens us — Form IV impersonal + نَا object). أَنَّ طَلَبَكُم (that your request — accusative). رُفِضَ (was rejected — passive). In formal rejection letters, the masdar (إِبلاغ) is preferred over the verbal clause (أَن نُبلِغَ). Soft refusal formulas: يُؤسِفُنا (it saddens us), نَأسَفُ لِ (we regret), لِلأَسَفِ الشَّديدِ (with deep regret), يَتَعَذَّرُ عَلَينا (it is impossible for us). Arabic formal communication softens bad news with elaborate apologetic phrasing.

items.question: السَّلامُ عَلَيكُم _____ اللهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ (Peace be upon you and God's mercy and blessings — complete formal greeting)
items.explanation: وَرَحمَةُ (and the mercy of — nominative, coordinated with السَّلامُ). اللهِ (God — genitive as مُضاف إليه). وَبَرَكَاتُهُ (and His blessings — nominative, coordinated). The full greeting: السَّلامُ عَلَيكُم وَرَحمَةُ اللهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ. Three levels of Islamic greeting: (1) السَّلامُ عَلَيكُم (basic), (2) السَّلامُ عَلَيكُم وَرَحمَةُ اللهِ (with mercy), (3) full version (with blessings). Each level is considered more virtuous. Response: وَعَلَيكُمُ السَّلامُ وَرَحمَةُ اللهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ. This greeting opens all Arabic formal and informal interactions.

items.question: _____ حَضرَةَ المُحتَرَمِ (Dear Respected Sir — formal letter salutation)
items.explanation: سَيِّدي (my Sir/Sir — possessive ي adds formality and personal respect). حَضرَةَ (the presence of — accusative as مُنادَى or apposition). المُحتَرَمِ (the respected — genitive مُضاف إليه or adjective). Arabic letter salutation levels: حَضرَةَ السَّيِّدِ (to Mr. — neutral), سَعَادَةَ السَّفيرِ (to His Excellency the Ambassador), مَعَالي الوَزيرِ (to His Eminence the Minister), فَخَامَةَ الرَّئيسِ (to His Excellency the President). Titles and honorifics are essential in Arabic formal correspondence and reflect careful attention to social hierarchy.

items.question: حَفِظَكُم اللهُ _____ (May God protect you and care for you — formal letter closing prayer)
items.explanation: وَرَعَاكُم (and He cared for you — past tense used as prayer/دُعَاء). حَفِظَكُم (He protected you — also prayer form). اللهُ (God — nominative فاعل shared between both verbs). These past-tense verbs function as wishes/prayers (الماضي الدُّعَائِيّ): the past tense expresses certainty of the wish. Formal letter closing prayers: حَفِظَكُم اللهُ وَرَعَاكُم (may God protect and care for you), أَطالَ اللهُ بَقَاءَكُم (may God prolong your life), أَدَامَ اللهُ عِزَّكُم (may God perpetuate your honor). These are culturally essential in Arabic formal communication.

name: Advanced Coordinating Conjunctions (حروف العطف المتقدمة)
description: Master the full range of Arabic coordinating conjunctions and their nuanced usage

items.question: جَاءَ مُحَمَّدٌ _____ عَلِيٌّ مَعًا (Muhammad and Ali came together)
items.explanation: وَ (and) is the most fundamental coordinating conjunction (حرف عطف) in Arabic, indicating simple connection without implying order or sequence. It joins two nouns, verbs, or sentences. Here, عَلِيٌّ follows مُحَمَّدٌ in nominative because the معطوف (conjoined element) takes the same case as the معطوف عليه (the element it joins). مَعًا (together) confirms simultaneity. وَ is distinct from فَ (then/so) and ثُمَّ (then, with delay).

items.question: دَخَلَ الطُّلَّابُ _____ جَلَسوا فَورًا (The students entered and immediately sat down)
items.explanation: فَ (then/so, immediately after) indicates quick succession — the sitting followed the entering without delay. This is the key distinction from وَ (simple conjunction) and ثُمَّ (delayed sequence). فَ also carries a causal or consequential nuance: because they entered, they sat. فَورًا (immediately) reinforces the speed. In Arabic grammar, فَ is called حرف عطف يفيد الترتيب والتعقيب — it shows ordered sequence with immediate follow-up. This conjunction is critical for narrative Arabic.

items.question: أَكَلنَا الطَّعَامَ _____ شَرِبنَا القَهوَةَ (We ate the food then [after a while] drank coffee)
items.explanation: ثُمَّ (then, after a delay) indicates sequential order with a gap in time — they ate first, then after some time, they had coffee. Compare: فَ would imply immediate succession, وَ would imply no particular order. ثُمَّ is called حرف عطف يفيد الترتيب والتراخي — ordered sequence WITH delay. This is one of Arabic's most important temporal distinctions that doesn't exist in English. The three form a hierarchy: وَ (no order), فَ (ordered + immediate), ثُمَّ (ordered + delayed).

items.question: هَل تُريدُ شَايًا _____ قَهوَةً؟ (Do you want tea or coffee?)
items.explanation: أَم (or — in questions offering two specific choices) is used after هَل or أَ to present mutually exclusive options. This is different from أَو (or — in statements or open questions). أَم is called مُتَّصِلَة (connected) because it connects two specified alternatives in a question. Compare: أَتُريدُ شَايًا أَم قَهوَةً؟ (tea OR coffee — choose one) vs. هَل تُريدُ شَايًا أَو شَيئًا آخَرَ؟ (tea or something else — open). The أَم/أَو distinction is a hallmark of precise Arabic expression.

items.question: اِقرَأ كِتابًا _____ مَقالَةً (Read a book or an article)
items.explanation: أَو (or — in statements/non-exclusive) appears in non-interrogative contexts or when the choice is open/non-exclusive. Here it's an imperative suggestion — either option is fine. أَو can mean: التَّخيير (choice: اِجلِس أَو قِف — sit or stand), الإِباحَة (permission: كُل تُفَّاحًا أَو بُرتُقالًا — eat apple or orange, both OK), الشَّكّ (doubt: جَاءَ زَيدٌ أَو عَمرٌ — Zayd or Amr came, I'm not sure which). Context determines which meaning applies.

items.question: الجَوُّ جَميلٌ _____ بَارِدٌ (The weather is beautiful but cold)
items.explanation: لَكِنَّ (but/however) is an adversative conjunction showing contrast — the weather is beautiful, contrary to what you might expect, it's cold. لَكِنَّ is one of إِنَّ وَأَخَوَاتُها (إنّ and its sisters), so technically it puts its subject in accusative. But in this sentence it functions as a light conjunction لَكِنْ (with sukūn, not شدّة) connecting two predicates. The heavier لَكِنَّ introduces a full clause: الجَوُّ جَميلٌ لَكِنَّ البَردَ شَديدٌ. Adversative conjunctions add sophistication to Arabic expression.

items.question: مَا جَاءَ زَيدٌ _____ عَمرٌو (Not Zayd came, but rather Amr)
items.explanation: بَل (rather/but instead) is a corrective conjunction — it negates or replaces what came before. After negation (مَا جَاءَ زَيدٌ), بَل corrects: 'not Zayd, but Amr.' After affirmative: جَاءَ زَيدٌ بَل عَمرٌو means 'Zayd came — no, rather Amr came' (retracting the first statement). بَل is called حرف إِضراب (retraction particle). It's stronger than لَكِن because it completely replaces rather than merely contrasts. عَمرٌو takes nominative matching the معطوف عليه زَيدٌ.

items.question: نَجَحَ الطَّالِبُ _____ اجتَهَدَ كَثيرًا (The student succeeded because he worked hard)
items.explanation: إِذ (since/because/when) is a causal-temporal conjunction that can explain why something happened. Here it gives the reason: he succeeded BECAUSE he worked hard. إِذ can also be temporal: كُنتُ سَعيدًا إِذ رَأَيتُهُ (I was happy when I saw him). It's a ظرف زمان مَبنيّ (indeclinable time adverb) that often carries causal force. The sentence after إِذ provides the explanation or temporal backdrop. اجتَهَدَ (he strived, Form VIII) shows the effort that led to success.

items.question: لا تَأكُل كَثيرًا _____ تَمرَضَ (Don't eat too much lest you get sick)
items.explanation: فَ here is فَاءُ السَّبَبِيَّة (the فَ of causality/consequence) — after a negative imperative, it introduces the subjunctive verb indicating the feared consequence. تَمرَضَ is subjunctive (fatḥa ending) because فَ after a request/prohibition + أَن (implied) triggers subjunctive. This is different from فَ العاطِفة (conjunctive فَ). Arabic has multiple types of فَ: فَ العاطِفة (sequential), فَ السَّبَبِيَّة (causal), فَ الفَصيحَة (explanatory), and فَ الجَزاء (conditional response).

items.question: جَاءَ الأُستَاذُ وَ_____ المُديرُ (The teacher came and also the principal)
items.explanation: كَذَلِكَ (likewise/also) reinforces the conjunction وَ to emphasize that the principal ALSO came. While وَ alone would suffice, كَذَلِكَ adds emphasis meaning 'and likewise.' Arabic has several emphatic addition words: كَذَلِكَ (likewise), أَيضًا (also — adverbial), بِالإِضافَةِ إِلى (in addition to), فَضلًا عَن (moreover). كَذَلِكَ literally means 'like that' (كَ + ذَلِكَ). المُديرُ takes nominative matching الأُستَاذُ as the conjoined element through واو العطف.

items.question: سَأُسافِرُ غَدًا _____ بَعدَ غَدٍ (I will travel tomorrow or the day after)
items.explanation: أَو (or) is used in this declarative statement expressing uncertainty about timing. The speaker doesn't know the exact day. This is أَو للشَّكّ (or of doubt). Compare with أَم which requires a preceding interrogative particle. أَو connects two ظرف زمان (time adverbs): غَدًا and بَعدَ غَدٍ. بَعدَ غَدٍ (the day after tomorrow — literally 'after tomorrow') is an idāfa construction. In casual Arabic, أَو is the default 'or' — أَم is reserved specifically for disjunctive questions.

items.question: نَامَ الطِّفلُ _____ بَكَى كَثيرًا (The child slept after he cried a lot)
items.explanation: بَعدَما (after — temporal conjunction) connects two clauses where the first action (sleeping) follows the second (crying). بَعدَما = بَعدَ + ما (the masdariyya ما that turns a verb into a masdar). Similar compound conjunctions: قَبلَما (before), عِندَما (when), بَينَما (while), حينَما (when). These are technically ظرف + ما المصدرية. Unlike ثُمَّ which coordinates, بَعدَما subordinates — making the crying clause dependent. This distinction between coordination and subordination is essential in advanced Arabic.

items.question: لا أُريدُ لا شَايًا _____ قَهوَةً (I want neither tea nor coffee)
items.explanation: وَلا (nor/and not) creates a negative coordination: neither X nor Y. The structure لا...وَلا mirrors English 'neither...nor.' Both nouns take accusative as objects of أُريدُ. وَلا is the negative counterpart of وَ — it's a conjunction (واو العطف) combined with لا النَّافِيَة. Other negative correlation patterns: لَم...وَلَم (didn't...and didn't), لَن...وَلَن (won't...and won't). Arabic uses this double negation for emphatic denial, common in formal and literary Arabic.

items.question: اِجتَهِد في دِراسَتِكَ _____ تَنجَحَ (Study hard so that you succeed)
items.explanation: لِ (so that/in order to) is لام التَّعليل (the lām of purpose/cause) followed by the subjunctive: تَنجَحَ (you succeed, subjunctive with fatḥa). This is different from لام الأمر (imperative lām: لِيَذهَبْ — let him go, jussive). The purpose lām answers 'why?' — study hard WHY? So that you succeed. Other purpose particles: كَي (so that), لِكَي (in order that), حَتَّى (until/so that). All trigger the subjunctive mood. This purpose construction is fundamental for expressing goals and intentions.

items.question: حَضَرَ جَميعُ الطُّلَّابِ _____ طَالِبًا وَاحِدًا (All students attended except one student)
items.explanation: إِلَّا (except) is the primary exception particle in Arabic. After a complete affirmative sentence, the excepted noun (المُستَثنَى) takes accusative: طَالِبًا وَاحِدًا. This is الاستِثناء التَّامّ المُوجَب (complete affirmative exception). Compare: مَا حَضَرَ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا طَالِبٌ (no one attended except a student — after negation, the excepted can follow the case of the مُستَثنَى مِنه). Other exception words: غَير, سِوَى, عَدَا, خَلَا, حَاشَا. Exception is a rich grammar topic in Arabic.

name: Subordinating Conjunctions (أدوات الربط التابعة)
description: Arabic subordinating conjunctions that create dependent clauses and complex sentences

items.question: سَأَنتَظِرُ _____ تَعودَ (I will wait until you return)
items.explanation: حَتَّى (until/so that) is a subordinating conjunction that triggers the subjunctive: تَعودَ (you return, subjunctive with fatḥa). حَتَّى has three functions: 1) غاية (endpoint): سِرتُ حَتَّى المَدينَةِ (I walked until the city — preposition + genitive), 2) تَعليل (purpose): اِجتَهِدْ حَتَّى تَنجَحَ (study hard so you succeed — + subjunctive), 3) عاطِفة (conjunction — rare). Here it's temporal/purposive. The subjunctive after حَتَّى shows futurity and intent.

items.question: _____ جَاءَ المُعَلِّمُ سَكَتَ الطُّلَّابُ (When the teacher came, the students fell silent)
items.explanation: لَمَّا (when — for past events) introduces a temporal clause describing a specific past occurrence. Unlike إِذا (when/if — for future or habitual), لَمَّا is exclusively for completed past actions. لَمَّا + past tense + past tense is the pattern: لَمَّا وَصَلَ هَطَلَ المَطَرُ (when he arrived, rain fell). لَمَّا is called ظرف زمان for past events. It's different from لَمَّا النَّافِيَة (لَم + مَا = hasn't yet): لَمَّا يَصِل (he hasn't arrived yet). Context distinguishes the two.

items.question: _____ تَدرُسْ تَنجَحْ (If you study, you will succeed)
items.explanation: إِن (if — conditional particle) is a جازِمَة (jussive-triggering particle) that puts BOTH the condition verb and the response verb in jussive: تَدرُسْ (sukūn) and تَنجَحْ (sukūn). إِن is the primary conditional particle for real/possible conditions. Compare: إِذا (when/if — takes past tense, more certain), لَو (if — counterfactual/impossible conditions, takes past tense). The jussive mood after إِن is a hallmark of classical Arabic grammar and appears in formal/literary Arabic and the Quran.

items.question: _____ كُنتُ مَكانَكَ لَفَعَلتُ ذَلِكَ (If I were in your place, I would have done that)
items.explanation: لَو (if — counterfactual) introduces impossible or hypothetical conditions about the past or present. لَو + past tense + لَ + past tense is the pattern. كُنتُ مَكانَكَ (I were in your place) is unreal — the speaker ISN'T in that place. لَفَعَلتُ (I would have done) — the لَ is called لام الجواب (response lām). لَو is distinct from إِن (real/possible conditions) and إِذا (certain/expected conditions). لَو never implies the condition might be true, making it perfect for regret, wishes, and hypotheticals.

items.question: أُحِبُّ العَرَبِيَّةَ _____ لُغَةً جَميلَةً (I love Arabic because it is a beautiful language)
items.explanation: لِأَنَّها (because it is) combines لِ (for/because) + أَنَّ (that — one of إنّ's sisters) + ها (it/she). أَنَّ puts its subject in accusative — here the pronoun suffix ها serves as the subject. لُغَةً جَميلَةً (a beautiful language) is the خبر of أَنَّ in accusative. Causal subordinators: لِأَنَّ (because), بِسَبَبِ (because of), نَظَرًا لِ (given that), بِمَا أَنَّ (since). لِأَنَّ is the most common way to express cause in Modern Standard Arabic.

items.question: _____ كَانَ الأَمرُ صَعبًا فَلَن نَستَسلِمَ (Even though the matter is difficult, we will not give up)
items.explanation: مَهمَا (whatever/no matter what/even though) is a concessive-conditional particle. مَهمَا كَانَ الأَمرُ (whatever the matter may be) creates a concessive clause: regardless of difficulty, we won't surrender. مَهمَا is classified as اسم شرط جازم (conditional noun triggering jussive) but here with كَان it takes past tense. فَلَن (then will not) — the فَ is فاء الجزاء (response فَ). نَستَسلِمَ (we surrender, Form X: اِستَسلَمَ) is subjunctive after لَن. Concessive structures add rhetorical power.

items.question: لَن أَخرُجَ _____ يَتَوَقَّفَ المَطَرُ (I won't go out until the rain stops)
items.explanation: حَتَّى (until) here introduces a temporal endpoint — the going-out depends on the rain stopping. يَتَوَقَّفَ (it stops, Form V) is subjunctive after حَتَّى (fatḥa ending). The negative لَن + present = 'will not' in the main clause. حَتَّى + subjunctive always points to a future/anticipated event. Compare with حَتَّى + past tense (indicative): مَشَيتُ حَتَّى وَصَلتُ (I walked until I arrived — completed). This temporal subordination creates sophisticated sentence structures essential for fluent Arabic.

items.question: _____ دَرَستَ بِجِدٍّ حَصَلتَ عَلى نَتيجَةٍ جَيِّدَةٍ (Whenever you study seriously, you get a good result)
items.explanation: كُلَّما (whenever/every time that) introduces a repeated temporal-conditional clause. كُلَّ (every) + ما (what/that) creates an iterative conjunction: every time X happens, Y follows. Both verbs are in past tense (habitual past). كُلَّما is accusative as ظرف زمان (time adverb). بِجِدٍّ (seriously — with seriousness) uses بِ + masdar. حَصَلتَ عَلى (you obtained/got) takes عَلى for its complement. كُلَّما is indispensable for expressing habitual correlations and repeated patterns in Arabic.

items.question: تَعَلَّمتُ العَرَبِيَّةَ _____ أُسافِرَ إِلى مِصرَ (I learned Arabic in order to travel to Egypt)
items.explanation: لِكَي (in order to/so that) is a purpose conjunction triggering the subjunctive: أُسافِرَ (I travel, subjunctive with fatḥa). لِكَي = لِ (for) + كَي (that/so that) — both purpose particles combined for emphasis. Purpose particles in Arabic: لِ (to), كَي (so that), لِكَي (in order to), لِأَجلِ أَن (for the sake of). All require the subjunctive verb. مِصرَ (Egypt) is diptote (no tanwīn, genitive with fatḥa). Purpose clauses are subordinate — they depend on the main clause.

items.question: سَأُساعِدُكَ _____ أَنَّكَ صَديقي (I will help you since you are my friend)
items.explanation: بِمَا أَنَّ (since/given that) is a causal subordinator — stronger and more formal than لِأَنَّ. بِمَا = بِ + ما (by what), أَنَّ (that). The suffix كَ on أَنَّ is the accusative subject: أَنَّكَ (that you are). صَديقي (my friend) is the خبر of أَنَّ. Other formal causal expressions: نَظَرًا لِكَونِ (given that), بِالنَّظَرِ إِلى أَنَّ (considering that), مِن حَيثُ أَنَّ (in that). These elevated causal forms appear in academic writing, journalism, and formal speech.

items.question: _____ ذَهَبتَ فَأَنَا مَعَكَ (Wherever you go, I am with you)
items.explanation: أَينَما (wherever) is a conditional adverb of place using ما الشرطية (conditional ما added to أَينَ). It creates a spatial condition: in ANY place you go. أَينَما is a جازِم (jussive trigger) but with past tense here, the jussive is not visible. فَأَنَا مَعَكَ (then I am with you) — فَ is فاء الجزاء (response فَ, required when the response is a nominal sentence). Other conditional adverbs: حَيثُما (wherever), مَتَى (whenever), كَيفَما (however). They combine question words with ما for universality.

items.question: أَخبِرني _____ تَنتَهي (Tell me when you finish)
items.explanation: مَتَى (when) here functions as an indirect question word introducing a noun clause: 'tell me WHEN you finish.' مَتَى has dual roles: 1) interrogative (مَتَى تَعودُ؟ — when do you return?), 2) conditional (مَتَى تَدرُسْ تَنجَحْ — whenever you study, you succeed — jussive). Here it's an interrogative in indirect speech. تَنتَهي (you finish, Form VIII) is indicative since it's a reported question, not a conditional. Arabic indirect questions keep the question word but change intonation.

items.question: سَأَبقَى هُنَا _____ تَأتي (I will stay here while/as long as you come)
items.explanation: مَا دَامَ (as long as/while) introduces a conditional-temporal clause. مَا here is مَا المَصدَرِيَّة الظَّرفِيَّة (masdariyya/adverbial ما) — it turns the verb into a time expression. دَامَ (it lasted/continued) is one of كَانَ's sisters (الأَفعال النَّاقِصة): its subject is nominative, predicate accusative. But after ما المصدرية, the whole مَا دَامَ clause functions as a time adverb. This is a deeply grammatical construction showing how Arabic creates temporal clauses from incomplete verbs.

items.question: لا أُحِبُّ هَذا المَكانَ _____ أَنَّهُ مُزعِجٌ (I don't like this place because it is noisy)
items.explanation: لِ (because/for — causal preposition) followed by أَنَّ creates a causal clause: لِأَنَّهُ (because it is). أَنَّهُ = أَنَّ + هُ (pronoun suffix as subject of أَنَّ). مُزعِجٌ (noisy/annoying, active participle of Form IV أَزعَجَ) is the خبر of أَنَّ in nominative. This لِ is لام التَّعليل (lām of reasoning/cause). It can combine with: أَنَّ (لِأَنَّ — because), كَي (لِكَي — in order to), أَجل (لِأَجلِ — for the sake of). The causal لِ is one of Arabic's most frequent subordinating elements.

items.question: _____ أَنَّكَ مَريضٌ اِبقَ في البَيتِ (Since you are sick, stay at home)
items.explanation: بِمَا (since/given that) + أَنَّ creates the causal conjunction بِمَا أَنَّ. أَنَّكَ مَريضٌ = أَنَّ + كَ (your being sick) — the clause functions as the causal premise. اِبقَ (stay!, imperative of بَقِيَ — defective verb, the final ي drops in imperative). في البَيتِ (at home) is locative. بِمَا أَنَّ is more formal than لِأَنَّ and appears at sentence beginnings for greater rhetorical effect. It implies 'given the established fact that...' — suggesting the reason is undeniable and well-known.

name: False Friends (الأصدقاء الزائفون)
description: Arabic words that look or sound similar but have different meanings — common traps for learners

items.question: الوَلَدُ _____ الكِتابَ بِاهتِمامٍ (The boy read the book with interest)
items.explanation: قَرَأَ (he read) vs. قَرَّرَ (he decided, Form II) vs. قَارَنَ (he compared, Form III) vs. قَرَعَ (he knocked). All share some letters but have completely different meanings. قَرَأَ is Form I from root ق-ر-أ (reading). قَرَّرَ is Form II from ق-ر-ر (decision/settling) — the doubled middle letter changes meaning entirely. بِاهتِمامٍ (with interest/care) uses بِ + masdar of Form VIII اِهتَمَّ (to be interested). Distinguishing similar-sounding roots is crucial for accurate Arabic comprehension.

items.question: _____ الرَّجُلُ مِنَ الحُفرَةِ (The man came out of the hole)
items.explanation: خَرَجَ (he went out, intransitive) vs. خَرَّجَ (he graduated/trained someone, Form II transitive) vs. أَخرَجَ (he took out/extracted, Form IV transitive). The same root خ-ر-ج produces radically different meanings across verb forms. خَرَجَ مِن (went out from) is intransitive — the man moved himself. أَخرَجَ الشَّيءَ (took out the thing) is transitive — someone moved something. خَرَّجَ الطُّلَّابَ (graduated the students). This demonstrates how Arabic verb forms systematically modify base meanings.

items.question: _____ صَوتٌ غَريبٌ مِنَ الخَارِجِ (A strange sound was heard from outside)
items.explanation: سُمِعَ (was heard — passive voice) vs. سَمِعَ (he heard — active Form I) vs. سَمَّعَ (he made someone hear — Form II causative) vs. اِستَمَعَ (he listened attentively — Form X). The passive سُمِعَ is needed because صَوتٌ (a sound) is the نائب فاعل (deputy subject) — sounds are heard, not hearing. The vowel pattern distinguishes active فَعِلَ from passive فُعِلَ. غَريبٌ (strange) agrees with صَوتٌ. من الخَارِجِ (from outside). These four forms of سمع are a classic false-friend set.

items.question: هَذا _____ مُفيدٌ لِلصِّحَّةِ (This food is beneficial for health)
items.explanation: غِذاءٌ (nourishment/food — general) vs. غَداءٌ (lunch — midday meal). These two are classic false friends: غِذاء (with kasra on غ) means nutritious food/sustenance in general, while غَداء (with fatḥa on غ) means specifically lunch. The vowel difference is critical: غِ vs. غَ. عَشاءٌ (dinner) is a third meal word. مُفيدٌ (beneficial, active participle of Form IV أَفادَ) modifies the food. لِلصِّحَّةِ (for health) uses لِ + genitive. Vowel precision in Arabic is meaning-critical.

items.question: _____ الأُستاذُ الدَّرسَ لِلطُّلَّابِ (The teacher explained the lesson to the students)
items.explanation: شَرَحَ (he explained) vs. سَرَحَ (he wandered/daydreamed) vs. صَرَحَ (he declared openly) vs. فَرَحَ (he rejoiced). These four verbs differ by their first letter only but have completely unrelated meanings. شَرَحَ is from root ش-ر-ح (explaining/opening), سَرَحَ from س-ر-ح (roaming), صَرَحَ from ص-ر-ح (being frank), فَرَحَ from ف-ر-ح (happiness). Arabic's root system means changing one consonant creates an entirely new word family. This is why precise pronunciation matters.

items.question: الطَّبيبُ _____ المَريضَ بِعِنايَةٍ (The doctor examined the patient carefully)
items.explanation: فَحَصَ (he examined/inspected) vs. فَحَشَ — actually not a common verb, but فاحِش means obscene. فَخَرَ (he boasted/was proud). These consonant-swap pairs trap learners: ص and ش sound similar to non-native ears, as do خ and ح. فَحَصَ المَريضَ (examined the patient) is standard medical Arabic. بِعِنايَةٍ (with care) uses بِ + masdar. Arabic emphatic consonants (ص vs. س, ط vs. ت, ض vs. د, ظ vs. ذ) create meaning distinctions that must be mastered for clear communication.

items.question: لا تَخلِط بَينَ _____ وَالحَرامِ (Don't confuse the lawful and the forbidden)
items.explanation: الحَلالِ (the lawful/permissible) vs. الهِلالِ (the crescent moon) vs. الجَلالِ (the majesty/grandeur). These words differ by one consonant or vowel but have drastically different meanings. حَلال (ح-ل-ل: to be lawful) vs. هِلال (ه-ل-ل: crescent/new moon) vs. جَلال (ج-ل-ل: majesty). لا تَخلِط (don't confuse) is a negative imperative. بَينَ...وَ (between...and) is the standard comparison structure. This set shows how Arabic's minimal pairs create distinct semantic fields from similar sounds.

items.question: _____ المُعَلِّمُ الطُّلَّابَ مِن أَخطائِهِم (The teacher warned the students of their mistakes)
items.explanation: حَذَّرَ (he warned, Form II — transitive) vs. حَضَّرَ (he prepared, Form II) vs. حَذَرَ (he was cautious, Form I — intransitive) vs. حَظَرَ (he prohibited/banned). حَذَّرَ مِن (warned of) is Form II with shadda on ذّ. حَضَّرَ has shadda on ضّ — the ذ/ض distinction is crucial. حَذَرَ (without shadda) means 'he was cautious himself' (intransitive). حَظَرَ changes the middle letter entirely (ظ instead of ذ) meaning 'to ban.' Four near-identical words, four completely different meanings.

items.question: _____ الأَبُ بِنَجاحِ ابنِهِ (The father was happy about his son's success)
items.explanation: فَرِحَ (he rejoiced/was happy — verb with kasra on middle letter) vs. فَرَحَ (same root but less standard voweling) vs. صَرَحَ (he declared/stated openly) vs. مَرِحَ (he was cheerful/playful). فَرِحَ بِ (was happy about) takes بِ for the cause of joy. نَجاح ابنِهِ (his son's success) is a double idāfa: نَجاح (masdar, success) + ابن (son) + هِ (his). The pattern فَعِلَ (with kasra on the second radical) often indicates emotions: حَزِنَ (was sad), غَضِبَ (was angry), فَرِحَ (was happy).

items.question: يَجِبُ أَن _____ بَينَ الخَيرِ وَالشَّرِّ (One must distinguish between good and evil)
items.explanation: نُمَيِّزَ (we distinguish, Form II of مَيَّزَ — subjunctive after أَن) vs. نُمارِسَ (we practice, Form III) vs. نُمَزِّقَ (we tear up, Form II). مَيَّزَ (to distinguish) and مَزَّقَ (to tear) share the letters م-ز but have entirely different meanings and roots: م-ي-ز vs. م-ز-ق. بَينَ (between) connects الخَيرِ (good) وَالشَّرِّ (and evil). The vowel pattern in Form II (فَعَّلَ/يُفَعِّلُ) is consistent, so the root consonants determine meaning. Learners must focus on root letters.

items.question: الحَياةُ في المَدينَةِ _____ مِنَ القَريَةِ (Life in the city is different from the village)
items.explanation: مُختَلِفَةٌ (different, Form VIII participle) vs. مُخَلِّفَةٌ (leaving behind, Form II participle) vs. مُتَخَلِّفَةٌ (backward/underdeveloped, Form V participle) vs. مُخالِفَةٌ (violating/opposing, Form III participle). All four derive from root خ-ل-ف (behind/after/different) but through different verb forms: VIII اِختَلَفَ (to differ), II خَلَّفَ (to leave behind), V تَخَلَّفَ (to be backward), III خَالَفَ (to oppose). This is a perfect example of how Arabic verb forms create a family of related-but-distinct meanings.

items.question: _____ الطَّائِرَةُ في الوَقتِ المُحَدَّدِ (The plane landed on time)
items.explanation: هَبَطَت (it descended/landed, feminine past) vs. هَبَّت (it blew — for wind, with shadda). هَبَطَ (root ه-ب-ط: to descend) and هَبَّ (root ه-ب-ب: to blow/rise) look nearly identical but have unrelated meanings. The shadda on هَبَّت doubles the second consonant, changing the root entirely. في الوَقتِ المُحَدَّدِ (on time — in the specified time) uses a passive participle. Arabic's reliance on consonantal roots means even tiny differences (single vs. doubled consonant) create entirely different word families.

items.question: _____ الطِّفلُ بِأُمِّهِ عِندَ الخَطَرِ (The child clung to his mother in danger)
items.explanation: تَمَسَّكَ بِ (he clung to/held onto, Form V — reflexive of grasping) vs. تَماسَكَ (he held himself together/composed himself, Form VI — mutual) vs. أَمسَكَ (he seized/grabbed, Form IV — causative) vs. مَسَكَ (he held, Form I — basic). All from root م-س-ك (holding/grasping). Form V تَمَسَّكَ implies tight personal attachment. Form VI تَماسَكَ implies reciprocal holding = composure. Form IV أَمسَكَ implies seizing actively. Each verb form adds a specific semantic layer to the root meaning.

items.question: عَلَيكَ أَن _____ مِنَ التَّدخينِ (You should quit smoking)
items.explanation: تَمتَنِعَ (you refrain/abstain, Form VIII — subjunctive) vs. تَمتَعَ (you enjoy, Form V) vs. تَمنَعَ (you prevent, Form I) vs. تَتَمَنَّى (you wish, Form V from م-ن-ي). اِمتَنَعَ عَن/مِن (to abstain from) and مَنَعَ (to prevent/forbid) share root م-ن-ع but differ in transitivity and meaning. اِمتَنَعَ is intransitive (I abstain), مَنَعَ is transitive (I prevent someone). تَمَتَّعَ (to enjoy) is from a different root م-ت-ع. These near-homophone verbs are constant traps for Arabic learners.

items.question: _____ المُديرُ القَرارَ رَسمِيًّا (The director announced the decision officially)
items.explanation: أَعلَنَ (he announced/declared, Form IV) vs. عَلَّمَ (he taught, Form II) vs. أَعلَمَ (he informed, Form IV from different root) vs. عَلِمَ (he knew, Form I). أَعلَنَ is from root ع-ل-ن (openness/publicity), while عَلَّمَ/عَلِمَ are from root ع-ل-م (knowledge). Despite sharing two consonants (ع-ل), these are entirely different roots: ع-ل-ن vs. ع-ل-م. رَسمِيًّا (officially — nisba adverb from رَسم: official/formal) is accusative. This false-friend pair — أَعلَنَ vs. أَعلَمَ — differs by only one letter but means 'announced' vs. 'informed.'

name: Mixed Revision (مراجعة شاملة)
description: Comprehensive review mixing grammar topics to test integrated Arabic knowledge

items.question: كَتَبَ الطَّالِبُ _____ طَويلَةً (The student wrote a long essay)
items.explanation: مَقالَةً (an essay) is the direct object of كَتَبَ (he wrote), taking accusative with tanwīn fatḥa. طَويلَةً (long, feminine) is an adjective agreeing in case (accusative), gender (feminine), number (singular), and definiteness (indefinite). This tests: verb-object case marking, adjective agreement, and tā' marbūṭa. مَقالَة is a noun of time/place from قَالَ (to say) — literally 'a piece of saying.' The sentence follows VSO order with a transitive verb and its direct object.
