items.question: إِنَّ _____ مُجتَهِدونَ (Indeed the students are hardworking)
items.explanation: الطُّلَّابَ (the students) is accusative because إِنَّ puts its subject (اسم إنّ) in the accusative case. مُجتَهِدونَ (hardworking, masculine plural) is the خبر of إِنَّ in nominative. This tests إِنَّ's case governance — one of the most frequently tested grammar points. الطُّلَّاب is the broken plural of طَالِب (student), treated as human plural so it takes masculine plural agreement. المجتَهِد is an active participle from Form VIII اِجتَهَدَ (to strive/work hard).

items.question: الكِتابُ الَّذي _____ أَمسِ مُمتِعٌ (The book that I read yesterday is enjoyable)
items.explanation: قَرَأتُهُ (I read it) — the relative pronoun الَّذي (which/that) introduces a relative clause. Arabic relative clauses for DEFINITE antecedents require: 1) the relative pronoun matching the antecedent's gender/number, 2) a عائد (resumptive pronoun) referring back — here هُ (it). Without the هُ suffix, the clause is incomplete: *الكتاب الذي قرأتُ is incorrect because the verb needs an explicit object (the resumptive pronoun). أَمسِ (yesterday) is an indeclinable adverb. مُمتِعٌ (enjoyable, Form IV participle) is the main clause's خبر.

items.question: لَيسَ في الغُرفَةِ _____ (There is no one in the room)
items.explanation: أَحَدٌ (anyone/someone) is the اسم ليس (subject of ليس) in nominative — the normal position is after the predicate في الغُرفَةِ. لَيسَ is one of كان وأخواتها (incomplete verbs), putting its subject in nominative and predicate in accusative. Here في الغُرفَةِ is the fronted predicate (خبر مُقَدَّم). أَحَد in negative contexts means 'anyone/no one': مَا جَاءَ أَحَدٌ (nobody came), لَم أَرَ أَحَدًا (I didn't see anyone). This tests negation with ليس, word order flexibility, and case system.

items.question: كَانَ الجَوُّ _____ عِندَما غَادَرنَا (The weather was cold when we left)
items.explanation: بَارِدًا (cold) is the predicate of كَانَ — خبر كان is always accusative. الجَوُّ (the weather/atmosphere) is the اسم كان in nominative. عِندَما (when) introduces a temporal clause with غَادَرنَا (we left). This tests: كان's case governance, the active participle as predicate, and temporal clause construction. بَارِد is an active participle from بَرُدَ (to be cold). Multiple grammar concepts intersect here: incomplete verbs, participles, temporal conjunctions, and case marking.

items.question: هَذِهِ المَدينَةُ أَجمَلُ _____ تِلكَ (This city is more beautiful than that one)
items.explanation: مِن (than) is the preposition used in comparative constructions: أَفعَل + مِن = 'more X than.' أَجمَلُ (more beautiful) is the elative/comparative form on the pattern أَفعَل from جَميل. هَذِهِ (this, feminine) matches المَدينَةُ (the city, feminine). تِلكَ (that, feminine) is the distal demonstrative. This tests: comparative structure (اسم التَّفضيل + مِن), demonstrative agreement, and Arabic comparison. مِن is the ONLY preposition used for comparison in Arabic — using عَن or another preposition is incorrect.

items.question: لَم _____ الطُّلَّابُ مِنَ الامتِحَانِ (The students did not finish the exam)
items.explanation: يَنتَهِ (didn't finish — jussive) is required after لَم, which is a جازِمة (jussive particle). The original form is يَنتَهي (he finishes, Form VIII) — the final ي drops in jussive for defective verbs. Note: even though الطُّلَّابُ (students) is plural, the VSO Rule applies: when the verb precedes its subject, it stays singular: لَم يَنتَهِ الطلابُ (not لَم يَنتَهوا الطلابُ). This tests: لَم + jussive, defective verb conjugation, and VSO verb-number agreement.

items.question: أُحِبُّ المَشيَ _____ الشَّاطِئِ (I love walking along the beach)
items.explanation: عَلى (on/along) is the correct preposition for walking on surfaces: المَشيَ عَلى الشَّاطِئِ (walking on/along the beach). المَشيَ is a masdar (verbal noun) from مَشَى (to walk) in accusative as the direct object of أُحِبُّ (I love). الشَّاطِئِ (the beach) is genitive after عَلى. Preposition choice in Arabic is fixed and must be memorized: مَشَى عَلى (walked on), ذَهَبَ إِلى (went to), خَرَجَ مِن (went out from), جَلَسَ في (sat in). Incorrect prepositions change meaning entirely.

items.question: الطَّبيبَتانِ _____ في المُستَشفَى (The two doctors [f.] work at the hospital)
items.explanation: تَعمَلانِ (they two [f.] work) — SVO order requires full agreement: feminine dual. تَ- prefix (feminine) + -انِ suffix (dual) matches الطَّبيبَتانِ (the two female doctors, dual nominative with ان-). This tests: dual verb conjugation, feminine agreement, and SVO agreement rules. The dual form الطبيبتان = طبيبة + ة + ان. With VSO order, the verb would be singular: تَعمَلُ الطَّبيبَتانِ. But SVO requires full number agreement. The dual is the most tested agreement point in Arabic.

items.question: قَالَت لي _____ أُغلِقَ البَابَ (She told me to close the door)
items.explanation: أَن (to/that — masdariyya) + subjunctive: أُغلِقَ (I close, subjunctive after أَن). This أَن is أَن المَصدَرِيَّة — it turns the verb into a masdar meaning 'closing.' قَالَت لي أَن أُغلِقَ = she told me to close. Compare: إِنَّ (indeed — emphatic with accusative subject), أَنَّ (that — with accusative subject for reported facts), لَن (will not — negative future + subjunctive). These four particles all begin with أ/إ but have completely different functions. This tests the أَن/أَنَّ/إِنَّ distinction.

items.question: في المَدرَسَةِ _____ طُلَّابٍ كَثيرونَ (In the school there are many students)
items.explanation: هُنَاكَ (there is/are) is the Arabic existential expression. طُلَّابٍ (students) is the مُبتدأ مُؤَخَّر (delayed subject — but note the tanwīn showing indefiniteness). كَثيرونَ (many, masculine plural) is an adjective. في المَدرَسَةِ is a fronted prepositional phrase. هُنَاكَ literally means 'there' (place) but functions like English 'there is/are.' The sentence structure: prep phrase + هُنَاكَ + indefinite noun. This tests existential sentences, adjective agreement, and word order.

items.question: الأَولادُ _____ لَعِبوا في الحَديقَةِ (The boys who played in the garden)
items.explanation: الَّذينَ (who — masculine plural relative pronoun) matches الأَولادُ (the boys — masculine human plural). Arabic relative pronouns agree with their antecedent: الَّذي (masculine singular), الَّتي (feminine singular), اللَّذانِ (masculine dual), اللَّتانِ (feminine dual), الَّذينَ (masculine plural), اللَّواتي/اللَّاتي (feminine plural). This tests relative pronoun selection based on gender and number. لَعِبوا (they played) uses the masculine plural past tense suffix.

items.question: سَيَزورُنا _____ الأُسبوعِ القَادِمِ (He will visit us at the beginning of next week)
items.explanation: بِدايَةَ (at the beginning of) is a ظرف زمان (adverb of time) in accusative — temporal nouns used adverbially take fatḥa. الأُسبوعِ القادِمِ (next week) is genitive — الأُسبوع is مُضاف إليه, القادِمِ is its adjective agreeing in case. سَيَزورُنا (he will visit us) — سَ for future + يَزورُ + نا (us). This tests: ظرف زمان case marking, idāfa construction, adjective agreement in genitive, and future tense. Multiple grammar concepts converge in one natural sentence.

items.question: كُل مَا تُريدُ _____ لا تُبَذِّرْ (Eat what you want but don't waste)
items.explanation: لَكِن (but/however) introduces an adversative clause contrasting permission with restriction. كُل (eat! — imperative of أَكَلَ, irregular — drops the hamza). مَا تُريدُ (what you want — relative clause with ما الموصولة). لا تُبَذِّرْ (don't waste — negative imperative, jussive after لا الناهية). This sentence tests: irregular imperative, مَا as relative pronoun, adversative conjunction, negative imperative with jussive. تُبَذِّرْ (Form II: to squander) has sukūn in jussive.

items.question: مَرَّت ثَلاثُ _____ مُنذُ سَفَرِهِ (Three years have passed since his travel)
items.explanation: سَنَواتٍ (years) — in the number gender polarity rule, 3-10 take the OPPOSITE gender of the counted noun. سَنَة (year) is feminine, so the number ثَلَاثُ has NO tā' marbūṭa (masculine form for feminine counted noun). سَنَوات is genitive plural (مُضاف إليه of ثَلاث). مُنذُ (since) is a preposition/adverb. سَفَرِهِ (his travel) is genitive after مُنذُ. مَرَّت (passed, feminine) matches the grammatically feminine ثَلاثُ سَنَواتٍ as subject. This tests number-gender polarity, one of Arabic's trickiest rules.

name: Exam Strategies (استراتيجيات الاختبار)
description: Practice with tricky grammar patterns commonly tested in Arabic proficiency exams

items.question: _____ المُسلِمونَ إِلى مَكَّةَ لِلحَجِّ (The Muslims travel to Mecca for pilgrimage)
items.explanation: يُسافِرُ (he travels — singular) is correct because in VSO order, the verb ALWAYS takes singular form regardless of the subject's number. This is الفعل يأتي مفردًا إذا تقدم على الفاعل — the verb stays singular when it precedes its subject. المُسلِمونَ is plural but the verb is singular يُسافِرُ. If the sentence were SVO: المُسلِمونَ يُسافِرونَ. This VSO singular-verb rule is THE most tested grammar point in Arabic exams and catches most learners.

items.question: رَأَيتُ _____ يَلعَبونَ (I saw the boys playing)
items.explanation: الأَولادَ (the boys) is accusative as the direct object of رَأَيتُ (I saw). يَلعَبونَ (they play) is a حال جُملَة (circumstantial clause in verbal form) describing what the boys were doing when seen. This tests: direct object case marking, حال clause recognition, and the concept that verbs of perception (رَأَى, سَمِعَ, وَجَدَ) take both a direct object AND a حال. The sentence structure is: verb + subject (hidden) + object + حال. Multiple grammar layers in one short sentence.

items.question: مَا أَجمَلَ _____ ! (How beautiful the sky is!)
items.explanation: السَّماءَ (the sky) is accusative — in the exclamatory structure مَا أَفعَلَ (how [adjective]!), the المُتَعَجَّب مِنه (object of wonder) takes accusative. مَا here is not interrogative or negative — it's مَا التَّعَجُّبِيَّة (exclamatory ما). أَجمَلَ is a fixed past tense verb used for exclamation, NOT a comparative adjective. This construction (مَا أَفعَلَهُ!) is one of two standard exclamatory forms in Arabic. The other is: أَجمِل بِالسَّماءِ! (أَفعِلْ بِهِ). Exams frequently test this structure.

items.question: حَضَرَ خَمسَةَ _____ طَالِبًا (Fifteen students attended)
items.explanation: خَمسَةَ عَشَرَ (fifteen) — compound numbers 11-19 in Arabic follow specific gender rules. For masculine counted nouns (طَالِب is masculine): the units (خَمسَة) take OPPOSITE gender (feminine — with tā' marbūṭa), while عَشَر takes SAME gender (masculine — no tā' marbūṭa). For feminine counted nouns it reverses: خَمسَ عَشرَةَ. طَالِبًا (student) is the تمييز (specification) in accusative singular — numbers 11-99 take singular accusative counted nouns. Numbers are the most complex grammar topic in Arabic.

items.question: المَدرَسَةُ _____ ذَهَبتُ إِلَيها قَريبَةٌ (The school that I went to is nearby)
items.explanation: الَّتي (which/that — feminine singular) matches المَدرَسَةُ (the school, feminine). ذَهَبتُ إِلَيها (I went to it) — إِلَيها contains the resumptive pronoun ها (her/it) referring back to the school. Without this عائد (resumptive pronoun), the relative clause would be incomplete. قَريبَةٌ (nearby, feminine) is the main sentence's خبر. This tests: relative pronoun gender agreement, resumptive pronoun requirement in relative clauses, and nominal sentence structure. Exams love testing relative clauses.

items.question: لَولا المَطَرُ _____ (Were it not for the rain, we would have gone out)
items.explanation: لَولا (were it not for/if not for) introduces a counterfactual condition. المَطَرُ (the rain) is the مُبتدأ — after لَولا, the subject is nominative with a suppressed خبر (مَوجود/كائن is implied). لَخَرَجنا (we would have gone out) — the لَ is لام الجواب (response lām), and خَرَجنا is past tense. لَولا + nominative noun + لَ + past tense is the fixed pattern. لَولا is distinct from لَو (if — counterfactual) and إِن (if — possible). Exams test the response لَ which is mandatory.

items.question: جَاءَ الرَّجُلُ _____ (The man came walking)
items.explanation: مَاشِيًا (walking) is a حال (circumstantial accusative) describing the manner of the man's coming. The حال is ALWAYS: 1) indefinite (no ال), 2) accusative, 3) derived (adjective/participle). مَاشِيًا is an active participle from مَشَى (to walk) — defective verb, so the participle ends in ي. جَاءَ الرَّجُلُ (the man came) — the subject is definite, which is required for the صاحب الحال (the entity described by the حال). Exams frequently test حال recognition and its three conditions.

items.question: كِلَا _____ نَجَحا في الامتِحَانِ (Both students succeeded in the exam)
items.explanation: الطَّالِبَينِ (the two students) is genitive/accusative dual — كِلَا (both) is an إضافة construct that takes its مُضاف إليه in genitive. كِلَا is unique: it's dual in meaning but treated as singular in verb agreement: كِلَا الطَّالِبَينِ نَجَحَ (singular verb) OR نَجَحا (dual verb — also acceptable). The dual genitive/accusative ending is يْنِ-. كِلَا/كِلتَا (both, masculine/feminine) are among Arabic's trickiest words for case and agreement. They only appear before definite nouns or pronouns.

items.question: يَا _____ اجتَهِدوا في دُروسِكُم (O students, work hard in your lessons)
items.explanation: طُلَّابُ (students) — after يَا (O! — vocative particle), a noun WITHOUT ال takes ḍamma (nominative-like) when it's مُفرَد (non-idāfa, non-described). This is المُنادَى المُفرَد العَلَم (simple proper/common vocative). Compare: يَا طُلَّابَ المَدرَسَةِ (O students OF the school — accusative because it's idāfa). يَا أَيُّهَا الطُّلَّابُ uses the أَيُّهَا construction for definite vocative. Vocative case rules are exam favorites: مُفرَد = ḍamma, مُضاف = fatḥa.

items.question: لا طَالِبَ _____ في الفَصلِ (No lazy student in the classroom)
items.explanation: كَسولَ (lazy) — after لا النَّافِيَة لِلجِنسِ (لا of absolute negation), the اسم لا (noun after لا) takes fatḥa when it's مُفرَد (non-idāfa): لا طَالِبَ كَسولَ. Both the noun and its adjective take fatḥa (built on fatḥa — مبني على الفتح). لا النافية للجنس negates the entire category: 'no lazy student whatsoever' — more emphatic than مَا مِن طالبٍ كَسولٍ. The مبني على الفتح construction is distinct from regular case marking and is heavily tested in proficiency exams.

items.question: عِندي كِتابٌ _____ غِلافُهُ أَحمَرُ (I have a book whose cover is red)
items.explanation: No relative pronoun is needed! For INDEFINITE antecedents (كِتابٌ — a book, with tanwīn), Arabic uses a relative clause WITHOUT الَّذي. The clause غِلافُهُ أَحمَرُ (its cover is red) directly follows the indefinite noun. If the book were definite: الكِتابُ الَّذي غِلافُهُ أَحمَرُ. This is one of Arabic's most commonly tested rules: definite → use الَّذي/الَّتي, indefinite → omit the relative pronoun. The — option represents the zero-connector. This distinction doesn't exist in English and trips many learners.

items.question: تَعَلَّمَ العَرَبِيَّةَ _____ يَفهَمَ القُرآنَ (He learned Arabic in order to understand the Quran)
items.explanation: Wait — the correct form should be لِ + subjunctive: لِيَفهَمَ. But the blank is before يَفهَمَ. The answer لِيَ represents the لام + the beginning of the subjunctive verb. Actually, examining the options: لِيَ is the لام التعليل merged with the verb's يَ- prefix. The purpose lām (لِ) triggers subjunctive: يَفهَمَ (subjunctive with fatḥa). All four options (لِ, أَن, حَتَّى, كَي) can express purpose, but لِ is the most common and direct. Each triggers subjunctive differently. This tests purpose clause formation.

items.question: نِعمَ _____ الصِّدقُ (How excellent a quality honesty is!)
items.explanation: الخُلُقُ (the quality/character trait) — after نِعمَ (how excellent!/what a good...) and بِئسَ (how bad!), the فاعل (subject) must be: 1) definite with ال, 2) a مُضاف to definite, or 3) a تمييز (specification) in accusative. Here الخُلُقُ is nominative with ال as the فاعل of نِعمَ. الصِّدقُ (honesty) is the مَخصوص بِالمَدح (specified praised thing) — nominative as مُبتدأ مُؤَخَّر. نِعمَ/بِئسَ praise/blame constructions are classic exam questions testing multiple grammar layers.

items.question: أَيُّ _____ تُفَضِّلُ؟ (Which book do you prefer?)
items.explanation: كِتابٍ (of book — genitive) is the مُضاف إليه of أَيُّ (which). أَيُّ is a question word that forms an idāfa: أَيُّ كِتابٍ (which book — literally 'which of book'). The مُضاف إليه is ALWAYS genitive and typically indefinite after أَيّ: أَيُّ طالبٍ (which student), أَيُّ مَدينَةٍ (which city). أَيُّ itself takes the case of its grammatical role: here it's accusative (مفعول به of تُفَضِّلُ) but the fatḥa is hidden on the ي. This tests idāfa after interrogatives — a frequent exam trap.

items.question: اِشتَرَيتُ الكِتابَ بِ_____ دَنانيرَ (I bought the book for five dinars)
items.explanation: خَمسَةِ (five — with tā' marbūṭa, genitive) follows gender polarity: دَنانيرَ (dinars) is masculine, so the number 3-10 takes the OPPOSITE gender (feminine — with ة). The genitive case is because بِ (for/with) governs genitive: بِخَمسَةِ. دَنانيرَ (dinars) is a diptote broken plural (مَمنوع من الصَّرف — no tanwīn, genitive with fatḥa: دَنانيرَ not دَنانيرِ). This tests: number-gender polarity, case after preposition, diptote genitive, and the idāfa between number and counted noun — maximum grammar density.

name: Sentence Parsing — الإعراب (I'rāb)
description: Master Arabic grammatical analysis by identifying the correct case endings and syntactic roles of words

items.question: في جُملَة 'قَرَأَ الطَّالِبُ الكِتَابَ'، إِعرَابُ 'الكِتَابَ' هُوَ _____ (In the sentence 'The student read the book,' the parsing of 'the book' is _____)
items.explanation: الكِتَابَ is مَفعولٌ بِهِ (direct object) in the accusative case (مَنصوب). In the verbal sentence قَرَأَ الطَّالِبُ الكِتَابَ, the verb is قَرَأَ (read), the فَاعِل (subject) is الطَّالِبُ (nominative — مَرفوع), and الكِتَابَ receives the action — hence مَفعول بِهِ مَنصوب. The fatḥa on the final letter (الكِتَابَ) signals accusative case. Identifying the مَفعول بِهِ is fundamental to Arabic إعراب (syntactic parsing).

items.question: في جُملَة 'الجَوُّ جَميلٌ'، إِعرَابُ 'جَميلٌ' هُوَ _____ (In 'The weather is beautiful,' the parsing of 'beautiful' is _____)
items.explanation: جَميلٌ is the خَبَر (predicate) of the nominal sentence, raised to nominative (مَرفوع) with tanwīn ḍamma. In the nominal sentence الجَوُّ جَميلٌ, الجَوُّ is the مُبتدأ (subject) and جَميلٌ is the خبر. Both take nominative case. The خبر completes the meaning of the مُبتدأ. This is distinct from a نَعت (adjective modifier), which would follow a noun it describes rather than predicate about the مبتدأ. Nominal sentence parsing is the foundation of Arabic إعراب.

items.question: في جُملَة 'ذَهَبَ أَحمَدُ إِلى المَدرَسَةِ'، إِعرَابُ 'المَدرَسَةِ' هُوَ _____ (In 'Ahmad went to school,' the parsing of 'the school' is _____)
items.explanation: المَدرَسَةِ is اسمٌ مَجرورٌ (a noun in the genitive case) because it follows the preposition إِلى. Prepositions (حُروف الجَرّ) always put the following noun into the genitive case (مَجرور). The kasra on المَدرَسَةِ signals genitive. In Arabic إعراب, you must identify the عَامِل (governing element) — here إِلى is the عامل that causes the جَرّ. Common prepositions: في, مِن, إِلى, عَلى, بِ, لِ, عَن, كَ. Every noun after them is مَجرور.

items.question: في جُملَة 'إِنَّ العِلمَ نَافِعٌ'، إِعرَابُ 'العِلمَ' هُوَ _____ (In 'Indeed knowledge is beneficial,' the parsing of 'knowledge' is _____)
items.explanation: العِلمَ is اسمُ إِنَّ (the subject of إِنَّ) and takes accusative case (مَنصوب). إِنَّ and its sisters (أَنَّ, لَكِنَّ, لَيتَ, لَعَلَّ, كَأَنَّ) enter upon nominal sentences and put the مُبتدأ into accusative (becoming اسم إنّ) while the خبر remains nominative (becoming خبر إنّ). So نَافِعٌ is خبر إنّ مرفوع. The fatḥa on العِلمَ confirms accusative. This إنّ transformation is one of the most important إعراب rules in Arabic grammar.

items.question: في جُملَة 'كَانَ الوَلَدُ نَائِمًا'، إِعرَابُ 'نَائِمًا' هُوَ _____ (In 'The boy was sleeping,' the parsing of 'sleeping' is _____)
items.explanation: نَائِمًا is خَبَرُ كَانَ (the predicate of كَان) in the accusative case (مَنصوب). كَانَ and its sisters (أَصبَحَ, أَمسَى, ظَلَّ, بَاتَ, صَارَ, لَيسَ, ما زَالَ, ما دَامَ) enter upon nominal sentences and keep the مُبتدأ nominative (becoming اسم كان) while putting the خبر into accusative (becoming خبر كان). الوَلَدُ is اسم كان مرفوع. The tanwīn fatḥa on نَائِمًا confirms accusative. Distinguishing خبر كان from حال is a key إعراب skill.

items.question: في جُملَة 'يَلعَبُ الأَولَادُ سَعيدينَ'، إِعرَابُ 'سَعيدينَ' هُوَ _____ (In 'The boys play happily,' the parsing of 'happily' is _____)
items.explanation: سَعيدينَ is حَال (circumstantial accusative) describing the state of الأَولَاد while they play. الحَال answers the question 'كَيفَ' (how?) — how are they playing? Happily. الحال is always مَنصوب (accusative). The ين- ending marks accusative for sound masculine plurals. Key حال rules: it's indefinite (نَكِرَة), accusative, and describes the state of the subject or object. If سَعيدينَ were a نَعت, it would need to match the noun it modifies in definiteness — but الأَولَاد is definite while سَعيدين is indefinite.

items.question: في جُملَة 'شَرِبتُ كَأسًا مَاءً'، إِعرَابُ 'مَاءً' هُوَ _____ (In 'I drank a cup of water,' the parsing of 'water' is _____)
items.explanation: مَاءً is تَمييز (specification/distinction) in the accusative case. التمييز clarifies the ambiguity in a noun — كَأسًا (a cup) is vague: a cup of what? مَاءً (water) specifies the content. This is تمييز مُفرَد (single-word specification) as opposed to تمييز جُملَة (sentence-level). تمييز is always indefinite (نَكِرَة) and accusative (مَنصوب). Other examples: اِشتَرَيتُ رِطلًا تُفَّاحًا (I bought a pound of apples), عِندي عِشرونَ كِتَابًا (I have twenty books — كِتَابًا is تمييز العدد).

items.question: في جُملَة 'جَاءَ مُحَمَّدٌ وَأَحمَدُ'، إِعرَابُ 'أَحمَدُ' هُوَ _____ (In 'Muhammad and Ahmad came,' the parsing of 'Ahmad' is _____)
items.explanation: أَحمَدُ is مَعطوف (coordinated element) following the conjunction وَ, and it takes the same case as the مَعطوف عَلَيهِ (the element it's coordinated with) — مُحَمَّدٌ is فَاعِل مَرفوع, so أَحمَدُ is also مَرفوع. The rule: المَعطوف يَتبَعُ المَعطوفَ عَلَيهِ في الإعراب (the coordinated element follows in case). Coordination particles: وَ (and), فَ (then/so), ثُمَّ (then/next), أَو (or), أَم (or — in questions), بَل (rather), لَكِن (but), لا (not). This is a fundamental تَابِع (follower) concept.

items.question: في جُملَة 'قَابَلتُ الرَّجُلَ الطَّويلَ'، إِعرَابُ 'الطَّويلَ' هُوَ _____ (In 'I met the tall man,' the parsing of 'the tall' is _____)
items.explanation: الطَّويلَ is نَعت (adjective/qualifier) modifying الرَّجُلَ, taking the same case (accusative — مَنصوب). النَّعت must agree with its مَنعوت (qualified noun) in four things: الإعراب (case), العَدَد (number), الجِنس (gender), and التَّعريف (definiteness). Here: both are مَنصوب, مُفرَد (singular), مُذَكَّر (masculine), and مُعَرَّف بِال (definite with ال). If any of these four don't match, it's NOT a نعت — it might be حال or another syntactic role. النعت is one of the four تَوابِع (followers) in Arabic grammar.

items.question: في جُملَة 'زُرتُ مِصرَ القَاهِرَةَ'، إِعرَابُ 'القَاهِرَةَ' هُوَ _____ (In 'I visited Egypt, Cairo,' the parsing of 'Cairo' is _____)
items.explanation: القَاهِرَةَ is بَدَل (apposition/substitution) specifically بَدَل بَعض مِن كُلّ (a part substituting for a whole) — Cairo is part of Egypt. It takes the same case as المُبدَل مِنهُ (the word it substitutes for): مِصرَ is مَفعول بِهِ مَنصوب, so القَاهِرَةَ is also مَنصوب. Types of بَدَل: بَدَل مُطَابِق (identical substitution), بَدَل بَعض مِن كُلّ (part from whole), بَدَل اشتِمَال (inclusive substitution). البَدَل is the fourth تَابِع alongside النَّعت, العَطف, and التَّوكيد.

items.question: في جُملَة 'رَأَيتُ عِشرينَ طَالِبًا'، إِعرَابُ 'طَالِبًا' هُوَ _____ (In 'I saw twenty students,' the parsing of 'student' is _____)
items.explanation: طَالِبًا is تَمييز العَدَد (number distinction) — the counted noun after numbers 11-99 is always singular accusative (مُفرَد مَنصوب). عِشرينَ (twenty) is the number, طَالِبًا specifies what is being counted. This is a specific type of تمييز مُفرَد. Number-tamyīz rules: 3-10: plural genitive (طُلَّابٍ), 11-99: singular accusative (طَالِبًا), 100+: singular genitive (طَالِبٍ). The number عِشرين itself is مَفعول بِهِ مَنصوب (direct object of رَأَيتُ). Number tamyīz is heavily tested in Arabic exams.

items.question: في جُملَة 'سَافَرتُ لِطَلَبِ العِلمِ'، إِعرَابُ 'طَلَبِ' هُوَ _____ (In 'I traveled seeking knowledge,' the parsing of 'seeking' is _____)
items.explanation: طَلَبِ is اسمٌ مَجرورٌ (genitive noun) governed by the preposition لِ (lām al-ta'līl — the lām of purpose/reasoning). The لِ here expresses purpose: 'for the sake of.' طَلَبِ is also مُضاف to العِلمِ (genitive as مُضاف إليه). Note: if the sentence were سَافَرتُ طَلَبًا لِلعِلمِ, then طَلَبًا would be مَفعول لأجله مَنصوب. The difference: with لِ preposition → مَجرور; without preposition → مَنصوب as مفعول لأجله. This distinction is critical for accurate إعراب.

items.question: في جُملَة 'المُعَلِّمُ الَّذي يُحِبُّهُ الطُّلَّابُ نَاجِحٌ'، إِعرَابُ 'نَاجِحٌ' هُوَ _____ (In 'The teacher whom the students love is successful,' the parsing of 'successful' is _____)
items.explanation: نَاجِحٌ is the خَبَر (predicate) of the مُبتدأ المُعَلِّمُ, in nominative case (مَرفوع). The entire relative clause الَّذي يُحِبُّهُ الطُّلَّابُ is a نَعت (adjective clause) modifying المُعَلِّمُ — it's a جُملَة صِلَة (relative clause) and is NOT the خبر. This is a common إعراب trap: learners mistake the relative clause for the predicate. The sentence structure is: المُعَلِّمُ (مبتدأ) + الَّذي يُحِبُّهُ الطُّلَّابُ (نعت/جملة صلة) + نَاجِحٌ (خبر). Parsing complex sentences requires identifying all layers.

items.question: في جُملَة 'جَلَستُ حَيثُ جَلَسَ المُعَلِّمُ'، إِعرَابُ 'حَيثُ' هُوَ _____ (In 'I sat where the teacher sat,' the parsing of 'where' is _____)
items.explanation: حَيثُ is a ظَرفُ مَكَانٍ (adverb of place) in the accusative position (مَبنِيٌّ عَلى الضَّمِّ في مَحَلِّ نَصبٍ — it's indeclinable with ḍamma but occupies an accusative position). حَيثُ always requires a complete sentence after it — جَلَسَ المُعَلِّمُ is the مُضاف إليه clause. Arabic ظُروف المَكان: حَيثُ (where), فَوقَ (above), تَحتَ (below), أَمامَ (in front), خَلفَ (behind). Indeclinable words (مَبنِيّ) have a position (مَحَلّ) in إعراب even though they don't show case endings.

items.question: في جُملَة 'لَم يَذهَبْ أَحمَدُ'، إِعرَابُ 'يَذهَبْ' هُوَ _____ (In 'Ahmad did not go,' the parsing of 'go' is _____)
items.explanation: يَذهَبْ is فِعلٌ مُضارِعٌ مَجزومٌ (a present tense verb in the jussive mood) because of لَم. The jussive marker is السُّكون (sukūn — absence of vowel on the final letter). لَم is one of the جَوازِم (jussive particles) that put the مضارع into مَجزوم. Other جوازم: لَا النَّاهِيَة (prohibitive لا), لَمَّا (not yet), لِ الأمر (command لِ). The three moods of المضارع: مَرفوع (default), مَنصوب (after أَن, لَن, كَي, etc.), مَجزوم (after لَم, لا الناهية, etc.). Identifying the عامِل determines the mood.

name: Cultural Expressions (تعبيرات ثقافية)
description: Culturally embedded Arabic expressions used in daily life, greetings, and social interactions

items.question: عِندَما يُعطِسُ شَخصٌ نَقولُ لَهُ _____ (When someone sneezes we say to them _____)
items.explanation: يَرحَمُكَ اللهُ (May God have mercy on you) is the culturally correct response to someone who sneezes. The sneezer first says الحَمدُ لِلَّهِ (praise be to God), then the listener responds يَرحَمُكَ اللهُ, and the sneezer replies يَهديكُمُ اللهُ وَيُصلِحُ بَالَكُم (May God guide you and improve your condition). يَرحَمُ is present tense used as a prayer/wish (دُعَاء). This three-part exchange is deeply rooted in Islamic and Arabic culture and is used across all Arabic-speaking countries.

items.question: عِندَ رُؤيَةِ شَيءٍ جَميلٍ نَقولُ _____ (When seeing something beautiful we say _____)
items.explanation: مَا شَاءَ اللهُ (What God has willed / God has willed it) is said when admiring something beautiful, a child, a success, etc. It serves as both admiration and protection against العَين (the evil eye) in Arab culture. مَا here is a relative pronoun (what), شَاءَ means 'willed,' and اللهُ is the subject. Grammatically, this is a جُملَة صِلَة (relative clause). In contrast, إِن شَاءَ اللهُ (if God wills) refers to future events, and الحَمدُ لِلَّهِ (praise be to God) expresses gratitude. Each has specific cultural contexts.

items.question: عِندَ تَقديمِ الطَّعَامِ لِلضُّيوفِ نَقولُ _____ (When serving food to guests we say _____)
items.explanation: تَفَضَّلوا (Please, go ahead / Help yourselves) is the standard invitation when presenting food. It comes from Form V تَفَضَّلَ (to do the favor of, to be kind enough to) — literally asking guests to bestow the favor of eating. The plural imperative ending وا addresses multiple guests. For one person: تَفَضَّلْ (masculine) / تَفَضَّلي (feminine). بِالعَافِيَةِ and صِحَّتَينِ are said DURING or AFTER eating, not when serving. Arab hospitality (الضِّيافَة) is a cornerstone of Arabic culture, and these expressions reflect deep social values.

items.question: عِندَ سَمَاعِ خَبَرٍ سَيِّئٍ نَقولُ _____ (When hearing bad news we say _____)
options: لَا حَولَ وَلا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ
items.explanation: إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيهِ رَاجِعونَ (Indeed we belong to God and to Him we shall return) is the standard response to bad news, especially death. Grammatically: إِنَّا = إِنَّ + نَا (indeed we), لِلَّهِ (belong to God — لِ of possession), رَاجِعونَ (returnees — active participle plural, خبر إنّ). This Quranic verse (2:156) is used universally across Muslim-majority Arab societies. لَا حَولَ وَلا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ is used for general distress or helplessness, which is different.

items.question: عِندَ الانتِهَاءِ مِنَ الأَكلِ نَقولُ _____ (When finishing eating we say _____)
items.explanation: الحَمدُ لِلَّهِ (Praise be to God / All praise is due to God) is said after finishing a meal to express gratitude. Grammatically: الحَمدُ is the مُبتدأ (nominative), لِلَّهِ is the خبر (prepositional phrase — لِ + اللهِ in genitive). This expression is used in many contexts: after eating, after recovery from illness, when asked 'how are you?' (كَيفَ حَالُكَ؟ — الحَمدُ لِلَّهِ). بِالعَافِيَةِ (with wellness) is said BY OTHERS to the person eating, not by the eater. These distinctions are crucial for cultural fluency.

items.question: عِندَ دُخولِ بَيتِ شَخصٍ نَقولُ _____ (When entering someone's home we say _____)
items.explanation: السَّلامُ عَلَيكُم (Peace be upon you) is the proper greeting when entering someone's home. Grammatically: السَّلامُ is مُبتدأ (nominative), عَلَيكُم is خبر (prepositional phrase — upon you). The response is وَعَلَيكُمُ السَّلامُ (And upon you peace). This greeting is used universally in Arab culture — Muslim and Christian alike — when entering any home or meeting someone. أَهلًا وَسَهلًا (welcome — literally 'family and ease') and مَرحَبًا (hello) are informal greetings, not specifically for entering homes.

items.question: عِندَ التَّحَدُّثِ عَن شَيءٍ في المُستَقبَلِ نَقولُ _____ (When talking about something in the future we say _____)
items.explanation: إِن شَاءَ اللهُ (If God wills / God willing) is said whenever mentioning future plans or intentions. Grammatically: إِن is a conditional particle (حَرف شَرط), شَاءَ is the past tense verb (willed), اللهُ is the فاعل. This is culturally mandatory in Arabic conversation — omitting it when discussing future plans is considered presumptuous or impolite. It can also colloquially mean 'hopefully' or even a polite 'maybe.' بِإِذنِ اللهِ (by God's permission) is similar but more emphatic and affirming — suggesting stronger confidence in the outcome.

items.question: عِندَ تَهنِئَةِ شَخصٍ بِمُنَاسَبَةٍ سَعيدَةٍ نَقولُ _____ (When congratulating someone on a happy occasion we say _____)
items.explanation: مَبروكٌ (Congratulations! — literally 'blessed') is the standard congratulatory expression for weddings, births, graduations, new jobs, etc. مَبروك is a passive participle from بَارَكَ (Form III: to bless). The response is اللهُ يُبَارِكُ فيكَ (May God bless you) or عَلَيكَ بِالبَرَكَة. In formal Arabic, بَارَكَ اللهُ لَكَ (May God bless you with it) is preferred. The root ب-ر-ك is central to Arabic blessing culture: بَرَكَة (blessing), مُبَارَك (blessed — used for Ramadan: رَمَضَان مُبَارَك), تَبَارَكَ (exalted be He).

items.question: عِندَ الاعتِذارِ نَقولُ _____ (When apologizing we say _____)
items.explanation: أَعتَذِرُ (I apologize — Form VIII from عَذَرَ to excuse) and عَفوًا (pardon — from عَفَا to pardon) are the standard apology expressions. أَعتَذِرُ is more formal (a full verb), while عَفوًا is casual and versatile — used for bumping into someone, interrupting, or minor mistakes. آسِف (sorry — active participle of أَسِفَ to regret) is also common. For deeper apologies: سَامِحني (forgive me), أَرجو المَعذِرَة (I seek pardon). Each level reflects the gravity of the situation. Arabic social etiquette values proper use of these graded expressions.

items.question: عِندَ تَوديعِ شَخصٍ مُسَافِرٍ نَقولُ _____ (When seeing off a traveler we say _____)
items.explanation: في أَمَانِ اللهِ (In God's protection/safety) is the traditional farewell specifically for travelers. Grammatically: في is a preposition, أَمَانِ (safety/protection) is مَجرور, اللهِ is مُضاف إليه. This expression asks for divine protection during the journey. مَعَ السَّلامَةِ (with safety/goodbye) is a general farewell, and إِلى اللِّقَاءِ (until we meet — similar to 'au revoir') is neutral. The traveler may respond: اللهُ يُسَلِّمَك (May God keep you safe). These layered farewells reflect Arabic culture's emphasis on invoking protection for those departing.

items.question: عِندَ رُؤيَةِ شَخصٍ مَريضٍ نَقولُ _____ (When visiting a sick person we say _____)
items.explanation: سَلامَتَك (Your safety / Get well soon — literally 'your wellbeing') is the standard expression when visiting or addressing a sick person. سَلامَة is a masdar from سَلِمَ (to be safe/healthy), and the كَ is the possessive pronoun 'your.' The response is اللهُ يُسَلِّمَك (May God keep you safe). For more formal contexts: شَفاكَ اللهُ (May God heal you — using شَفَى Form I: to heal), or عَافاكَ اللهُ (May God grant you wellness). Visiting the sick (عيَادَة المَريض) is considered a social obligation in Arabic culture.

items.question: عِندَ شُربِ القَهوَةِ عِندَ العَرَبِ يُقالُ لِلمُضيفِ _____ (When drinking coffee among Arabs, one says to the host _____)
items.explanation: دَائِمًا (Always / May it always be so) is the traditional response to the host when offered Arabic coffee, wishing that the host always has the means and generosity to offer coffee. The host may respond اللهُ يُديمُ عِزَّكَ (May God perpetuate your honor). This exchange is central to Arab hospitality culture, especially in Gulf and Levantine traditions. دَائِمًا is grammatically an adverb (ظرف زمان) in accusative from دَوَامَ (perpetuity). Arabic coffee culture has specific etiquette: pour with the right hand, serve elders first, shake the cup to signal enough.

items.question: عِندَ بِدَايَةِ الأَكلِ نَقولُ _____ (When starting to eat we say _____)
items.explanation: بِسمِ اللهِ (In the name of God) is said before starting any meal. Grammatically: بِ is a preposition (in/with), اسمِ is مَجرور (genitive — the alif is dropped in writing by convention), اللهِ is مُضاف إليه. This expression is also said before any important action: starting a journey, beginning work, entering a house. The full form is بِسمِ اللهِ الرَّحمَنِ الرَّحيمِ (In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful). الحَمدُ لِلَّهِ is said AFTER eating, not before. This sequence — بسملة before, حمدلة after — is fundamental to Arabic cultural practice.

items.question: عِندَ التَّعبيرِ عَن الإِعجابِ بِشَخصٍ نَقولُ _____ (When expressing admiration for someone we say _____)
items.explanation: بَارَكَ اللهُ فيكَ (May God bless you) is used to express admiration, gratitude, or as a protective blessing. بَارَكَ is Form III (to bless — mutual pattern but used here as a prayer). فيكَ (in you) uses في + second person pronoun. This expression serves double duty: it expresses sincere admiration while also protecting the admired person from العَين (the evil eye) — reflecting the Arabic cultural belief that praise without invoking God's blessing may cause harm. Variations: اللهُ يَحفَظَك (May God protect you), اللهُ يُعين (May God help).

items.question: عِندَ شُكرِ شَخصٍ عَلى هَدِيَّةٍ نَقولُ _____ (When thanking someone for a gift we say _____)
items.explanation: جَزَاكَ اللهُ خَيرًا (May God reward you with goodness) is the most culturally rich way to express deep gratitude. Grammatically: جَزَى (to reward — Form I), كَ is the direct object (you), اللهُ is the فاعل, خَيرًا is a second مَفعول بِهِ or تمييز (with goodness). While شُكرًا (thanks) is common, جَزَاكَ اللهُ خَيرًا carries deeper cultural significance — it invokes divine reward for the giver, which is considered the highest form of thanks in Arabic culture. The response is وَإِيَّاكَ (and you too) or آمين (amen).
